- While gaining energy, the electron undergoes **isotropic scattering** (acoustic phonon, optical phonon).
- A fraction of electrons are scattered with a velocity component **toward the gate oxide** (momentum redirection).

### **Stage 3 — Energy Must Exceed the Barrier**
- The electron must still have $\mathcal{E} > \phi_B^n = 3.15\,\text{eV}$ at the instant it reaches the interface.
- The **probability** of this event is governed by the **Lucky Electron Model (LEEM)**.

### **Stage 4 — Injection + Interface State Creation**
- Upon entering the oxide, the electron is collected by the gate (forms **gate current $I_G$**) **or** becomes trapped in the oxide, creating **fixed oxide charge $Q_{ox}$** and **interface states $N_{it}$**.
- The **net result**: $V_{TH}$ shifts, $g_m$ degrades, $I_{D,sat}$ reduces.

## 2.2 Hot Electron vs. Hot Hole — Critical Distinction

| Property | Hot Electron (CHE) | Hot Hole (DAHC) |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Carrier type | electron (NMOS) | hole (PMOS) |
| Required energy | ≥ 3.15 eV | ≥ 4.68 eV |
| Dominant in | NMOS at $V_G \approx V_D/2$ | NMOS at $V_G \approx V_D$ (avalanche-generated) |
| Damage type | Interface states + electron trapping | Hole trapping + anomalous $V_{TH}$ shift |
| Frequency in KTU QP | Very high | Moderate |

## 2.3 Three Sub-Categories of Hot Carrier Effects (KTU Must-Know)

1. **Channel Hot Electron (CHE) Injection** — carriers heated *in the channel*.
2. **Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) Injection** — carriers generated by *impact ionization* near drain.
3. **Substrate Hot Carrier (SHC) Injection** — carriers heated *in the substrate* (less common, not exam-critical).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **golden reference** for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on Hot Electron Effects. Memorize these equations.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $I_{sub} = K_1 \, I_{DS} \, (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_2}{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}\right)$ | Substrate current (most tested) | A |
| 2 | $I_{sub} \propto I_{DS} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_{ii}}{qE_m \lambda}\right)$ | Substrate current — fundamental form | A |
| 3 | $\alpha = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E}\right)$ | Impact ionization coefficient (Chynoweth) | cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{1}{1 - \alpha L_{eff}}$ | Avalanche multiplication factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $I_{sub} = (M - 1) I_{DS}$ | Substrate current from multiplication | A |
| 6 | $I_G = C_1 I_{DS} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{qE_m \lambda}\right)$ | Gate current (Lucky Electron Model) | A |
| 7 | $\tau_{HCI} \propto \left(\dfrac{I_{sub}}{W}\right)^{-n}$ | Hot-carrier lifetime (n ≈ 2.5–3) | seconds |
| 8 | $E_m \approx \dfrac{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}{l}$ | Peak lateral field | V/cm |
| 9 | $\phi_{ii} = 1.5\,E_g \approx 1.68\,\text{eV}$ | Threshold energy for impact ionization | eV |
| 10 | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{qE_m\lambda}\right)$ | Injection probability per unit length | cm⁻¹ |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all KTU exam scripts, the absolute-value or "divide" bars inside the exponent (e.g., $E_m$ in the denominator) must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX — never use the bare pipe `|` inside markdown tables. KTU's e-learning portal renders `\vert` correctly.

## 2.5 The Lucky Electron Model (LEEM) — Detailed Physics

The **Lucky Electron Model** (Shappir–Frohman-Bentchkowsky, 1982) treats the hot electron injection as a **sequence of independent probabilistic events**:

$$I_G = \int_0^{L} I(y) \cdot P_{heat}(y) \cdot P_{redirect}(y) \cdot P_{interface}(y) \cdot dy$$

where each $P$ factor is a Boltzmann-like exponential:

- $P_{heat} = \exp(-d/\lambda)$ — probability of travelling distance $d$ without collision
- $P_{redirect}$ — probability of redirection toward interface
- $P_{interface} = \exp(-\phi_B / qE_m\lambda)$ — probability of retaining enough energy to cross the barrier

The compact form used in KTU problems is:

$$I_G = C_1 \, I_{DS} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{q E_m \lambda}\right)$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | How HCE Knowledge is Used |
|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Reliability qualification** | 10-year lifetime prediction: $t_{10\%\,\Delta V_T}$ vs. stress voltage |
| **LDO / Analog design** | Ensures analog transistor $V_{DS}$ does not exceed HCI safe-operating area |
| **Automotive CMOS** | AEC-Q100 stress at $V_{DS} = 1.3\,V_{DD,max}$ for 1000 hrs |
| **Memory design** | DRAM pass transistors subjected to repeated hot-carrier stress |
| **Low-power design** | Multi-$V_{TH}$ design avoids over-driving thin-oxide I/O devices |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Substrate Current from Impact Ionization

**Starting physical law (Chynoweth ionization coefficient):**

The number of electron-hole pairs generated per unit length of travel is:

$$\alpha(E) = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E}\right)$$

where $A \approx 7 \times 10^5\,\text{cm}^{-1}$ and $B \approx 1.23 \times 10^6\,\text{V/cm}$ for electrons in Si at 300 K.

**Step 1: Define the multiplication factor $M$.**

If a carrier traverses a length $L_{eff}$ in the high-field region, the total number of ionizing collisions per parent carrier is:

$$M = \exp\!\left(\int_0^{L_{eff}} \alpha(E(y))\,dy\right)$$

**Step 2: Linearize the integral.**

For a first-order estimate, assume $E(y) \approx E_m$ is constant across the pinch-off region of length $l$:

$$\int_0^{l} \alpha\,dy \approx \alpha(E_m) \cdot l$$

**Step 3: Compute the multiplication.**

$$M = \exp(\alpha l) = \exp\!\left(A l \exp(-B/E_m)\right)$$

**Step 4: Substrate current from multiplied channel current.**

Each avalanche event produces **one extra hole** (substrate). The hole current is therefore:

$$I_{sub} = (M - 1) \, I_{DS}$$

**Step 5: Approximate $M - 1 \approx \alpha l$ for small $\alpha l$ (low $V_{DS}$).**

$$I_{sub} \approx I_{DS} \cdot A l \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E_m}\right)$$

**Step 6: Substitute $E_m = (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})/l$:**

$$I_{sub} \approx I_{DS} \cdot A l \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B l}{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}\right)$$

**Step 7: Group constants into $K_1$ and $K_2$:**

$$\boxed{\,I_{sub} = K_1 \, I_{DS} \, (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_2}{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}\right)\,}$$

This is the **K2 expression** that KTU examiners quote verbatim. $K_1$ and $K_2$ are technology-dependent empirical constants (typical: $K_1 \approx 30\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $K_2 \approx 5\,\text{V}$ for 0.25 µm NMOS).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lucky Electron Model — Gate Current

**Step 1: Probability an electron travels distance $d$ without collision.**

Random scattering follows Poisson statistics. The mean free path is $\lambda$. The probability of travelling distance $d$ without a collision is:

$$P_{travel}(d) = \exp(-d/\lambda)$$

**Step 2: Probability of being redirected toward the interface.**

The redirection isotropically redistributes momentum over the unit sphere. The fraction of electrons directed into the small solid angle $\Delta\Omega$ facing the Si–SiO₂ interface is:

$$P_{redirect} = \dfrac{\Delta\Omega}{4\pi} \approx \text{const}$$

This constant is absorbed into the empirical coefficient $C_1$ later.

**Step 3: Probability of retaining barrier-crossing energy.**

The electron has lost energy $\mathcal{E}_{lost} = qE_m \lambda$ in the most recent phonon scattering. To cross the Si–SiO₂ barrier, it must still have:

$$\mathcal{E} \geq \phi_B = 3.15\,\text{eV}$$

Since energy distribution is Boltzmann-like, the survival probability is:

$$P_{energy} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{qE_m\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 4: Combine all probabilities (LEEM equation).**

For a channel position $y$, the local contribution to gate current is:

$$dI_G(y) = I_{DS}(y) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{qE_m\lambda}\right) dy$$

**Step 5: Integrate over the high-field region near drain.**

$$\int_0^{l} dI_G = C_1 \, I_{DS} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{qE_m\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 6: Final LEEM expression:**

$$\boxed{\,I_G = C_1 \, I_{DS} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{q E_m \lambda}\right)\,}$$

The exponential dependence on $1/E_m$ is the **key insight** — small increases in $V_{DS}$ cause *huge* increases in $I_G$, because $E_m$ appears in the denominator of the exponent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hot-Carrier Lifetime (Design Reliability)

**Step 1: Define lifetime $\tau_{HCI}$.**

The time for $\vert\Delta V_{TH}\vert$ to reach 10% (or 50 mV) under DC stress.

**Step 2: Empirical law (industry standard).**

$$\tau_{HCI} \propto \left(\dfrac{I_{sub}}{W}\right)^{-n}$$

where $W$ is the channel width and $n \approx 2.5$–$3$ for NMOS, $n \approx 2.7$–$3.2$ for PMOS.

**Step 3: Substitute substrate current from §3.1:**

$$\tau_{HCI} = A_{HCI} \left[\dfrac{1}{W} I_{DS} (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_2}{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}\right)\right]^{-n}$$

**Step 4: Practical design rule (KTU favourite).**

For 10-year lifetime, designers enforce:

$$V_{DS} \leq V_{DS,\text{safe}} \quad \text{where} \quad V_{DS,\text{safe}} \approx 0.9 \, V_{DD}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor with $L = 0.25\,\mu\text{m}$, $W = 10\,\mu\text{m}$, $\lambda = 10\,\text{nm}$ is operated at $V_{DS} = 2.5\,\text{V}$, $V_{GS} = 1.25\,\text{V}$, $V_{TH} = 0.5\,\text{V}$. Given $K_1 = 30\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $K_2 = 5\,\text{V}$, $I_{DS} = 1\,\text{mA}$, find (a) the substrate current and (b) the hot-carrier lifetime (assume $n = 2.5$, $A_{HCI} = 10^{11}$).

**Solution (a):**

$$V_{DSAT} \approx V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.25 - 0.5 = 0.75\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{DS} - V_{DSAT} = 2.5 - 0.75 = 1.75\,\text{V}$$

$$I_{sub} = (30) \times (10^{-3}) \times (1.75) \times \exp(-5/1.75)$$

$$= 0.0525 \times \exp(-2.857)$$

$$= 0.0525 \times 0.0575$$

$$\boxed{I_{sub} \approx 3.02\,\mu\text{A}}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\tau_{HCI} = 10^{11} \times \left[\dfrac{3.02 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-6}}\right]^{-2.5}$$

$$= 10^{11} \times (0.302)^{-2.5}$$

$$= 10^{11} \times 6.79$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{HCI} \approx 6.79 \times 10^{11}\,\text{s} \approx 21{,}500\,\text{years}}$$

This is a **comfortable** 10-year margin. If the designer instead used $V_{DS} = 3.0\,\text{V}$, the substrate current would jump ~7× and lifetime would drop below 1000 years — a **reliability fail**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | CHE (Channel Hot Electron) | DAHC (Drain Avalanche HC) | SHC (Substrate Hot Carrier) |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Heating location | Channel | Drain depletion | Substrate |
| Required $V_{DS}$ | Moderate | High | Low $V_{DS}$ but back-bias |
| Worst-case $V_G$ | $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}/2$ | $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}$ | $V_{GS} \approx 0$ |
| Primary symptom | $g_m$ degradation | $V_{TH}$ shift | Hump in $I_D$–$V_G$ |
| Dominant in | NMOS analog | NMOS digital I/O | SOI, radiation-hard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Hot Electron Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NMOS in Saturation] --> B[High Lateral Electric Field E at Drain End]
    B --> C[Channel Electron Acceleration]
    C --> D[Electron Gains Kinetic Energy greater than 3 kT]
    D --> E{Energy Check 1: E greater than 1.68 eV}
    E -- Yes --> F[Impact Ionization Event]
    E -- No --> G[Phonon Emission: Energy Loss]
    F --> H[Electron-Hole Pair Generated]
    H --> I[Hole Drifts to Substrate Terminal]
    H --> J[Secondary Electron Re-heats in Field]
    J --> K{Avalanche Multiplication M greater than 1}
    K -- Yes --> L[Substrate Current I_sub Measurable]
    K -- No --> M[Single Ionization Event]
    D --> N{Energy Check 2: E greater than 3.15 eV}
    N -- Yes --> O[Electron Redirected to Interface]
    O --> P[Crosses Si-SiO2 Barrier]
    P --> Q[Trapped in Oxide as Q_ox]
    P --> R[Collected at Gate as I_G]
    N -- No --> G
    Q --> S[Interface States N_it Created]
    S --> T[Threshold Voltage V_TH Shifts]
    R --> T
    T --> U[Device Degradation: Reliability Fail]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — HCI Degradation Effects on MOSFET

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V_DS Stress] --> B[High Field at Drain]
    B --> C[Hot Electron Injection]
    C --> D[Trapped Oxide Charge Q_ox]
    C --> E[Interface States N_it]
    D --> F[V_TH Shift and Magnitude]
    E --> F
    D --> G[Reduction in Channel Mobility mu]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Transconductance g_m Degradation]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Reduced Drive Current I_DSAT]
    I --> J[Circuit Speed Slowdown]
    F --> K[Leakage Current I_leak Increase]
    K --> L[Static Power Penalty]
    J --> M[Total Power Budget Violated]
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Three Hot Carrier Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Carrier Effects Family] --> B[Channel Hot Electron CHE]
    A --> C[Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier DAHC]
    A --> D[Substrate Hot Carrier SHC]
    B --> B1[Carrier Heated in Channel]
    B --> B2[Direct Injection into Oxide]
    B --> B3[Worst at V_GS = V_DS/2]
    C --> C1[Carrier Heated in Drain Field]
    C --> C2[Impact Ionization Dominant]
    C --> C3[Worst at V_GS = V_DS]
    D --> D1[Carrier Heated in Substrate]
    D --> D2[Back-gate Injection]
    D --> D3[Worst at V_GS = 0 and V_sub Negative]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block — HCI Reliability Mitigation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Circuit Designer] --> B[Choose V_DS less than V_DS_safe]
    A --> C[Use Lightly Doped Drain LDD]
    A --> D[Use HALO Implant]
    A --> E[Use Multi-VTH Design]
    A --> F[Apply Drain Engineering: FinFET or GAA]
    B --> G[Reduced E_m]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> H[Lower V_DD on I/O Pads]
    F --> I[Better Gate Electrostatic Control]
    G --> J[Reduced I_sub and I_G]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Hot Carrier Lifetime greater than 10 Years]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEVT741]**
**Define Hot Electron Effect. Why is it a critical reliability concern in modern short-channel MOSFETs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Hot Electron Effect is the phenomenon in which channel electrons in a short-channel MOSFET, accelerated by the high lateral electric field near the drain end, gain kinetic energy far exceeding the thermal equilibrium value ($3kT/2 \approx 0.038$ eV at 300 K). When this energy exceeds the Si–SiO₂ barrier height of 3.15 eV, electrons can be injected into the gate oxide.

> **Mechanism (1 mark):** The injected electrons either get collected as gate current ($I_G$) or become trapped in the oxide ($Q_{ox}$) and at the Si–SiO₂ interface ($N_{it}$), causing a permanent shift in threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ and degradation of transconductance $g_m$.

> **Criticality (1 mark):** In modern devices with channel lengths below 100 nm, supply voltages cannot be scaled proportionally due to leakage concerns. The drain-end lateral field $E_m$ can exceed $10^6$ V/cm, making HCI a dominant reliability limiter for both digital and analog CMOS.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEVT741]**
**Differentiate between Channel Hot Electron (CHE) injection and Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) injection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CHE (1.5 marks):** In CHE injection, the channel electron itself is heated by the high field and directly injected into the gate oxide. The damage is maximum when $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}/2$, because at this bias the electron has high energy and the vertical field pulls it toward the gate.

> **DAHC (1.5 marks):** In DAHC injection, an electron first undergoes impact ionization near the drain (creating an electron–hole pair), and the *secondary* carrier (hole in NMOS) is injected into the oxide. The worst-case bias is $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}$, where impact ionization rate is maximum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Writing $\exp(-B/V_{DS})$ instead of $\exp(-K_2/(V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}))$. Always carry the $V_{DSAT}$ term — losing 2 marks.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting units in the substrate current formula. $K_1$ has units of V⁻¹, $K_2$ has units of V. Mismatch of units = 1 mark lost.
> - **Common mistake #3:** In the Lucky Electron Model, confusing $\phi_{ii}$ (1.68 eV, ionization) with $\phi_B$ (3.15 eV, oxide barrier). Examiner deducts 2 marks for this swap.
> - **Common mistake #4:** For the lifetime derivation, students often write $\tau \propto I_{sub}$ (linear) — the correct relation is **power-law** with exponent $n \approx 2.5$–3.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEVT741, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the substrate current expression $I_{sub} = K_1 I_{DS} (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}) \exp[-K_2 / (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})]$ starting from the Chynoweth ionization coefficient. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NMOS device with $L = 0.18\,\mu\text{m}$, $W = 5\,\mu\text{m}$, $V_{TH} = 0.4\,\text{V}$, $V_{GS} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $V_{DS} = 1.8\,\text{V}$, $I_{DS} = 500\,\mu\text{A}$, $K_1 = 35\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $K_2 = 6\,\text{V}$, and $n = 2.7$, $A_{HCI} = 5 \times 10^{10}$, calculate (i) the substrate current and (ii) the hot-carrier lifetime. Comment on whether the device meets the 10-year reliability target. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start from Chynoweth's ionization coefficient (1 mark):**

$$\alpha(E) = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E}\right)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants. The physical meaning: $\alpha$ is the number of electron–hole pairs generated per unit length by a single carrier in field $E$.

**Step 2 — Identify the high-field region (1 mark):**

In a saturated NMOS, the pinch-off region near the drain has a roughly constant field $E_m \approx (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})/l$ of length $l$. We assume $E = E_m$ throughout this region.

**Step 3 — Multiplication factor (1.5 marks):**

The total number of ionizing collisions per parent carrier in length $l$ is:

$$M - 1 = \exp(\alpha l) - 1$$

For small $\alpha l$ (typical low-bias case), $M - 1 \approx \alpha l$:

$$M - 1 \approx A l \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E_m}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substrate current (1.5 marks):**

Each ionization creates one hole. The hole current to substrate is:

$$I_{sub} = (M - 1) I_{DS} \approx I_{DS} A l \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B l}{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Group constants (2 marks):**

Define $K_2 = B l$ (units of V) and $K_1 = A l / l = A$ for matching, but typically $K_1$ is empirically fitted to absorb the linear $V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}$ dependence. The standard empirical form is:

$$\boxed{\,I_{sub} = K_1 I_{DS} (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_2}{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}\right)\,}$$

**Assumptions stated (extra 0 mark — but show awareness):**
- Field is constant in pinch-off region
- Ionization is single-carrier initiated (not photon- or trap-assisted)
- $K_1$, $K_2$ are temperature- and technology-dependent

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute $V_{DSAT}$:

$$V_{DSAT} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.0 - 0.4 = 0.6\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute $V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}$:

$$V_{DS} - V_{DSAT} = 1.8 - 0.6 = 1.2\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute into substrate current formula:

$$I_{sub} = 35 \times (500 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.2 \times \exp(-6/1.2)$$

$$= 0.021 \times \exp(-5.0)$$

$$= 0.021 \times 0.00674$$

$$\boxed{I_{sub} \approx 141.5\,\text{nA}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Hot-carrier lifetime:

$$\tau_{HCI} = A_{HCI} \left[\dfrac{I_{sub}}{W}\right]^{-n}$$

$$= 5 \times 10^{10} \times \left[\dfrac{141.5 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}\right]^{-2.7}$$

$$= 5 \times 10^{10} \times (0.0283)^{-2.7}$$

$$= 5 \times 10^{10} \times 4.18 \times 10^{3}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{HCI} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{14}\,\text{s} \approx 6.6 \times 10^6\,\text{years}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Comment — The lifetime vastly exceeds the 10-year ($3.15 \times 10^8$ s) target. The device comfortably meets the reliability requirement. The $V_{DS} = 1.8\,\text{V}$ is safe for HCI in this technology.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEVT741, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of the Lucky Electron Model, derive the expression for the gate current $I_G$ due to hot electron injection. Explain why the dependence is exponential in $1/E_m$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the degradation mechanisms (i) Channel Hot Electron (CHE) injection, (ii) Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) injection, and (iii) Substrate Hot Carrier (SHC) injection in terms of the bias condition, generated damage, and the typical bias at which degradation is maximum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### Part (a) — Lucky Electron Model Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the model premise — an electron in the high-field region near drain gains energy, gets redirected toward the Si–SiO₂ interface, and crosses the barrier only if it still has energy $\geq \phi_B$ at the moment of arrival.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Probability of traveling distance $d$ without scattering:

$$P_{travel}(d) = \exp(-d/\lambda)$$

with $\lambda \approx 10\,\text{nm}$ as the optical-phonon mean free path.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Energy-gain over one mean free path:

$$\Delta\mathcal{E} = q E_m \lambda$$

If $\Delta\mathcal{E} < \phi_B$, the electron cannot inject. Survival probability (Boltzmann tail):

$$P_{energy} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{q E_m \lambda}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Probability of redirection toward the interface (geometric, isotropic scattering): absorbed into empirical constant $C_1$.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Combine all three factors and integrate over the high-field region near the drain:

$$I_G = C_1 I_{DS} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi_B}{q E_m \lambda}\right)$$

**Why exponential in $1/E_m$ (1 mark, included in step 3):** The exponential arises from the Boltzmann distribution of carrier energies. As $E_m$ decreases, the number of carriers with $\mathcal{E} > \phi_B$ drops exponentially — this is the steep gate-current roll-off observed in sub-100 nm devices.

### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

**Construct a structured comparison table in exam (2 marks for organization):**

| Feature | CHE | DAHC | SHC |
|---------|-----|------|-----|
| **Heating region** | Channel | Drain depletion | Substrate |
| **Bias condition** | $V_{DS}$ moderate, $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}/2$ | $V_{DS}$ high, $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}$ | $V_{BS}$ reverse, $V_{GS} \approx 0$ |
| **Carrier source** | Channel electrons | Avalanche-generated | Substrate bulk |
| **Damage type** | Interface states, electron trap | Hole trap, $V_{TH}$ shift | Back-channel interface states |
| **Worst-bias logic** | Max $I_G$ (gate collects) | Max $I_{sub}$ (avalanche) | Max $V_{BS}$ (substrate field) |
| **Common in** | NMOS analog | NMOS I/O | SOI, rad-hard |
| **Observable** | $g_m \downarrow$, $\Delta V_{TH}$ | $\Delta V_{TH}$ sign-flip | Sub-threshold hump |

**Explanation of each row (5 marks, 1.7 per row):**
- *Bias conditions:* explain why each mode peaks at a specific $V_{GS}/V_{DS}$ ratio (vertical field for CHE, impact-ionization for DAHC, back-bias for SHC).
- *Damage type:* each mode produces a distinct fingerprint in the $I_D$–$V_G$ curve.
- *Common in:* CHE in analog (mid-rail bias), DAHC in digital I/O (over-driven $V_{DS}$), SHC in SOI/radiation-hardened devices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Hot Electron Effects**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Hot electrons = carriers with $\mathcal{E} \gg 3kT/2$ due to high field near drain.
- ⭐ **Critical barrier heights:** $\phi_B^n = 3.15$ eV, $\phi_B^p = 4.68$ eV, $\phi_{ii} = 1.5 E_g \approx 1.68$ eV.
- ⭐ **Mean free path:** $\lambda \approx 10$ nm at 300 K in Si.
- ⭐ **Substrate current formula:** $I_{sub} = K_1 I_{DS} (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}) \exp[-K_2 / (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})]$ — KTU golden equation.
- ⭐ **Gate current (LEEM):** $I_G = C_1 I_{DS} \exp[-\phi_B / (q E_m \lambda)]$.
- ⭐ **Three modes:** CHE (mid-rail), DAHC (max $V_{GS}$), SHC (substrate back-bias).
- ⭐ **Worst-case NMOS bias for CHE:** $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}/2$.
- ⭐ **Worst-case NMOS bias for DAHC:** $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}$.
- ⭐ **Lifetime law:** $\tau_{HCI} \propto (I_{sub}/W)^{-n}$, with $n \approx 2.5$–3.
- ⭐ **Design rule:** 10-year lifetime requires $V_{DS} \leq 0.9 V_{DD}$ (typical).
- ⭐ **Mitigations:** LDD, HALO implants, FinFET/GAA, multi-$V_{TH}$, low-$V_{DD}$ I/O.
- ⭐ **Damage symptoms:** $V_{TH}$ shift, $g_m$ degradation, $I_{DSAT}$ reduction, $I_{leak}$ increase.
- ⭐ **Power penalty:** HCI indirectly increases static power by raising leakage.
- ⭐ **Exam tip:** Always include $V_{DSAT}$ in the denominator; never use bare `|` in markdown/LTX — use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- ⭐ **Examiner trap:** confusing $\phi_{ii}$ (1.68 eV) with $\phi_B$ (3.15 eV) costs 2 marks.
- ⭐ **Cross-link to Module 1:** Hot electron effects are one of the **static power dissipation sources** in sub-100 nm CMOS — relevant to CO1 of PEEVT741.
- ⭐ **Cross-link to Module 2:** HCI mitigation shares techniques with **leakage reduction** (reverse body bias, multi-$V_{TH}$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Body Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Body Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Body Effect (Substrate Bias Effect):** In a MOSFET, the **Body Effect** is the phenomenon in which the **threshold voltage ($V_T$)** of the transistor changes when a non-zero potential difference is applied between the **source** and the **body (substrate/bulk)** terminal. Mathematically, the source-to-body voltage $V_{SB}$ modulates the depletion region charge, which in turn shifts the gate voltage required to form the inversion layer.

For an **NMOS transistor** (p-type body), the source is normally tied to the lowest potential. If the body is biased at a potential lower than the source (i.e., $V_{SB} > 0$), the source-body pn-junction becomes more reverse-biased, the depletion region widens, more charge must be terminated by the gate, and **$V_T$ increases**.

For a **PMOS transistor** (n-type body/n-well), the convention is reversed. A more positive body bias relative to the source (i.e., $V_{SB} > 0$ magnitude) again causes **$|V_{Tp}|$ to increase in magnitude**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Balloon in a Bathtub

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a balloon floating at the surface of a bathtub. The water level required to "activate" (inflate/balance) the balloon depends on the **weight of the water already inside the tub** beneath it. If you pour extra water into the tub (analogous to applying reverse body bias), the water level rises, and the balloon needs *more* effort (extra gate voltage) to pop above the surface. That extra effort is precisely the **rise in threshold voltage due to body effect**.

A second, perhaps more accurate analogy: imagine the MOSFET channel as a **valve that opens only when gate pressure exceeds a certain threshold**. The body terminal acts like an **underground spring pushing against the channel from below**. If the spring pushes harder (higher reverse bias on source-body junction), the valve needs **more gate pressure** to open — threshold voltage rises.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Constants

- **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at $T = 300\ K$
- **Silicon permittivity** $\epsilon_s = \mathbf{11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ F/cm} \approx \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-12}\ F/cm}$
- **Oxide permittivity** $\epsilon_{ox} = \mathbf{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ F/cm} \approx \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ F/cm}$
- **Electronic charge** $q = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ C}$
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si** $n_i \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10}\ cm^{-3}}$ at 300 K

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin — Step-by-Step Logic

The threshold voltage of a MOSFET is derived from the condition that the surface potential under the gate equals **$2\phi_F$** (twice the Fermi potential) at the onset of strong inversion. The gate voltage must:

1. **Support the flat-band voltage** $V_{FB}$ (work-function difference + oxide charge effects).
2. **Drop across the oxide** to induce the depletion charge $Q_b$ beneath the gate.
3. **Equal the surface potential** $2\phi_F$ needed to invert the surface.

$$
V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{|Q_b|}{C_{ox}}
$$

When $V_{SB} \neq 0$, the surface potential required for inversion becomes **$(2\phi_F + V_{SB})$**, and the depletion charge magnitude becomes:

$$
|Q_b| = \sqrt{2\,q\,\epsilon_s\,N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

Therefore, $V_T$ becomes a **function of $V_{SB}$**:

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

where:
- $V_{T0}$ is the **zero-bias threshold voltage** (when $V_{SB} = 0$).
- $\gamma$ is the **body-effect coefficient** (units: $V^{1/2}$).

### 2.2 Why Does This Matter in Low-Power VLSI?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Body effect is *not* just a second-order curiosity — it is a **first-class design lever** in modern low-power CMOS. By deliberately applying a **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)**, designers can **raise $V_T$** to cut **subthreshold leakage** ($I_{off}$), and by applying **Forward Body Bias (FBB)**, they can **lower $V_T$** to boost drive current for performance. This is the cornerstone of **Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB)** and **Dynamic Threshold Scaling (DTCMOS)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Threshold voltage with body effect | $V$ (volts) |
| 2 | $V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{4 q \epsilon_s N_A \phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | Zero-bias threshold voltage | $V$ |
| 3 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \dfrac{t_{ox}}{\epsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}$ | Body-effect coefficient | $V^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | $F/cm^2$ |
| 5 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential (NMOS, p-substrate) | $V$ |
| 6 | $2\phi_F = \dfrac{2kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Surface potential at strong inversion onset | $V$ |
| 7 | $\vert Q_b \vert = \sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$ | Bulk depletion charge density | $C/cm^2$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial V_T}{\partial V_{SB}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}$ | Body-effect sensitivity (slope) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $I_{sub} = I_0\, e^{\frac{V_{GS}-V_T}{nV_{th}}}\!\left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_{th}}}\right)$ | Subthreshold current — $V_T$ raised by body effect → $I_{sub}$ drops exponentially | $A$ |
| 10 | $n = 1 + \dfrac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}$ | Subthreshold swing factor (couples body effect) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - $V_{SB} = 0 \Rightarrow V_T = V_{T0}$ (no body effect)
> - $V_{SB} > 0$ (NMOS, RBB) $\Rightarrow V_T$ **increases**
> - $V_{SB} < 0$ (NMOS, FBB) $\Rightarrow V_T$ **decreases**, but limited to $|V_{SB}| < 0.7\ V$ to avoid source-body forward conduction
> - Typical $\gamma$ values: $\mathbf{0.3\ V^{1/2}}$ to $\mathbf{0.7\ V^{1/2}}$ for modern bulk CMOS

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Body-Effect Coefficient $\gamma$

We begin from Poisson's equation in 1-D for the depletion region under the gate. Assume full depletion (charge density $\rho = -qN_A$ for p-type substrate) and charge neutrality:

$$
\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \frac{\rho}{\epsilon_s} = \frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s}
$$

Integrate once with respect to depth $x$ into the substrate (boundary: $\phi \to 0$ and $d\phi/dx \to 0$ deep in the bulk):

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s}\, x
$$

Integrate again from the surface ($x=0$, $\phi = \phi_s$) to the depletion edge ($x = x_d$, $\phi = 0$):

$$
-\phi_s = \frac{q N_A}{2\epsilon_s}\, x_d^{\,2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x_d = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s \, \phi_s}{q N_A}}
$$

The total depletion charge per unit area is:

$$
|Q_b| = q N_A \, x_d = \sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A \, \phi_s}
$$

For strong inversion with body bias $V_{SB}$, the surface potential $\phi_s = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$. Substituting:

$$
|Q_b(V_{SB})| = \sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

Now, the threshold voltage expression (with $V_{FB}$ the flat-band voltage):

$$
V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{|Q_b|}{C_{ox}}
$$

Substitute $|Q_b|$:

$$
V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

When $V_{SB} = 0$:

$$
V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}
$$

Subtract the two expressions:

$$
V_T - V_{T0} = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}}\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

Define the **body-effect coefficient**:

$$
\gamma \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}} \;=\; \frac{t_{ox}}{\epsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}
$$

Final compact form:

$$
\boxed{\;V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** For an NMOS transistor in a 0.18 µm process: $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17}\ cm^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 4\ nm$, $V_{T0} = 0.45\ V$. Compute:
1. The Fermi potential $\phi_F$.
2. The body-effect coefficient $\gamma$.
3. The new $V_T$ when $V_{SB} = 1.0\ V$.

Assume $T = 300\ K$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ cm^{-3}$.

---

**Step 1 — Compute $\phi_F$:**

$$
\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)
= 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5\times 10^{17}}{1.5\times 10^{10}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{N_A}{n_i} = \frac{5\times 10^{17}}{1.5\times 10^{10}} = 3.333 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\ln(3.333 \times 10^{7}) = \ln(3.333) + 7\ln(10) = 1.2040 + 16.118 = 17.322
$$

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \times 17.322 \approx 0.4478\ V \;\approx\; 0.448\ V
$$

So $2\phi_F \approx \mathbf{0.896\ V}$.

---

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{ox}$ and $\gamma$:**

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ F/cm}{4 \times 10^{-7}\ cm} = 8.625 \times 10^{-7}\ F/cm^2
$$

Now compute $\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}$:

$$
2 q \epsilon_s N_A = 2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \times (5 \times 10^{17})
$$

$$
= 2 \times 1.602 \times 1.04 \times 5 \times 10^{-19-12+17}
= 16.661 \times 10^{-14}
= 1.6661 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.6661 \times 10^{-13}} = \sqrt{1.6661} \times 10^{-6.5} = 1.2908 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-7} = 4.081 \times 10^{-7}\ C^{1/2}\,F^{1/2}\,cm^{-1}
$$

Thus:

$$
\gamma = \frac{4.081 \times 10^{-7}}{8.625 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.473\ V^{1/2}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute $V_T$ at $V_{SB} = 1.0\ V$:**

$$
V_T = 0.45 + 0.473\left(\sqrt{0.896 + 1.0} - \sqrt{0.896}\right)
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.896} \approx 0.9466,\qquad \sqrt{1.896} \approx 1.3771
$$

$$
V_T = 0.45 + 0.473 \times (1.3771 - 0.9466)
= 0.45 + 0.473 \times 0.4305
= 0.45 + 0.2036
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_T \approx 0.654\ V\;}
$$

So applying a reverse body bias of 1 V **raised the threshold by ≈ 0.20 V** (≈ 45 % increase) — a significant lever for leakage reduction.

### 3.3 Derivation of Body-Effect Sensitivity $\partial V_T / \partial V_{SB}$

Differentiating the master $V_T$ equation:

$$
\frac{\partial V_T}{\partial V_{SB}} = \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}
$$

At $V_{SB} = 0$:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial V_T}{\partial V_{SB}}\right\vert_{V_{SB}=0} = \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F}}
$$

This is a key quantity called the **body transconductance**, and it shows that body effect is *strongest* at low $V_{SB}$ and *weakens* at high $V_{SB}$ (saturation-like behaviour in the square-root term).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Define the Body Effect in a MOSFET. Write the expression for threshold voltage incorporating the body effect.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Body Effect** is the modulation of the threshold voltage $V_T$ of a MOSFET due to a voltage applied between the source and the body (substrate) terminal. When $V_{SB} > 0$ (for NMOS), the source-body pn-junction becomes more reverse-biased, the depletion region widens, and additional gate charge is required to invert the channel, raising $V_T$.

The threshold voltage incorporating body effect is:

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

where $V_{T0}$ is the zero-bias threshold voltage, $\gamma$ is the body-effect coefficient, $\phi_F$ is the Fermi potential, and $V_{SB}$ is the source-to-body voltage.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` What is the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$? Write its expression and explain how it depends on process parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

The body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ quantifies the sensitivity of the threshold voltage to changes in the source-body bias. Its expression is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{t_{ox}}{\epsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}
$$

**Dependencies:**
- **Doping $N_A$:** $\gamma \propto \sqrt{N_A}$. Higher substrate doping → larger body effect.
- **Oxide thickness $t_{ox}$:** $\gamma \propto t_{ox}$. Thicker oxide → larger body effect.
- **Oxide permittivity $\epsilon_{ox}$:** $\gamma \propto 1/\epsilon_{ox}$. Higher-$\kappa$ dielectrics → smaller body effect.

Modern processes with thin $t_{ox}$ and lightly-doped substrates (e.g., SOI, retrograde wells) minimize $\gamma$ to allow more aggressive $V_{SB}$ tuning without excessive $V_T$ inflation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor, clearly showing the role of body bias $V_{SB}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NMOS transistor, the following data is given:
$N_A = 10^{16}\ cm^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 10\ nm$, $V_{FB} = -0.9\ V$, $V_{SB} = 0\ V$ and $0.5\ V$. Determine: (i) $\phi_F$, (ii) the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$, and (iii) the threshold voltages $V_{T0}$ and $V_T$ at $V_{SB} = 0.5\ V$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** above. The student should reproduce:

1. Poisson's equation in the depletion region.
2. Integration yielding $x_d = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s \phi_s/(qN_A)}$.
3. Total bulk charge $Q_b = q N_A x_d$.
4. Threshold equation $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + |Q_b|/C_{ox}$.
5. Final body-affected form $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
6. Interpretation: increasing $V_{SB}$ widens depletion, increases $|Q_b|$, raises $V_T$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $N_A = 10^{16}\ cm^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 10\ nm = 10^{-6}\ cm$, $V_{FB} = -0.9\ V$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ cm^{-3}$.

**(i) Compute $\phi_F$:**

$$
\phi_F = 0.02585 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)
= 0.02585 \cdot \ln(6.667 \times 10^{5})
= 0.02585 \cdot (13.41)
\approx 0.347\ V
$$

So $2\phi_F \approx 0.694\ V$.

**(ii) Compute $\gamma$:**

$$
C_{ox} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-6}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-7}\ F/cm^2
$$

$$
2 q \epsilon_s N_A = 2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{16}
= 3.332 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.332 \times 10^{-15}} = 1.825 \times 10^{-7.5} = 1.825 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-8} = 5.77 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{5.77 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.167\ V^{1/2}
$$

**(iii) Compute $V_{T0}$ and $V_T(V_{SB}=0.5\ V)$:**

At $V_{SB} = 0$:

$$
V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F}
= -0.9 + 0.694 + 0.167 \times \sqrt{0.694}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.694} \approx 0.833
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F} \approx 0.167 \times 0.833 \approx 0.139
$$

$$
V_{T0} \approx -0.9 + 0.694 + 0.139 \approx -0.067\ V
$$

*(Note: the negative value reflects a depletion-mode device due to the heavy negative $V_{FB}$ — the method remains valid.)*

At $V_{SB} = 0.5\ V$:

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + 0.5} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.694 + 0.5} = \sqrt{1.194} \approx 1.093
$$

$$
V_T = -0.067 + 0.167 \times (1.093 - 0.833)
= -0.067 + 0.167 \times 0.260
= -0.067 + 0.043
$$

$$
\boxed{\;V_T(V_{SB}=0.5\,V) \approx -0.024\ V\;}
$$

The body bias raised $V_T$ by **≈ 0.043 V** (≈ 64 % of its previous magnitude).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the physical origin of the body effect in an NMOS transistor. Discuss how it impacts (i) threshold voltage, (ii) subthreshold leakage current, and (iii) drive current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain **Reverse Body Biasing (RBB)** and **Forward Body Biasing (FBB)** techniques used in low-power VLSI design. Mention their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **Physical origin:** Sketch a cross-section showing source, gate, drain, oxide, and p-substrate with the depletion region underneath. Indicate the widening of the depletion region when $V_{SB}$ increases. *(Refer Section 4.1 diagram.)*
- **Impact on $V_T$:** As shown by $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$, increasing $V_{SB}$ increases $V_T$.
- **Impact on subthreshold leakage:** $I_{sub} \propto \exp(-V_T/(nV_{th}))$. A larger $V_T$ → exponentially smaller leakage (each 100 mV rise in $V_T$ reduces $I_{sub}$ by ≈ 10× at room temperature).
- **Impact on drive current:** $I_{on} \propto (V_{GS} - V_T)^2$. A larger $V_T$ → lower overdrive → quadratically reduced drive current and slower switching → performance penalty.

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **Reverse Body Biasing (RBB):** Body is biased *more negative* than source for NMOS ($V_{SB} > 0$). Raises $V_T$ → reduces $I_{off}$. Used in **standby/sleep mode** to cut leakage. Advantage: no static current drawn (purely electrostatic). Limitation: performance penalty if applied during active mode; $V_T$ rise saturates at high $V_{SB}$ due to square-root dependence.
- **Forward Body Biasing (FBB):** Body is biased *less negative* (or positive) than source for NMOS ($V_{SB} < 0$). Lowers $V_T$ → boosts $I_{on}$. Used in **active mode** for high performance. Advantage: faster switching. Limitation: risk of **forward-biased source-body junction** → large parasitic currents; limited to $|V_{SB}| < 0.7\ V$ to keep diode off.
- **Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB):** Combines RBB (idle) + FBB (active), controlled by process/temperature sensors.
- **Block diagram:** Charge pump → VBB generator → body contact of transistor.

*(Refer Section 4.2 architecture block.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $V_{FB}$ term:** Many students write $V_T = 2\phi_F + |Q_b|/C_{ox}$ and skip the flat-band voltage. This loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion:** $V_{SB}$ is defined as $V_S - V_B$. For NMOS with body grounded and source at ground, $V_{SB}=0$. If source is at $+1\ V$ and body at $0\ V$, then $V_{SB} = +1\ V$ (RBB for NMOS).
> 3. **Mixing $\phi_F$ and $2\phi_F$:** The surface potential at inversion is **$2\phi_F$**, not $\phi_F$. A common slip that propagates numerically.
> 4. **Units of $\gamma$:** Always write $\mathbf{V^{1/2}}$ (square-root-volt) — not "volts". Examiners are strict on units.
> 5. **Ignoring forward-bias limit:** Stating "FBB can reduce $V_T$ arbitrarily" is wrong — it is clamped to about $0.7\ V$ to prevent source-body junction turn-on.
> 6. **Skipping the derivation:** KTU expects a full derivation of the $V_T$ vs. $V_{SB}$ relationship from Poisson's equation. Memorizing the formula alone earns partial credit only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Body Effect** = modulation of $V_T$ by source-body voltage $V_{SB}$.
- [x] Master equation: $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- [x] Zero-bias threshold: $V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + (\gamma)\sqrt{2\phi_F}$.
- [x] Body coefficient: $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}/C_{ox} = (t_{ox}/\epsilon_{ox})\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}$.
- [x] Fermi potential: $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i) \approx 0.35\text{–}0.45\ V$ for typical doping.
- [x] Surface potential at inversion: $\phi_s = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$.
- [x] **NMOS RBB** ($V_{SB} > 0$) → **$V_T$ increases** → leakage ↓, speed ↓.
- [x] **NMOS FBB** ($V_{SB} < 0$, $|V_{SB}| < 0.7\ V$) → **$V_T$ decreases** → leakage ↑, speed ↑.
- [x] Body-effect sensitivity: $\partial V_T / \partial V_{SB} = \gamma/(2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}})$ — strongest at low $V_{SB}$.
- [x] Modern usage: **Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB)** in low-power SoCs (e.g., mobile CPUs in idle/active modes).
- [x] Thin $t_{ox}$ and SOI/retrograde wells → smaller $\gamma$ → better low-$-V_{DD}$ scalability.
- [x] Subthreshold swing coupling: $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox} = 1 + \gamma/(2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}})$.
- [x] Each 100 mV rise in $V_T$ cuts $I_{off}$ by ≈ 10× at room temperature (DIBL and body effect together).
- [x] Always quote units: $\gamma$ in $V^{1/2}$, $\phi_F$ and $V_T$ in $V$, $N_A$ in $cm^{-3}$, $t_{ox}$ in $cm$ or $nm$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Narrow Width Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Narrow Width Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> The **Narrow Width Effect (NWE)** is a second-order short-channel phenomenon observed in MOSFETs where the **threshold voltage ($V_{TN}$) increases** as the channel width ($W$) is reduced below a certain critical dimension. It arises due to the additional fringing electric field lines originating from the gate electrode and terminating on the **sidewall depletion regions** formed beneath the thick field oxide (LOCOS) or shallow trench isolation (STI).

Mathematically, the threshold voltage incorporating NWE is expressed as:

$$V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \Delta V_{TN}$$

where $V_{T0}$ is the long, wide-channel threshold voltage and $\Delta V_{TN}$ is the narrow-width correction term (always positive).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — "The River Between Two Hills"**
> Imagine a river (the conducting channel) flowing between two mountains (the isolation oxide, or "LOCOS bird's beak" regions on either side). When the river is **very wide**, the mountains are far away and do not affect the water level (threshold voltage). However, when the river is **narrowed down** (W is reduced), the mountains push the water sideways, raising the river's effective level — you need more rainfall (gate voltage) before the water flows.
>
> Similarly, in a narrow MOSFET, the sidewall depletion regions from the field-oxide encroachment **"squeeze" the channel**, demanding a higher gate voltage to invert it. This is why narrow-width MOSFETs exhibit a **higher $V_{TN}$** than wide devices.

---

### 1.3 Physical Origin of the Effect

The narrow width effect originates from three coupled mechanisms:

1. **Field-Oxide Encroachment (Bird's Beak):** During LOCOS oxidation, the field oxide "eats into" the active area near the channel edges, creating a tapered oxide transition region.
2. **Fringing Gate Field:** Additional electric field lines emanate from the gate electrode over the bird's beak region into the channel sidewalls.
3. **Sidewall Depletion Charge:** Extra depletion charge is induced at the channel periphery, which must be compensated by additional gate charge — increasing $V_{TN}$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard KTU Metrics / Physical Constants**
> - **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - **Silicon permittivity:** $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 = 1.036 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m
> - **Oxide permittivity:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 = 3.453 \times 10^{-11}$ F/m
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = 26$ mV (NOTE: not to be confused with threshold voltage symbol $V_{TN}$)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1 — Channel Width Reduction:** As the lithographic dimension $W$ is shrunk to sub-micron regime (typically $W < 1\ \mu m$ in older nodes, $W < 100$ nm in deep-submicron), the active channel area becomes comparable to the lateral extent of the depletion region.

2. **Step 2 — Fringing Field Penetration:** The gate electrode's fringing field lines no longer terminate purely on the channel-substrate depletion region beneath the thin gate oxide. A fraction of the field lines **terminate on the lateral depletion regions** beneath the bird's beak / field-oxide taper.

3. **Step 3 — Extra Depletion Charge:** The depletion region now extends laterally by a characteristic length $\xi_W$ (often modeled as the depth of the field-oxide "bird's beak" or the lateral depletion width). The total bulk depletion charge becomes:

$$Q_B^{total} = Q_B^{planar} + Q_B^{sidewall}$$

4. **Step 4 — Increased Threshold Voltage:** Since the gate must now induce inversion across a larger effective depletion volume, the required gate charge rises, leading to an **increase** in $V_{TN}$.

5. **Step 5 — Inverse Width Dependence:** Because $Q_B^{sidewall}$ scales as the perimeter-to-area ratio (i.e., $\propto 1/W$), $\Delta V_{TN}$ becomes more pronounced as $W$ decreases.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \Delta V_{TN}$ | Modified threshold with NWE | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\Delta V_{TN} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{q \, N_A \, x_{d,max}}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{d,max}}{W}$ | Sidewall depletion correction (classical form) | V |
| 3 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² |
| 4 | $x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \phi_F}{q \, N_A}}$ | Maximum depletion width | m |
| 5 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential (p-type substrate) | V |
| 6 | $V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, N_A \, \varepsilon_{si} \, 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | Long, wide-channel threshold voltage | V |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{TN} \approx \dfrac{q \, N_A \, \xi_W}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{W}$ | Simplified NWE correction (proportional to $1/W$) | V |
| 8 | $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration (Si, 300 K) | cm$^{-3}$ |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Note:** The symbol $V_T$ (thermal voltage) and $V_{TN}$ (threshold voltage) are **NOT** the same. Examiners specifically test whether students correctly distinguish them. Always use $V_{TN}$ for threshold and $V_t$ or $kT/q$ for thermal voltage.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

The narrow width effect has critical real-world implications in modern VLSI:

- **Analog/Mixed-Signal Design:** In narrow-width analog transistors (e.g., current mirrors with small W), the elevated $V_{TN}$ can cause **mismatch** and degrade current matching ratios.
- **SRAM Cell Design:** The 6T SRAM cell uses minimum-width pull-down and access transistors. NWE-induced $V_{TN}$ shift affects **read/write stability margins** and **static noise margin (SNM)**.
- **I/O Driver Design:** High-current drivers use large W; NWE is negligible. For narrow drivers, however, NWE must be compensated.
- **Process Compensations:** Designers combat NWE through **dummy poly gates**, **halo implants**, and **STI stress engineering**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Narrow-Width Threshold Voltage Correction $\Delta V_{TN}$

We will derive the expression from fundamental charge-sheet and depletion-region analysis.

**Step 1 — Total Bulk Charge in a Narrow Channel**

The total bulk depletion charge per unit length in a MOSFET of width $W$ is the sum of the planar (bottom) depletion charge and the sidewall (edge) depletion charge:

$$Q_B^{total} = q \, N_A \, W \, x_{d,max} + 2 \, q \, N_A \, \xi_W \, x_{d,max}$$

where:
- $q \, N_A \, W \, x_{d,max}$ → planar depletion charge beneath the gate oxide
- $2 \, q \, N_A \, \xi_W \, x_{d,max}$ → sidewall depletion charge from both edges (factor of 2)
- $\xi_W$ → characteristic lateral depletion length (bird's beak depth)

**Step 2 — Compare with the Long-Wide Channel Case**

In a long, wide channel, only the planar charge is present:

$$Q_B^{0} = q \, N_A \, W \, x_{d,max}$$

**Step 3 — Express the Excess Charge Ratio**

The fractional excess bulk charge is:

$$\frac{\Delta Q_B}{Q_B^{0}} = \frac{2 \, q \, N_A \, \xi_W \, x_{d,max}}{q \, N_A \, W \, x_{d,max}} = \frac{2 \, \xi_W}{W}$$

**Step 4 — Threshold Voltage Shift Relation**

Since $V_{TN}$ scales linearly with the bulk charge $Q_B$ (from the standard MOSFET electrostatics), the threshold voltage shift is:

$$\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\Delta Q_B}{C_{ox} \, W} = \frac{2 \, q \, N_A \, \xi_W \, x_{d,max}}{C_{ox} \, W}$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Expression**

Recognising that $\xi_W \approx \frac{\pi}{4} x_{d,max}$ (a geometry factor for the sidewall depletion profile), we substitute:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{q \, N_A \, x_{d,max}}{C_{ox}} \cdot \frac{x_{d,max}}{W}}$$

This is the **classical narrow-width correction**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A p-type silicon substrate has $N_A = 1 \times 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$, gate oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, and channel width $W = 0.5\ \mu m$. Calculate the narrow-width effect contribution $\Delta V_{TN}$. Assume $\xi_W = 0.3\ \mu m$ and room temperature operation.

**Given Data:**

$$N_A = 1 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3} = 1 \times 10^{22}\ \text{m}^{-3}$$

$$t_{ox} = 10\ \text{nm} = 10 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}$$

$$W = 0.5\ \mu m = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

$$\xi_W = 0.3\ \mu m = 0.3 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate $C_{ox}$:**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{1 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.453 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{F/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $x_{d,max}$ (Fermi potential needed first):**

$$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(6.667 \times 10^5)$$

$$\ln(6.667 \times 10^5) = \ln(6.667) + 5 \ln(10) = 1.897 + 11.513 = 13.410$$

$$\phi_F = 0.0259 \times 13.410 = 0.347\ \text{V}$$

$$x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, \varepsilon_{si} \, \phi_F}{q \, N_A}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.347}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{22}}}$$

$$x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\frac{1.438 \times 10^{-10}}{1.602 \times 10^{3}}} = \sqrt{8.974 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.996 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 0.3\ \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta V_{TN}$ using simplified formula:**

$$\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{2 \, q \, N_A \, \xi_W \, x_{d,max}}{C_{ox} \, W}$$

$$\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (10^{22}) \times (0.3 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.3 \times 10^{-6})}{(3.453 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{22} \times 9 \times 10^{-14}$$

$$= 2 \times 1.602 \times 9 \times 10^{-19+22-14}$$

$$= 28.836 \times 10^{-11} = 2.8836 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 3.453 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.7265 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\Delta V_{TN} = \frac{2.8836 \times 10^{-10}}{1.7265 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.167\ \text{V} \approx 167\ \text{mV}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** A narrow MOSFET with $W = 0.5\ \mu m$ exhibits a threshold voltage that is **167 mV higher** than a wide device of the same length, solely due to the narrow width effect. This is a substantial shift that significantly impacts low-power circuit design.

---

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Narrow Width Effect vs Short Channel Effect

| Parameter | Narrow Width Effect (NWE) | Short Channel Effect (SCE) |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Affected dimension | Channel width ($W$) | Channel length ($L$) |
| $V_{TN}$ trend | **Increases** as $W \downarrow$ | **Decreases** as $L \downarrow$ |
| Primary cause | Sidewall depletion / field-oxide encroachment | Drain-induced barrier lowering (DIBL) |
| Dominant depletion region | Lateral / sidewall | Longitudinal / drain-side |
| $\Delta V_{TN}$ dependence | $\propto 1/W$ | $\propto 1/L$ (linearized) |
| Cure | Dummy poly, STI stress relief, well engineering | Halo implants, retrograde wells, raised S/D |
| Importance in | Analog narrow transistors, SRAM | Digital sub-100 nm logic |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Physical Architecture of a Narrow-Width MOSFET

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[GATE OXIDE THIN t_ox]:::core --> B[INVERSION LAYER]
    B --> C[P-TYPE SILICON SUBSTRATE]
    C --> D[DEPLETION REGION PLANAR x_d]
    D --> E[SUBSTRATE NEUTRAL REGION]
    C --> F[DEPLETION REGION SIDEWALL]
    F --> G[FIELDOX BIRD BEAK]
    G --> H[STI OR LOCOS ISOLATION]
    H --> I[CHANNEL EDGE]
    F -.->|FRINGING FIELD| A
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The **red regions** represent the depletion zones. Notice the **extra sidewall depletion** (left side) extending laterally under the bird's beak.
> - The **dashed line** indicates the fringing electric field that connects the gate oxide edge to the sidewall depletion.
> - For a **wider** channel, the sidewall depletion is a negligible fraction of the total depletion charge. For a **narrow** channel, it becomes dominant.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the Threshold Voltage Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VT TOTAL NARROW MOSFET] --> B[VT0 LONG WIDE CHANNEL]
    A --> C[DELTA VT NARROW WIDTH EFFECT]
    A --> D[DELTA VT SHORT CHANNEL EFFECT]
    A --> E[DELTA VT DIBL]
    A --> F[DELTA VT BODY EFFECT]
    C --> G[SIDEWALL DEPLETION]
    C --> H[FRINGING FIELD COUPLING]
    C --> I[FIELDOX BIRD BEAK ENCROACHMENT]
    style A fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** The total threshold voltage in a real nanoscale MOSFET is a sum of multiple corrections. The narrow width effect contributes a **positive** (threshold-raising) component, while short-channel effects and DIBL contribute **negative** (threshold-lowering) components. Designers balance these to land at the target $V_{TN}$.

---

### 4.3 Process Flow Architecture for NWE Mitigation

```mermaid

    E --> F[FABRICATE TEST STRUCTURE]
```
