```mermaid
    D --> E[High Lateral Electric Field ES Established]
    E --> F[Steep Energy Band Bending at Silicon Surface]
    F --> G{Tunneling Condition Met: Band Bending greater than EG}
    G -->|Yes| H[Valence Band Electrons Tunnel to Conduction Band]
    H --> I[BTBT Electron-Hole Pairs Generated]
    I --> J[Electrons Collected at Drain Terminal]
    I --> K[Holes Collected at Substrate Terminal]
    J --> L[IGIDL Flows from Drain to Substrate]
    K --> L
    G -->|No| M[Negligible GIDL Current]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Flow: GIDL Suppression Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original GIDL Problem] --> B[High-K Gate Dielectric]
    A --> C[LDD Lightly Doped Drain Structure]
    A --> D[Heterojunction Channel Engineering]
    A --> E[Multi Vt CMOS Design]
    A --> F[Reverse Body Biasing]
    A --> G[Thicker Gate Oxide at Drain Side]
    B --> H[Reduced Vertical Field]
    C --> I[Reduced Lateral Field at Drain Corner]
    D --> J[Lower Effective EG]
    E --> K[Selective High Vt for Pass Transistors]
    F --> L[Increased Depletion Width]
    G --> H
    H --> M[Suppressed IGIDL]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Low Standby Power VLSI Chip]
```

### 4.3 Energy Band Diagram (Conceptual Description)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per protocol, since a true band diagram is a continuous curve best drawn by hand, we describe it textually:*
> At the drain-side surface, $E_C$ and $E_V$ bend downward sharply over a tunneling distance of approximately $\mathbf{5 \text{ nm}}$ to $\mathbf{10 \text{ nm}}$. The vertical separation between $E_V$ (p-body side) and $E_C$ (n-drain side) at the same horizontal position is forced below $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$, opening the "tunneling window" through which electrons traverse.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define Gate Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL). Under what bias condition does it occur in an NMOS transistor?*

**Model Answer:**

Gate Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL) is the off-state leakage current that flows from the drain to the substrate of a MOSFET due to **band-to-band tunneling (BTBT)** of electrons in the gate-drain overlap region. In an NMOS transistor, it occurs when the gate is biased at **$V_{GS} = 0 \text{ V}$** (or a negative voltage) while the drain is held at a high potential, typically **$V_{DS} = V_{DD}$**. The resulting large $V_{DG}$ creates a high electric field at the drain corner, causing the energy bands to bend steeply enough for valence-band electrons to tunnel into the conduction band, producing a measurable drain-to-substrate current even though the channel is off.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for bias condition, 1 for the BTBT mechanism)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List any three techniques used in modern CMOS technologies to suppress GIDL.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lightly Doped Drain (LDD) Structure:** Reduces the peak electric field at the drain junction by introducing a low-doped n-region between the channel and the heavily doped n$^+$ drain.
2. **High-K Gate Dielectrics:** Allow a physically thicker oxide while maintaining the same capacitance, thereby reducing the vertical electric field across the gate-drain overlap.
3. **Multi-Threshold CMOS (MTCMOS):** Uses high-$V_t$ transistors in non-critical paths so that the off-state $V_{DG}$ across sensitive devices is reduced, suppressing GIDL.
4. *(Optional 4th)* Use of a **graded or heterojunction drain** to lower the effective tunneling barrier.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct technique with a brief justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat energy band diagram, explain the physical mechanism of Gate Induced Drain Leakage in a short-channel NMOS transistor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the GIDL current in terms of the surface electric field $E_s$ and the terminal voltage $V_{DG}$. Clearly state the assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. Draw (or describe) the cross-section of the NMOS showing the **gate**, **gate oxide**, **p-type body**, and **n$^+$ drain** in the gate-drain overlap region. *(2 marks)*
2. Mark the bias condition: $V_{GS} = 0 \text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$, $V_{BS} = 0 \text{ V}$. *(1 mark)*
3. Describe the formation of the **deep depletion region** under the gate oxide near the drain. *(1 mark)*
4. Describe the **band-bending** phenomenon: $E_C$ and $E_V$ are pulled down, and the vertical separation across the junction becomes less than $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$. *(1 mark)*
5. Explain that valence-band electrons **tunnel** horizontally through the triangular barrier to empty states in the conduction band of the drain. *(1 mark)*
6. State that the resulting current from drain to substrate is the GIDL current. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. State the starting point — the **WKB tunneling probability**:

$$
T \;\propto\; \exp\!\left( - \frac{B}{E_s} \right)
$$
*(1 mark)*

2. Define the surface field using the parallel-plate model:

$$
E_s \;=\; \frac{V_{DG} - V_{FB} - \phi_s}{3 \, T_{ox}}
$$
*(1 mark)*

3. Express the tunneling barrier constant:

$$
B \;=\; \frac{4 \sqrt{2 m^*} (E_g)^{3/2}}{3 q \hbar}
$$
*(1 mark)*

4. Write the current density as a product of the supply function and tunneling probability:

$$
J_{GIDL} \;=\; A \cdot E_s \cdot \exp\!\left( - \frac{B}{E_s} \right)
$$
*(1 mark)*

5. **State the assumptions** clearly:
   - Triangular potential barrier approximation.
   - Uniform field across the tunneling region.
   - Constant effective mass $m^*$.
   - Neglect of interface traps and quantum confinement effects. *(1 mark)*

6. Convert to total current by multiplying by effective width $W$:

$$
I_{GIDL} \;=\; W \cdot J_{GIDL}
$$
*(1 mark)*

7. Substitute $E_s$ and simplify to obtain the final voltage-domain form:

$$
I_{GIDL} \;\approx\; W \cdot C_{ox} \cdot V_{DG} \cdot \exp\!\left( - \frac{\alpha}{V_{DG}} \right)
$$
where $\alpha$ is a constant. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]*

**(a)** Compare Sub-threshold Leakage and Gate Induced Drain Leakage with respect to: (i) physical mechanism, (ii) temperature dependence, (iii) scaling behavior, (iv) dominant device region. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 45 nm NMOS has $T_{ox} = 1.0 \text{ nm}$, $W = 0.5 \text{ \mu m}$, $V_{FB} = -0.5 \text{ V}$, and $\phi_s = 0.7 \text{ V}$. If the device is biased at $V_{GS} = 0 \text{ V}$ and $V_{DS} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, compute the surface electric field $E_s$ and comment on whether significant GIDL is expected. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

| **Parameter** | **Sub-threshold Leakage** | **GIDL** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| (i) Mechanism | Diffusion of carriers over the potential barrier in the channel | Band-to-band tunneling at the drain junction |
| (ii) Temperature Dependence | Strong (exponential in $1/T$) | Weak (mainly field-driven) |
| (iii) Scaling Behavior | Worsens with $V_t$ reduction | Worsens with $T_{ox}$ and $V_{DD}$ scaling |
| (iv) Dominant Region | Channel (under the gate) | Gate-drain overlap region |

*(7 marks: 1.5 for each correct row + 0.5 for overall accuracy and neatness)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. Identify the relevant terminal voltage:

$$
V_{DG} \;=\; V_{DS} - V_{GS} \;=\; 1.0 - 0 \;=\; 1.0 \text{ V}
$$
*(1 mark)*

2. Compute the numerator:

$$
V_{DG} - V_{FB} - \phi_s \;=\; 1.0 - (-0.5) - 0.7 \;=\; 0.8 \text{ V}
$$
*(1 mark)*

3. Convert oxide thickness to cm:

$$
T_{ox} \;=\; 1.0 \text{ nm} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}
$$
*(1 mark)*

4. Compute the surface field:

$$
E_s \;=\; \frac{0.8}{3 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}} \;\text{V/cm}
$$

$$
E_s \;\approx\; 2.67 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/cm}
$$
*(2 marks)*

5. **Comment:** The computed $E_s$ exceeds the typical BTBT threshold of $10^6 \text{ V/cm}$, so **significant GIDL is expected** in this 45 nm device. To suppress it, one could use an **LDD structure** or a **high-K dielectric** to reduce the field. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse GIDL with sub-threshold leakage.** Examiners frequently award **zero marks** if a student uses thermal-diffusion language (e.g., "thermionic emission over the barrier") to describe GIDL — it is strictly a **quantum tunneling** phenomenon.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** The drain-to-gate voltage $V_{DG} = V_{DS} - V_{GS}$ must be **positive** for GIDL to occur. Forgetting to subtract $V_{GS}$ in numerical problems leads to wrong $E_s$ values.
> 3. **Missing the factor of 3:** The "3" in the denominator of the $E_s$ formula arises from the voltage-division between the oxide and the silicon surface. Omitting it leads to an $E_s$ value that is too small by a factor of three, which examiners will mark as a calculation error.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in the derivation costs at least **1 mark** in a 7-mark derivation question.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GIDL** is an off-state leakage current flowing from **drain to substrate** caused by **band-to-band tunneling** in the **gate-drain overlap region**.
- It occurs in NMOS when **$V_{GS} = 0$** and **$V_{DS} = V_{DD}$** (max $V_{DG}$).
- Core formula: $I_{GIDL} \;\propto\; V_{DG} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{\alpha}{V_{DG}} \right)$.
- Surface field: $E_s \;=\; \dfrac{V_{DG} - V_{FB} - \phi_s}{3 \, T_{ox}}$.
- Critical field for BTBT in silicon is approximately $\mathbf{10^6 \text{ V/cm}}$.
- **Temperature dependence is weak** (unlike sub-threshold leakage).
- GIDL **worsens** as $T_{ox}$ shrinks and as $V_{DD}$ remains high in advanced nodes.
- Major suppression techniques: **LDD structure, High-K dielectrics, Multi-Threshold CMOS, thicker oxide at drain corner**.
- Engineering impact: dominates **standby leakage in SRAM cells, DRAM refresh loss, and pass-transistor logic** in deep-submicron chips.
- GIDL is a **quantum mechanical** effect — classical drift-diffusion equations cannot predict it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunneling Through Gate Oxide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tunneling Through Gate Oxide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gate Oxide Tunneling** (also known as **Quantum Mechanical Tunneling** through the gate dielectric) is a leakage mechanism in MOSFETs whereby charge carriers (electrons or holes) traverse the thin gate oxide (SiO₂) layer via the quantum mechanical tunneling effect, even when their energy is below the classical barrier height of the oxide. This phenomenon becomes dominant when the physical oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$) is scaled below approximately **3 nm**, leading to a gate leakage current ($I_{gate}$) that flows directly between the gate electrode and the silicon substrate (channel, source, or drain).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEVT741 - Low Power VLSI Design, Module 1), tunneling through the gate oxide is identified as one of the **primary sources of static (leakage) power dissipation** in deep-submicron CMOS technologies (65 nm and below). The phenomenon directly limits oxide scaling and is a critical design concern for low-power VLSI circuits.

The two principal tunneling mechanisms are:

1. **Fowler–Nordheim (FN) Tunneling**: Carrier traverses a triangular potential barrier under a high applied electric field. Dominant when the oxide is moderately thin (~3–5 nm) and the field is high ($E_{ox} \geq 6$ MV/cm).
2. **Direct Tunneling**: Carrier traverses the entire rectangular oxide barrier when the oxide is thin enough ($t_{ox} < 3$ nm) and the applied voltage is low. This is the dominant mechanism in modern nanoscale MOSFETs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a deep valley separated from the next valley by a tall, thick mountain. Classically, if your energy (your "voltage") is less than the height of the mountain, you can never reach the other side. This is **classical physics** — the barrier blocks you completely.

Now, in the **quantum world**, there is a small but finite probability that you will *magically appear* on the other side of the mountain, even though you don't have enough energy to climb over it. This is **quantum tunneling**.

Applied to a MOSFET:
- **You (the electron)** are in the silicon channel or the polysilicon gate.
- **The mountain (oxide barrier)** is the SiO₂ layer separating the gate from the channel.
- **The mountain's height** is the **conduction band offset** between silicon and SiO₂ (~3.15 eV for electrons).
- **The mountain's thickness** is the **physical oxide thickness** $t_{ox}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for Low Power VLSI?**
> In low-power design, we aggressively scale $t_{ox}$ to improve drive current, reduce short-channel effects, and achieve higher integration density. However, tunneling probability depends **exponentially** on barrier thickness. Therefore, as we make the oxide thinner, the leakage current through the gate grows **exponentially**, dramatically increasing the static power consumption — sometimes making the chip unviable for battery-powered (mobile/IoT) applications.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Si–SiO₂ conduction band offset (electron barrier) | $\Phi_B$ | **3.15 eV** |
| Si–SiO₂ valence band offset (hole barrier) | $\Phi_{B,h}$ | **4.68 eV** |
| Effective electron mass in SiO₂ | $m_{ox}^*$ | **0.50 $m_0$** |
| Effective hole mass in SiO₂ | $m_{ox,h}^*$ | **0.33 $m_0$** |
| Reduced Planck's constant | $\hbar$ | **1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| Oxide electric field | $E_{ox}$ | **MV/cm** range |
| Critical oxide thickness (direct tunneling) | $t_{ox}$ | **< 3 nm** |

where $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg is the free electron rest mass.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Tunneling

In a MOS structure under an applied gate bias, the energy band diagram shows a potential barrier formed by the SiO₂ layer. Classically, electrons in the silicon (with energy below the conduction band of SiO₂) cannot enter the oxide. However, because the electron has a wave-like nature (de Broglie wavelength), its wavefunction does **not** abruptly drop to zero at the barrier — it **decays exponentially** inside the barrier.

The transmission probability $T$ through a rectangular barrier of height $\Phi_B$ and width $t_{ox}$ is given by the WKB approximation:

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-2 \int_0^{t_{ox}} \kappa(x)\, dx\right)$$

where $\kappa(x)$ is the imaginary wavevector inside the barrier:

$$\kappa(x) = \frac{\sqrt{2\, m_{ox}^*\, [q\,\Phi(x) - E]}}{\hbar}$$

The **key insight**: $T$ depends exponentially on $t_{ox}$. Halving the oxide thickness **massively increases** the tunneling current.

### 2.2 Fowler–Nordheim (FN) Tunneling

This occurs at **high electric fields** where the applied bias tilts the oxide barrier into a triangular shape. The electron tunnels through the narrowed triangular barrier near the gate electrode (or substrate interface).

The standard FN current density formula:

$$J_{FN} = A_{FN}\, E_{ox}^{\,2}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

where:

$$A_{FN} = \frac{q^3}{16\pi^2 \hbar\, \Phi_B}$$

$$B_{FN} = \frac{4\sqrt{2\, m_{ox}^*}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}}{3\,\hbar\, q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** FN tunneling is **field-driven**. The current depends on $E_{ox}^2$ in the prefactor and exponentially on $-1/E_{ox}$ in the exponent. A plot of $\ln(J/E_{ox}^2)$ vs $1/E_{ox}$ (a Fowler-Nordheim plot) yields a **straight line**, which is a hallmark signature used experimentally to identify FN tunneling.

### 2.3 Direct Tunneling

When $t_{ox} < 3$ nm, even at low $V_{GS}$, the rectangular barrier is thin enough that the electron wavefunction penetrates completely through. This is **Direct Tunneling**, and it dominates in nanoscale CMOS at operational voltages.

The current density is also exponential in $t_{ox}$:

$$J_{DT} = \frac{A_{FN}\, E_{ox}^{\,2}}{\left[1 - \left(\frac{\Phi_B - qV_{ox}}{\Phi_B}\right)^{1/2}\right]^{2}}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}}\, \frac{\Phi_B^{3/2} - (\Phi_B - qV_{ox})^{3/2}}{\Phi_B^{3/2}}\right)$$

where $V_{ox}$ is the voltage drop across the oxide.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility & Engineering Relevance

- **Technology scaling**: Forced the industry to abandon pure SiO₂ in favor of **high-κ dielectrics** (HfO₂, HfSiON) starting at the **45 nm node** (Intel, 2007) and beyond.
- **Memory design**: Floating-gate flash memories rely on FN tunneling for program/erase operations — here tunneling is *desired*.
- **Power budgeting**: In 32 nm and below, gate leakage can rival or exceed subthreshold leakage in total static power contribution.
- **Reliability**: Time-Dependent Dielectric Breakdown (TDDB) is closely linked to tunneling-induced oxide wearout.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T \approx e^{-2\kappa t_{ox}}$, with $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* \Phi_B}}{\hbar}$ | Transmission probability (rectangular barrier, WKB) |
| 2 | $J_{FN} = A_{FN}\, E_{ox}^{\,2}\, e^{-B_{FN}/E_{ox}}$ | Fowler–Nordheim current density (high-field, triangular barrier) |
| 3 | $A_{FN} = \dfrac{q^3}{16\pi^2 \hbar\, \Phi_B}$ | FN prefactor constant |
| 4 | $B_{FN} = \dfrac{4\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^*}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}}{3\hbar\, q}$ | FN exponential constant |
| 5 | $J_{DT}$ (above) | Direct tunneling current (rectangular barrier, thin oxide) |
| 6 | $E_{ox} = \dfrac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{FB} - \psi_s}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide electric field (with $V_{FB}$ flat-band voltage, $\psi_s$ surface potential) |
| 7 | $I_{gate} = J \cdot A_{gate}$ | Total gate leakage current (A: gate area) |
| 8 | $P_{gate} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{gate}$ | Static leakage power dissipated via gate tunneling |
| 9 | $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV (electrons), $4.68$ eV (holes) | Si–SiO₂ barrier heights |
| 10 | $t_{ox} < 3$ nm | Threshold for dominant direct tunneling |

> [!NOTE]
> Always express energy in **eV** and length in **cm** when using these formulas with standard textbook constants. In SI units, convert $q\Phi_B$ to joules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transmission Probability Across a Rectangular Barrier (WKB Approximation)

**Goal**: Derive the expression for the tunneling probability $T$ of an electron through a rectangular potential barrier of height $\Phi_B$ and width $t_{ox}$.

#### Step 1: Write the time-independent Schrödinger equation in the barrier region

In the classically forbidden region, the electron energy $E < q\Phi_B$, so the kinetic energy is negative. The Schrödinger equation reads:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2 m_{ox}^*} \frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} + q\Phi_B\, \psi = E\, \psi$$

#### Step 2: Rearrange to isolate the second derivative

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2 m_{ox}^* (q\Phi_B - E)}{\hbar^2}\, \psi = \kappa^2\, \psi$$

where we define the **decay constant**:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* (q\Phi_B - E)}}{\hbar}$$

#### Step 3: Solve the differential equation

The general solution inside the barrier is:

$$\psi(x) = C\, e^{\kappa x} + D\, e^{-\kappa x}$$

This is an **exponential decay** (growing and decaying exponentials). For a thick barrier, the growing exponential is suppressed by matching boundary conditions.

#### Step 4: Apply the WKB approximation for the transmission coefficient

For a slowly varying barrier, the WKB transmission is:

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-2 \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \kappa(x)\, dx\right)$$

For a rectangular barrier (constant $\kappa$):

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-2\, \kappa\, t_{ox}\right)$$

#### Step 5: Substitute the expression for $\kappa$

$$\boxed{T \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\, t_{ox}}{\hbar}\, \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^*\, (q\Phi_B - E)}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** As $t_{ox}$ increases by 0.1 nm, the exponent grows linearly, and $T$ drops by orders of magnitude. This is why **direct tunneling is so sensitive** to oxide thickness.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fowler–Nordheim Current Density Formula

**Goal**: Show that for a triangular barrier under high field, $J_{FN}$ takes the canonical form.

#### Step 1: Model the tilted barrier

Under an applied field $E_{ox}$, the conduction band of the oxide tilts linearly. The barrier shape becomes triangular with a width that depends on the field. The point where the electron energy equals the barrier top is at distance $x_t$ from the injecting interface:

$$x_t = \frac{\Phi_B}{E_{ox}}$$

#### Step 2: Use WKB for a triangular barrier

For a triangular barrier, the integral in the exponent becomes:

$$\int_0^{x_t} \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^*\, (q\Phi_B - qE_{ox}\, x)}\, dx$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate the integral

Substitute $u = \Phi_B - E_{ox}\, x$, $du = -E_{ox}\, dx$:

$$\int_0^{\Phi_B/E_{ox}} \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^*\, q\, u}\, \frac{du}{E_{ox}}$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}}{E_{ox}} \cdot \frac{2}{3}\, u^{3/2} \Big|_0^{\Phi_B/E_{ox}}$$

$$= \frac{2\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}}{3 E_{ox}} \cdot \Phi_B^{3/2}$$

#### Step 4: Insert into the WKB expression

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}}{3 \hbar E_{ox}}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

with:

$$B_{FN} = \frac{4\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}}{3\hbar}$$

#### Step 5: Multiply by the supply function (number of electrons attempting to tunnel)

Using the independent-electron supply function approach (Fowler–Nordheim 1928; later refined by Murphy & Good), the incident flux of electrons with energy near the Fermi level yields the factor $E_{ox}^2$ in front. The final current density:

$$\boxed{J_{FN} = A_{FN}\, E_{ox}^2\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}}\right)}$$

with:

$$A_{FN} = \frac{q^3}{16 \pi^2 \hbar\, \Phi_B}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Numerical Estimation of Gate Leakage Current

**Given**:
- $t_{ox} = 1.5$ nm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ cm
- $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V, $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV
- $m_{ox}^* = 0.5\, m_0$
- Gate area $A = 1\, \mu\text{m}^2 = 10^{-8}$ cm²

**Step 1: Compute the oxide field**

Assume $V_{ox} \approx V_{GS} = 1$ V (ignoring flat-band and surface potential for simplicity):

$$E_{ox} = \frac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{1.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.67 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/cm} = 6.67\ \text{MV/cm}$$

**Step 2: Compute $B_{FN}$**

$$B_{FN} = \frac{4\sqrt{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{3 \cdot 1.054 \times 10^{-34}}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}$$

Compute piece by piece:
- $\sqrt{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = \sqrt{1.4576 \times 10^{-49}} = 1.207 \times 10^{-24.5}$
- More cleanly: $\sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = \sqrt{1.4576 \times 10^{-49}} \approx 1.207 \times 10^{-24.5}$
- $\Phi_B^{3/2} = (3.15)^{1.5} \approx 5.59$ eV$^{1.5}$
- Convert: $\Phi_B^{3/2}$ in SI: $(3.15 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19})^{1.5} = (5.04 \times 10^{-19})^{1.5} \approx 1.131 \times 10^{-28}$

Numerator:
$$4 \cdot 1.207 \times 10^{-24.5} \cdot 1.131 \times 10^{-28} \approx 5.46 \times 10^{-52}$$

(Note: the actual computation in CGS units, where $B_{FN} \approx 230$ MV/cm for SiO₂, is standard. Using CGS units directly yields $B_{FN} \approx 2.38 \times 10^{8}$ V/cm.)

**Step 3: Compute the exponent**

$$\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}} = \frac{2.38 \times 10^{8}}{6.67 \times 10^{6}} \approx 35.7$$

**Step 4: Compute the prefactor**

$$A_{FN} = \frac{q^3}{16\pi^2 \hbar \Phi_B} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A/V}^2\ (\text{in CGS})$$

**Step 5: Compute current density**

$$J_{FN} = 3.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (6.67 \times 10^{6})^2 \cdot e^{-35.7}$$

$$= 3.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4.45 \times 10^{13} \cdot 3.4 \times 10^{-16}$$

$$\approx 5.6 \times 10^{1}\ \text{A/cm}^2 \approx 56\ \text{A/cm}^2$$

**Step 6: Compute total current**

$$I_{gate} = J \cdot A = 56 \cdot 10^{-8} = 5.6 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A} \approx 0.56\ \mu\text{A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **per-micrometer-squared** gate leakage of nearly a microampere. In a modern processor with billions of transistors, this translates to **amps of total gate leakage** if no countermeasures are taken — making low-power design impossible without high-κ dielectrics.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tunneling vs. Oxide Thickness

| $t_{ox}$ (nm) | Dominant Mechanism | Approx. $J_{gate}$ (A/cm²) | Engineering Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | Negligible tunneling | $\sim 10^{-12}$ | Used in 0.5 µm technology |
| 5 | Fowler–Nordheim onset | $\sim 10^{-3}$ | 180 nm node |
| 3 | FN + weak direct | $\sim 1$ | 130 nm node |
| 2 | Strong direct tunneling | $\sim 10$ | 90 nm node |
| 1.5 | Direct tunneling dominates | $\sim 50$–$100$ | 65 nm node (last pure SiO₂) |
| 1.0 (SiO₂ equiv.) | Catastrophic | $\sim 10^{3+}$ | Required **high-κ** replacement |

> [!NOTE]
> The "1.0 nm" row uses **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)**. Physically, the dielectric is replaced by HfO₂ (κ ≈ 25 vs. SiO₂ κ ≈ 3.9), giving the same capacitance with a physically thicker barrier — thus suppressing tunneling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tunneling-Aware Low-Power Design Stack

This diagram maps the hierarchical design decisions that mitigate gate oxide tunneling in modern low-power VLSI systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Device Level: High-k Dielectric Choice] --> nodeB[Material Selection: HfO2 HfSiON La2O3]
    nodeB --> nodeC[EOT Scaling Below 1 nm Maintained]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Circuit Level: Multi VDD and Power Gating]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stack: Sleep Transistors and Header Footer Switches]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Architectural Level: Clock Gating]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Duty Cycle Reduction of Active Toggling]
    nodeG --> nodeH[System Level: Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling DVFS]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Adaptive VDD Minimization to Reduce Eox]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Goal: Minimize J Gate Leakage Power]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Energy Band Diagram: Direct Tunneling in NMOS Under Inversion

A semantic block representation of the band structure when direct tunneling dominates. Each labeled region represents a physical band edge as a function of position through the gate stack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Poly Si Gate] -->|Conduction Band Edge EC| nodeB[Gate Oxide Layer tox]
    nodeB -->|Barrier Height Phi B 3.15 eV| nodeC[Si Substrate Inversion Layer]
    nodeC -->|Tunneling Path Through Triangular plus Rectangular Barrier| nodeB
    nodeB -->|J_direct Exponential in tox| nodeA
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Fowler–Nordheim vs. Direct Tunneling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Evaluate Oxide Thickness tox and Applied VGS] --> nodeQ1{Is tox less than 3 nm}
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeFN[Fowler Nordheim Tunneling Dominates]
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ2{Is Eox greater than 6 MV per cm}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeFN2[FN Tunneling Triangular Barrier]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeDT[Direct Tunneling Dominates]
    nodeFN --> nodeOut[J_FN Expression with Exponential minus B over Eox]
    nodeFN2 --> nodeOut
    nodeDT --> nodeOut2[J_DT Expression Rectangular Barrier]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram of Mitigation Techniques (Taxonomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[Mitigation of Gate Oxide Tunneling] --> nodeP1[Material Level]
    nodeRoot --> nodeP2[Device Level]
    nodeRoot --> nodeP3[Circuit Level]
    nodeP1 --> nodeM1[High-k Dielectric HfO2]
    nodeP1 --> nodeM2[Metal Gate Workfunction Tuning]
    nodeP2 --> nodeM3[Reverse Body Biasing]
    nodeP2 --> nodeM4[Multiple Gate FinFET Structures]
    nodeP3 --> nodeM5[Power Gating with Sleep Transistor]
    nodeP3 --> nodeM6[Dual VDD and DVFS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeM1`, `nodeRoot`) — none use reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style`. Labels are kept clean (no bold/italics/HTML) for compiler safety.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is gate oxide tunneling? Why does it become significant in nanoscale CMOS technologies?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gate oxide tunneling is a **quantum mechanical phenomenon** in which charge carriers traverse the thin gate dielectric (SiO₂) of a MOSFET even when their energy is below the classical barrier height. It becomes significant in nanoscale CMOS because:

1. **Aggressive oxide scaling** ($t_{ox} < 3$ nm) increases tunneling probability exponentially. (1 mark)
2. The resulting **gate leakage current** contributes directly to static power dissipation, undermining low-power design goals. (1 mark)
3. The industry responded by introducing **high-κ dielectrics** (e.g., HfO₂) to increase physical thickness while maintaining low EOT. (1 mark)

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling** and **direct tunneling** through the gate oxide.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fowler–Nordheim Tunneling | Direct Tunneling |
|---|---|---|
| Barrier shape | Triangular (tilted by high $E_{ox}$) | Rectangular |
| Dominant condition | High electric field ($E_{ox} \geq 6$ MV/cm) | Thin oxide ($t_{ox} < 3$ nm), low $V_{GS}$ |
| Key formula | $J_{FN} = A E_{ox}^2 e^{-B/E_{ox}}$ | $J_{DT} \propto e^{-\kappa t_{ox}}$ |
| Oxide regime | Moderately thin (~3–5 nm) | Ultra-thin (< 3 nm) |
| Verification | $\ln(J/E_{ox}^2)$ vs $1/E_{ox}$ is linear | Exponential in $t_{ox}$ at low bias |

(Each distinct feature earns 0.5 marks; allocate marks to three major contrasts.)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing $t_{ox}$ (physical thickness) with **EOT** (Equivalent Oxide Thickness, $t_{EOT} = t_{high\kappa} \cdot \kappa_{SiO_2}/\kappa_{high\kappa}$). EOT is what determines capacitance and drive current; physical thickness determines tunneling. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** if these are not distinguished.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting units when using the FN formula. Use **CGS** units ($E_{ox}$ in V/cm, $\Phi_B$ in eV) for the standard textbook constants. Mixing SI without conversion costs marks.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Writing $J = A E e^{-B/E}$ instead of $J = A E^2 e^{-B/E}$. The squared field is in the prefactor, not the exponent — a one-mark deduction.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**Q (a)** Derive the expression for the **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling current density** through a thin gate oxide, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** For an NMOS transistor with $t_{ox} = 1.2$ nm, $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV, $m_{ox}^* = 0.5 m_0$, and an applied $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V, compute the FN tunneling current density and the gate leakage current for a device with gate area $W \times L = 1 \mu\text{m} \times 0.1 \mu\text{m}$. Comment on the implications for low-power design. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Define the system.** Consider a MOS structure under a high gate bias such that the oxide conduction band is tilted into a triangular barrier of width $x_t = \Phi_B / E_{ox}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Apply WKB approximation.** The tunneling probability:

$$T = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2}{\hbar} \int_0^{x_t} \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* (q\Phi_B - qE_{ox} x)}\, dx\right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Evaluate the integral.** Substituting $u = \Phi_B - E_{ox} x$:

$$\int_0^{x_t} \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q\, u}\, dx = \frac{2 \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}}{3 E_{ox}}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}$$

Therefore:

$$T = \exp\!\left(-\frac{4 \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}}{3 \hbar E_{ox}}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Multiply by the supply function.** Using the free-electron supply function (Murphy–Good), the number of electrons per unit area per unit time incident at the barrier with the correct energy is proportional to $E_{ox}^2$. Combining:

$$J_{FN} = A_{FN}\, E_{ox}^2\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

with $A_{FN} = \dfrac{q^3}{16 \pi^2 \hbar \Phi_B}$ and $B_{FN} = \dfrac{4 \sqrt{2 m_{ox}^* q}\, \Phi_B^{3/2}}{3 \hbar q}$ (in SI; the CGS form has no $q$ in the denominator).

**Step 5 (1 mark): State assumptions.** (i) Single-electron tunneling, (ii) parabolic bands, (iii) constant effective mass in the oxide, (iv) uniform $E_{ox}$, (v) ignore image-force barrier lowering.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute $E_{ox}$.**

Assume $V_{ox} \approx V_{GS} = 1.0$ V (idealized):

$$E_{ox} = \frac{1.0}{1.2 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.33 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/cm} = 8.33\ \text{MV/cm}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute $B_{FN}$ in CGS.**

Using $m_{ox}^* = 0.5 m_0 = 0.5 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-28}$ g (CGS) and $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV:

$$B_{FN} = \frac{4 \sqrt{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-28} \cdot 4.803 \times 10^{-10}}}{3 \cdot 1.054 \times 10^{-27}} \cdot (3.15)^{3/2}$$

$$B_{FN} \approx 2.38 \times 10^{8}\ \text{V/cm} = 238\ \text{MV/cm}$$

(Student may quote the standard textbook value $\approx 2.36$–$2.4 \times 10^{8}$ V/cm.)

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute the exponent.**

$$\frac{B_{FN}}{E_{ox}} = \frac{2.38 \times 10^{8}}{8.33 \times 10^{6}} \approx 28.6$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Compute $J_{FN}$.**

Using $A_{FN} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V² (CGS, with $E_{ox}$ in V/cm):

$$J_{FN} = 3.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (8.33 \times 10^{6})^2 \cdot e^{-28.6}$$

$$= 3.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6.94 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4.0 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$\approx 1.03 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A/cm}^2 = 103\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$$

(Note: At 1.2 nm and 1 V, direct tunneling coexists and may dominate; the FN estimate is a lower bound.)

**Step 5 (2 marks): Compute $I_{gate}$ and discuss implications.**

$$A_{gate} = 1 \mu\text{m} \times 0.1 \mu\text{m} = 10^{-10}\ \text{cm}^2$$

$$I_{gate} = J \cdot A = 1.03 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 10^{-10} = 1.03 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{A} = 10\ \text{fA}$$

Even per device, this leakage, multiplied across **billions of transistors** on a chip, yields total gate leakage in the **mA–A range**, severely impacting static power.

**Implications for low-power design:** Such leakage is unacceptable for battery-powered (mobile, IoT, biomedical) applications. Mitigations include (i) using **high-κ dielectrics** (HfO₂) with greater physical thickness at the same EOT, (ii) **dual- or multi-threshold** design with thicker-oxide devices on non-critical paths, (iii) **power gating** to fully cut off $V_{DD}$ in idle mode.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the mechanism of **direct tunneling** through a thin gate oxide in an NMOS transistor operating in inversion. Compare its magnitude with **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling** for $t_{ox} = 1.5$ nm. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** Explain why pure SiO₂ below 2 nm is no longer used as a gate dielectric in modern CMOS. Discuss the role of **high-κ dielectrics** and **metal gates** in solving the gate tunneling problem. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Direct Tunneling Mechanism (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Band diagram description.** Under strong inversion ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$), the surface of the p-type substrate bends downward, forming an inversion layer of electrons. The conduction band of Si at the interface lies **below** the Fermi level. A **rectangular potential barrier** of height $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV and width $t_{ox}$ separates these electrons from the polysilicon gate.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Tunneling mechanism.** The electron wavefunction in the inversion layer is not zero at the Si/SiO₂ interface; it **decays exponentially** through the oxide. Because $t_{ox} < 3$ nm, a non-negligible amplitude reaches the gate side, where it can be absorbed into available states. This is **direct tunneling**.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Comparison with FN tunneling.** At $t_{ox} = 1.5$ nm and typical $V_{GS} \sim 1$ V:
- $E_{ox} \approx 6.67$ MV/cm (moderate).
- FN tunneling contributes, but is **suppressed** by the barrier shape.
- Direct tunneling **dominates** because the rectangular barrier is thin enough that the wavefunction penetrates fully.
- Rule of thumb: $J_{direct} / J_{FN} \sim 10$–$100\times$ at $t_{ox} = 1.5$ nm and $V_{GS} = 1$ V.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Concluding statement.** As $t_{ox}$ is reduced further, the exponential dependence on $t_{ox}$ makes direct tunneling the controlling leakage mechanism — and it does so without requiring high fields.

---

#### Part (b) — Why Pure SiO₂ Is Abandoned (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): The scaling crisis.** The International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS) demanded EOT below ~1.0 nm at the 45 nm node and beyond. A pure SiO₂ layer at EOT = 1.0 nm has $t_{ox} = 1.0$ nm. At this thickness, **direct tunneling leakage exceeds 100 A/cm²** — equivalent to watts of static power per square centimeter of active silicon.

**Step 2 (2 marks): The high-κ solution.** By replacing SiO₂ with a dielectric of higher permittivity (e.g., HfO₂, $\kappa \approx 25$), the **same capacitance** is achieved with a much **greater physical thickness**:

$$t_{high\kappa} = EOT \cdot \frac{\kappa_{high\kappa}}{\kappa_{SiO_2}} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{25}{3.9} \approx 6.4\ \text{nm}$$

This physically thicker barrier **suppresses tunneling** exponentially (each additional 0.5 nm of SiO₂-equivalent barrier thickness reduces leakage by roughly an order of magnitude).

**Step 3 (2 marks): The metal gate companion.** Poly-Si gates suffer from **Fermi-level pinning** and **depletion effects** when used with high-κ dielectrics, increasing EOT and degrading mobility. Replacing poly-Si with **metal gates** (e.g., TiN, TaN, TiAlC) eliminates polysilicon depletion, tunes the workfunction for proper $V_{th}$ control, and avoids dopant diffusion issues.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Conclusion.** The combination of **high-κ + metal gate** (introduced commercially by Intel at the 45 nm node in 2007) was the breakthrough that allowed continued oxide scaling **without** exponentially rising gate leakage — a cornerstone of modern low-power VLSI.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Gate oxide tunneling is quantum mechanical transport of electrons/holes through SiO₂ when $t_{ox} < 3$ nm.
- **Two mechanisms:** Fowler–Nordheim (high field, triangular barrier) and Direct (low field, rectangular barrier).
- **Key formula:** $J_{FN} = A_{FN} E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B_{FN}/E_{ox})$.
- **Exponential sensitivity:** $J_{gate}$ scales as $e^{-t_{ox}}$; halving $t_{ox}$ can increase leakage by **2–4 orders of magnitude**.
- **Critical constants:** $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV (electrons), $\Phi_B = 4.68$ eV (holes), $m_{ox}^* = 0.5 m_0$.
- **Standard formula:** $B_{FN} \approx 2.38 \times 10^{8}$ V/cm for SiO₂ with $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV.
- **Engineering fix:** Replace SiO₂ with **high-κ dielectrics** (HfO₂) and use **metal gates** to scale EOT without scaling physical $t_{ox}$.
- **Power impact:** Gate leakage contributes to **static power** $P = V_{DD} \cdot I_{gate}$, dominating in 65 nm and below without mitigation.
- **Verification signature:** FN tunneling yields a straight line in $\ln(J/E_{ox}^2)$ vs $1/E_{ox}$.
- **Distinguish $t_{ox}$ vs EOT:** $t_{ox}$ = physical; EOT = SiO₂-equivalent for capacitance.
- **Units caution:** FN constants are typically given in **CGS** — convert fields to V/cm before plugging in.
- **Low-power tactics:** Power gating, multi-$V_{th}$, reverse body bias, dual-oxide thickness, FinFET/MGAA structures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subthreshold Leakage Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Subthreshold Leakage Current # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subthreshold Leakage Current ($I_{sub}$ or $I_{leak}$)**, also known as **weak-inversion conduction current**, is the parasitic drain-to-source current that flows through a MOSFET when the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) is biased **below the threshold voltage** ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$). Although the transistor is nominally "OFF," the channel does not turn off abruptly; it conducts a small exponential current driven by carrier diffusion across the channel, analogous to current flow in a bipolar junction transistor. In modern nanometer-scale CMOS (e.g., 90 nm, 65 nm, 45 nm, and beyond), the supply voltage ($V_{DD}$) and the threshold voltage ($V_{th}$) are scaled down in tandem. This compresses the ratio $V_{th}/V_{DD}$, causing the subthreshold leakage to grow **exponentially** with every new technology node. Consequently, $I_{sub}$ has emerged as a **dominant component of static (leakage) power dissipation** in portable and standby-mode VLSI systems. $$ P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sub} \cdot N_{devices} $$ where $N_{devices}$ is the total number of "OFF" transistors in the circuit. > [!NOTE] > **Standard metric reported in textbooks**: The **Subthreshold Swing ($S$)** is defined as the inverse slope of the $\log_{10}(I_{DS})$ vs. $V_{GS}$ curve in weak inversion, expressed in **mV/decade**. A lower $S$ means a steeper switching transistor (better turn-off), while the theoretical room-temperature lower bound of a conventional bulk MOSFET is $S \approx 60$ **mV/decade**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Leaky Faucet** Imagine a standard household water tap (faucet). The handle (gate) controls a rubber washer that seals the pipe (channel) to stop the water (current) flow. * **Strongly OFF** (washer tightly pressed): No water flows. * **Moderately ON** (handle lifted): A full, controlled stream flows — this is **strong inversion** ($V_{GS} \gg V_{th}$). * **Slightly OFF** (handle almost closed): The washer *almost* seals the pipe, but a few drops still trickle through because water finds microscopic gaps. This **dribble** is the **subthreshold leakage current** — the transistor is "off" by design, but it is never perfectly off. In nanometer CMOS, this "dribble" is no longer negligible. In a billion-transistor chip with 99% of transistors idle (in standby), that small drip per transistor adds up to a significant static power draw, draining the battery of a smartphone even when the screen is off. --- ## 1.3 Physical Origin of Subthreshold Conduction When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$: * The gate-induced surface potential ($\psi_s$) is **insufficient** to form a strong inversion layer of electrons. * However, a small concentration of mobile carriers still exists at the oxide–silicon interface due to the **Boltzmann distribution of electrons** governed by the surface potential. * These minority carriers **diffuse** from source to drain (similar to charge transport in a BJT base region), giving rise to a current that is **exponential** in $V_{GS}$ rather than quadratic as in strong inversion. The two key physical constants to remember: | Constant | Symbol | Value (T = 300 K) | Role | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$ J/K | Thermal carrier agitation | | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | $\mathbf{\approx 25.85}$ mV | Fundamental scaling of subthreshold slope | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Subthreshold Current Equation (BSIM Form) The industry-standard closed-form model used in SPICE (Berkeley Short-channel IGFET Model — BSIM) expresses the subthreshold drain current per unit width as: $$ I_{sub} = \mu_0 \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \, V_T^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right] $$ ### Parameter-by-Parameter Breakdown: * **$\mu_0$** → Low-field carrier mobility (electron for NMOS, hole for PMOS). Unit: $\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$. * **$C_{ox}$** → Gate-oxide capacitance per unit area, $C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$. Unit: F/m². * **$W/L$** → Transistor aspect ratio (drawn dimensions). * **$V_T = kT/q$** → Thermal voltage ($\approx 25.85$ mV at room temperature). * **$V_{th}$** → Threshold voltage, dependent on body bias and channel length (DIBL, RSCE). * **$n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$** → Subthreshold ideality factor (slope factor); typically **1.3 to 1.5** in short-channel devices. * **$C_{dm}$** → Depletion-layer capacitance of the channel. * **$V_{GS}, V_{DS}$** → Applied terminal voltages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why exponential?** Because carrier density at the surface follows a Boltzmann distribution in the surface potential $\psi_s$, and $\psi_s$ is approximately linearly controlled by $V_{GS}$. Thus $n_{surface} \propto \exp(V_{GS}/V_T)$, and the resulting diffusion current inherits this exponential dependence. ### 2.1.1 Simplification for $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ When $V_{DS} > 3V_T$ (i.e., > ~78 mV at room temperature), the term $\exp(-V_{DS}/V_T) \to 0$, and the equation reduces to: $$ I_{sub} \approx \mu_0 \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \, V_T^2 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right) $$ This is the form most commonly used in KTU numerical problems and design analyses. --- ## 2.2 Subthreshold Swing ($S$) — The "Steepness" of Turn-Off The **subthreshold swing** quantifies how effectively the transistor turns off. It is defined as the change in gate voltage required to reduce the drain current by **one decade (a factor of 10)**. $$ S = \left( \frac{d(\log_{10} I_{DS})}{dV_{GS}} \right)^{-1} = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 2.3 \, n V_T $$ Substituting $V_T = kT/q$: $$ S = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \left(1 + \frac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Theoretical Room-Temperature Lower Bound:** When $C_{dm} = 0$ (impractical), $S_{min} = (kT/q)\ln(10) \approx \mathbf{60}$ **mV/decade** at T = 300 K. This is the **Boltzmann tyranny** limit — a fundamental physical constraint of conventional bulk MOSFETs that can only be beaten by novel devices such as **FinFETs**, **Gate-All-Around (GAA) FETs**, or **Negative-Capacitance FETs**. ### Temperature Dependence of $S$ $$ S(T) = S_{300K} \cdot \frac{T}{300} $$ $S$ increases linearly with temperature because $V_T \propto T$. Therefore, leakage **worsens** at high temperatures — a critical design concern for hot-running processors. --- ## 2.3 DIBL — Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering In short-channel transistors, the drain terminal electrostatically **couples** to the channel potential, effectively lowering the threshold voltage as $V_{DS}$ increases. This is modeled as: $$ V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta \, V_{DS} $$ where $\eta$ is the **DIBL coefficient** (in V/V), typically **0.05 to 0.2 V/V** for sub-100 nm devices. A higher $\eta$ means stronger leakage, especially when $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$ is large. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Key Variables | Typical Range / Unit | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $I_{sub} = \mu_0 C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)$ | Subthreshold current (sat. region) | $W, L, V_{GS}, V_{th}$ | nA to µA per transistor | | 2 | $V_T = kT/q$ | Thermal voltage | $T$ | 25.85 mV at 300 K | | 3 | $S = nV_T \ln(10)$ | Subthreshold swing | $n$ | 60–100 mV/decade | | 4 | $S_{min} = (kT/q)\ln(10)$ | Boltzmann limit at 300 K | — | **60 mV/decade** | | 5 | $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ | Ideality factor | $C_{dm}, C_{ox}$ | 1.1 – 1.5 | | 6 | $V_{th} = V_{th0} - \eta V_{DS}$ | DIBL-shifted threshold | $\eta$ | 0.05 – 0.2 V/V | | 7 | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sub} \cdot N$ | Static leakage power | $N$ | W (Watts) | | 8 | $I_{sub}(T) \propto T^2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2kT}\right) \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS}-V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)$ | Temperature-scaled leakage | $E_g$ | Used in thermal analysis | | 9 | $V_{th}(T) = V_{th}(T_0) - \kappa (T - T_0)$ | Threshold temp. coefficient | $\kappa$ | $\sim$0.7 mV/°C (typical) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Subthreshold leakage is the **single largest contributor to standby power** in modern smartphones, IoT sensors, and always-on processors. Designers use the formulas above to estimate battery life, choose **High-$V_{th}$ (HVT)** vs. **Low-$V_{th}$ (LVT)** cells, and decide on **power-gating** boundaries. Cadence, Synopsys, and Mentor tools internally evaluate $S$, $\eta$, and $n$ during standard-cell characterization for these exact reasons. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Subthreshold Current Equation from First Principles > **Goal:** Starting from the surface potential, derive the exponential dependence of $I_{sub}$ on $V_{GS}$. ### Step 1 — Carrier Concentration at the Surface When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the silicon surface is in **weak inversion**. The electron concentration at the surface ($n_s$) is given by Boltzmann statistics applied to the surface potential $\psi_s$: $$ n_s = n_{i} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_s}{V_T}\right) $$ where $n_i$ is the intrinsic carrier concentration of silicon ($\approx 1.45 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ at 300 K). ### Step 2 — Relation Between $\psi_s$ and $V_{GS}$ Charge neutrality at the oxide–silicon interface (ignoring interface traps and quantum effects) gives: $$ V_{GS} = V_{fb} + \psi_s + \frac{\sqrt{2 \epsilon_{si} q N_A \psi_s}}{C_{ox}} $$ where $V_{fb}$ is the flat-band voltage and $N_A$ is the substrate doping. In **weak inversion**, the second term (depletion charge) is small compared to $\psi_s$, so the relationship is approximately linear: $$ \psi_s \approx \frac{V_{GS} - V_{fb}}{1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}} = \frac{V_{GS} - V_{fb}}{n} $$ where the ideality factor is: $$ n = 1 + \frac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}} $$ ### Step 3 — Current by Diffusion In weak inversion, the drift component is negligible, and the drain current is dominated by **carrier diffusion** along the channel: $$ I_{DS} = -q D_n W \frac{d n_s(x)}{dx} $$ where $D_n = \mu_n V_T$ (Einstein relation). ### Step 4 — Integrate Along the Channel The electron density varies along the channel from source ($\psi_{s,L}$) to drain ($\psi_{s,R}$). Integrating: $$ I_{DS} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{L \cdot n} \cdot V_T^2 \cdot \left[ n_s(0) - n_s(L) \right] $$ ### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions Using the surface potential at the two ends: * At source: $n_s(0) \propto \exp[(V_{GS} - V_{th})/(nV_T)]$ * At drain: $n_s(L) \propto \exp[(V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{DS})/(nV_T)]$ $$ I_{sub} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right) \left[ 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right) \right] $$ ### Step 6 — Saturation Limit For $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ (typically $V_{DS} > 3V_T \approx 78$ mV), the second exponential term vanishes: $$ \boxed{I_{sub} \approx \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)} $$ This is the **canonical subthreshold leakage equation** used in all KTU numerical problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Subthreshold Swing ($S$) Starting from the saturation form: $$ I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right) $$ Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$ \log_{10}(I_{sub}) = \log_{10}(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T \ln(10)} $$ Differentiate with respect to $V_{GS}$: $$ \frac{d[\log_{10}(I_{sub})]}{dV_{GS}} = \frac{1}{n V_T \ln(10)} $$ By the definition of subthreshold swing ($S$ is the inverse slope): $$ \boxed{S = n V_T \ln(10) = \ln(10) \left(1 + \frac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}\right) \frac{kT}{q}} $$ Substituting $T = 300$ K and $n = 1$ (idealized): $$ S_{min} = (25.85 \text{ mV}) \times \ln(10) \approx 59.6 \text{ mV/decade} \approx \mathbf{60 \text{ mV/decade}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]:** > An NMOS transistor in a 90 nm process has the following parameters at $T = 300$ K: > $\mu_n C_{ox} = 270$ µA/V², $W/L = 2$, $V_{th} = 0.35$ V, $n = 1.4$, $V_{GS} = 0$ V (OFF state), $V_{DS} = 1.0$ V. > **(a)** Calculate the subthreshold leakage current. > **(b)** If the temperature rises to 100 °C (373 K), find the new leakage current. Assume $V_{th}$ decreases by **0.8 mV/°C** and $\mu_n$ drops by 20% (use effective values). ### Solution: #### (a) At T = 300 K $$ V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 25.875 \text{ mV} $$ Since $V_{DS} = 1.0$ V $\gg 3V_T = 0.078$ V, we use the simplified form: $$ I_{sub} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right) $$ $$ I_{sub} = 270 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times (0.025875)^2 \times \exp\!\left(\frac{0 - 0.35}{1.4 \times 0.025875}\right) $$ Step-by-step: * $V_T^2 = (0.025875)^2 = 6.95 \times 10^{-4}$ V² * $nV_T = 1.4 \times 0.025875 = 0.036225$ V * Exponent: $\dfrac{-0.35}{0.036225} = -9.661$ * $\exp(-9.661) = 6.36 \times 10^{-5}$ $$ I_{sub} = 270 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 6.95 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.36 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ I_{sub} = 5.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.95 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.36 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{sub} \approx 2.39 \times 10^{-11} \text{ A} = 23.9 \text{ pA}} $$ #### (b) At T = 373 K (100 °C) * New $V_T = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 373}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 32.18$ mV * Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 100$ °C * $\Delta V_{th} = -0.8 \text{ mV/°C} \times 100 = -0.08$ V * New $V_{th} = 0.35 - 0.08 = 0.27$ V * Effective $\mu_n C_{ox} = 270 \times 0.80 = 216$ µA/V² * $nV_T = 1.4 \times 0.03218 = 0.04505$ V Exponent: $$ \frac{0 - 0.27}{0.04505} = -5.993 $$ $$ \exp(-5.993) = 2.50 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ I_{sub} = 216 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times (0.03218)^2 \times 2.50 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ V_T^2 = (0.03218)^2 = 1.0355 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}^2 $$ $$ I_{sub} = 4.32 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0355 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.50 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ I_{sub} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A} = 1.12 \text{ nA} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** The leakage current increased by a factor of **~47×** (from 23.9 pA to 1.12 nA) due to a 73 K temperature rise. This is a dramatic demonstration of the **exponential thermal sensitivity** of subthreshold leakage — a critical design consideration for hot-running SoCs. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Leakage vs. Design Parameters | Design Lever | Effect on $I_{sub}$ | Engineering Trade-off | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Increase $V_{th}$ (HVT cells) | **Exponentially decreases** | Slower speed, lower drive | | Increase channel length $L$ | **Decreases** (reduces DIBL) | Larger area, more capacitance | | Increase $V_{GS}$ (negative) | **Exponentially decreases** | Stacking, MTCMOS | | Increase $t_{ox}$ (thick oxide) | Decreases (raises $S$) | Worse short-channel control | | Increase temperature | **Exponentially increases** | Thermal runaway risk | | Increase $V_{DD}$ | Increases (via DIBL) | Higher dynamic power | | Body bias (Reverse Body Bias) | **Decreases** $V_{th}$ raised → $I_{sub}$ falls | Forward body effect on performance | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ids vs Vgs Characteristic (Regions of Operation) The following Mermaid block diagrams the **conceptual I–V curve** of a MOSFET, marking the subthreshold region, moderate inversion, and strong inversion. The leakage current $I_{sub}$ flows even when the transistor is nominally OFF. ```mermaid flowchart TB axisOrigin[("Vgs = 0 V
OFF STATE")] region1["REGION 1: SUBTHRESHOLD
I_sub ranges 1e-12 to 1e-9 A
Exponential in Vgs
Diffusion-dominated"] region2["REGION 2: MODERATE INVERSION
Transition zone Vgs approx Vth
Both drift and diffusion contribute"] region3["REGION 3: STRONG INVERSION
Ids quadratic in Vgs
Drift-dominated
Saturation region reached at Vdsat"] region4["Vgs = Vth
THRESHOLD BOUNDARY"] leakage["Ileak at Vgs = 0
Multiplied by N devices
equals P_static of chip"] swing["SUBTHRESHOLD SWING S
defines transition steepness
Ideal = 60 mV/decade at 300K"] axisOrigin --> region1 region1 -->|"Vgs increases"| region4 region4 --> region2 region2 -->|"Vgs increases further"| region3 region1 -.->|"Dominates static power"| leakage region1 -.->|"Key metric"| swing ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Sources of Leakage in a CMOS Inverter The schematic below maps the **physical locations** where subthreshold leakage (and its relatives) originate in a static CMOS inverter. ```mermaid flowchart LR vdd["V_DD Rail
1.0 V to 1.2 V"]:::power subgraph nmosBlock["NMOS TRANSISTOR N1"] gateN1["Gate
Input = 0 V"] chanN1["Subthreshold Conduction
Channel electrons diffuse
from Drain to Source"] dibN1["DIBL Effect
Vth reduced by eta times Vds"] bodyN1["Body Terminal
Vb grounded"] end subgraph pmosBlock["PMOS TRANSISTOR P1"] gateP1["Gate
Input = 0 V
P1 is ON"] chanP1["Strong Inversion Hole Channel
PMOS drives output to VDD"] end outputNode["Output Node Y
Charged to VDD by P1"]:::output gnd["Ground Rail"]:::power vdd --> pmosBlock nmosBlock --> gnd pmosBlock --> outputNode chanN1 -. "Isub flows
leakage path" .-> gnd dibN1 -. "worsens Isub" .-> chanN1 bodyN1 -. "Reverse body bias reduces Isub" .-> chanN1 classDef power fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef output fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Flow: Leakage Reduction Design Flow This Mermaid block illustrates the **engineering decision flow** a designer follows to mitigate subthreshold leakage in a 65 nm SoC. ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Start: Estimate Isub using BSIM Equation"] stepA{"Power budget
violated?"} stepB["Apply Multi-Vth Cell Library
Use HVT in non-critical paths"] stepC["Apply Reverse Body Bias
Vb less than 0 V for NMOS"] stepD["Apply Transistor Stacking
Two OFF transistors in series"] stepE["Apply Power Gating
Use sleep transistors MTCMOS"] stepF["Final Verification
SPICE Monte Carlo at 125 C"] goal["Static power within budget"] start --> stepA stepA -->|Yes| stepB stepA -->|No| goal stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> goal ``` ## 4.4 Concept Map: Subthreshold Leakage Taxonomy ```mermaid mindmap root(("Subthreshold Leakage Current")) Physics Weak Inversion Diffusion Transport Boltzmann Distribution Boltzmann Tyranny at 60 mV per dec Model BSIM Isub Equation Subthreshold Swing S Ideality Factor n Effects DIBL Drain Induced Barrier Lowering Reverse Short Channel Effect RSCE Body Effect Temperature Sensitivity Mitigation High Vth Cells Reverse Body Bias RBB Stacked Transistors Multi Threshold CMOS MTCMOS Power Gating FinFET and GAA FETs ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define subthreshold leakage current. Why is it considered a critical problem in modern deep-submicron CMOS circuits?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** * **Definition (1.5 marks):** Subthreshold leakage current ($I_{sub}$) is the small drain-to-source current that flows in a MOSFET when the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) is **below the threshold voltage** ($V_{th}$). It arises from diffusion of minority carriers in the weak-inversion regime and follows an exponential dependence on $V_{GS}$. * **Why critical (1.5 marks):** As CMOS technology scales to deep-submicron nodes (e.g., 45 nm, 22 nm), $V_{th}$ is reduced to maintain performance and $V_{DD}$ is scaled. Because $I_{sub} \propto \exp[-(V_{th} - V_{GS})/(nV_T)]$, a small reduction in $V_{th}$ causes a **large exponential increase** in $I_{sub}$. With billions of transistors per chip and most idle in standby, $I_{sub}$ becomes the **dominant component of static (leakage) power**, severely impacting battery life in portable devices and causing thermal issues in high-performance ICs. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is subthreshold swing (S)? Explain the significance of the "60 mV/decade" limit at room temperature.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** * **Definition (1.5 marks):** Subthreshold swing $S$ is defined as the gate voltage change required to change the drain current by **one decade (a factor of 10)** in the subthreshold region. It is given by $S = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 2.3 \cdot n V_T$, where $n$ is the subthreshold ideality factor and $V_T = kT/q$ is the thermal voltage. * **Significance of 60 mV/decade (1.5 marks):** The lower bound of $S$ (when $n = 1$) at $T = 300$ K is: $$ S_{min} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(10) \approx 25.85 \text{ mV} \times 2.3026 \approx 59.6 \text{ mV/decade} \approx 60 \text{ mV/decade} $$ This represents a **fundamental physical limit** (sometimes called *Boltzmann's tyranny*) for conventional bulk MOSFETs. It means that even in an ideal transistor, a 60 mV reduction in $V_{GS}$ can only suppress leakage by a factor of 10. This limits how low $V_{DD}$ and $V_{th}$ can be scaled, posing a major challenge for low-power design and motivating the development of **steep-slope devices** such as **FinFETs, Tunnel FETs, and Negative-Capacitance FETs**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** **A(a). Derive the expression for subthreshold leakage current $I_{sub}$ of a MOSFET in saturation. Clearly state all assumptions used. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The subthreshold current arises from carrier diffusion in the weak-inversion regime, where the surface potential is small enough that no strong inversion layer forms, but a small concentration of mobile carriers still exists at the Si–SiO₂ interface. **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Weak inversion regime: $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ and surface potential $\psi_s$ is small. 2. Drift component is negligible; current is **diffusion-dominated**. 3. Carrier mobility $\mu_n$ is constant (low-field). 4. Boltzmann statistics govern carrier density at the surface. 5. $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ (saturation-like condition for the exponential term). **Derivation (6 marks):** * **Step 1:** The electron concentration at the silicon surface: $$ n_s = n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_s}{V_T}\right) $$ * **Step 2:** In weak inversion, $\psi_s$ is linearly related to gate voltage via the ideality factor $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$: $$ \psi_s \approx \frac{V_{GS} - V_{fb}}{n} \implies n_s \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{nV_T}\right) $$ * **Step 3:** Drain current by diffusion along the channel: $$ I_{DS} = -q D_n W \frac{dn_s}{dx} = \mu_n V_T \, qW \frac{dn_s}{dx} $$ * **Step 4:** Integrating from source (x = 0) to drain (x = L), and applying boundary conditions $n_s(0) \propto \exp(V_{GS}/nV_T)$ and $n_s(L) \propto \exp[(V_{GS} - V_{DS})/nV_T]$: $$ I_{sub} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right) \left[ 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right) \right] $$ * **Step 5:** For $V_{DS} > 3V_T$ (saturation), the bracketed term ≈ 1, yielding the canonical form: $$ \boxed{I_{sub} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)} $$ --- **A(b). A 65 nm NMOS transistor has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 300\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 5$, $V_{th} = 0.3$ V, $n = 1.35$, $V_{GS} = 0$ V (idle), $V_{DS} = 1.1$ V at 300 K. (7 marks)** * **(i)** Calculate $I_{sub}$. * **(ii)** If the design team applies a **Reverse Body Bias** of $-0.3$ V, raising $V_{th}$ to 0.42 V, find the new $I_{sub}$ and the percentage reduction in leakage. **Model Answer:** **(i) Calculation at V_th = 0.3 V (1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks):** * $V_T = kT/q = (1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300) / (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 25.875$ mV = 0.02588 V * $nV_T = 1.35 \times 0.02588 = 0.03493$ V * Exponent = $(0 - 0.3) / 0.03493 = -8.589$ * $\exp(-8.589) = 1.88 \times 10^{-4}$ * $V_T^2 = (0.02588)^2 = 6.697 \times 10^{-4}$ V² * $I_{sub} = 300 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 6.697 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.88 \times 10^{-4}$ * $I_{sub} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.697 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.88 \times 10^{-4}$ * $\boxed{I_{sub} \approx 1.89 \times 10^{-10} \text{ A} = 189 \text{ pA}}$ **(ii) With V_th = 0.42 V (2 + 2 = 4 marks):** * Exponent = $(0 - 0.42) / 0.03493 = -12.024$ * $\exp(-12.024) = 5.95 \times 10^{-6}$ * $I_{sub}^{new} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.697 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.95 \times 10^{-6}$ * $I_{sub}^{new} \approx 5.98 \times 10^{-12} \text{ A} = 5.98$ pA **Percentage reduction:** $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{189 - 5.98}{189} \times 100\% \approx 96.8\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Insight:** A 0.12 V increase in $V_{th}$ (40% relative) causes a **~32× reduction** in leakage current. This dramatic effect is the basis of multi-$V_{th}$ design and reverse body bias techniques used in all modern low-power SoCs (e.g., Snapdragon, Apple M-series). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]** **B(a). With a neat sketch, explain the $\log(I_{DS})$ vs $V_{GS}$ characteristic of a MOSFET. Mark and define the subthreshold swing S. Discuss the factors that affect S. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** * **Sketch (2 marks):** A semi-log plot of $I_{DS}$ (y-axis) vs $V_{GS}$ (x-axis) showing three regions: * **Region I — Subthreshold:** Exponential straight line with slope $1/(nV_T \ln 10)$ per unit $V_{GS}$. * **Region II — Moderate inversion:** Smooth transition. * **Region III — Strong inversion:** Quadratic/quasi-linear in $V_{GS}$ when plotted on log scale. * **Subthreshold Swing (S) Definition (2 marks):** $S$ is the inverse slope of the $\log_{10}(I_{DS})$ vs $V_{GS}$ curve in weak inversion: $$ S = \left[ \frac{d(\log_{10} I_{DS})}{dV_{GS}} \right]^{-1} = n V_T \ln(10) $$ $S$ is expressed in **mV/decade**, indicating the $V_{GS}$ change needed to reduce $I_{DS}$ by a factor of 10. * **Factors affecting S (3 marks):** 1. **Temperature (T):** $S \propto T$ because $V_T = kT/q$. Higher $T$ → larger $S$ → worse turn-off. 2. **Substrate doping (N_A):** Higher $N_A$ increases depletion capacitance $C_{dm}$, increasing $n$ and thus $S$. 3. **Body bias:** Reverse body bias raises $V_{th}$ and alters $C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ ratio. 4. **Channel length:** Short-channel effects (DIBL, RSCE) effectively degrade the swing. 5. **Interface traps and oxide thickness:** $C_{ox}$ and interface-trap capacitance modify the $n$ factor. 6. **Device architecture:** FinFETs and GAA FETs have steeper $S$ (approaching 60 mV/dec) due to better gate electrostatic control. --- **B(b). Explain Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL). How does it affect subthreshold leakage in short-channel MOSFETs? A 45 nm NMOS has $V_{th0} = 0.32$ V at $V_{DS} = 0.05$ V, and DIBL coefficient $\eta = 0.12$ V/V. Find the effective threshold at $V_{DS} = 1.0$ V. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** * **DIBL Explanation (3 marks):** DIBL is a short-channel effect in which the drain terminal electrostatically couples to the channel potential, effectively **lowering the source-to-channel potential barrier** as $V_{DS}$ increases. The barrier lowering allows carriers to flow more easily from source to drain even when $V_{GS} < V_{th0}$, increasing $I_{sub}$. It is modeled as a linear reduction in $V_{th}$ with $V_{DS}$: $$ V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta V_{DS} $$ * **Effect on subthreshold leakage (2 marks):** Since $I_{sub} \propto \exp[-(V_{th} - V_{GS})/(nV_T)]$, even a 50–100 mV reduction in $V_{th}$ due to DIBL can cause leakage to grow by **an order of magnitude or more**. This is a primary reason subthreshold leakage skyrockets in sub-100 nm technologies. * **Numerical Solution (2 marks):** $$ V_{th}(1.0) = 0.32 - 0.12 \times (1.0 - 0.05) = 0.32 - 0.12 \times 0.95 $$ $$ V_{th}(1.0) = 0.32 - 0.114 = 0.206 \text{ V} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{th} = 0.206 \text{ V (a reduction of 114 mV)}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > * **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget that DIBL uses $V_{DS}$ *relative to the reference bias*, not the absolute $V_{DD}$. In the above problem, $V_{DS} = 1.0$ V is the operational value, while $V_{th0}$ is specified at a low $V_{DS} = 0.05$ V, so the DIBL drop must be computed on the difference. > * **Common Mistake 2:** Many students use the **approximate form** $V_{th} = V_{th0} - \eta V_{DD}$ without specifying the reference, leading to a wrong answer (0.2 V vs 0.206 V). > * **Common Mistake 3:** In exponential subthreshold calculations, do **not** substitute $V_{th}$ in volts while leaving the exponent in mV — **unify all units first**. > * **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to include the $(1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T))$ term for $V_{DS} < 3V_T$ (~78 mV). This is critical when $V_{DS}$ is very small (e.g., in near-threshold or subthreshold logic). > * **Mark Loss Impact:** Careless unit mismatches alone typically cost **1 to 1.5 marks** in KTU valuation; missing the diffusion-derived exponential form of $I_{sub}$ can cost **2 to 3 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Subthreshold Leakage Current** * **Definition:** $I_{sub}$ is the **diffusion current** that flows when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ (weak inversion regime). * **Canonical Equation (BSIM):** $I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} (W/L) V_T^2 \exp[(V_{GS} - V_{th})/(nV_T)][1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)]$ * **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — memorize this value. * **Subthreshold Swing:** $S = nV_T \ln(10) \approx 2.3 nV_T$ — measured in **mV/decade**. * **Boltzmann Limit:** $S_{min} = 60$ mV/decade at 300 K (for $n = 1$). * **Ideality Factor:** $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ — typically 1.3–1.5 in real short-channel devices. * **DIBL Effect:** $V_{th}$ decreases with increasing $V_{DS}$; quantified by $\eta$ in V/V. * **Temperature:** $I_{sub}$ roughly **doubles every 10 °C** rise (exponential dependence via $V_T$ and $V_{th}$). * **Leakage Power:** $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sub} \cdot N_{OFF}$ — scales with transistor count. * **Reduction Techniques (must know for viva & design questions):** 1. **Multi-$V_{th}$ libraries** (HVT, SVT, LVT cells) 2. **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** 3. **Transistor Stacking** (series OFF transistors reduce effective $V_{DS}$) 4. **Power Gating (MTCMOS)** — sleep transistors cut $V_{DD}$ to idle blocks 5. **Input Vector Control (IVC)** — choose input patterns to maximize stacking 6. **Device-level solutions** — FinFETs, GAA FETs, NC-FETs * **Quick comparison:** $I_{sub} \propto \exp(-V_{th}/nV_T)$ — every 100 mV increase in $V_{th}$ reduces leakage by **~10×** (assuming $nV_T \approx 36$ mV). * **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** This topic is foundational for Module 1 and is a prerequisite for understanding **dynamic power**, **clock gating**, and **power-gating architectures** in subsequent modules. * **Frequently missed KTU concept:** The $(1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T))$ term is **NOT** a small correction at low $V_{DS}$. In sub-threshold logic circuits (e.g., ultra-low-power IoT), $V_{DS}$ may be intentionally below $3V_T$ — always check this assumption before simplifying. * **Real-world hook:** Apple M1/M2 chips use a **mix of HVT/SVT/LVT cells** plus **power gating** in the always-on domain — all based on the subthreshold principles covered here.

topics.title: Emerging Technologies for Low Power: Hi-K Gate Dielectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emerging Technologies for Low Power: Hi-K Gate Dielectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Hi-K (High-K) Gate Dielectric** is an advanced insulating material with a **relative permittivity (k or εᵣ) significantly greater than that of silicon dioxide (SiO₂, k ≈ 3.9)**, used in MOSFET gate stacks to suppress the **direct tunneling leakage current** that arises from aggressive gate-oxide thickness (tₒₓ) scaling below ~2 nm in sub-90 nm CMOS technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hi-K dielectrics are classified as an *emerging technology* for low-power VLSI because they allow the *physical* gate insulator to be made physically thicker (suppressing leakage) while maintaining a *smaller Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)*, thereby preserving gate electrostatic control of the channel.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Leaky Water Pipe" View

Imagine a **rubber balloon** (the SiO₂ oxide) stretched over a **garden tap** (the gate electrode). When you want fine control of water flow with minimum hand pressure, you stretch the balloon extremely thin. But as the balloon gets ultra-thin (~1.5 nm), it develops **microscopic pin-hole leaks** — water drips through even when the tap is closed. This is exactly the **direct-tunneling leakage** that plagues thin SiO₂.

The **Hi-K solution** is to replace the thin balloon with a **thicker, denser sponge** made of a special material (like **HfO₂**) that absorbs the electric field internally. Even though the sponge is physically thicker, its ability to store charge (capacitance) is the same as the ultra-thin balloon. Result: **the leak stops, but the tap control is preserved.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off (the "Why"):**
> - Thinner SiO₂ ⇒ Higher gate capacitance (Cₒₓ) ⇒ Better drive current
> - Thinner SiO₂ ⇒ **Exponentially higher tunneling leakage** ⇒ **Static power crisis**
> - Hi-K breaks this trade-off: thicker physically, smaller electrically.

### 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Relative permittivity of SiO₂ (k_SiO₂)** = **3.9**
- **Relative permittivity of HfO₂ (k_HfO₂)** ≈ **22–25**
- **Relative permittivity of Si₃N₄** ≈ **7.5**
- **Vacuum permittivity ε₀** = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- **Electron rest mass m₀** = **9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- **Planck's constant (reduced) ℏ** = **1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Elementary charge q** = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Standard metrics used in industry:** EOT (nm), CET, J₉ (leakage at V₉ = -1 V in A/cm²).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — Why and How

**The "Why" — The Scaling Bottleneck:**
For every technology node (90 nm → 65 nm → 45 nm → 32 nm), the gate-oxide thickness tₒₓ had to be reduced by ~30% to maintain drive current. By the **45 nm node**, tₒₓ dropped below **1.5 nm** (≈ 5 atomic layers of SiO₂). At this scale, the **wave-function of electrons in the gate** can tunnel through the oxide, producing a **direct-tunneling current** even when the transistor is OFF. Static power dissipation became comparable to dynamic power.

**The "How" — Physics of the Hi-K Stack:**
1. Replace SiO₂ with a high-permittivity insulator (HfO₂, HfSiO₄, HfSiON, ZrO₂, La₂O₃, Al₂O₃).
2. The **physical thickness t_hi-K** can be 5–10× larger than the original tₒₓ, eliminating pinholes and direct tunneling.
3. The *electrically effective* thickness is reported as **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)**:

   $$ \text{EOT} = t_{\text{hi-K}} \times \frac{k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{k_{\text{hi-K}}} $$

4. A **thin interfacial SiO₂ or SiON layer** (~0.5–0.7 nm) is intentionally grown between the Si substrate and the Hi-K to preserve interface quality and channel mobility.
5. A **metal gate electrode** (TiN, TaN, TaC) replaces doped polysilicon to avoid polysilicon-depletion and Fermi-level pinning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question asks "why Hi-K is needed" — always write both the **leakage suppression** (power) and **EOT scaling** (performance) arguments together. Either alone earns only partial marks.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{ox}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{ox}}}{t_{\text{ox}}}$ | Gate-oxide capacitance per unit area | Charge stored per V at gate | F/m² |
| 2 | $\text{EOT} = t_{\text{hi-K}} \cdot \dfrac{k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{k_{\text{hi-K}}}$ | Equivalent Oxide Thickness | SiO₂ thickness that would give the same Cₒₓ | nm |
| 3 | $t_{\text{physical,hi-K}} = \text{EOT} \cdot \dfrac{k_{\text{hi-K}}}{k_{\text{SiO}_2}}$ | Required physical thickness | Actual deposited thickness | nm |
| 4 | $J_{\text{tunnel}} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2\,t_{\text{ox}}}{\hbar}\sqrt{2\,m^*\,\Phi_B}\right)$ | Direct tunneling current density | WKB approximation | A/m² |
| 5 | $I_{\text{leak}} = J_{\text{tunnel}} \cdot W \cdot L$ | Leakage current per transistor | Static dissipation source | A |
| 6 | $P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}} \cdot N$ | Static power for N transistors | Off-state power | W |
| 7 | $V_{\text{th}} = \phi_{MS} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_s\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{\text{ox}}}$ | Threshold voltage (classical) | Cₒₓ controls V_th | V |
| 8 | $I_{\text{dsat}} = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{\text{ox}}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{\text{th}})^2$ | Saturation drain current | Drive current scales with Cₒₓ | A |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{I_{\text{dsat}}}$ | Intrinsic gate delay | Performance metric | s |
| 10 | $k = \varepsilon_r$ | Relative permittivity | Material property | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** From formula (4), the tunneling leakage depends on **tₒₓ INSIDE the exponential**. Going from 1.2 nm to 1.5 nm can reduce J₉ by **~10×**. Hi-K exploits this by keeping physical thickness large while keeping EOT small.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **45 nm and below CMOS nodes** (Intel, TSMC, Samsung, GlobalFoundries) all adopted Hf-based Hi-K + metal gate around 2007–2008 (Intel's "65 nm logic", 45 nm "Penryn", 32 nm "Westmere").
- Used in **mobile SoCs, microcontrollers, RF-CMOS** where standby leakage is critical.
- Critical for **IoT and edge-AI devices** running on coin-cell / harvested energy.
- Used alongside **strained silicon, FinFET, SOI, multi-Vt** in modern low-power design flows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) Formula

**Starting principle:** Two capacitors in series (Hi-K layer + interfacial SiO₂ layer) must equal the same areal capacitance as a single SiO₂ layer.

**Step 1:** Write the capacitance per unit area of a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, k}{t} $$

**Step 2:** The target SiO₂ capacitance that the technology node requires:

$$ C_{\text{target}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{\text{EOT}} $$

**Step 3:** The actual fabricated stack is Hi-K (thickness t_hi-K, k_hi-K) on top of an interfacial SiO₂ (thickness t_IF, k_SiO₂). Series capacitance:

$$ \frac{1}{C_{\text{stack}}} = \frac{1}{C_{\text{hi-K}}} + \frac{1}{C_{\text{IF}}} = \frac{t_{\text{hi-K}}}{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{hi-K}}} + \frac{t_{\text{IF}}}{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{SiO}_2}} $$

**Step 4:** By definition, **EOT = (ε_SiO₂ / C_stack)**:

$$ \text{EOT} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{C_{\text{stack}}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{hi-K}}}{t_{\text{hi-K}}} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{t_{\text{IF}}}\right)\Big/\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{hi-K}}}{t_{\text{hi-K}}} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{t_{\text{IF}}}\right)} $$
