**Step 5:** Algebraically simplify (cancel ε₀):

$$ \text{EOT} = t_{\text{IF}} + t_{\text{hi-K}} \cdot \frac{k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{k_{\text{hi-K}}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the working formula used in process integration. For example, an EOT of 0.9 nm with 0.5 nm interfacial oxide and HfO₂ (k=22) requires:
> t_hi-K = (0.9 − 0.5) × 22/3.9 ≈ **2.26 nm**, roughly **5× the EOT**, easily suppressing tunneling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tunneling Current Scaling (WKB Approximation)

**Step 1:** Direct tunneling occurs when the oxide thickness becomes comparable to the electron de-Broglie wavelength. The transmission probability through a rectangular barrier of height Φ_B and width tₒₓ is given by the WKB (Wentzel–Kramers–Brillouin) approximation:

$$ T \approx \exp\!\left(-2 \, \kappa \, t_{\text{ox}}\right) $$

**Step 2:** The decay constant κ for a particle of effective mass m* in a barrier of height Φ_B is:

$$ \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \, m^* \, \Phi_B}}{\hbar} $$

**Step 3:** Substituting κ back:

$$ T = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\,t_{\text{ox}}}{\hbar}\sqrt{2\,m^*\,\Phi_B}\right) $$

**Step 4:** Tunneling current density is proportional to the supply of electrons (n) times the transmission:

$$ J_{\text{tunnel}} = q \cdot n \cdot v_{\text{th}} \cdot T \;\;\propto\;\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\,t_{\text{ox}}}{\hbar}\sqrt{2\,m^*\,\Phi_B}\right) $$

**Step 5:** Numerical illustration — compare SiO₂ and HfO₂ for the same EOT:

- SiO₂: tₒₓ = 0.9 nm, m* ≈ 0.5 m₀, Φ_B ≈ 3.15 eV ⇒ exponent ≈ −13.1
- HfO₂ stack: t_phys = 5 nm, but effective barrier height Φ_B(HfO₂) ≈ 1.5 eV, m* ≈ 0.18 m₀ ⇒ exponent magnitude ~10× larger ⇒ **J_tunnel reduced by ~10⁴–10⁶**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners love asking "explain the role of EOT and physical thickness in leakage". The answer must explicitly cite the WKB formula and show why a thicker physical layer with a higher k does NOT just preserve Cₒₓ but also attacks the exponent in J_tunnel.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SiO₂ vs Hi-K

| Parameter | SiO₂ (Legacy) | HfO₂ (Modern Hi-K) | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative permittivity k | 3.9 | 22 | 5.6× capacitance per unit thickness |
| Bandgap E_g (eV) | ~9.0 | ~5.7 | Lower barrier ⇒ must check leakage |
| Conduction-band offset to Si (eV) | 3.15 | ~1.5 | Smaller — partial leak recovery |
| Physical thickness at 0.9 nm EOT | 0.9 nm | ~5 nm | 5.6× thicker ⇒ tunneling suppressed |
| Breakdown field (MV/cm) | >15 | ~5–8 | Adequate for low-V_DD |
| Thermal stability | Excellent | OK with capping (e.g., La₂O₃) | Process integration needed |
| Compatibility with poly-Si | Yes | No (Fermi pinning) ⇒ Metal gate mandatory | Process complexity |
| Interface trap density D_it | ~10¹⁰ cm⁻²eV⁻¹ | ~10¹¹ cm⁻²eV⁻¹ (HfO₂) | Mobility degradation |
| Leakage J at 1 V (A/cm²) | ~10² (too high) | ~10⁻³ (acceptable) | ~10⁵× improvement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MOSFET Cross-Section — SiO₂ vs Hi-K Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OLD["LEGACY SiO2 GATE STACK"]
        direction TB
        A1["Metal / Poly-Si Gate Electrode"] --> A2["SiO2 Dielectric 1.2 nm"]
        A2 --> A3["Interfacial Layer 0.0 nm"]
        A3 --> A4["p-type Si Channel"]
    end
    subgraph NEW["MODERN Hi-K + METAL GATE STACK"]
        direction TB
        B1["Metal Gate TiN / TaN"] --> B2["HfO2 Hi-K Dielectric 2.5 nm"]
        B2 --> B3["Interfacial SiO2 0.5 nm"]
        B3 --> B4["p-type Si Channel"]
    end
    style OLD fill:#fdd
    style NEW fill:#dfd
    style A2 fill:#ff9
    style B2 fill:#9f9
```

### 4.2 Energy Band Diagram — Tunneling Suppression Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GATE["GATE Si poly"]
        G1["Ec Gate"]
        G2["Ef Gate  Fermi Level"]
    end
    subgraph OX1["SiO2 1.2 nm THIN"]
        O1["Barrier Height 3.15 eV"]
        O2["Direct Tunneling YES"]
    end
    subgraph OX2["HfO2 5.0 nm THICK"]
        O3["Barrier Height 1.5 eV"]
        O4["Direct Tunneling NO"]
    end
    subgraph CHAN["Si CHANNEL"]
        C1["Ec Channel"]
        C2["Ef Channel"]
    end
    G2 -. thin barrier .-> O1
    G2 -. thick barrier .-> O3
    style OX1 fill:#fbb
    style OX2 fill:#bfb
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hi-K Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start  Technology node target EOT 0.9 nm"] --> S2["Choose Hi-K material  HfO2 k=22"]
    S2 --> S3["Grow ultra thin SiO2 interfacial layer  0.5 nm"]
    S3 --> S4["Deposit HfO2 by ALD  compute t_phys from EOT formula"]
    S4 --> S5["Deposit metal gate  TiN or TaN"]
    S5 --> S6["Pattern gate using RIE"]
    S6 --> S7["Validate C_ox and J_tunnel measurements"]
    S7 --> S8{"Leakage within spec?"}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9["Accept Hi-K stack  process complete"]
    S8 -- No --> S10["Tune annealing or capping layer La2O3"]
    S10 --> S4
    style S1 fill:#bdf
    style S9 fill:#9f9
    style S10 fill:#fd9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** The two side-by-side subgraphs in 4.1 visually prove the central insight — the modern stack is **physically taller** (better) but **electrically equivalent** (same EOT). This single comparison alone is a 3-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** Define *Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)*. Why is it a preferred metric over physical oxide thickness in advanced CMOS nodes?

**Model Answer:**
EOT is the thickness of SiO₂ that would produce the same gate-oxide capacitance per unit area as the actual dielectric stack used:

$$ \text{EOT} = t_{\text{IF}} + t_{\text{hi-K}} \cdot \frac{k_{\text{SiO}_2}}{k_{\text{hi-K}}} $$

It is preferred because two stacks with the *same* EOT deliver the *same* gate control over the channel, regardless of the physical thickness or material. Hence it allows fair comparison across generations and cleanly captures the **electrical** performance independent of **physical** geometry. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** List any two advantages and one disadvantage of using Hi-K dielectrics over SiO₂ in MOSFETs.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Advantage — Leakage reduction:** The physical thickness can be 4–5× larger at the same EOT, exponentially reducing direct-tunneling current. **[1 mark]**
2. **Advantage — Scalability:** EOT can be pushed below 0.8 nm (e.g., Intel's 45 nm used ~1.0 nm EOT HfO₂) without the leakage catastrophe of sub-1 nm SiO₂. **[1 mark]**
3. **Disadvantage — Interface trap / mobility degradation:** Hi-K materials have higher D_it and fixed charges than thermal SiO₂, reducing carrier mobility and risking V_th instability; a metal gate is also required to avoid Fermi-level pinning. **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) of a dual-layer gate stack consisting of a high-K dielectric on top of a thin interfacial SiO₂ layer. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 32 nm CMOS process uses HfO₂ (k = 22) on a 0.6 nm SiO₂ interfacial layer. The target EOT is 1.0 nm. Calculate (i) the required physical HfO₂ thickness, and (ii) the percentage increase in physical thickness compared to a pure SiO₂ gate with the same EOT. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Derivation:* See Section 3.1 above for the full 5-step derivation. The student must show:
- C_target = ε₀·k_SiO₂ / EOT **[1 mark]**
- 1/C_stack = 1/C_hiK + 1/C_IF **[2 marks]**
- Series combination algebra leading to EOT = t_IF + t_hiK · (k_SiO₂ / k_hiK) **[3 marks]**
- Final boxed expression with units and physical meaning **[1 mark]**

**(b)** *Numerical solution:*

(i) Using the formula:

$$ t_{\text{HfO}_2} = \frac{(\text{EOT} - t_{\text{IF}}) \cdot k_{\text{hi-K}}}{k_{\text{SiO}_2}} $$

$$ t_{\text{HfO}_2} = \frac{(1.0 - 0.6) \times 22}{3.9} = \frac{0.4 \times 22}{3.9} = \frac{8.8}{3.9} \approx 2.26 \;\text{nm} $$

**t_HfO₂ ≈ 2.26 nm** **[3 marks]**

(ii) Pure SiO₂ at EOT = 1.0 nm ⇒ tₒₓ = 1.0 nm. The Hi-K physical thickness (HfO₂ + interfacial) = 2.26 + 0.6 = **2.86 nm**.

$$ \text{Percentage increase} = \frac{2.86 - 1.0}{1.0} \times 100\% = 186\% $$

**≈ 186% thicker** physically than the equivalent pure SiO₂ stack. **[4 marks]**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat energy-band diagram, explain how the Hi-K dielectric suppresses direct-tunneling leakage in a MOSFET. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare SiO₂, Si₃N₄, and HfO₂ in terms of relative permittivity, bandgap, conduction-band offset, and leakage performance. Why is HfO₂ preferred despite its lower bandgap? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Band-diagram explanation:*
- Draw a labelled band diagram with gate, oxide, channel regions. **[2 marks]**
- Show that for a thin SiO₂ (~1.2 nm), electrons can directly tunnel through the ~3.15 eV barrier — refer to WKB transmission. **[2 marks]**
- Show that for the same EOT, the Hi-K stack is physically thicker (~3–5 nm) and the transmission probability drops exponentially. Even though Φ_B(HfO₂) is lower, the dominant tₒₓ factor in the exponent reduces J by 4–6 orders of magnitude. **[2 marks]**
- Conclude with a one-line summary statement linking EOT, Φ_B, and exponential leakage suppression. **[1 mark]**

**(b)** *Comparison table* (must be drawn in the answer script): **[4 marks]**

| Material | k | E_g (eV) | ΔE_C to Si (eV) | Leakage trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SiO₂ | 3.9 | 9.0 | 3.15 | Poor below 1.5 nm |
| Si₃N₄ | 7.5 | 5.3 | 2.4 | Moderate |
| HfO₂ | 22 | 5.7 | 1.5 | Excellent at 0.8–1.2 nm EOT |

**Why HfO₂ is preferred despite lower bandgap:** **[3 marks]**
- The exponential factor tₒₓ in the tunneling formula dominates over the weaker Φ_B dependence.
- A 5× larger physical thickness of HfO₂ gives ~10⁴–10⁶ reduction in J_tunnel, easily overcoming the smaller barrier height.
- Additional benefits: high thermal stability, compatibility with ALD deposition, mature industrial supply chain.
- Hence the *net* leakage of HfO₂ at sub-1 nm EOT is **vastly lower** than SiO₂ at the same EOT.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the interfacial layer** in the EOT formula — the most common mistake. EOT = t_IF + t_hiK·(k_SiO₂/k_hiK), NOT just t_hiK·(k_SiO₂/k_hiK). **[−2 marks]**
> 2. **Confusing EOT and physical thickness** in numerical problems — read the question carefully: "physical thickness" vs "equivalent oxide thickness". **[−1 to −2 marks]**
> 3. **Not drawing a band diagram** when asked — for 7-mark questions on tunneling suppression, the band diagram is mandatory. **[−3 marks]**
> 4. **Citing the WKB formula but failing to explain** why exponential reduction in J_tunnel justifies Hi-K — examiners want the *qualitative* argument tied to the formula. **[−1 mark]**
> 5. **Ignoring metal-gate necessity** — Hi-K without a metal gate causes Fermi-level pinning; full credit requires acknowledging this dependency. **[−1 mark]**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hi-K dielectric** = insulator with k ≫ 3.9 (e.g., HfO₂ with k ≈ 22) used to replace SiO₂.
- ✅ **Purpose:** Suppress gate direct-tunneling leakage at sub-1 nm EOT nodes.
- ✅ **Key formula — EOT:** `EOT = t_IF + t_hiK · (k_SiO₂ / k_hiK)`.
- ✅ **Tunneling leakage (WKB):** `J ∝ exp(−2·tₒₓ/ℏ · √(2·m*·Φ_B))` — tₒₓ dominates inside the exponent.
- ✅ **Physical thickness grows** ~k_hiK/k_SiO₂ times the EOT, killing the tunneling exponent.
- ✅ **Mandatory co-technologies:** metal gate (avoid poly-depletion and Fermi pinning), thin SiO₂ interfacial layer (preserve mobility).
- ✅ **Industrial examples:** Intel 45 nm (Penryn, 2007), 32 nm (Westmere), 22 nm (Ivy Bridge) and beyond all use Hf-based Hi-K + metal gate.
- ✅ **Companion emerging technologies** (often asked together in the same module): strained silicon, FinFET / tri-gate, SOI, multi-Vt design, dynamic V_DD scaling, clock gating, power gating.
- ✅ **Quick comparison values to memorize:** k_SiO₂ = 3.9, k_HfO₂ ≈ 22, Φ_B(SiO₂) = 3.15 eV, Φ_B(HfO₂) ≈ 1.5 eV, m*(HfO₂) ≈ 0.18 m₀.
- ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** *same EOT, bigger physical thickness, exponentially less leakage, same drive current* — write this line in every related answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lightly Doped Drain-Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Lightly Doped Drain-Source ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Lightly Doped Drain-Source (LDD)** is an advanced MOS transistor engineering technique in which a **lightly doped n⁻ (or p⁻) region** is intentionally introduced between the channel and the heavily doped n⁺ (or p⁺) source/drain contact regions. This structural modification is specifically engineered to suppress **Hot Carrier Effects (HCE)**, reduce the peak lateral electric field near the drain-channel junction, and mitigate **short-channel effects (SCE)** in deep submicron CMOS technologies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LDD is a fundamental device-level low-power technique classified under *Leakage Power Reduction & Hot-Carrier Reliability* in Module 1 of Low Power VLSI Design. It is a **structural (device-geometry) approach** rather than a circuit-level or system-level power reduction technique. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speed Bumper" on a Highway Imagine a high-speed highway (the **drain current path**) where fast-moving vehicles (electrons) approach a sharp, steep cliff at the end (the **drain edge in a conventional MOSFET**). The cliff represents the abrupt potential drop and the **high electric field peak** that accelerates electrons to damaging energies (hot carriers). If we instead place a **gradual, gentle slope** before the cliff (the **n⁻ lightly doped region**), vehicles slow down progressively. The peak field is "spread out" over a longer distance, reducing the maximum acceleration any single electron experiences. This gentle slope is the **LDD region** — it doesn't stop traffic (current still flows), but it controls the energy and prevents destructive collisions (impact ionization and hot-carrier injection into the gate oxide). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard semiconductor constants and metrics are essential for LDD analysis: - **Silicon bandgap:** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$ (at 300 K) - **Hot carrier threshold energy:** $E_{hot} \approx 1.5 E_g \approx 1.68 \text{ eV}$ - **Substrate doping (p-type bulk):** $N_A \approx 10^{15} \text{ to } 10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ - **LDD n⁻ doping concentration:** $N_{LDD} \approx 10^{18} \text{ to } 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ - **LDD length (typical):** $L_{n^-} \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.2 \text{ μm}$ - **n⁺ S/D doping concentration:** $N_{D} \approx 10^{20} \text{ to } 10^{21} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the n⁻ region reduces the field:** By replacing the abrupt n⁺/p junction with a graded n⁻/p junction, the depletion region widens, and the **maximum lateral electric field $E_{max}$** near the drain is reduced. Lower $E_{max}$ means fewer electrons acquire energy $\geq 1.5 E_g$, dramatically reducing impact ionization. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Motivation: Why Conventional MOSFETs Fail at Short Channel Lengths In conventional single-diffused drain (SDD) MOSFETs, as channel length $L$ shrinks into the deep submicron regime (≤ 0.25 μm), three catastrophic phenomena emerge: 1. **Channel-Length Modulation:** The drain depletion region encroaches a significant fraction of the channel, causing $V_{DS,eff}$ to deviate from the applied $V_{DS}$. 2. **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL):** High drain bias couples into the channel, lowering the source-channel barrier and increasing $I_{off}$ (subthreshold leakage). 3. **Hot Carrier Injection (HCI):** Electrons accelerated by the high lateral field near the drain gain kinetic energy ≥ 1.5 $E_g$, become "hot," and are injected into the gate oxide, causing permanent $V_T$ shift and device degradation. The LDD structure addresses (1)–(3) by **grading the drain doping profile**. ### 2.2 The LDD Structure — Operational Breakdown The LDD MOSFET features a **two-step drain (and source) doping profile**: | Region | Doping | Purpose | |--------|--------|---------| | **n⁺ S/D** | $10^{20} \text{–}10^{21} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ | Low parasitic resistance contact | | **n⁻ LDD spacer region** | $10^{18} \text{–}10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ | Reduces peak electric field | | **p-type channel/substrate** | $10^{15} \text{–}10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ | MOSFET body | **Key design parameters:** - **LDD length** ($L_{n^-}$): Controlled by the sidewall spacer width (typically SiO₂ or Si₃N₄). - **LDD doping dose:** Critical — too low → high series resistance $R_{par}$, too high → field reduction insufficient. ### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' of LDD Operation **HOW it works (step-by-step logic):** 1. When $V_{DS}$ is applied, a depletion region forms at the drain-channel junction. 2. In a conventional abrupt n⁺/p junction, this depletion region is **narrow**, so the voltage drop is concentrated over a small distance → **high peak field**. 3. In the LDD structure, the **n⁻ region has lower doping** than n⁺, so the depletion region **extends further into the n⁻ side** (since depletion width $\propto 1/\sqrt{N}$). 4. The same voltage $V_{DS}$ is now dropped over a **longer distance**, lowering $E_{max}$. 5. With reduced $E_{max}$, electrons experience **less impact ionization** (impact ionization rate $A_i$ is a strong super-linear function of $E$). 6. **Hot carrier injection into gate oxide is suppressed** → improved long-term device reliability. **WHY the trade-off exists:** The n⁻ region, while suppressing hot carriers, also adds a **series resistance** $R_{LDD}$ between the channel and the n⁺ contact, reducing the effective $V_{GS}$ and degrading the drive current $I_{on}$. This is the fundamental **LDD reliability–performance trade-off** that KTU examiners frequently test. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions | |---|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $E_{max}^{SDD} \approx \dfrac{V_{DS}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A \cdot V_{DS}}}$ | Peak field in conventional abrupt drain | V/cm | | 2 | $E_{max}^{LDD} = \dfrac{V_{DS}}{L_{n^-} + L_{dep}}$ | Peak field in LDD structure (approximate) | V/cm | | 3 | $W_{dep} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}\,(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{qN_{eff}}}$ | Depletion width (graded junction) | cm | | 4 | $A_i(E) = A \cdot e^{-B/E}$ | Impact ionization rate (Chynoweth model) | cm⁻¹ | | 5 | $I_{sub} = I_{sub,0} \cdot e^{-B/E_{max}}$ | Substrate (impact ionization) current | A | | 6 | $R_{LDD} = \dfrac{\rho_{n^-} \cdot L_{n^-}}{W \cdot t_{n^-}}$ | LDD parasitic resistance per side | Ω | | 7 | $I_{on}^{LDD} = \dfrac{I_{on}^{SDD}}{1 + R_{LDD}\cdot \mu C_{ox} W/L}$ | Drive current degradation factor | A | | 8 | $V_T$ shift: $\Delta V_T \propto A_i \cdot t^n$ | Hot carrier aging law | V | > [!NOTE] > In formulas above, use \vert for absolute value to avoid markdown conflicts: e.g., $\vert E \vert$ instead of $|E|$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Foundry Standard:** LDD (often called "MDD" — moderately doped drain, or "HDD" — heavily doped drain in modern nodes) has been the **industry standard for all CMOS nodes from 0.25 μm down to 65 nm**. - **Modern descendants:** *Raised Source/Drain (RSD)*, *SiGe S/D stressors*, and *FinFETs* are the modern evolutionary replacements of conventional LDD, retaining the field-reduction principle. - **Application impact:** LDD enables reliable operation at supply voltages $V_{DD} \geq 2.5$ V in 0.25 μm and below — a critical enabler for digital VLSI scaling. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why LDD Reduces Peak Electric Field We derive the peak lateral field for a **one-sided abrupt junction** (conventional) and then show how the LDD structure modifies it. **Step 1: Poisson's equation in the depletion region** In the depletion region, charge density is the ionized acceptor concentration $-qN_A$ (one-sided assumption, $N_A \ll N_D$). $$ \frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_{si}} = \frac{-qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}} $$ **Step 2: Integrating once to obtain electric field** $$ E(x) = -\frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}\,(x - x_n) \quad \text{for } x_n \leq x \leq x_n + W_{dep} $$ **Step 3: Integrating again to obtain potential (boundary: $E(W_{dep}) = 0$)** $$ \phi(x) = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}\left[ W_{dep}\cdot x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right] + C_1 $$ Applying $\phi(W_{dep}) = V_{bi} + V_{DS}$ and $\phi(0) = 0$: $$ V_{bi} + V_{DS} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}} \cdot \frac{W_{dep}^{2}}{2} $$ **Step 4: Solving for depletion width $W_{dep}$ (one-sided abrupt)** $$ W_{dep} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}\,(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{qN_A}} $$ **Step 5: Peak electric field (occurs at the metallurgical junction $x = 0$)** $$ E_{max}^{SDD} = \frac{qN_A W_{dep}}{\varepsilon_{si}} = \frac{2(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{W_{dep}} $$ **Step 6: LDD modification — graded junction** In the LDD case, the depletion width extends predominantly into the lightly doped n⁻ region. The effective doping on the n-side is $N_{LDD}$ rather than $N_D$: $$ W_{dep}^{LDD} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}\,(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{q}\left( \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_{LDD}} \right)} $$ **Step 7: Peak field in LDD structure** $$ E_{max}^{LDD} = \frac{2(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{W_{dep}^{LDD}} $$ **Step 8: Quantitative comparison** Define the field-reduction ratio: $$ R_E = \frac{E_{max}^{SDD}}{E_{max}^{LDD}} = \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_{LDD}}}{\dfrac{1}{N_A}}} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{N_A}{N_{LDD}}} $$ **Numerical example (typical 0.25 μm CMOS):** - $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $N_{LDD} = 5 \times 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ $$ R_E = \sqrt{1 + \frac{5 \times 10^{16}}{5 \times 10^{18}}} = \sqrt{1 + 0.01} = \sqrt{1.01} \approx 1.005 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This single-sided result looks small because most depletion is on the p-side. In **real LDD**, the n⁻ region is *also* depleted substantially — we must use the symmetric two-sided expression. With $N_A \sim N_{LDD}$, the depletion extends equally on both sides, and $W_{dep}$ increases by $\sqrt{2}$ → $E_{max}$ drops by $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$, i.e., a **~30% reduction** in peak field. **Step 9: Refined symmetric LDD analysis** If we take the symmetric case ($N_A \approx N_{LDD}$), the depletion widths on each side are equal, and the total $W_{dep}$ doubles compared to a one-sided abrupt junction. The peak field then becomes: $$ E_{max}^{LDD,sym} = \frac{V_{bi} + V_{DS}}{W_{dep}^{total}/2} = \frac{2(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{q}\cdot \dfrac{2}{N_{LDD}}}} $$ $$ E_{max}^{LDD,sym} = \sqrt{\frac{2qN_{LDD}(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{\varepsilon_{si}}} $$ Compared to the abrupt case with $N_D \gg N_A$: $$ \frac{E_{max}^{LDD,sym}}{E_{max}^{SDD}} = \sqrt{\frac{N_{LDD}}{N_D}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{18}}{10^{21}}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.07 $$ This gives an **order-of-magnitude reduction in peak field**, which is the basis of LDD's effectiveness. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: LDD vs. Conventional SDD | Parameter | Conventional SDD | LDD Structure | KTU Exam Relevance | |-----------|------------------|---------------|--------------------| | Peak lateral field $E_{max}$ | Very high (~10⁶ V/cm) | Reduced ~10× | ★★★★★ | | Hot carrier injection | Severe | Suppressed by 1–2 orders | ★★★★★ | | Drive current $I_{on}$ | High | Lower (R_par penalty) | ★★★★ | | Subthreshold leakage | Higher (DIBL) | Reduced (graded junction) | ★★★★ | | Fabrication complexity | Simple | Extra spacer + implant step | ★★★ | | Series resistance $R_S$ | Low | Higher (~200–500 Ω/□) | ★★★★ | | Suitable for $V_{DD}$ | ≥ 3.3 V (long channel) | ≤ 2.5 V (short channel) | ★★★ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Schematic of LDD MOSFET ```mermaid graph TB subgraph S["SOURCE SIDE (nMOS)"] A1["n+ Source Contact
N_D = 1e20 cm-3"] A2["n- LDD Region
N_LDD = 1e18 cm-3"] A3["p-type Channel
N_A = 1e16 cm-3"] end subgraph M["MIDDLE: GATE"] B1["Poly-Si Gate"] B2["Gate Oxide SiO2
t_ox = 2 to 5 nm"] B3["Sidewall Spacer
SiO2 or Si3N4"] end subgraph D["DRAIN SIDE (nMOS)"] C1["n- LDD Region
Defines the field grading zone"] C2["n+ Drain Contact
Hot carrier sink"] end B3 --- A2 B3 --- C1 A1 --- A2 A2 --- A3 A3 --- C1 C1 --- C2 B2 --- A3 B1 --- B2 B2 --- C1 ``` ### 4.2 LDD Fabrication Process Flow ```mermaid graph LR stepA["Step 1: Form gate stack
Poly on thin oxide"] --> stepB["Step 2: LDD n- implant
N_LDD dose 1e13 cm-2"] stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Deposit spacer dielectric
SiO2 or Si3N4 CVD"] stepC --> stepD["Step 4: Anisotropic etch
Forms sidewall spacer"] stepD --> stepE["Step 5: n+ S/D implant
N_D dose 5e15 cm-2"] stepE --> stepF["Step 6: Rapid thermal anneal
Activates dopants"] stepF --> stepG["Step 7: Silicidation
Low-resistance contacts"] ``` ### 4.3 Electric Field Profile Comparison (Block-Level Functional) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph P1["CONVENTIONAL MOSFET"] F1["Channel: E ~ 1e4 V/cm"] F2["Drain Edge: E_peak ~ 1e6 V/cm (sharp spike)"] F3["Hot carriers generated = HIGH"] end subgraph P2["LDD MOSFET"] G1["Channel: E ~ 1e4 V/cm"] G2["n- region: E rises GRADUALLY"] G3["Drain Edge: E_peak ~ 1e5 V/cm (10x lower)"] G4["Hot carriers generated = LOW"] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In the LDD structure, the sidewall spacer is the *physical mask* that defines where the n⁻ implant is shadowed. The spacer width (≈ 80–200 nm) is **directly equal to the LDD length $L_{n^-}$**, making it a critical layout parameter in KTU device physics problems. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *Define the term "Lightly Doped Drain" (LDD) structure in a MOSFET. Why is it used in short-channel CMOS devices?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Lightly Doped Drain (LDD)** is a modified MOSFET drain (and source) structure in which a **lightly doped n⁻ region** (doping $10^{18}$–$10^{19}$ cm⁻³) is introduced between the channel and the heavily doped n⁺ contact region. It is fabricated by using a sidewall spacer as a mask during the n⁻ implant. **Why it is used (1.5 marks):** In short-channel MOSFETs, the high lateral electric field at the drain edge causes **hot-carrier injection** (HCI) into the gate oxide, leading to permanent $V_T$ shift and device degradation. The LDD structure **grades the drain doping**, widening the depletion region and **reducing the peak electric field** by 5–10×, thereby suppressing hot-carrier generation and improving long-term device reliability. It also mitigates DIBL and punchthrough. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the main drawback of the LDD structure and how is it addressed?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The main drawback of the LDD structure is the **increased parasitic source/drain series resistance** $R_{LDD}$ caused by the lightly doped n⁻ region, which **degrades the drive current** $I_{on}$ and switching speed. **Addressing the drawback (1.5 marks):** - Use **silicidation** (e.g., TiSi₂, CoSi₂, NiSi) over the n⁺ and n⁻ regions to lower the sheet resistance. - Optimize the spacer width and n⁻ dose to balance hot-carrier reliability vs. current drive. - In advanced nodes, use **raised source/drain** or **FinFET** architectures that retain the field-grading benefit without the resistance penalty. --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions #### **Question A (14 Marks) — LDD Structure & Hot Carrier Physics** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, S6 B.Tech ECE]** **(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and operation of an **LDD MOSFET**. Compare its electric field profile with that of a conventional single-diffused drain (SDD) MOSFET. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive an expression for the peak lateral electric field in: (i) a conventional abrupt-junction MOSFET (ii) an LDD MOSFET Show that the LDD structure reduces $E_{max}$ by the factor $\sqrt{N_{LDD}/N_D}$ when the n⁻ and p-side dopings are comparable. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and operation (7 marks):** 1. **Structure (3 marks):** Draw the cross-section showing — p-substrate, gate oxide, poly-Si gate, two **sidewall spacers** (SiO₂/Si₃N₄) on either side of the gate, **n⁻ LDD regions** beneath the spacers, and **n⁺ source/drain** regions outside the spacers. Use standard CMOS symbols. 2. **Operation (2 marks):** When $V_{DS}$ is applied, the depletion region at the drain extends into the lightly doped n⁻ region (which is of comparable doping to the p-substrate). This **grades the potential drop** over a longer distance, reducing the peak electric field $E_{max}$ at the drain edge. 3. **Field profile comparison (2 marks):** Conventional MOSFET: sharp single peak at the drain edge (high $E_{max}$). LDD: two smaller peaks (one at the channel/n⁻ interface and one at the n⁻/n⁺ interface), with the **maximum peak significantly lower** (~10× reduction). Plot the field vs. position from channel to drain. **(b) Derivations (7 marks):** **(i) Conventional abrupt junction (3.5 marks):** For a one-sided abrupt n⁺/p junction, Poisson's equation gives: $$ \frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}} \quad (0 \leq x \leq W_{dep}) $$ Integrating with $E(W_{dep}) = 0$ and $\phi(0) = V_{bi} + V_{DS}$: $$ V_{bi} + V_{DS} = \frac{qN_A W_{dep}^2}{2\varepsilon_{si}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{dep} = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{qN_A}} $$ The peak field at $x = 0$ is: $$ E_{max}^{SDD} = \frac{qN_A W_{dep}}{\varepsilon_{si}} = \frac{2(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{W_{dep}} = \sqrt{\frac{2qN_A(V_{bi}+V_{DS})}{\varepsilon_{si}}} $$ **(ii) LDD structure (3.5 marks):** In an LDD, the depletion region extends into both the p-substrate and the n⁻ region. Treating the junction as symmetric with $N_A \approx N_{LDD}$: $$ W_{dep}^{LDD} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(V_{bi} + V_{DS})}{qN_{LDD}}} $$ (Note: factor 2 because depletion extends equally on both sides.) The peak field is: $$ E_{max}^{LDD} = \frac{V_{bi} + V_{DS}}{W_{dep}^{LDD}/2} = \sqrt{\frac{2qN_{LDD}(V_{bi}+V_{DS})}{\varepsilon_{si}}} $$ **Comparison:** $$ \frac{E_{max}^{LDD}}{E_{max}^{SDD}} = \sqrt{\frac{N_{LDD}}{N_D}} $$ With $N_{LDD} = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³ and $N_D = 10^{20}$ cm⁻³: $$ \frac{E_{max}^{LDD}}{E_{max}^{SDD}} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{18}}{10^{20}}} = 0.1 $$ i.e., the LDD reduces the peak field by **a factor of 10**. ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — LDD Design Trade-offs & Reliability** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, S6 B.Tech ECE]** **(a)** Explain the **hot-carrier effect** in short-channel MOSFETs. Discuss how the LDD structure mitigates this effect. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the disadvantages of the LDD structure? Discuss the **LDD design trade-off** between reliability and drive current. How is this trade-off optimized in modern CMOS? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Hot-carrier effect and LDD mitigation (7 marks):** **Hot-carrier effect (4 marks):** In short-channel MOSFETs, the lateral electric field near the drain edge becomes very high (≥ 10⁵ V/cm). Electrons (or holes) accelerated by this field gain kinetic energy ≥ 1.5 $E_g$ (≈ 1.68 eV in Si) and become "hot carriers." These hot carriers can: 1. Get injected into the gate oxide → **Hot-Carrier Injection (HCI)**, causing trapped charge and permanent $V_T$ shift. 2. Cause **impact ionization** near the drain → generate electron-hole pairs → large substrate current $I_{sub}$ and potential latch-up. 3. Degrade device lifetime according to the empirical law: $\tau \propto (I_{sub}/I_D)^{-n}$ where $n \approx 3$. **LDD mitigation (3 marks):** The LDD structure reduces $E_{max}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{N_{LDD}/N_D}$, which dramatically lowers the impact ionization rate (since $A_i \propto e^{-B/E}$, an exponential function of $1/E$). This: - Reduces substrate current by 1–2 orders of magnitude - Suppresses hot-carrier injection into oxide - Extends device lifetime by 10–100× **(b) LDD trade-offs and modern optimization (7 marks):** **Disadvantages (3 marks):** 1. **Increased series resistance:** $R_{LDD} \approx \rho_{n^-} L_{n^-} / (W \cdot t_{n^-})$ can be 200–500 Ω/□ per side, reducing $I_{on}$ by 5–15%. 2. **Self-heating:** The narrow n⁻ region can lead to localized heating. 3. **Process complexity:** Extra sidewall spacer deposition, etch, and n⁻ implant step. **Reliability–performance trade-off (2 marks):** - Lighter n⁻ doping and longer $L_{n^-}$ → better HCI reliability but worse $I_{on}$. - Heavier n⁻ doping and shorter $L_{n^-}$ → better $I_{on}$ but worse reliability. - KTU standard practice: optimize at the iso-reliability point where 10-year lifetime is maintained at maximum $I_{on}$. **Modern optimization (2 marks):** - **Silicided LDD:** TiSi₂/CoSi₂/NiSi over n⁻ reduces $R_{LDD}$ by 5–10×. - **Indiffused LDD (or HDD):** Carefully shaped doping profiles to minimize resistance. - **Raised S/D (RSD):** Epitaxial Si/SiGe grown on n⁻ region to lower resistance. - **FinFETs (≤ 22 nm):** Gated channel controls field; LDD is implicitly built into the fin geometry. - **Strained Si / SiGe channels:** Higher mobility compensates for any $I_{on}$ loss. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LDD Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "LDD length" with "sidewall spacer width":** Many students interchange them. Clarify: *LDD length* is the lateral extent of the n⁻ region under the spacer; *spacer width* is the dielectric thickness on the gate sidewall. They are **equal in dimension** but different physical entities. > 2. **Forgetting the $1/\sqrt{N_D}$ dependence:** Students often write $E_{max}$ scales as $1/N_D$ — wrong. It scales as $1/\sqrt{N_D}$ because the depletion width is the dominant term. > 3. **Missing the drive current penalty:** LDD is **not a free lunch**. Any answer that praises LDD without mentioning the $R_{LDD}$ cost loses at least 1–2 marks. > 4. **Drawing without labels:** KTU examiners award partial credit for *labelled* diagrams. Always label: gate, gate oxide, n⁺, n⁻, spacer, p-substrate, depletion region. > 5. **Confusing LDD with DDD (double-diffused drain):** DDD is an older predecessor. LDD uses a *single* light implant with a *spacer-defined* mask, not a double diffusion. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** LDD = lightly doped n⁻ region between channel and n⁺ S/D, fabricated via sidewall spacer–defined n⁻ implant. - **Primary purpose:** Suppress **hot-carrier injection (HCI)** and improve long-term MOSFET reliability in submicron devices. - **Physical mechanism:** Grades the drain doping → widens depletion region → reduces peak lateral electric field $E_{max}$. - **Quantitative result:** $E_{max}^{LDD} / E_{max}^{SDD} = \sqrt{N_{LDD}/N_D}$ → typically a **5–10× field reduction**. - **Key formula (Chynoweth impact ionization):** $A_i(E) = A \cdot \exp(-B/E)$ — exponential sensitivity to $E_{max}$. - **Main drawback:** Increased series resistance $R_{LDD}$ → reduced $I_{on}$ (the **reliability–performance trade-off**). - **Modern solutions:** Silicided LDD, raised S/D, FinFETs, strained-Si channels. - **Design parameters:** LDD dose ($\sim 10^{13}$ cm⁻²), spacer width ($\sim 0.1$–$0.2$ μm), thermal budget. - **KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** *graded junction*, *peak electric field*, *hot-carrier injection*, *impact ionization*, *sidewall spacer*, *drive current degradation*, *long-term reliability*, *subthreshold leakage reduction*. - **What to draw in exams:** Cross-section with n⁺ | n⁻ | channel | n⁻ | n⁺ profile; $E(x)$ vs. position plot showing the suppressed peak.

topics.title: Silicon on Insulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silicon on Insulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Silicon on Insulator (SOI)** is an advanced semiconductor fabrication工艺 (process) in which a thin active silicon film is placed on top of an insulating dielectric layer (typically **Silicon Dioxide — SiO₂**, also called the "buried oxide" or BOX), which itself rests on a bulk silicon handle wafer. In the SOI MOSFET, the channel and the source/drain regions are formed within this top silicon film, completely isolated from the bulk substrate by the buried oxide.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEEVT741 — Low Power VLSI Design**, SOI is treated as a *device-level technology enabler* — a structural modification of the conventional bulk MOSFET that intrinsically reduces junction capacitances, suppresses the body effect, and allows aggressive threshold voltage ($V_{th}$) scaling without the leakage penalty seen in bulk CMOS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** SOI belongs to the family of *alternative device structures for low power*. Along with Multi-Threshold CMOS (MTCMOS) and Variable Threshold CMOS (VTCMOS), SOI is a foundational CMOS modification that the examiner may compare against bulk CMOS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a regular MOSFET as a person standing **directly on wet, conductive ground** (the bulk silicon wafer). Any small disturbance (body bias, leakage, noise from the substrate) propagates up through their feet and affects how they walk (the transistor's switching behavior).

Now imagine placing that same person on a **floating wooden raft (the thin Si film) that itself sits on a slab of thick glass (the BOX layer)**, which is on the ground. The glass:
- **Blocks electrical coupling** → the person's footing is *electrically isolated* from the ground.
- **Reduces the contact area** with the earth → smaller capacitive coupling (lower parasitic capacitance).
- **Allows the raft to be very thin** → the person sits in a "shallow tub" of silicon.

This "raft on glass" is exactly the SOI structure. The thin Si film (the "raft") carries the transistor, and the SiO₂ (the "glass") severs the electrical path to the handle wafer.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Geometric Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard SOI Layer Thickness Metrics (industry-typical):**
> - **Top Si film thickness ($t_{Si}$)** : **5 nm – 100 nm** (modern FD-SOI uses **≤ 25 nm**)
> - **Buried Oxide thickness ($t_{BOX}$)** : **10 nm – 200 nm** (advanced nodes use **≈ 25 nm**)
> - **Handle wafer thickness** : typically **300 mm diameter**, ~ 750 µm thick (mechanical support only)
> - **Dielectric constant of BOX** : $k_{ox} = 3.9$ (SiO₂)

## 1.4 Why SOI Matters for Low Power VLSI

| Concern in Bulk CMOS | How SOI Helps |
|---|---|
| Large **junction capacitance** ($C_j$) at source/drain–substrate p-n junctions | Junction area is *eliminated*; only the thin film–BOX capacitance remains — up to **30% lower** total parasitic capacitance |
| **Body effect** — $V_{th}$ rises with $V_{SB}$ | Source, body, and channel are shorted through the thin film; body effect is **drastically reduced or eliminated** in FD-SOI |
| **Sub-threshold leakage** scaling limit | FD-SOI enables steeper subthreshold slope and lower $I_{off}$ |
| **Latch-up** via parasitic SCR in bulk | Buried oxide provides **complete isolation**; latch-up is structurally impossible |
| Soft errors from substrate injection | Buried oxide **blocks minority carriers** from substrate |
| Heavy doping needed to control short-channel effects | Ultra-thin Si film suppresses SCE naturally |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Variants of SOI

SOI MOSFETs are classified based on whether the silicon film is **fully depleted** or **partially depleted** at the operating gate bias.

### 2.1.1 Partially Depleted SOI (PD-SOI)

- The top silicon film thickness $t_{Si}$ is **larger than the maximum depletion width** that the gate can induce.
- Therefore, a **neutral (non-depleted) body region** exists between the front channel and the back interface.
- Because this body is *electrically floating* (no body contact in most designs), it can charge up to a non-zero potential.

> [!WARNING]
> **Floating Body Effect (FBE)** — the principal drawback of PD-SOI. Holes generated by impact ionization near the drain accumulate in the floating body, raising the body potential $V_{BS}$ and thereby **lowering $V_{th}$** dynamically. This produces the infamous **"kink effect"** in the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve.

### 2.1.2 Fully Depleted SOI (FD-SOI)

- The top silicon film thickness $t_{Si}$ is **smaller than the maximum depletion width**.
- The entire Si film is depleted of mobile carriers at typical operating $V_{GS}$.
- There is **no neutral body region**, so the floating body problem is **largely eliminated**.
- Threshold voltage is tightly controlled by the Si film thickness, the BOX, and the gate stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FD-SOI is the **modern, low-power-relevant SOI variant** and is the focus of the 28 nm and 22 nm ultra-thin-body-and-BOX (UTBB) technologies commercialized by STMicroelectronics and Samsung.

## 2.2 Why FD-SOI is the *Low-Power Workhorse*

The key physical argument is **gate electrostatic control**. In a thin Si film, the depletion region extends from the gate oxide **all the way to the buried oxide**, so the field lines from the gate fully terminate on the channel charges. The "natural length scale" of electrostatic integrity is given by:

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{Si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}} \, t_{ox} \, t_{Si}}
$$

A smaller $\lambda$ (which requires smaller $t_{Si}$ and $t_{ox}$) means **better short-channel-effect (SCE) suppression** and allows $V_{th}$ to scale down without paying a leakage penalty — the central tenet of low-power CMOS design.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically self-contained and standard. Use the LaTeX-rendered form during the exam. Remember to use `\vert` instead of `|` in any table cell containing absolute-value expressions to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use / Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{th,SOI} = V_{FB,SOI} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si} q N_A \, 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox,front}}$ | $V_{FB,SOI}$ flat-band voltage, $\phi_F$ Fermi potential, $N_A$ channel doping, $C_{ox,front}$ front-gate oxide capacitance | First-order threshold voltage for an SOI MOSFET |
| 2 | $C_{ox,front} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox,front}}$ | $t_{ox,front}$ front-gate oxide thickness | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area |
| 3 | $C_{Si} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Si}}{t_{Si}}$ | $t_{Si}$ silicon film thickness | Capacitance of the fully-depleted Si body |
| 4 | $C_{BOX} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{BOX}}$ | $t_{BOX}$ buried oxide thickness | Back-gate coupling capacitance per unit area |
| 5 | $\gamma_{SOI} = \dfrac{C_{Si}}{C_{ox,front}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Si}/t_{Si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox,front}}$ | SOI body factor (analog of bulk body factor $\gamma$) | Quantifies how strongly $V_{th}$ is modulated by the back-gate bias $V_{BS}$ |
| 6 | $V_{th,FD-SOI} = V_{th,\infty} - \gamma_{SOI} \left( 2\phi_F + V_{BS} \right)$ | $V_{th,\infty}$ is the fully-depleted long-channel threshold; $V_{BS}$ is the back-gate (substrate) bias | Shows the **linear** dependence of $V_{th}$ on back-gate bias — the basis of **body-bias-based dynamic $V_{th}$** control |
| 7 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox,front} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda \, V_{DS})$ | $\mu_n$ mobility, $W/L$ aspect ratio, $\lambda$ channel-length modulation | Saturation drain current; in SOI, $\lambda$ is reduced because DIBL is smaller |
| 8 | $S = \left( \ln 10 \right) \dfrac{kT}{q} \left( 1 + \dfrac{C_{Si}}{C_{ox,front}} \right)$ | Subthreshold swing | In fully-depleted ideal case, $C_{Si} \to 0$, so $S$ approaches the **Boltzmann limit 60 mV/decade at 300 K** |
| 9 | $C_{par,SOI} \approx C_{ox,front} + C_{BOX}$ (no junction capacitance contribution) | Total input capacitance per device | The **elimination of $C_j$** is the dominant reason SOI is faster and lower power than bulk |
| 10 | $t_{Si,max}^{FD} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4\varepsilon_{Si} \phi_F}{q N_A}}$ | Maximum $t_{Si}$ for which full depletion is achieved | **Design rule** — pick $t_{Si}$ below this to guarantee FD operation |
| 11 | $P_{dyn,SOI} = \alpha C_{par,SOI} V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power per gate | Because $C_{par,SOI} < C_{par,bulk}$, dynamic power is reduced |
| 12 | $I_{leak,SOI} = I_0 \, 10^{-V_{th}/S}$ | Subthreshold leakage current | Lower $S$ (FD-SOI) → orders-of-magnitude lower $I_{leak}$ for the same $V_{th}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of SOI in Engineering

- **Mobile Application Processors** — Samsung's 28 nm FD-SOI Exynos chips and ST's 28 nm STM32 automotive MCUs use FD-SOI for both **performance boost** (under forward body bias) and **retention power reduction** (under reverse body bias).
- **IoT and Wearable SoCs** — ultra-low standby current requirements of 100s of nA are achieved using FD-SOI's steep subthreshold slope.
- **RF and mm-Wave** — SOI's high resistivity handle wafer (HR-Si substrate on BOX) suppresses substrate losses in 5G/6G front-end modules (Qualcomm, Skyworks use SOI for RF switches and power amplifiers).
- **Aerospace / Radiation-Hardened Electronics** — total isolation from the substrate makes SOI naturally resistant to single-event upsets (SEU).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Threshold Voltage of a Fully-Depleted SOI MOSFET

### 3.1.1 Starting Setup

Consider an n-channel FD-SOI MOSFET. The structure, top to bottom:

1. **Gate electrode** (e.g., n⁺ poly-Si)
2. **Front gate oxide** of thickness $t_{ox,front}$
3. **p-type Si film** of thickness $t_{Si}$, doping $N_A$
4. **Buried oxide (BOX)** of thickness $t_{BOX}$
5. **Handle wafer** (Si substrate, acts as a "back gate")

Let $V_{GS,f}$ be the front-gate voltage and $V_{BS}$ the back-gate (substrate) voltage referenced to the body (which is the same as the source in a fully-depleted, source-tied SOI device).

### 3.1.2 Charge Neutrality and Gauss's Law

At the threshold of strong inversion, the inversion charge per unit area $Q_{inv}$ becomes non-negligible. The total semiconductor charge in the FD film is the sum of:
- The **ionized depletion charge** $Q_d = -q N_A t_{Si}$ (negative because it is ionized acceptor charge)
- The **inversion charge** $Q_{inv}$ at the front interface (negative, electrons)
- The **accumulation charge** at the back interface (negligible in FD-SOI at threshold)

**Step 1 — Apply the 1-D Poisson equation across the film.** Integrating from front interface to back interface:

$$
\frac{d^2 \phi}{dy^2} = \frac{q N_A}{\varepsilon_{Si}}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice with boundary conditions.** The potential at the front surface is $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ at threshold (this is the *definition* of the threshold condition in MOS physics, identical to bulk). The potential at the back interface is $\phi_b$.

$$
\phi(y) = \phi_s - \left( \phi_s - \phi_b \right) \frac{y}{t_{Si}} - \frac{q N_A}{2 \varepsilon_{Si}} y \left( t_{Si} - y \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Gauss's law at the front oxide–Si interface.** The displacement field in the front oxide equals the total semiconductor charge:

$$
\varepsilon_{ox} \frac{V_{GS,f} - V_{FB,f} - \phi_s}{t_{ox,front}} = - \left( Q_{inv} + Q_d \right)
$$

Solving for $V_{GS,f}$ at threshold (where $Q_{inv} \approx 0$ and $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$):

$$
V_{th,f} = V_{FB,f} + 2\phi_F - \frac{Q_d}{C_{ox,front}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_d = -q N_A t_{Si}$.** Then $-Q_d / C_{ox,front} = + q N_A t_{Si} / C_{ox,front}$. Recognizing that the depletion charge is *uniform* and limited by the film thickness (not by the gate-induced depletion width, as in bulk):

$$
V_{th,f} = V_{FB,f} + 2\phi_F + \frac{q N_A t_{Si}}{C_{ox,front}}
$$

This is the **fully-depleted SOI threshold voltage**.

### 3.1.3 Compare with Bulk MOSFET Threshold

For a bulk MOSFET, the depletion charge at threshold is

$$
Q_{d,bulk} = - \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{Si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}
$$

with corresponding $V_{th,bulk}$:

$$
V_{th,bulk} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{Si} q N_A \, 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
$$

The **key qualitative difference** is that in SOI, the depletion charge is **capped by the film thickness** $t_{Si}$, not by the gate-induced depletion width. Hence:

- For the same $N_A$, **$V_{th,FD-SOI} < V_{th,bulk}$** if $t_{Si}$ is small.
- $V_{th}$ can be **engineered by adjusting $t_{Si}$** — a *geometric* knob unavailable in bulk.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Body Factor and Back-Gate Coupling in FD-SOI

A defining feature of FD-SOI is that the **back gate (substrate) modulates the front-gate threshold linearly**.

### 3.2.1 Capacitor Divider Model

The Si film is sandwiched between two capacitors: front oxide ($C_{ox,f}$) and BOX ($C_{BOX}$). The body potential $V_B$ (relative to the source) is determined by capacitive division of the applied terminal voltages:

$$
V_B = \frac{C_{ox,f} \, V_{GS,f} + C_{BOX} \, V_{BS}}{C_{ox,f} + C_{Si} + C_{BOX}}
$$

In a fully-depleted, source-tied SOI device, the body is the Si film itself. At threshold, the semiconductor charge $Q_{sem} = -q N_A t_{Si}$ is constant, so any change in $V_B$ directly shifts $\phi_s$ at the front interface.

### 3.2.2 Derive $V_{th}$ Sensitivity to $V_{BS}$

Differentiate $V_{th,f}$ with respect to $V_{BS}$, holding $V_{GS,f}$ and $Q_{inv}$ fixed. The front surface potential must remain at $2\phi_F$, so the increased charge on $C_{ox,f}$ is offset by the increased charge induced via $C_{BOX}$. The result is:

$$
\frac{\partial V_{th,f}}{\partial V_{BS}} = - \frac{C_{Si} \, C_{ox,f}}{C_{ox,f} \left( C_{Si} + C_{BOX} \right)} = - \frac{C_{Si}}{C_{ox,f}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + C_{BOX}/C_{Si}}
$$

When $C_{BOX} \gg C_{Si}$ (thin film, moderate BOX), this reduces to:

$$
\frac{\partial V_{th,f}}{\partial V_{BS}} \approx - \frac{C_{Si}}{C_{ox,f}} = - \gamma_{SOI}
$$

Integrating:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{th,f}(V_{BS}) = V_{th,f}(0) - \gamma_{SOI} \, V_{BS}\,}
$$

This is **Equation 6 in the Cheat Sheet**. The negative sign means that a **positive $V_{BS}$ (forward back bias) reduces $V_{th}$**, increasing performance, while a **negative $V_{BS}$ (reverse back bias) increases $V_{th}$**, reducing leakage. This is the working principle of **Adaptive Body Biasing in FD-SOI** — a critical low-power technique.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Subthreshold Swing in FD-SOI

### 3.3.1 Starting Point — General MOS Subthreshold Slope

For any MOS structure, the subthreshold swing is:

$$
S = \frac{dV_{GS}}{d(\log_{10} I_D)} = \ln 10 \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{C_{sem}}{C_{ox}} \right)
$$

where $C_{sem}$ is the semiconductor depletion capacitance.

### 3.3.2 Substitute the FD-SOI $C_{sem}$

In FD-SOI, the semiconductor is fully depleted, so $C_{sem} = C_{Si} = \varepsilon_{Si}/t_{Si}$ (constant, set by geometry):

$$
S_{FD-SOI} = \ln 10 \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\varepsilon_{Si}/t_{Si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox,f}} \right)
$$

### 3.3.3 Take the Fully-Depleted Limit

As $t_{Si} \to 0$, $C_{Si} \to 0$, and:

$$
S_{FD-SOI} \xrightarrow{t_{Si} \to 0} \ln 10 \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \approx 60 \; \text{mV/dec} \;\; \text{at 300 K}
$$

**Engineering insight:** at room temperature, **no MOSFET — bulk or SOI — beats the 60 mV/dec thermodynamic limit**. SOI *approaches* it; it does not break it. Devices like *tunnel FETs* and *negative-capacitance FETs* attempt to break this limit, but those are beyond KTU Module 1's scope.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SOI vs Bulk CMOS

| Parameter | Bulk CMOS | PD-SOI | FD-SOI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Body contact | Always present (tied to source or to a bias) | Floating | Floating but body-effect suppressed |
| Body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ | $\gamma_{bulk} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Like bulk when depleted, but body floats | $\gamma_{SOI} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Si}/t_{Si}}{C_{ox,f}}$ (much smaller) |
| Junction capacitance $C_j$ | **Large** (area + perimeter) | **Near zero** | **Near zero** |
| Subthreshold swing $S$ | $\ge 70$ mV/dec typical | ~ 70–80 mV/dec | Approaches **60 mV/dec** |
| Latch-up | Possible (parasitic SCR) | **Impossible** | **Impossible** |
| Kink effect | Absent | **Present** (floating body) | Absent |
| Self-heating | Mild | Severe (poor thermal path) | Severe (poor thermal path) |
| $V_{th}$ engineering knob | Doping only | Doping + film thickness | **$t_{Si}$ + back-gate bias** (richest) |
| Soft-error rate | Higher | Lower | Lowest |
| Process maturity | Highest | Mature (legacy 180/130 nm) | Production at 22 nm, 18 nm (Samsung) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a SOI MOSFET

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GATE_STACK["GATE STACK"]
        GATE["GATE ELECTRODE poly Si or metal"]
        FOX["FRONT GATE OXIDE SiO2 or HfO2"]
    end
    subgraph BODY["THIN Si FILM t_Si p type"]
        SRC["SOURCE n plus"]
        CH["CHANNEL p type intrinsic or lightly doped"]
        DRN["DRAIN n plus"]
    end
    subgraph ISOLATION["INSULATING LAYER"]
        BOX["BURIED OXIDE SiO2 t BOX"]
    end
    SUB["HANDLE WAFER Si bulk mechanical support"]

    GATE --- FOX
    FOX --- CH
    SRC --- CH
    CH --- DRN
    CH --- BOX
    BOX --- SUB
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — How SOI Reduces Power

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT V_GS applied at front gate"] --> B["FRONT OXIDE C_ox_f controls channel"]
    B --> C["THIN Si FILM t_Si fully depleted"]
    C --> D["BURIED OXIDE C_BOX couples to back gate"]
    D --> E["BACK GATE V_BS adaptive bias"]
    E --> F["V_th modulated linearly by V_BS"]
    F --> G["DRAIN CURRENT I_D adjusted"]
    C --> H["NO JUNCTION CAPACITANCE C_j approx 0"]
    H --> I["TOTAL C_par lower than bulk"]
    I --> J["DYNAMIC POWER alpha C_par V_DD squared f reduced"]
    C --> K["NO LATCH UP possible"]
    K --> L["NO SUBSTRATE NOISE"]
    L --> M["LOW POWER OPERATION achieved"]
```

## 4.3 PD-SOI vs FD-SOI — Depletion Profile Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PD["PARTIALLY DEPLETED SOI large t_Si"]
        P1["FRONT GATE"] --> P2["DEPLETED REGION x_dmax"]
        P2 --> P3["NEUTRAL BODY floating region q N_A t_Si minus x_dmax"]
        P3 --> P4["BACK OXIDE BOX"]
    end
    subgraph FD["FULLY DEPLETED SOI small t_Si"]
        F1["FRONT GATE"] --> F2["DEPLETED REGION equals t_Si entire film"]
        F2 --> F3["NO NEUTRAL BODY"]
        F3 --> F4["BACK OXIDE BOX"]
    end
```

## 4.4 SOI Fabrication — SIMOX Process Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1 Start with bulk Si wafer"] --> S2["Step 2 High dose O plus ion implantation 1 to 2 x 10 to power 18 per cm squared at 200 keV"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 High temperature anneal above 1300 C in Ar plus O2 ambient"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Si plus O2 react to form SiO2 BOX layer"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Top Si recrystallizes to form thin Si film on BOX"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Result SOI wafer ready for CMOS process"]
```

## 4.5 SOI Fabrication — Smart Cut Process Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Step 1 Oxidize donor wafer to form BOX precursor"] --> A2["Step 2 Hydrogen ion H plus implantation at low dose to define cleave plane"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3 Bond donor wafer face down to handle wafer"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4 Anneal at approx 500 C induces cleavage along H plane"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5 Touch polish to smooth the top Si film"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6 Result SOI wafer with engineered t_Si and t_BOX"]
```

## 4.6 Adaptive Body-Bias Control Loop in FD-SOI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD["V_DD supply"] --> SENSOR["PERFORMANCE / LEAKAGE SENSOR"]
    SENSOR --> CTRL["BODY BIAS CONTROLLER"]
    CTRL -->|V_BS forward bias| VBS_HIGH["V_BS positive: lower V_th, higher speed, higher leakage"]
    CTRL -->|V_BS reverse bias| VBS_LOW["V_BS negative: higher V_th, lower leakage, lower speed"]
    VBS_HIGH --> SOI_DEV["FD SOI MOSFET"]
    VBS_LOW --> SOI_DEV
    SOI_DEV --> SENSOR
```
