- **I/O Pad Design:** I/O pads drive **huge off-chip capacitances** (nF range) with relatively slow edges — short-circuit power is again a small fraction.
- **Asynchronous / Self-Timed Circuits:** Variable input ramp times make I_sc **unpredictable** and design becomes harder.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Peak Short-Circuit Current

We consider a **symmetric CMOS inverter** with $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp} \vert = V_t$ and $\beta_n = \beta_p = \beta$.

### Step 1: Set up the saturation current expressions.

During the short-circuit window ($V_t \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} − V_t$), both transistors are in **saturation**:

$$
I_{DP} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta_p}{2}\,(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)^2
$$

$$
I_{DN} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta_n}{2}\,(V_{in} - V_t)^2
$$

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the output node (steady-state short circuit).

At the moment when $I_{sc}$ is at its **peak**, the output node is quasi-static (no charging/discharging dominates), so:

$$
I_{sc} = I_{DP} = I_{DN}
$$

$$
\dfrac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)^2 \;=\; \dfrac{\beta_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2
$$

### Step 3: Solve for V_in at peak I_sc.

Taking the positive square root (both sides positive in the conduction window):

$$
\sqrt{\beta_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t) \;=\; \sqrt{\beta_n}\,(V_{in} - V_t)
$$

For the symmetric case $\beta_n = \beta_p = \beta$:

$$
V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t \;=\; V_{in} - V_t
$$

$$
V_{in}^{*} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute back to get peak I_sc.

$$
I_{sc,peak} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{2}\left(V_{DD} - \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t\right)^{2}
$$

$$
I_{sc,peak} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc,peak} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{8}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **important factor-of-4 difference** from the commonly-cited $\beta/2 (V_{DD}-2V_t)^2$ formula (which holds for a *step* input, not for the symmetric *ramp*-input case).

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Average Short-Circuit Current Over One Ramp Transition

We integrate the saturation current over the short-circuit window $[V_t,\, V_{DD} - V_t]$.

### Step 1: Express V_in as a function of time during the ramp.

$$
V_{in}(t) \;=\; \dfrac{V_{DD}}{\tau}\,t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; t \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{V_{DD}}\,V_{in}
$$

$$
dt \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{V_{DD}}\,dV_{in}
$$

### Step 2: Set up the integral of I_sc over the conduction window.

The short-circuit current at any V_in is the smaller of $I_{DP}$ and $I_{DN}$. By symmetry, the **rising half** and **falling half** of the symmetric short-circuit pulse contribute equally, so we can integrate over the half-window $[V_t,\, V_{DD}/2]$ and double.

$$
I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\tau}\int_{0}^{\tau} I_{sc}(t)\,dt \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\tau}\int_{V_t}^{V_{DD}/2} \dfrac{\beta}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2 \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{V_{DD}}\,dV_{in}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integral.

$$
I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{V_{DD}} \int_{V_t}^{V_{DD}/2} (V_{in} - V_t)^2\,dV_{in}
$$

Let $u = V_{in} - V_t$, $du = dV_{in}$. Limits: $u = 0$ to $u = V_{DD}/2 - V_t$.

$$
\int_{0}^{V_{DD}/2 - V_t} u^2\,du \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t\right)^{3}
$$

$$
I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{3V_{DD}}\left(\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t\right)^{3} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^{3}
$$

### Step 4: Introduce the matching factor η.

For a **non-matched** case where $\tau_{in} \ne t_{p,out}$, the short-circuit current scales as:

$$
I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^{3} \cdot \min\!\left(1,\; \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{t_{p,out}}\right)
$$

Using $\eta = \tau_{in}/t_{p,out}$:

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc,avg} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^{3} \cdot \min(1,\;\eta)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master KTU equation** for short-circuit current. Notice that when $\eta \ge 1$ (slow output, large C_L), the I_sc is **capped** at a constant. When $\eta < 1$ (fast output, small C_L), I_sc scales **linearly with η** — i.e., **inversely with C_L**.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — C_L Dependence of I_sc

Substituting $t_{p,out} = \dfrac{C_L\,V_{DD}}{\beta\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^2}$ into the matching ratio:

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{t_{p,out}} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{in}\,\beta\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^2}{C_L\,V_{DD}}
$$

### Case A: Small C_L (η ≥ 1) — I_sc is C_L-independent (saturated)

$$
I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^{3}
$$

### Case B: Large C_L (η < 1) — I_sc decreases as 1/C_L

$$
I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^{3} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}\,\beta\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^2}{C_L\,V_{DD}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{in}\,\beta^2\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^{3}\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^{2}}{24\,C_L\,V_{DD}^{2}}\,}
$$

This **1/C_L dependence** is the core of this topic.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Regime | Condition on C_L | Matching Ratio η | I_sc Behavior | Physical Reason |
|--------|------------------|------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Very small C_L** | $C_L \to 0$ | $\eta \gg 1$ | **C_L-independent (saturated)** | Output switches instantly; full V_DD across the conduction path |
| **Intermediate C_L** | $C_L \approx \tau_{in}\beta V_{DD}^{-1}$ | $\eta \approx 1$ | **Peak / Transition** | I_sc is at its maximum useful value |
| **Very large C_L** | $C_L \to \infty$ | $\eta \to 0$ | **Decreases as 1/C_L** | Output barely moves during the input ramp; V_DS of transistors collapses |
| **Asymptotic limit** | $C_L \to \infty$ | $\eta \to 0$ | **I_sc → 0** | No voltage across the transistors during the conduction window |

> [!NOTE]
> **The key KTU takeaway:** Increasing the load capacitance **reduces** the short-circuit current. This is why **heavily-loaded gates** (e.g., clock buffers) have *negligible* short-circuit power, while **lightly-loaded gates** (e.g., high-fanout, low-slew, internal nodes) can have *significant* short-circuit power.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Short-Circuit Current Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDDA["V_DD SUPPLY RAIL"]:::supply
    PMOS[("PMOS NETWORK"):::pmos
    NCOUT["OUTPUT NODE V_out"]:::node
    CLOAD(("LOAD CAPACITANCE C_L")):::cap
    NMOS[("NMOS NETWORK"):::nmos
    GND["GROUND RAIL"]:::ground
    VIN["RAMP INPUT V_in t"]:::input

    VDDA -- "V_DS_p" --> PMOS
    PMOS -- "ON when V_in < V_DD - V_tp" --> NCOUT
    NCOUT -- "Charging / Discharging" --> CLOAD
    NCOUT -- "V_DS_n" --> NMOS
    NMOS -- "ON when V_in > V_tn" --> GND
    VIN -- "Controls both" --> PMOS
    VIN -- "Controls both" --> NMOS

    classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef pmos fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef nmos fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#008080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cap fill:#A8DADC,stroke:#457B9D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ground fill:#2C3E50,stroke:#1B2631,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef node fill:#F1C40F,stroke:#B7950B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef input fill:#E67E22,stroke:#A04000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 Variation of I_sc with C_L — Behavioural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Consider CMOS Inverter Switching"]:::start
    B{"What is C_L?"}:::decision
    C["C_L VERY SMALL"]:::case
    D["Output switches almost instantly"]:::explain
    E["Full V_DD across transistors during conduction"]:::explain
    F["I_sc reaches PEAK / SATURATED value"]:::peak
    G["C_L INTERMEDIATE"]:::case
    H["Output switches at comparable rate to input"]:::explain
    I["tau_in approx equal to t_p,out"]:::explain
    J["Matching regime - I_sc still high"]:::peak
    K["C_L VERY LARGE"]:::case
    L["Output barely moves during input ramp"]:::explain
    M["V_DS of transistors collapses early"]:::explain
    N["I_sc decreases as 1 divided by C_L"]:::decrease
    O["I_sc eventually tends to ZERO"]:::zero
    P["SUMMARY: I_sc peaks at intermediate C_L"]:::summary

    A --> B
    B -- "C_L small" --> C --> D --> E --> F
    B -- "C_L medium" --> G --> H --> I --> J
    B -- "C_L large" --> K --> L --> M --> N --> O
    F --> P
    J --> P
    O --> P

    classDef start fill:#1ABC9C,stroke:#117A65,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decision fill:#F39C12,stroke:#B9770E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef case fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef explain fill:#ECF0F1,stroke:#7B7D7D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef peak fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#922B21,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decrease fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#5B2C6F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef zero fill:#34495E,stroke:#212F3D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef summary fill:#16A085,stroke:#0E6251,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 I_sc vs C_L — Qualitative Curve (ASCII)

```
    I_sc (A)
      │
  I_pk├─────────────────╮                  
      │                 ╲                  
      │                  ╲                 
      │                   ╲                
      │                    ╲               
      │                     ╲              
      │                      ╲             
      │                       ╲            
      │                        ╲           
      │                         ╲          
      │                          ╲___      
      │                              ──────
      └─────┬─────────┬─────────┬──────────► C_L
         SMALL     INTERMEDIATE   LARGE
        (η >> 1)     (η ≈ 1)    (η << 1)
        SATURATED    PEAK       1/C_L DECAY
```

**Reading the curve:**
- **Left plateau (small C_L):** I_sc is *constant*, limited by transistor drive strength. Adding a smaller C_L doesn't help.
- **Peak region (intermediate C_L):** I_sc is at its highest value.
- **Decay region (large C_L):** I_sc falls as 1/C_L.
- **Asymptote (very large C_L):** I_sc → 0.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define short-circuit current in a CMOS inverter. Under what condition does it become zero?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Short-circuit current (I_sc) is the direct current that flows from V_DD to GND through the simultaneously-conducting PMOS and NMOS transistors of a CMOS gate during the finite input transition interval when both devices are ON. It exists only when $V_{tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - |V_{tp}|$.

I_sc becomes **zero** under the condition:
$$
V_{tn} + |V_{tp}| \;\ge\; V_{DD}
$$
i.e., when the sum of the threshold voltages exceeds the supply voltage, the conduction window **vanishes**, and no direct current path forms.

**(1 mark)** for definition, **(1 mark)** for the conduction window, **(1 mark)** for the zero-current condition.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**How does the short-circuit current of a CMOS inverter vary with the load capacitance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The short-circuit current **decreases as the load capacitance C_L increases**. The relationship is governed by the matching ratio $\eta = \tau_{in}/t_{p,out}$, where $t_{p,out} \propto C_L$.

- For **small C_L** ($\eta \ge 1$): I_sc is **saturated** at a constant value independent of C_L.
- For **large C_L** ($\eta < 1$): I_sc scales as **1/C_L** because the slow output node reduces V_DS across the conducting transistors.

**(1 mark)** for stating the relationship, **(1 mark)** for explaining small C_L behavior, **(1 mark)** for explaining large C_L behavior with the 1/C_L law.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average short-circuit current** in a symmetric CMOS inverter driven by a ramp input with ramp time $\tau$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result from part (a), explain with appropriate **equations and a sketch** how the short-circuit current varies with the load capacitance C_L. Discuss the engineering implications for low-power VLSI design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Define the symmetric inverter.**
Assume $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = V_t$ and $\beta_n = \beta_p = \beta$.

**Step 2: Write the saturation currents.**

$$
I_{DN} = \frac{\beta}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2, \quad I_{DP} = \frac{\beta}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)^2
$$

**Step 3: Identify the short-circuit window.**
Both transistors conduct when $V_t \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - V_t$.

**Step 4: Time-average over the ramp.**

$$
I_{sc,avg} = \frac{1}{\tau}\int_{0}^{\tau} I_{sc}(t)\,dt
$$

Using $V_{in} = (V_{DD}/\tau)t$ and symmetry, integrating from $V_t$ to $V_{DD}/2$ and doubling:

$$
I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta}{3V_{DD}}\int_{V_t}^{V_{DD}/2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2\,dV_{in}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate.**

$$
I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta}{3V_{DD}} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t\right)^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3\,}
$$

**Mark split:** Assumptions (1), saturation equations (2), integration setup (2), final result (2).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Introduce t_p,out.**

$$
t_{p,out} = \frac{C_L\,V_{DD}}{\beta\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^2}
$$

**Step 2: Define the matching ratio.**

$$
\eta = \frac{\tau}{t_{p,out}} = \frac{\tau\,\beta\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^2}{C_L\,V_{DD}}
$$

**Step 3: Write the general I_sc expression.**

$$
I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot \min(1, \eta)
$$

**Step 4: Two regimes.**

- **Small C_L ($\eta \ge 1$):** I_sc is saturated, independent of C_L.
- **Large C_L ($\eta < 1$):** $I_{sc,avg} = \dfrac{\beta^2\,\tau\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3\,(V_{DD} - V_t)^2}{24\,C_L\,V_{DD}^2}$, varying as **1/C_L**.

**Step 5: Sketch.** Plot of I_sc vs C_L shows a plateau at low C_L, transition around matched condition, and 1/C_L decay at high C_L (see Section 4.3).

**Step 6: Engineering implications.**

- **Large C_L is good for I_sc** (e.g., clock nets, output pads).
- **Small C_L + slow input ramp** is the **worst case** for I_sc.
- At **low V_DD** (near/sub-threshold), the (V_DD − 2V_t)³ factor shrinks I_sc dramatically.
- Use **steeper input ramps** to reduce I_sc when C_L is small.

**Mark split:** t_p,out derivation (2), η and regime analysis (2), sketch (1), implications (2).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a **circuit diagram** and relevant **waveforms**, explain the origin of short-circuit current in a CMOS inverter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **peak short-circuit current** and show how it depends on the load capacitance. Discuss the case when C_L is very large and very small. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Circuit.** Standard CMOS inverter with PMOS pull-up (source to V_DD), NMOS pull-down (source to GND), common drain to V_out, with load capacitor C_L. Input ramp $V_{in}(t) = (V_{DD}/\tau)t$ for $0 \le t \le \tau$.

**Step 2: Waveforms description.** Plot $V_{in}(t)$ as a ramp, and $V_{out}(t)$ as an inverted, exponentially-decaying waveform with time constant $t_{p,out} = C_L V_{DD}/[\beta(V_{DD} - V_t)^2]$. The current $I_{sc}(t)$ is a **triangular / bell-shaped pulse** that rises as both transistors begin to conduct and falls as the output voltage causes one transistor to leave saturation.

**Step 3: Three phases of operation.**

- **Phase 1** (0 to V_t): only PMOS conducts → charging C_L.
- **Phase 2** (V_t to V_DD − V_t): **both** in saturation → I_sc flows.
- **Phase 3** (V_DD − V_t to V_DD): only NMOS conducts → discharging C_L.

**Step 4: Identification of I_sc.** I_sc is the current flowing through *both* transistors during Phase 2.

**Mark split:** Circuit (2), input/output waveforms (2), current pulse (1), three phases (2).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Write the KCL at the output node during conduction.**

$$
I_{sc} = I_{DP} = I_{DN}
$$

$$
\frac{\beta}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)^2 = \frac{\beta}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2
$$

**Step 2: Solve for V_in at peak.**

$$
V_{in}^{*} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute peak I_sc.**

$$
\boxed{\,I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\beta}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2\,}
$$

**Step 4: Dependence on C_L.**

- **C_L very small:** Output transitions much faster than input. The transistor's V_DS remains close to V_DD throughout the input transition, so $I_{sc,peak}$ is reached and **saturated**.
- **C_L very large:** Output is slow. As the input reaches the peak-conduction point (V_DD/2), the output is still near 0 (for falling transition). V_DS of NMOS is small → I_sc is limited. **As C_L → ∞, I_sc → 0**.

**Step 5: Conclusion.** I_sc,peak is independent of C_L at small C_L, and decreases monotonically as C_L increases. Maximum I_sc occurs when $\tau \approx t_{p,out}$.

**Mark split:** KCL setup (2), solving for V_in (1), I_sc,peak expression (1), C_L limits (2), conclusion (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the (V_DD − 2V_t) factor.** The short-circuit current is *zero* when V_DD ≤ 2V_t. Many students write $V_{DD} - V_t$ instead. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing peak and average I_sc.** Peak is the instantaneous maximum current; average is integrated over the ramp. They differ by a factor of ~3. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Not stating assumptions.** Always explicitly state: symmetric inverter, ramp input, $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}|$, both transistors in saturation. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Mixing up I_sc and switching current.** Switching current flows *into* C_L to charge it. I_sc flows *through* both transistors to ground. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting the 1/C_L decay regime.** Many students assume I_sc is *always* proportional to C_L. The correct behaviour is: **constant for small C_L, 1/C_L for large C_L**. **−2 marks.**
> 6. **No units in final answer.** Always include Amps / Watts / Farads. **−0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- ✅ **Definition:** I_sc is the crowbar current during the simultaneous ON-state of PMOS and NMOS in the input transition window $V_{tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - |V_{tp}|$.
- ✅ **Conduction Window Width:** $\Delta V_{in} = V_{DD} - V_{tn} - |V_{tp}|$.
- ✅ **Zero I_sc Condition:** $V_{DD} \le V_{tn} + |V_{tp}|$ (no overlap of conduction regions).
- ✅ **Master Formula (Symmetric Inverter, Ramp Input):**
$$I_{sc,avg} = \dfrac{\beta}{24\,V_{DD}}\,(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot \min\!\left(1,\; \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{t_{p,out}}\right)$$
- ✅ **Matching Ratio:** $\eta = \tau_{in}/t_{p,out}$, where $t_{p,out} \propto C_L$.
- ✅ **Behaviour with C_L:**
  - **Small C_L →** I_sc **saturated** (constant, independent of C_L).
  - **Large C_L →** I_sc **decays as 1/C_L**.
  - **Maximum I_sc at intermediate C_L** where $\tau_{in} \approx t_{p,out}$.
- ✅ **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Frequently Tested):**
  - Heavily loaded gates (clock trees, I/O pads) → negligible I_sc.
  - Lightly loaded gates with slow inputs → I_sc is significant.
  - Steeper input transitions → smaller I_sc.
- ✅ **Power Dissipation:** $P_{sc} = I_{sc,avg} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f_{sw}$ — scales **linearly** with frequency (unlike quadratic switching power).
- ✅ **Low-Power Design Implications:**
  - Use **steep input ramps** to the gates.
  - **Buffer** high-fanout nets to sharpen the ramp.
  - At low V_DD, I_sc naturally reduces (V_DD − 2V_t)³ term collapses.
  - For **multi-Vt** designs, I_sc window shrinks as V_t increases.
- ✅ **Sketch Reminder:** I_sc vs C_L is a **constant-then-decay** curve (NOT a monotonic one).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Power Dissipation: Switching Power of CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switching Power Dissipation: Switching Power of CMOS Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Switching Power Dissipation** (also called **Dynamic Power Dissipation** due to switching activity) is the power consumed in a CMOS inverter (and any CMOS logic gate) every time its output node transitions between logic levels (0 → 1 and 1 → 0). It arises because the **load capacitance** ($C_L$) connected to the output node must be charged to $V_{DD}$ and discharged back to ground through the PMOS and NMOS transistors respectively. Every charge–discharge cycle draws energy directly from the supply rail $V_{DD}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:**
> *"Switching power is the power dissipated in the CMOS inverter due to the charging and discharging of the load capacitance at the output node during logic transitions. It is the dominant component of total power consumption in modern CMOS VLSI circuits."*

### The Fundamental Energy Event

When the input to a CMOS inverter changes state:
1. The **PMOS transistor turns ON** and the **NMOS turns OFF** (or vice versa).
2. Current flows from $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS to charge $C_L$.
3. Energy is drawn from the supply. Half of this energy is stored on $C_L$, the other half is **dissipated as heat** in the PMOS.
4. When $C_L$ discharges through the NMOS, the stored energy is **dissipated as heat** in the NMOS.
5. Hence, **per switching cycle**, energy = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ is taken from supply, and energy = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model

Imagine $C_L$ as a **bucket of water** placed at the top of a hill:
- To **fill** the bucket, a pump (PMOS) pulls water up from a well at the bottom (the $V_{DD}$ source). The pump burns fuel = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ while filling, and the bucket stores potential energy = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.
- To **empty** the bucket, you pour the water down the hill. As it tumbles down (through the NMOS), **all** the stored water energy is converted into heat (dissipated in the NMOS).
- **Total energy wasted per cycle = 1 full bucket of water's worth**, which is $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$.

The number of times per second you do this is the **switching frequency** $f$, and the fraction of clock cycles in which a transition actually occurs is the **activity factor** $\alpha$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The supply must provide **$C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ joules per transition**, even though only **$\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$** is stored on the capacitor. The other half is permanently lost as heat.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Modern CMOS) |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **0.7 V – 1.2 V** |
| Load Capacitance | $C_L$ | **fF to pF range** |
| Switching Frequency | $f$ | **100 MHz – 5 GHz** |
| Activity Factor | $\alpha$ | **0.0 – 1.0** (typically 0.1 – 0.3) |
| Energy per Transition | $E_{sw}$ | $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Sources of Switching Power

In any CMOS gate, switching power has two underlying physical causes:

1. **Capacitive Charging Power ($P_{cap}$):** Power needed to charge and discharge the load capacitance on every output transition. This is the **dominant** component.
2. **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{sc}$):** Power dissipated during the brief interval when both PMOS and NMOS are simultaneously ON (during the input ramp). Usually 10–20% of $P_{cap}$ in well-designed gates.

### 2.2 Logic Flow — Why Power is Dissipated

- **Step 1 — Pre-switch:** Output node is at 0 V (capacitor $C_L$ empty, voltage = 0).
- **Step 2 — Input rises:** NMOS turns ON, PMOS turns OFF. $C_L$ discharges to ground. **No current from supply**, but the stored energy in $C_L$ is **dissipated in the NMOS channel** as heat ($I^2R$ loss).
- **Step 3 — Input falls:** PMOS turns ON, NMOS turns OFF. $V_{DD}$ source pushes charge into $C_L$.
   - Charge delivered = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}$
   - Energy drawn from supply = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$
   - Energy stored on capacitor = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
   - Energy dissipated in PMOS = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
- **Step 4 — Repeat:** Every rising + falling transition consumes energy $C_L V_{DD}^2$.

### 2.3 Why Each Variable Matters (Engineering Utility)

| Variable | Effect on Power | Design Leverage |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{DD}$ | **Quadratic** ($\propto V_{DD}^2$) — most powerful knob | Voltage scaling is the #1 power-saving technique |
| $C_L$ | **Linear** | Reduce wire/load capacitance via layout, smaller transistors |
| $f$ | **Linear** | Clock gating, frequency scaling |
| $\alpha$ | **Linear** | Activity reduction via encoding, gating, logic choice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $V_{DD}^2$?** Because the charge moved per cycle is $Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$, and energy is $Q \cdot V_{DD}$, giving $\propto V_{DD}^2$. This is why **voltage scaling** has such a dramatic effect on power — halving $V_{DD}$ quarters the switching power!

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | **Master switching power equation** | **Watts (W)** |
| 2 | $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy per complete 0→1→0 transition | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy stored on $C_L$ after charging | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $E_{PMOS\,loss} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | Heat dissipated in PMOS during charging | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $E_{NMOS\,loss} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | Heat dissipated in NMOS during discharge | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $Q_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$ | Charge moved per transition | Coulombs (C) |
| 7 | $I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f$ | Average current drawn from $V_{DD}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | $P_{sc} = \alpha_{sc} \cdot C_{sc} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Short-circuit power (minor term) | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{sc} + P_{leak}$ | Total CMOS power | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $\alpha = \frac{\text{number of transitions}}{\text{number of clock cycles}}$ | Activity factor definition | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$
> - $V_{DD} > 0$
> - $C_L > 0$
> - $f > 0$
> - Assumes **ideal step input** (no short-circuit current during transition)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This equation is the foundation of:
- **Mobile/SoC power budgeting** — Apple, Qualcomm, and Intel engineers use this exact formula to predict battery life.
- **Clock-tree synthesis** — since the clock toggles every cycle ($\alpha \approx 1$), clock networks can consume **30–40%** of total chip power.
- **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** — directly leverages the $V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ relationship to save power in mobile CPUs/GPUs.
- **Power gating & clock gating** — reduce $\alpha$ to near zero for idle blocks.
- **Energy-efficient IoT design** — entire sub-threshold design philosophy exists because $V_{DD}^2$ dominates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Switching Power from First Principles

We will derive the master equation $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ from scratch using basic circuit analysis.

#### Stage A — Single Charging Event

Consider the CMOS inverter with its output capacitance $C_L$ being charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$ via the PMOS.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous current flowing into $C_L$ is:

$$i(t) = C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

The instantaneous power delivered by the supply is:

$$p_{VDD}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot i(t) = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate over the charging interval (from $V_{out} = 0$ to $V_{out} = V_{DD}$):

$$
E_{VDD} = \int_{0}^{T_{ch}} p_{VDD}(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{T_{ch}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \, dt
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $dt$ to convert the integration variable from time to voltage:

$$
E_{VDD} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV_{out}
$$

**Step 4:** Pull out the constant $V_{DD}$ and integrate:

$$
E_{VDD} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot [V_{out}]_{0}^{V_{DD}} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - 0)
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$
\boxed{E_{VDD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}}
$$

> **Conclusion of Stage A:** The supply must deliver exactly $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ joules to charge the capacitor to $V_{DD}$, regardless of the PMOS resistance or the rise time.

#### Stage B — Energy Stored on the Capacitor

**Step 6:** The energy stored on a capacitor at voltage $V$ is:

$$
E_{stored} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} v \cdot C_L \, dv = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$
\boxed{E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}}
$$

#### Stage C — Energy Dissipated in the PMOS

**Step 8:** By conservation of energy:

$$
E_{PMOS} = E_{VDD} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 9:** Simplify:

$$
\boxed{E_{PMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}}
$$

> **Conclusion of Stage C:** Exactly **half** of the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor. This is a fundamental result — independent of the transistor's size or resistance.

#### Stage D — Discharge Energy Dissipated in NMOS

**Step 10:** When the NMOS discharges $C_L$ from $V_{DD}$ to 0, **no current** flows from the supply. The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is entirely converted to heat in the NMOS channel.

**Step 11:** Therefore:

$$
\boxed{E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}}
$$

#### Stage E — Total Energy per Complete Cycle

**Step 12:** Total energy dissipated in one full 0→1→0 transition:

$$
E_{total} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 13:** Simplify:

$$
\boxed{E_{total} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}}
$$

#### Stage F — From Energy to Power

**Step 14:** If the clock frequency is $f$, there are $f$ cycles per second. If only a fraction $\alpha$ of those cycles contain a transition (activity factor), then the **number of transitions per second** is $\alpha \cdot f$.

**Step 15:** Average power = energy per transition × number of transitions per second:

$$
P_{sw} = E_{total} \cdot (\alpha \cdot f)
$$

**Step 16:** Substitute:

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q.E.D.** The master switching power equation is derived rigorously from energy conservation and basic capacitor physics.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$, operates at $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, with clock frequency $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$ and activity factor $\alpha = 0.25$. Calculate the switching power, energy per transition, and average supply current.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy per transition:**

$$
E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.0)^2
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{sw} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 50 \text{ fJ}}
$$

**Step 2 — Switching power:**

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.25 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot 500 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.25 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 5 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.25 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = 6.25 \, \mu\text{W}}
$$

**Step 3 — Average supply current:**

$$
I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.25 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.0 \cdot 500 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{avg} = 6.25 \, \mu\text{A}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $P = V \cdot I = 1.0 \cdot 6.25 \, \mu\text{A} = 6.25 \, \mu\text{W}$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CMOS Inverter Switching Activity Flow (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal VIN rising edge] --> B{Input Voltage Level}
    B -- Greater than Vthn --> C[NMOS turns ON]
    B --> D[PMOS turns OFF]
    C --> E[Output capacitance CL discharges to GND]
    E --> F[Stored energy dissipated as heat in NMOS]
    A2[Input Signal VIN falling edge] --> B2{Input Voltage Level}
    B2 -- Less than VDD minus Vthp --> D2[PMOS turns ON]
    B2 --> C2[NMOS turns OFF]
    D2 --> E2[Current flows from VDD through PMOS]
    E2 --> F2[Output capacitance CL charges to VDD]
    F2 --> G2[Energy drawn from supply equals CL times VDD squared]
    G2 --> H2[Half stored on CL, half dissipated in PMOS]
    F --> I[Total energy lost per cycle equals CL times VDD squared]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Average switching power equals alpha times CL times VDD squared times f]
```

### 4.2 Energy Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VDD Supply Source] -- Energy equals CL times VDD squared --> B[PMOS Transistor]
    B -- Half energy dissipated as heat --> C[Heat Loss in PMOS]
    B -- Half energy stored --> D[Load Capacitor CL charged to VDD]
    D -- Stored energy fully dissipated --> E[NMOS Transistor]
    E -- Heat Loss in NMOS --> F[Heat Loss in NMOS]
    C --> G[Total Energy Dissipated equals CL times VDD squared per cycle]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Multiply by alpha times f --> Switching Power]
```

### 4.3 Power Components of CMOS Inverter (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Total CMOS Inverter Power PTotal]
    P --> Psw[Switching Power Psw]
    P --> Psc[Short Circuit Power Psc]
    P --> Pleak[Leakage Power Pleak]
    Psw --> Psw1[Dominant Component]
    Psw1 --> PswEq[Equals alpha times CL times VDD squared times f]
    Psc --> PscEq[Equals alpha sc times VDD times Ipeak times tsc times f]
    Pleak --> Pleak1[Subthreshold current times VDD]
    PswEq --> Q[KTU Exam Focus: Most Important]
    PscEq --> Q2[Minor Term usually 10 to 20 percent]
    Pleak1 --> Q3[Dominant in nanometer technologies]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Exams:**
> - The energy flow diagram clearly shows that switching power is **independent of transistor sizing, resistance, or rise time** — it depends only on $C_L$, $V_{DD}$, $f$, and $\alpha$.
> - The "half-and-half" rule (half stored, half dissipated) is a **favourite KTU board question**.

### 4.4 Table — Power Contribution by Source (Typical 45 nm CMOS)

| Component | Symbol | Formula | Approx. % of Total Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switching Power** | $P_{sw}$ | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ | **50–70%** |
| **Short-Circuit Power** | $P_{sc}$ | $\alpha_{sc} C_{sc} V_{DD}^{2} f$ | **5–15%** |
| **Subthreshold Leakage** | $P_{sub}$ | $I_{sub} \cdot V_{DD}$ | **15–30%** |
| **Gate Leakage** | $P_{gate}$ | $I_{gate} \cdot V_{DD}$ | **1–5%** |
| **Diode Leakage (Junction)** | $P_{junc}$ | $I_{j} \cdot V_{DD}$ | **Negligible** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table:** Memorize this — switching power remains the **single largest component** in active CMOS logic, even at advanced nodes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define switching power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Mention the master equation relating it to circuit parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Switching power dissipation is the power consumed in a CMOS inverter due to the **charging and discharging of the load capacitance** at the output node whenever the input changes state, causing the output to switch between logic '0' and logic '1'.

The master equation is:

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = activity factor (fraction of clock cycles with a transition)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance at the output node
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = clock frequency

This is the **dominant** component of total power dissipation in active CMOS circuits.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by the activity factor $\alpha$ in CMOS switching power? Why is $\alpha \approx 1$ for a clock signal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **activity factor** $\alpha$ is defined as the ratio of the number of output transitions to the total number of clock cycles:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\text{Number of transitions}}{\text{Number of clock cycles}}
$$

It lies in the range $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$.

For a **clock signal**, the output transitions from 0 to 1 and 1 to 0 in every single clock cycle, hence the number of transitions per cycle = 1, giving $\alpha = 1$.

This is why **clock distribution networks** are responsible for a disproportionately large share (often 30–40%) of total chip power consumption.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for switching power dissipation of a CMOS inverter from first principles. Clearly show that exactly half of the energy drawn from the supply per charging cycle is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter drives a total load capacitance of 100 fF. The supply voltage is 1.2 V, operating frequency is 250 MHz, and the activity factor is 0.2. Calculate:
   (i) The energy dissipated per switching cycle.
   (ii) The average switching power.
   (iii) The average current drawn from the supply.
   (iv) If the supply voltage is reduced to 0.7 V (keeping all else constant), find the percentage reduction in switching power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** When the input to the CMOS inverter falls (0 → 0 logic at input), the PMOS turns ON and the NMOS turns OFF. The PMOS charges the output capacitance $C_L$ from 0 V to $V_{DD}$.

**Step 2:** Instantaneous current into $C_L$:

$$
i(t) = C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Instantaneous power from supply:

$$
p_{VDD}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot i(t) = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** Energy delivered by supply during charging:

$$
E_{VDD} = \int_{0}^{T_{ch}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \, dt = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV_{out}
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate:

$$
E_{VDD} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Energy stored on the capacitor:

$$
E_{stored} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} v \cdot C_L \, dv = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 7:** By energy conservation, energy dissipated in PMOS:

$$
E_{PMOS} = E_{VDD} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Step 8:** Therefore, **exactly half** of $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS, regardless of transistor size. This is the **"half-energy rule"**.

**Conclusion:** The supply provides $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ joules per charging event, of which $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is stored and $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is permanently lost as heat. ✓

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $C_L = 100 \text{ fF} = 100 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$
- $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$
- $f = 250 \text{ MHz} = 250 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- $\alpha = 0.2$

**(i) Energy dissipated per switching cycle:**

Energy per transition is $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ (full cycle = charge + discharge):

$$
E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} = 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.2)^2
$$

$$
E_{sw} = 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 = 144 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{sw} = 144 \text{ fJ}}
$$

**(ii) Average switching power:**

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.2 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 250 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.2 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.2 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-5} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = 7.2 \, \mu\text{W}}
$$

**(iii) Average supply current:**

$$
I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.2 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.2 \cdot 250 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.2 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3 \times 10^{8} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{avg} = 6 \, \mu\text{A}}
$$

**Verification:** $P_{sw} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{avg} = 1.2 \cdot 6 \, \mu\text{A} = 7.2 \, \mu\text{W}$ ✓

**(iv) Percentage reduction when $V_{DD}$ reduces to 0.7 V:**

Since $P_{sw} \propto V_{DD}^{2}$:

$$
P_{sw,new} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot (0.7)^2 \cdot f
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \frac{(0.7)^2}{(1.2)^2} = \frac{0.49}{1.44} = 0.3403
$$

Reduction percentage:

$$
\Delta P\,\% = (1 - 0.3403) \times 100\% = 65.97\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Switching power reduces by approximately } 66\%}
$$

---

### ✦ **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain with proper energy flow diagrams how switching power dissipation occurs in a CMOS inverter during both the charging (low-to-high) and discharging (high-to-low) phases of the output. Why is the energy lost independent of the transistor's ON resistance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the switching power contributions in three scenarios for the same CMOS inverter:
   - **Scenario I:** $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, $f = 1 \text{ GHz}$, $\alpha = 0.3$
   - **Scenario II:** $V_{DD} = 0.8 \text{ V}$, $f = 1 \text{ GHz}$, $\alpha = 0.3$
   - **Scenario III:** $V_{DD} = 0.8 \text{ V}$, $f = 0.8 \text{ GHz}$, $\alpha = 0.3$

   Take $C_L = 200 \text{ fF}$. Compute $P_{sw}$ in each case and comment on which technique gives the maximum power saving. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Energy Flow Explanation (7 marks)**

**Charging Phase (Output 0 → 1, Input 1 → 0):**
- PMOS is ON; NMOS is OFF.
- Current path: $V_{DD} \rightarrow$ PMOS channel $\rightarrow$ $C_L \rightarrow$ GND (return).
- Energy delivered by $V_{DD}$ = $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.
- Of this, $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is stored on $C_L$ (recoverable).
- The remaining $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS channel (permanent loss).

**Discharging Phase (Output 1 → 0, Input 0 → 1):**
- NMOS is ON; PMOS is OFF.
- $C_L$ discharges through the NMOS to GND.
- No current flows from $V_{DD}$.
- The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is **entirely** dissipated as heat in the NMOS channel.

**Total Energy Lost per Cycle:**
$$
E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} \text{ (PMOS)} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} \text{ (NMOS)} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

**Why is the energy lost independent of ON resistance?**

The energy stored on the capacitor $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ depends **only on** $C_L$ and $V_{DD}$, not on the path through which it was charged. Similarly, the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ = $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is determined purely by the charge $C_L V_{DD}$ moved through a potential difference $V_{DD}$. The PMOS resistance only affects **how fast** the charging happens (the rise time), not **how much** energy is dissipated. This is a beautiful consequence of charge conservation — exactly half is always lost, no matter the transistor sizing.

---

**Part (b) — Three-Scenario Comparison (7 marks)**

**Common parameters:** $C_L = 200 \text{ fF} = 200 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$, $\alpha = 0.3$.

**Scenario I:** $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, $f = 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$

$$
P_{sw,I} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{sw,I} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 10^{9} = 0.3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sw,I} = 60 \, \mu\text{W}}
$$

**Scenario II:** $V_{DD} = 0.8 \text{ V}$, $f = 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$

$$
P_{sw,II} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (0.8)^2 \cdot 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{sw,II} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 0.64 \cdot 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{sw,II} = 0.3 \cdot 0.64 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.384 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sw,II} = 38.4 \, \mu\text{W}}
$$

**Scenario III:** $V_{DD} = 0.8 \text{ V}$, $f = 0.8 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$

$$
P_{sw,III} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (0.8)^2 \cdot 0.8 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{sw,III} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 0.64 \cdot 0.8 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{sw,III} = 0.3 \cdot 0.512 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.3072 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sw,III} = 30.72 \, \mu\text{W}}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Scenario | $V_{DD}$ (V) | $f$ (GHz) | $P_{sw}$ ($\mu$W) | Reduction vs I |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | 1.0 | 1.0 | **60.00** | Baseline |
| II | 0.8 | 1.0 | **38.40** | 36% saving |
| III | 0.8 | 0.8 | **30.72** | **48.8% saving** |

**Comment:**
- Going from Scenario I → II: Only voltage scaling. Power drops by 36% (because $V_{DD}^2$ changes by factor $0.64$).
- Going from Scenario II → III: Both voltage AND frequency scaling. Power drops further to 30.72 $\mu$W.
- **Scenario III is the best**, achieving nearly **49% power saving** vs Scenario I.
- This illustrates the principle of **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** used in modern mobile processors.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$:** Many students write $P_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ without including $\alpha$. This is wrong unless $\alpha = 1$ is specified. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Confusing energy stored vs energy dissipated:** Some students write energy stored = $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ (incorrect). The correct answer is **stored = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$**, and **dissipated = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ per transistor**. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Unit errors:** Writing $C_L$ in fF without converting to Farads before substituting into the formula. Always convert: $1 \text{ fF} = 10^{-15} \text{ F}$. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Saying "short-circuit power is zero":** In a real CMOS inverter, both PMOS and NMOS are ON briefly during input transitions, contributing $P_{sc}$. Mention it for full marks. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Stating that energy lost depends on rise time:** This is a trap. Energy lost = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ in each transistor is independent of rise time. Only the **peak current** depends on rise time, not the energy. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Ignoring activity factor definition:** When asked "what is $\alpha$?", students sometimes write "transition frequency" — incorrect. It is the **ratio** of transitions to clock cycles.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$
- ✅ **Energy per cycle:** $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ (entirely dissipated, not stored)
- ✅ **Stored energy on $C_L$:** $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$
- ✅ **Half-energy rule:** Exactly half of $E_{VDD}$ is dissipated as heat in PMOS; the other half is stored.
- ✅ **Stored energy is fully dissipated in NMOS during discharge.**
- ✅ **$V_{DD}^2$ dependence** makes voltage scaling the most powerful power-saving technique.
- ✅ **Activity factor $\alpha \in [0, 1]$** — $\alpha = 1$ for clock signals (hence high power in clock trees).
- ✅ **Switching power is dominant (50–70%)** of total CMOS power.
- ✅ **Independent of transistor ON resistance** — depends only on $C_L$, $V_{DD}$, $\alpha$, $f$.
- ✅ **DVFS** combines voltage + frequency scaling for maximum energy efficiency.
- ✅ **Clock gating, power gating** reduce $\alpha$ to near zero for idle blocks.
- ✅ **Short-circuit power** is a minor secondary term, contributing 5–15% in typical designs.
- ✅ **Average supply current:** $I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f$
- ✅ **The derivation** uses energy conservation and basic capacitor charging — no transistor-level model needed.
- ✅ **KTU board favourite:** "Show that exactly half the energy is dissipated" — derive it cleanly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Activity and Its Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switching Activity and Its Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switching Activity (α):** In CMOS digital VLSI design, switching activity is the *probability* or *normalized rate* at which a logic node (gate output, wire, or signal line) undergoes a logical transition (0 → 1 or 1 → 0) within a given clock cycle. It is a **dimensionless quantity** lying in the closed interval **[0, 1]**, where 0 represents a perfectly static signal and 1 represents a transition every clock cycle.

In formal probabilistic terms, switching activity is the **transition density** of a binary random process $x(t)$:

$$\alpha_x = D(x) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{n_x(T)}{2T}$$

where $n_x(T)$ is the total number of transitions (both rising and falling) of signal $x$ in observation time $T$.

Switching activity is the **single most important behavioral parameter** in low-power VLSI design because dynamic power dissipation in CMOS is **directly proportional to it**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **bank of 100 electric switches** in a factory. Each switch can be ON or OFF. *Switching activity* is essentially the **fraction of switches that get flipped every hour**.

* If the factory lights stay ON continuously → switching activity ≈ 0 (no flipping).
* If the security guard flips each switch once every hour → switching activity ≈ 1 (full flipping).
* In real CMOS chips, gates flip depending on **input data patterns** (e.g., a 32-bit adder toggles differently for `0000…0001` versus `0101…0101`).

**The electricity bill of the factory is proportional to how many switches flip per hour** — exactly the same way a CMOS chip's dynamic power is proportional to how many internal nodes flip per clock cycle.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Deep Sub-micron CMOS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching Activity Factor** | $\alpha$ | **0.05 – 0.20** (typical); 0.5 (max for random data) |
| **Transition Density** | $D(x)$ | transitions/sec |
| **Static Probability (Signal Probability)** | $P(x=1)$ | 0 ≤ P ≤ 1 |
| **Static Power Component** | $P_{static}$ | dominated by sub-threshold leakage |
| **Supply Voltage** | $V_{DD}$ | **0.7 V – 1.2 V** (modern nodes) |
| **Clock Frequency** | $f_{clk}$ | **100 MHz – 5 GHz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Low-Power CMOS:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ saves power *quadratically* (since $P \propto V_{DD}^{2}$), but reducing switching activity $\alpha$ saves power *linearly*. Hence, $\alpha$-reduction techniques (clock gating, bus encoding, operand isolation) are the first line of attack before resorting to voltage scaling.

---

## 1.4 Why Switching Activity Matters — The Three Effects

> [!NOTE]
> Switching activity has three primary consequences in CMOS circuits:
>
> 1. **Dynamic Power Dissipation** — Each transition charges/discharges the load capacitance through the supply rail.
> 2. **Short-Circuit Power Dissipation** — During the finite rise/fall time of inputs, both PMOS and NMOS conduct momentarily, creating a direct $V_{DD} \to GND$ current.
> 3. **Glitching (Hazards) Power Dissipation** — Unnecessary transitions caused by unbalanced gate delays before the signal stabilizes to its correct value.
>
> Secondary effects include power-supply noise (IR drop, L·di/dt), coupling noise, electromigration, and reliability degradation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Power Dissipation in CMOS

Total power dissipation in a CMOS gate is mathematically decomposed as:

$$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{short\text{-}circuit} + P_{leakage} + P_{glitching}$$

### 2.1.1 Dynamic (Switching) Power — The Dominant Component

The dynamic power dissipated in charging and discharging a load capacitance $C_L$ once per clock cycle is:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

* **Step 1 — Capacitive Charge Storage:** Every time a gate output transitions from 0 → 1, the load capacitance $C_L$ draws charge $\Delta Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$ from the supply rail.
* **Step 2 — Energy Per Transition:** Energy drawn from supply = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$. Half of this energy is stored on the capacitor; the other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor.
* **Step 3 — Discharge Phase (1 → 0):** When the output falls, the stored charge is dumped to ground through the NMOS. No energy is drawn from $V_{DD}$ in this half-cycle, but the stored energy $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated in the NMOS.
* **Step 4 — Net Energy Per Full Cycle (0→1→0):** Total energy dissipated = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$.
* **Step 5 — Activity Scaling:** If only a fraction $\alpha$ of clock cycles actually see a transition, multiply by $\alpha$.
* **Step 6 — Rate Multiplication:** If $f_{clk}$ clock cycles occur per second, multiply by $f_{clk}$ to obtain average power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The $V_{DD}^{2}$ dependence makes voltage scaling far more powerful than activity scaling. However, reducing $\alpha$ is "free" — it requires no transistor resizing or threshold-voltage tuning.

### 2.1.2 Short-Circuit Power

During the finite rise/fall time of the input signal, both PMOS and NMOS networks of a CMOS gate are simultaneously ON for a short duration, creating a direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND:

$$P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{T})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk}$$

where $\beta$ is the transconductance parameter and $\tau$ is the input rise/fall time. For well-designed gates ($\tau \ll$ gate delay), $P_{sc} < 10\%$ of $P_{dynamic}$.

### 2.1.3 Glitching Power (Dynamic Hazards)

A **glitch** is an unwanted, spurious transition that occurs at a gate output before it settles to its correct logic value. Glitches occur because of unequal propagation delays along reconvergent paths in a combinational network.

Each glitch is a real 0→1 or 1→0 transition — and therefore **consumes real switching power**. Studies (e.g., on 16-bit adders) show glitches can contribute **20%–70%** of total dynamic power.

### 2.1.4 Static (Leakage) Power

$$P_{leakage} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot (I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{BTBT})$$

This component is **independent of switching activity** but becomes dominant in sub-90 nm technologies due to sub-threshold conduction.

---

## 2.2 Switching Activity of Basic Logic Gates

For input signal probabilities $P(A=1) = p_a$ and $P(B=1) = p_b$ (assuming spatial and temporal independence):

| Gate | Output Signal Probability | Switching Activity $\alpha = 2 P_0 P_1$ |
|---|---|---|
| **NOT** | $P(\bar{A}) = 1 - p_a$ | $2 p_a (1 - p_a)$ |
| **AND** | $P(A \cdot B) = p_a p_b$ | $2 p_a p_b (1 - p_a p_b)$ |
| **OR** | $P(A + B) = 1 - (1-p_a)(1-p_b)$ | $2 [1 - (1-p_a)(1-p_b)] (1-p_a)(1-p_b)$ |
| **XOR** | $P(A \oplus B) = p_a + p_b - 2 p_a p_b$ | $2 [p_a + p_b - 2p_a p_b] [1 - p_a - p_b + 2 p_a p_b]$ |
| **NAND** | $P = 1 - p_a p_b$ | $2 p_a p_b (1 - p_a p_b)$ *(identical to AND)* |
| **Buffer** | $P = p_a$ | $2 p_a (1 - p_a)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Max switching activity** for any gate occurs when input probability $p = 0.5$ (random data). At $p = 0.5$, the switching activity **peaks at $\alpha = 0.5$** per output node per clock cycle (since $\alpha = 2 p(1-p)$ is maximized at $p = 0.5$).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on switching activity.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f_{clk}$ | Dynamic switching power | Watts |
| 2 | $\alpha = 2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)$ | Switching activity (single wire, random data) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy dissipated per second (per node) | Joules/sec |
| 4 | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_{d}$ | Power-Delay Product (figure of merit) | Joules |
| 5 | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_{d}$ | Energy-Delay Product | Joules·sec |
| 6 | $I_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f$ | Average dynamic current | Amps |
| 7 | $P_{sc} = \tfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD}-2V_T)^3 \tau f$ | Short-circuit power | Watts |
| 8 | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ | Static (leakage) power | Watts |
| 9 | $D(x) = \lim_{T\to\infty} \tfrac{n_x(T)}{2T}$ | Transition density definition | transitions/sec |
| 10 | $\tau = R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | RC delay (Elmore approximation) | seconds |
| 11 | $\mathrm{glitch}\% = \tfrac{P_{glitch}}{P_{dyn}} \times 100$ | Fractional glitch power contribution | % |
| 12 | $P_{avg}(n) = \alpha_n C_n V_{DD}^2 f$ | Aggregated power of $n$ nodes | Watts |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters in Industry

| Application Area | Relevance of Switching Activity |
|---|---|
| **Mobile SoC Design (Apple M-series, Snapdragon)** | Activity-driven power gating and clock gating cut dynamic power by 30–60%. |
| **AI Accelerators (TPU, GPUs)** | Massive data movement → switching activity dominates; HBM encoding schemes target $\alpha$. |
| **IoT / Wearables** | Ultra-low duty-cycle operation means $\alpha \to 0$; leakage dominates instead. |
| **Automotive ECU** | Functional safety (ISO 26262) demands bounded switching noise for EMI/EMC compliance. |
| **FPGA Routing** | Activity-aware placer reduces dynamic power by 15–25%. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Tip:** In a modern 7 nm smartphone SoC, the **clock network** alone can consume 30–40% of total dynamic power because it switches every cycle ($\alpha_{clk} = 1$). This is why *clock gating* is the single most deployed low-power technique in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Dynamic Switching Power Equation

**Goal:** Starting from first principles (charge transfer), derive $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$.

### Step 1: Charge Transfer During a Single Transition (0 → 1)

When the output node of a CMOS gate switches from logic 0 to logic 1, the load capacitance $C_L$ charges from $0\,\text{V}$ to $V_{DD}$. The total charge supplied by the $V_{DD}$ rail is:

$$Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

*Textual note:* By definition of capacitance $C = Q/V$, solving for charge on a fully charged capacitor gives $Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$.

### Step 2: Energy Drawn from the Supply Rail

The energy drawn from $V_{DD}$ to perform this charge transfer is the product of charge and voltage:

$$E_{supply} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

*Textual note:* Energy = Charge × Voltage. This is the total energy delivered by the battery/supply during the 0 → 1 transition.

### Step 3: Energy Stored on the Capacitor

Of the energy delivered, half is stored as electrostatic energy in the capacitor:

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

*Textual note:* The energy stored in a capacitor is $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$. The "missing" half is dissipated as heat in the ON-resistance of the PMOS transistor during charging.

### Step 4: Energy Dissipated During Discharge (1 → 0)

When the output falls from $V_{DD}$ to 0, the stored energy is dumped to ground through the NMOS. This stored energy is **completely dissipated** as heat:

$$E_{discharge} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

*Textual note:* The capacitor does not return energy to $V_{DD}$ — it dumps it to ground. Hence, all stored energy is lost as heat.

### Step 5: Total Energy Per Full Switching Cycle (0 → 1 → 0)

Adding the energy dissipated during charging (PMOS) and discharging (NMOS):

$$E_{cycle} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}}_{\text{PMOS heat}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}}_{\text{NMOS heat}} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### Step 6: Introducing the Switching Activity Factor $\alpha$

In real circuits, a node does not necessarily switch every cycle. Let $\alpha$ be the average fraction of cycles in which a transition occurs. The effective energy per cycle becomes:

$$E_{eff} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

### Step 7: Converting Energy per Cycle to Power (Rate Multiplication)

If $f_{clk}$ clock cycles occur per second, the average power dissipated is:

$$P_{dyn} = E_{eff} \cdot f_{clk} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f_{clk}\;}$$

**Valued by KTU Examiners as a *full-marks derivation* — commit each step to memory.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Switching Activity for Random Binary Data

**Goal:** Show that for a uniformly random binary stream, the switching activity per cycle is $\alpha = 2p(1-p)$ and is maximized at $p = 0.5$.

### Step 1: Define Signal Probability

Let $p$ = probability that the signal is logic 1 in any given cycle. Then:

$$P(x = 1) = p, \quad P(x = 0) = 1 - p$$

### Step 2: Compute Transition Probabilities

For a transition to occur, the signal must change state between two consecutive cycles. Define:

$$P(0 \to 1) = (1-p) \cdot p$$
$$P(1 \to 0) = p \cdot (1-p)$$

*Textual note:* These follow from the assumption of temporal independence — the value in cycle $n+1$ is independent of the value in cycle $n$.

### Step 3: Total Switching Probability (Any Transition)

The probability that *any* transition occurs in a cycle is the sum of both directions:

$$\alpha = P(0 \to 1) + P(1 \to 0) = (1-p)p + p(1-p)$$

### Step 4: Simplify Algebraically

$$\alpha = 2p(1-p) = 2p - 2p^{2}$$

### Step 5: Find the Maximum via Calculus

To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to $p$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dp} = 2 - 4p = 0 \implies p = 0.5$$

### Step 6: Substitute Back to Get the Maximum Switching Activity

$$\alpha_{max} = 2(0.5)(0.5) = 0.5$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{max} = 0.5 \text{ per node per clock cycle (for random data)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even for *completely random* data, a single node transitions only **half the cycles** on average. This means **50% is the absolute upper bound** of switching activity for any single CMOS node.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Switching Activity of a 2-Input AND Gate

**Goal:** Compute $\alpha_{AND}$ given $P(A=1) = p_a$ and $P(B=1) = p_b$, assuming spatial independence.

### Step 1: Output Signal Probability

The output of an AND gate is logic 1 only when **both** inputs are 1:

$$P(Y=1) = P(A=1) \cdot P(B=1) = p_a \, p_b$$

### Step 2: Complement Probability

$$P(Y=0) = 1 - p_a p_b$$

### Step 3: Apply the Switching Activity Formula

Using $\alpha = 2 P_0 P_1$:

$$\alpha_{AND} = 2 \cdot P(Y=0) \cdot P(Y=1)$$

$$\alpha_{AND} = 2 \cdot (1 - p_a p_b) \cdot (p_a p_b)$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{AND} = 2 \, p_a \, p_b \, (1 - p_a p_b)\;}$$

### Step 4: Verification — Equal Probabilities Case ($p_a = p_b = p$)

$$\alpha_{AND} = 2 p^{2} (1 - p^{2})$$

At $p = 0.5$: $\alpha_{AND} = 2 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.75 = 0.375$. This is *less than* the 0.5 maximum of a single node because AND gates inherently suppress activity (output is rarely 1).

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Glitch Power Contribution

**Goal:** Show that glitch power can exceed the "useful" transition power in unbalanced combinational logic.

### Step 1: Define Glitch Activity

Let $\alpha_{glitch}$ be the fraction of clock cycles in which one or more glitches occur at a gate output. Total effective activity is:

$$\alpha_{eff} = \alpha_{useful} + \alpha_{glitch}$$

### Step 2: Glitch Power Expression

$$P_{glitch} = \alpha_{glitch} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example (Carry Chain in a Ripple-Carry Adder)

Consider a 16-bit ripple-carry adder. The carry path propagates from bit 0 to bit 15, taking ~16 gate delays. For input transitions like `0111…1111 → 1000…0000`, the carry must ripple through every stage, generating glitches at intermediate sums.

Empirical studies show $\alpha_{glitch}/\alpha_{useful}$ ratios of:

* 0.3 – 0.5 for **ripple-carry adders**
* 0.05 – 0.1 for **carry-lookahead adders** (balanced delay tree)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Glitch power is a *structural* property of the logic network. Balanced-tree synthesis (e.g., carry-select, CLA, prefix adders) drastically reduces glitches and hence switching activity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of Switching Activity Through a CMOS Pipeline

The diagram below traces how switching activity originates at the inputs, propagates through combinational logic, and produces dynamic power dissipation at every capacitive node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Primary Inputs"] --> nodeB["Input Register Latch"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Combinational Logic Block"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Output Register Latch"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Primary Outputs"]

    nodeF["Clock Network"] --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeD

    nodeG["VDD Supply Rail"] --> nodeC
    nodeH["Ground Rail"] --> nodeC

    nodeC --> nodeI["Load Capacitance CL per Gate"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Charge Current During 0 to 1"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Discharge Current During 1 to 0"]

    nodeJ --> nodeL["Dynamic Power Pdyn"]
    nodeK --> nodeL

    nodeC --> nodeM["Glitch Generation due to Path Delay Mismatch"]
    nodeM --> nodeL

    nodeL --> nodeN["Total Chip Power Budget"]

    style nodeL fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    style nodeM fill:#ef476f,stroke:#9d0208,color:#fff
    style nodeN fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#023e3a,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

* Every logic block has a **load capacitance** $C_L$ (intrinsic + interconnect + fan-out).
* When inputs toggle, charge/discharge currents flow between $V_{DD}$, the internal nodes, and ground.
* Mismatched path delays in `Combinational Logic Block` produce **glitches** — spurious transitions that consume power without doing useful computation.
* The summation of all node-level powers yields the **total chip power budget**.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Classification of Power Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Total CMOS Power Dissipation Ptotal"]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1{"Signal is switching?"}

    nodeQ1 -- "Yes, alpha greater than 0" --> nodePdyn["Dynamic Switching Power: alpha CL VDD squared f"]
    nodeQ1 -- "No, static node" --> nodePleak["Static Leakage Power: VDD times Ileak"]

    nodePdyn --> nodeQ2{"Glitches present?"}
    nodeQ2 -- "Yes" --> nodeGlitch["Glitch Power Pglitch"]
    nodeQ2 -- "No" --> nodeUseful["Useful Switching Power Puseful"]

    nodePdyn --> nodeQ3{"Finite input rise time?"}
    nodeQ3 -- "Yes" --> nodeSC["Short Circuit Power Psc"]
    nodeQ3 -- "No, ideal step" --> nodeUseful

    nodePleak --> nodeSub["Subthreshold Leakage Isub"]
    nodePleak --> nodeGate["Gate Leakage Igate"]
    nodePleak --> nodeBTBT["Band to Band Tunneling IBTBT"]

    style nodeStart fill:#118ab2,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff
    style nodePdyn fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    style nodeGlitch fill:#ef476f,stroke:#9d0208,color:#fff
    style nodeSC fill:#f78c6b,stroke:#9d4a3a,color:#000
    style nodePleak fill:#073b4c,stroke:#118ab2,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Switching Activity Propagation Through Gate Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInA["Input A, Pa equals 0.5"] --> nodeAND["AND Gate"]
    nodeInB["Input B, Pb equals 0.5"] --> nodeAND
    nodeInC["Input C, Pc equals 0.5"] --> nodeOR["OR Gate"]
    nodeInD["Input D, Pd equals 0.5"] --> nodeOR

    nodeAND --> nodeOut1["Y equals A AND B, alpha equals 0.375"]
    nodeOR --> nodeOut2["Z equals C OR D, alpha equals 0.375"]

    nodeOut1 --> nodeNext["Next Stage Logic"]
    nodeOut2 --> nodeNext

    style nodeAND fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#023e3a,color:#000
    style nodeOR fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#023e3a,color:#000
    style nodeOut1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    style nodeOut2 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Even though the inputs to both gates have maximum possible switching activity ($\alpha_{in} = 0.5$), the gate outputs have $\alpha_{out} = 0.375$. This demonstrates that **logic gates inherently filter switching activity**, especially AND/OR/NAND/NOR gates. XOR gates, in contrast, *amplify* activity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT741]**
**Define switching activity. Why is it the most important behavioral parameter for dynamic power estimation in CMOS circuits?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Switching activity $\alpha$ of a CMOS node is the probability that the node undergoes a logic transition (0 → 1 or 1 → 0) in a given clock cycle. It lies in the range [0, 1].
>
> It is the most important behavioral parameter because the dynamic (switching) power of a CMOS gate is given by $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f_{clk}$, which is **directly and linearly proportional** to $\alpha$. Unlike $V_{DD}$ or $f_{clk}$, $\alpha$ is a property of the input data and the logic structure — it can be reduced "for free" without resizing transistors.
>
> **Mark split:** Definition (1) + Role in $P_{dyn}$ equation (1) + Justification of importance (1).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT741]**
**Distinguish between *signal probability* and *switching activity*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signal Probability $P(x=1)$ | Switching Activity $\alpha_x$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Probability that node $x$ is logic 1 at a given instant | Probability that node $x$ transitions per cycle |
| **Range** | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 0.5]$ (max 0.5 for random data) |
| **Depends on** | Static combinational behaviour | Both combinational behaviour **and** temporal correlation |
| **Formula** | $P(x=1) = p$ | $\alpha = 2 p (1-p)$ for independent streams |
| **Use** | DC analysis, leakage estimation | Dynamic power estimation |

**Mark split:** Two definitions (1) + Tabular contrast (2).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **ONE question of 14 marks** from Module 2 internal choice (Q9 or Q10). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT741]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the dynamic switching power equation $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f$ starting from the principle of charge transfer on a load capacitance.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 4-bit ripple-carry adder is implemented in a 0.18 µm CMOS process. The output load capacitance per full-adder is $C_L = 50\,\text{fF}$. The supply voltage is $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$, and the clock frequency is $f = 200\,\text{MHz}$. The average switching activity measured across the four sum outputs and four carry outputs is $\alpha = 0.25$. Estimate the total dynamic power dissipated by the adder.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define load capacitance $C_L$ on the gate output node.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** During a 0 → 1 transition, charge supplied from $V_{DD}$ is $Q = C_L V_{DD}$, and energy drawn is $E = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$. Of this, $\tfrac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is stored and $\tfrac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** During the subsequent 1 → 0 transition, the stored energy $\tfrac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated in the NMOS. Total energy per full cycle = $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Introduce switching activity factor $\alpha$ to account for non-switching cycles.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Multiply by clock frequency $f$ to obtain average power $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$.

**Examiner Tip:** The student **must explicitly state** the role of $\alpha$ as a probability (not a ratio of events). Use energy balance between charging/discharging phases — this is the most commonly missed step.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data (1 mark):**
* Number of switching nodes = 4 sum + 4 carry = $N = 8$
* $C_L = 50 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$
* $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$
* $f = 200 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$
* $\alpha = 0.25$

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$P_{dyn} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$P_{dyn} = 8 \times 0.25 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.8)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{6})$$

**Intermediate Calculations (2 marks):**

$$P_{dyn} = 2.0 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \times 200 \times 10^{6}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 2.0 \times 3.24 \times 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Final Result (1 mark):**

$$P_{dyn} = 64.8 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W} = 64.8\,\mu\text{W}$$

**Answer:** The 4-bit ripple-carry adder dissipates approximately **64.8 µW** of dynamic power.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT741]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define *transition density*. Explain how it differs from switching activity, and derive the relationship $\alpha = 2p(1-p)$ for a random binary stream with signal probability $p$.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain how *glitches* originate in combinational CMOS logic. Discuss two techniques to reduce glitch-induced switching activity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
Transition density $D(x)$ of a binary signal $x(t)$ is the *average number of transitions per unit time*:

$$D(x) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{n_x(T)}{2T}$$

The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for the fact that $n_x(T)$ counts **both** rising and falling transitions.

**Difference from Switching Activity (2 marks):**
* $D(x)$ has units of *transitions per second* (a rate).
* $\alpha$ is *dimensionless* (a probability per cycle).
* $D(x) = \alpha \cdot f_{clk}$ when transitions are uniformly distributed in time.

**Derivation of $\alpha = 2p(1-p)$ (3 marks):**

Assuming temporal independence between consecutive cycles:

$$P(0 \to 1) = (1-p) \cdot p$$
$$P(1 \to 0) = p \cdot (1-p)$$

$$\alpha = P(0 \to 1) + P(1 \to 0) = 2p(1-p)$$

Setting $\tfrac{d\alpha}{dp} = 2 - 4p = 0$ yields the maximum at $p = 0.5$, where $\alpha_{max} = 0.5$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Glitch Origin (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Input X Transitioning at t0"] --> nodePath1["Short Path: Gate Delay equals 1 ns"]
    nodeIn --> nodePath2["Long Path: Gate Delay equals 5 ns"]
    nodePath1 --> nodeOR["OR Gate Output"]
    nodePath2 --> nodeOR
    nodeOR --> nodeGlitch["Spurious Transition Glitch before Steady State"]
```

In combinational logic with **unbalanced reconvergent paths**, an input transition propagates via faster paths and momentarily sets the output to an intermediate, incorrect value before the slower-path signal arrives and corrects it. Each such spurious transition is a **glitch** that consumes full dynamic power.

**Techniques to Reduce Glitch Power (4 marks — 2 each):**

1. **Balanced-Path Synthesis (Delay Equalization):** Insert buffers along shorter paths so that all reconvergent paths have nearly equal delay. Common in CLA, prefix adders, and balanced carry-select adders.

2. **Hazard Filtering (Glitch Masking):** Add a latch or register at the output of glitch-prone combinational logic, clocked to capture only the steady-state value. This is also known as *pulse-suppression filtering*.

(Other acceptable answers: gate freezing, transistor sizing for equal rise/fall, retiming, multi-Vt synthesis.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Switching-Activity Questions (Examiner's Eye):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 2 in $\alpha = 2p(1-p)$.** Many students write $\alpha = p(1-p)$, losing 1 mark instantly. **Always state** that $\alpha$ counts both rising and falling transitions.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ with frequency $f$.** They have different units. Dynamic power depends on *both*: $P_{dyn} \propto \alpha \cdot f$.
>
> 3. **Treating glitches as "free" transitions.** Examiners explicitly deduct marks if students say glitches "don't consume power." Glitches *do* consume power — they are real 0→1→0 sequences.
>
> 4. **Neglecting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in stored energy.** A correct derivation must state that *half* the energy from the supply is stored on $C_L$, while the *other half* is dissipated in the PMOS during charging.
>
> 5. **Using signal probability instead of switching activity in the $P_{dyn}$ formula.** $P(x=1)$ is *not* the same as $\alpha$. A common error: writing $P_{dyn} = p \, C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$.
>
> 6. **Wrong units in numerical problems.** Keep $C_L$ in farads (not pF without conversion), $f$ in Hz, and $V_{DD}$ in volts.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention temporal independence** when deriving $\alpha = 2p(1-p)$. This assumption must be explicitly stated for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before stepping into the exam hall, ensure you can confidently state/explain each of the following:**

* [ ] Switching activity $\alpha$ is the **probability of a transition per clock cycle**; it is **dimensionless** and lies in $[0, 0.5]$ for a single node.
* [ ] The dynamic power equation $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — be ready to **derive it from charge-transfer principles** in 6–7 steps.
* [ ] The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in stored energy on a capacitor: $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
* [ ] For random binary data, $\alpha = 2p(1-p)$, **maximized at $p = 0.5$** with $\alpha_{max} = 0.5$.
* [ ] AND/NAND/OR/NOR gates **suppress** switching activity; XOR/XNOR gates **preserve or amplify** it.
* [ ] Glitches are *spurious transitions* caused by **path-delay mismatch**; they can contribute 20–70% of total dynamic power.
* [ ] Transition density $D(x)$ has units of *transitions/sec*, whereas switching activity is *dimensionless*.
* [ ] Short-circuit power occurs during the **finite rise/fall time** of inputs when both PMOS and NMOS conduct.
* [ ] Static (leakage) power is **independent** of switching activity but dominates in deep sub-micron nodes.
* [ ] **Reduction techniques:** Clock gating ($\downarrow\alpha$), voltage scaling ($\downarrow V_{DD}^{2}$), operand isolation, balanced-tree synthesis, glitch-masking latches, bus encoding (invert, gray, T0).
* [ ] Industry rule of thumb: clock network consumes **30–40%** of total dynamic power because $\alpha_{clk} = 1$.
* [ ] **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** = $P \cdot t_d$ is the standard figure of merit for energy efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Exam Mantra:** *"In CMOS, dynamic power is the product of how often a node flips ($\alpha$), how big its capacitor is ($C_L$), how high the rail is ($V_{DD}^{2}$), and how fast we run ($f$). Of these, switching activity is the only one we can attack algorithmically — without changing the silicon."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glitching Power: Glitches and Its Effect on Power Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Glitching Power: Glitches and Its Effect on Power Dissipation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Glitching Power (P_glitch):** The component of dynamic switching power dissipated in a CMOS gate due to **unwanted, spurious (false) transitions** at the gate's output, which occur *before* the output settles to its final, correct, steady-state logic value.
