These unwanted transitions are called **glitches** (also referred to as **dynamic hazards** or **spurious transitions**). In an ideal digital CMOS circuit, the output of any gate should transition **exactly once** for any single change at its inputs. However, due to **finite and unequal propagation delays** through the reconvergent paths of a multi-level logic network, intermediate nodes may toggle multiple times before stabilizing. Each of these *extra* transitions consumes energy from $V_{DD}$ and dissipates it as heat — this wasted energy constitutes the **glitching power**. Mathematically, the total dynamic power can be decomposed as: $$P_{dynamic} = P_{functional} + P_{glitching}$$ where: - $P_{functional}$ = power consumed due to the *intended* single transition per input change. - $P_{glitching}$ = power consumed due to the *unintended* spurious transitions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In deep-submicron and nanometer CMOS designs, glitching power can account for **20% to 70%** of the total dynamic power, making it a critical topic in low-power VLSI design. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Office Water Cooler** > Imagine an office with one water tap and three workers in series, each filling a cup before passing the bottle. The first worker (Gate A) is fast, the second (Gate B) is slow, and the third (Gate C) is medium. If all three try to drink at once, the bottle will move back and forth multiple times — open, close, open again — before reaching the final state (empty or full). Each back-and-forth "wasted movement" is a **glitch**. Energy (electricity) is spent in moving the bottle, even though the *net* result is just one full drink. In CMOS terms: - The **water bottle** is the electrical charge at the output node. - The **workers** are the gates in the propagation path. - The **wasted back-and-forth** represents charge flowing into and out of the load capacitance multiple times. - The **final stable state** is the correct logic value. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **$V_{DD}$** → Supply voltage (typically **0.7 V – 1.2 V** in modern nanometer nodes). - **$C_L$** → Load capacitance at the glitching node. - **$\alpha_{glitch}$** → Activity factor of glitching transitions (extra transitions per clock cycle). - **$f_{clk}$** → Clock frequency. - **$E_{glitch}$** → Energy dissipated per glitch transition = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** Glitching power is often reported as a **percentage of total dynamic power**, i.e., $\%P_{glitch} = \frac{P_{glitch}}{P_{dynamic}} \times 100\%$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of Glitches — Operational Breakdown Glitches arise in **combinational logic networks** when there exist **multiple input signal paths** with **unequal propagation delays** that reconverge at a downstream gate. The mechanism can be broken into the following logical steps: - **Step 1 — Multiple Signal Paths:** The output of a gate $G$ depends on two or more input variables that travel through *different* internal transistor paths or *different* gate-level routes. - **Step 2 — Delay Mismatch:** The propagation delay along Path-1 ($t_{p1}$) differs from that along Path-2 ($t_{p2}$). This mismatch is intrinsic because the number of transistors, fan-out, and wire capacitance differ per path. - **Step 3 — Reconvergence at a Downstream Gate:** The two paths *converge* at a logic gate (commonly a NAND, AND, OR, or XOR). When the inputs arrive at *different times*, the gate's output may momentarily assume an incorrect value. - **Step 4 — Spurious Output Transition:** The gate output flips to the wrong logic level, and after the *slower* input finally arrives, the output flips *back* to the correct value. This double-flip is a **glitch pulse**. - **Step 5 — Capacitive Charging/Discharging:** Each glitch transition charges or discharges the load capacitance $C_L$ of the next stage, consuming energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ per transition. - **Step 6 — Propagation Forward:** The glitch pulse itself propagates downstream, potentially creating *secondary* glitches (a phenomenon called **glitch propagation** or **glitch masking** depending on the receiving gate's threshold). - **Step 7 — Power Waste:** All these extra transitions waste dynamic power with *no useful computational benefit*. --- ## 2.2 Classification of Hazards (Glitch Variants) | Hazard Type | Triggering Condition | Boolean Condition for Output | Common Remediation | |-------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------| | **Static-1 Hazard** | Output should remain at logic 1 | $\bar{x} \cdot f_1 + x \cdot f_2 = 1$ for all $x$ | Add redundant consensus term | | **Static-0 Hazard** | Output should remain at logic 0 | $(\bar{x} + f_1)(x + f_2) = 0$ for all $x$ | Add redundant consensus term | | **Dynamic Hazard** | Output *should* change once, but changes multiple times | Multiple variable transitions | Balanced path delays | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Note:** Static hazards do **not** consume dynamic power if the output remains stable. Only **dynamic hazards** and the *transient* behavior of static hazards contribute to glitching power. --- ## 2.3 Conditions Required for Glitch Generation For a glitch to manifest, **all** of the following conditions must be satisfied simultaneously: - **Condition A — Multiple Inputs:** The downstream gate has at least **2 inputs** that change. - **Condition B — Different Path Delays:** The inputs arrive at the gate at *different* times. - **Condition C — Boolean Sensitivity:** At least one input transition causes the output to *try* to change (the gate is not "masking" the input change). - **Condition D — Non-overlapping Switching:** The input transitions must be *separated in time* (not perfectly simultaneous). If any one of these conditions is violated, the glitch is **masked** (suppressed). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $E_{transition} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy of one ideal transition | Joules | | 2 | $E_{glitch} = N_g \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per glitch (with $N_g$ = number of glitch pulses) | Joules | | 3 | $P_{glitch} = \alpha_{glitch} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Glitching power | Watts | | 4 | $\alpha_{total} = \alpha_{func} + \alpha_{glitch}$ | Total activity factor | dimensionless | | 5 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha_{total} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Total dynamic power | Watts | | 6 | $\%P_{glitch} = \frac{\alpha_{glitch}}{\alpha_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Glitch power fraction | % | | 7 | $\beta = \frac{N_{glitch}}{N_{transition}}$ | Glitching balance factor (extra transitions per useful transition) | dimensionless | | 8 | $P_{glitch} = \beta \cdot P_{functional}$ | Glitch power expressed as a multiple of functional power | Watts | | 9 | $T_{glitch} \approx \vert t_{p1} - t_{p2} \vert$ | Glitch pulse width (approximation) | seconds | | 10 | $C_{eff} = C_{L,node} \cdot (1 + \beta)$ | Effective switched capacitance including glitches | Farads | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** The glitching balance factor $\beta$ in well-designed circuits is in the range **0.05 to 0.3**, but in poorly balanced arithmetic units (e.g., ripple-carry adders), $\beta$ can approach **1.0**, meaning **glitches can double the dynamic power dissipation**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs):** Adders and multipliers are notorious glitch generators; balancing carry paths reduces power by 30–50%. - **FPGA Routing:** FPGA place-and-route tools specifically optimize for **glitch minimization** in the post-route optimization stage. - **Asynchronous Design:** Glitch-free logic families (e.g., dual-rail encoding) are used in ultra-low-power IoT nodes. - **AI Accelerators:** Systolic arrays and MAC units use balanced tree structures to avoid the deep glitches of ripple architectures. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Insight:** Synopsys Power Compiler and Cadence Genus both report glitching power as a *separate* component in their power analysis reports, allowing designers to optimize $\beta$ directly. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Dissipated by a Single Glitch Pulse Consider a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ at its output node. Suppose due to delay mismatch, the input to this inverter toggles **twice** (once incorrectly, once correctly) within one clock period. We compute the energy drawn from $V_{DD}$ during this event. **Step 1 — Define the energy per transition:** When a CMOS gate output switches from logic-0 to logic-1, the load capacitance $C_L$ is charged from $0$ V to $V_{DD}$. The energy stored in the capacitor is: $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ The energy delivered by the supply during this charging event is: $$E_{supply} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute the energy dissipated in the PMOS transistor:** The energy lost as heat in the PMOS during charging is the *difference* between supplied and stored energy: $$E_{dissipated, charge} = E_{supply} - E_{stored}$$ $$E_{dissipated, charge} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ $$E_{dissipated, charge} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 3 — Discharging event (logic-1 to logic-0):** During discharge, the charge stored in $C_L$ flows to ground through the NMOS transistor. The energy stored $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated entirely as heat in the NMOS. The supply does **not** deliver any new energy. $$E_{dissipated, discharge} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 4 — Total energy per single 0→1→0 transition cycle (one full glitch):** $$E_{glitch} = E_{dissipated, charge} + E_{dissipated, discharge}$$ $$E_{glitch} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ $$E_{glitch} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 5 — Generalization for $N_g$ glitch pulses:** For $N_g$ complete glitch pulses per clock cycle, the total glitching energy is: $$E_{glitch, total} = N_g \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A single glitch pulse dissipates **twice** the energy of a useful transition (since it involves both charging and discharging). This is why even a small number of glitches can significantly increase total power. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Glitching Power as a Function of Activity Factor **Step 1 — Define the activity factor:** The activity factor $\alpha$ represents the average number of transitions per clock cycle at a node: $$\alpha = \frac{N_{transitions}}{N_{cycles}}$$ **Step 2 — Decompose $\alpha$ into functional and glitching components:** $$\alpha_{total} = \alpha_{func} + \alpha_{glitch}$$ **Step 3 — Write the power equation using $\alpha_{total}$:** $$P_{dynamic} = \alpha_{total} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and isolate glitching power:** $$P_{dynamic} = (\alpha_{func} + \alpha_{glitch}) \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ $$P_{dynamic} = \alpha_{func} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} + \alpha_{glitch} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the glitching power term:** $$P_{glitch} = \alpha_{glitch} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ $$P_{functional} = \alpha_{func} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** By measuring $\alpha_{total}$ and $\alpha_{func}$ separately using power simulation tools, designers can compute the exact fraction of power wasted as glitches and apply targeted optimizations. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Glitch Pulse Width from Delay Mismatch **Step 1 — Consider two paths reconverging at a 2-input AND gate:** - Input A travels through Path-1 with delay $t_{p1}$. - Input B travels through Path-2 with delay $t_{p2}$. **Step 2 — Define the time-of-arrival difference:** $$\Delta t = \vert t_{p1} - t_{p2} \vert$$ **Step 3 — For a *positive glitch* on an AND gate (A arrives first as 1, B arrives later as 0, then B changes to 1):** The AND output is 1 → 0 → 1, creating a glitch of width: $$T_{glitch} = \Delta t = \vert t_{p1} - t_{p2} \vert$$ **Step 4 — For a *negative glitch* on an OR gate (similar analysis):** $$T_{glitch} = \Delta t = \vert t_{p1} - t_{p2} \vert$$ **Step 5 — Generalize using the inertial delay model of CMOS gates:** A CMOS gate will *not* propagate a glitch whose width is shorter than its **inertial delay** $t_{inertial}$ (typically $\approx 0.5 \cdot t_{p}$ for the gate). The propagation condition is: $$T_{glitch} > t_{inertial, receiver}$$ If $T_{glitch} \leq t_{inertial, receiver}$, the glitch is **absorbed** (masked). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** This is the basis of **glitch masking** in static CMOS — short glitches get filtered by the natural low-pass behavior of the gate's input capacitance. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Glitch Reduction Techniques | Technique | Mechanism | Power Savings | Design Cost | |-----------|-----------|---------------|-------------| | **Path Delay Balancing** | Insert buffers to equalize $t_{p1}$ and $t_{p2}$ | 20%–50% | Area, buffers | | **Hazard-Free Logic Synthesis** | Add consensus terms to Boolean equations | 30%–60% | Gate count | | **Gate Sizing** | Slow down fast paths / speed up slow paths | 10%–30% | None (sizing only) | | **Glitch Filtering / Inertial Delay** | Rely on CMOS gate inertia to filter short glitches | 5%–15% | None | | **Pipelining** | Isolate combinational depth per stage | 30%–70% | Latency, registers | | **Use of Domino/C²MOS with Keeper** | Controlled evaluation reduces glitches | 20%–40% | Complexity | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A 2-input AND gate drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 50$ fF. Due to delay mismatch in the reconvergent paths, the output exhibits **3 glitch pulses per clock cycle** in addition to 1 functional transition. Given $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V and $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, compute: - (i) Glitching power. - (ii) Functional power. - (iii) Total dynamic power. - (iv) Percentage of power wasted as glitches. **Solution:** Given: $C_L = 50 \times 10^{-15}$ F, $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f_{clk} = 500 \times 10^6$ Hz, $N_g = 3$. **Step 1 — Energy per glitch (each glitch = 1 charge + 1 discharge):** $$E_{glitch} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.0)^2$$ $$E_{glitch} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 50 \text{ fJ per glitch}$$ **Step 2 — Energy per functional transition:** $$E_{func} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 25 \text{ fJ}$$ **Step 3 — Energy per clock cycle:** $$E_{cycle} = E_{func} + N_g \cdot E_{glitch} = 25 + 3 \cdot 50 = 25 + 150 = 175 \text{ fJ}$$ **Step 4 — Glitching power:** $$P_{glitch} = N_g \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ $$P_{glitch} = 3 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.0 \cdot 500 \times 10^6$$ $$P_{glitch} = 3 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$ $$P_{glitch} = 75 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Functional power:** $$P_{func} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} = 25 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 500 \times 10^6 = 12.5 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 6 — Total dynamic power:** $$P_{dynamic} = P_{func} + P_{glitch} = 12.5 + 75 = 87.5 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 7 — Glitch power percentage:** $$\%P_{glitch} = \frac{75}{87.5} \times 100\% \approx 85.7\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** In this example, **~86% of the total dynamic power is wasted on glitches**! This vividly demonstrates why glitching power minimization is critical in low-power VLSI design. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define glitching power. What are the primary causes of glitches in CMOS combinational logic?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Glitching power is the component of dynamic switching power dissipated due to unwanted, spurious transitions (glitches) at a gate's output, which occur *before* the output settles to its final correct logic value. > **Causes (1.5 Marks):** Glitches are caused primarily by: > 1. **Unequal propagation delays** along multiple signal paths that reconverge at a downstream gate. > 2. **Multi-input switching** events at a gate whose inputs arrive at different times. > 3. **Boolean hazards** (static-1, static-0, and dynamic hazards) that arise from incomplete hazard-covering in the synthesized logic. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Explain the role of the glitching balance factor $\beta$ in estimating dynamic power dissipation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** The glitching balance factor $\beta$ is defined as the ratio of the number of glitch-induced transitions to the number of functional (useful) transitions at a given node. $$\beta = \frac{N_{glitch}}{N_{functional}}$$ > **Role in Power Estimation (2 Marks):** The balance factor directly scales the effective switched capacitance. The glitching power can be expressed as: > $$P_{glitch} = \beta \cdot P_{functional} = \beta \cdot \alpha_{func} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ > In well-balanced designs, $\beta \approx 0.05$–$0.3$, but in unbalanced arithmetic circuits, $\beta$ can approach $1.0$, effectively doubling the dynamic power. Hence, $\beta$ is a key metric for assessing power quality of a synthesized netlist. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Part B questions on glitching power, students commonly lose marks for: (1) failing to distinguish between *static* and *dynamic* hazards, (2) omitting the energy factor of $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ in derivations, (3) confusing glitching power with short-circuit power, and (4) forgetting to specify units. Always box your final answer and state the assumptions clearly. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the mechanism of generation of glitches in a CMOS combinational logic network. Discuss the conditions under which glitches are masked and not propagated to the next stage. **(7 Marks)** **b)** Derive an expression for the glitching power in a CMOS gate. A 2-input NAND gate drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 100$ fF. Due to path delay mismatch, the output generates **4 glitch pulses per clock cycle** in addition to 1 functional transition. Given $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V and $f_{clk} = 250$ MHz, calculate the total dynamic power dissipated and the percentage of power wasted as glitches. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Mechanism of Glitch Generation (4 Marks):** - **Step 1:** When two or more input signals travel through **different combinational paths** with **unequal propagation delays** and reconverge at a downstream gate, the inputs arrive at the gate at *different instants*. - **Step 2:** If the Boolean function of the reconvergent gate is *sensitive* to these intermediate input combinations, the output momentarily assumes an **incorrect logic level**. - **Step 3:** Once the slower input finally arrives, the output *corrects* itself, producing a **double transition** (glitch pulse) at the output. - **Step 4:** This glitch pulse has a width approximately equal to the **delay mismatch** $\Delta t = \vert t_{p1} - t_{p2} \vert$ and charges/discharges the load capacitance $C_L$ wastefully. **Functional Block Diagram (1.5 Marks):** ``` Input A ──► [Path 1: tp1] ──┐ ├──► [Reconvergent Gate] ──► Glitch Pulse ──► Next Stage Input B ──► [Path 2: tp2] ──┘ ``` **Glitch Masking Conditions (1.5 Marks):** A glitch is **masked** (suppressed) if **any one** of the following holds: 1. The receiving gate's **inertial delay** exceeds the glitch pulse width ($t_{inertial} > T_{glitch}$). 2. The glitch occurs during a **clock-high period** in a clocked (domino) logic gate where the gate is in hold state. 3. The downstream gate is **logically insensitive** to the glitched input transition (e.g., a NAND gate with the other input held at 0). 4. The glitch pulse amplitude is too small to overcome the gate's **noise margin** ($V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Derivation of Glitching Power Expression (3 Marks):** The energy dissipated per single 0→1→0 transition (one glitch pulse) is the sum of charging and discharging energy: $$E_{glitch} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ For $N_g$ glitch pulses per clock cycle: $$E_{glitch, total} = N_g \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$$ The total dynamic power per cycle is the sum of functional and glitching power: $$P_{dynamic} = (N_g + 0.5) \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ **Numerical Calculation (4 Marks):** Given: $C_L = 100$ fF $= 100 \times 10^{-15}$ F, $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f_{clk} = 250 \times 10^6$ Hz, $N_g = 4$. **Step 1 — Compute $V_{DD}^2$:** $$V_{DD}^2 = (1.2)^2 = 1.44 \text{ V}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute energy per functional transition:** $$E_{func} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 0.5 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44$$ $$E_{func} = 72 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 72 \text{ fJ}$$ **Step 3 — Compute energy per glitch pulse:** $$E_{glitch} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 = 144 \text{ fJ}$$ **Step 4 — Compute energy per clock cycle:** $$E_{cycle} = E_{func} + N_g \cdot E_{glitch} = 72 + 4 \cdot 144 = 72 + 576 = 648 \text{ fJ}$$ **Step 5 — Compute total dynamic power:** $$P_{dynamic} = E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk} = 648 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 250 \times 10^6$$ $$P_{dynamic} = 162 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = \boxed{162 \text{ }\mu\text{W}}$$ **Step 6 — Compute glitching power:** $$P_{glitch} = N_g \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} = 4 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 250 \times 10^6$$ $$P_{glitch} = 144 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 144 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 7 — Compute percentage of power wasted as glitches:** $$\%P_{glitch} = \frac{P_{glitch}}{P_{dynamic}} \times 100\% = \frac{144}{162} \times 100\%$$ $$\%P_{glitch} \approx \boxed{88.9\%}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice* **a)** Classify the different types of hazards (static-1, static-0, and dynamic hazards) in combinational logic. For each type, give a representative Boolean expression and explain why it gives rise to a glitch. **(7 Marks)** **b)** With a relevant circuit example, illustrate the technique of **path delay balancing** for glitch power reduction. If a critical path has $t_{p,fast} = 1.5$ ns and $t_{p,slow} = 4.0$ ns, design a buffering strategy using identical buffers (each with intrinsic delay $t_{buf} = 0.5$ ns) to equalize the delays. Estimate the resulting percentage reduction in glitching power. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Classification Table (4 Marks):** | Hazard Type | Boolean Condition | Representative Expression | Glitch Behavior | |-------------|-------------------|---------------------------|-----------------| | **Static-1** | Output should remain at 1 for all input transitions | $F = \bar{A} \cdot C + A \cdot B$ | When $A$ toggles, $F$ momentarily drops to 0, then returns to 1 | | **Static-0** | Output should remain at 0 for all input transitions | $F = (A + C) \cdot (\bar{A} + B)$ | When $A$ toggles, $F$ momentarily rises to 1, then returns to 0 | | **Dynamic** | Output should change once, but changes multiple times | $F = A \cdot \bar{B} + B \cdot C$ (with $A \neq C$) | Output undergoes 0→1→0→1 (or 1→0→1→0) for a single input change | **Explanation (3 Marks):** - A **static-1 hazard** arises when, during a single input transition, the product-of-sums form fails to cover the transition interval. The Boolean expression lacks the **consensus term** $B \cdot C$ (in the example above), which is required to maintain the output at logic-1 throughout the transition. The result is a momentary dip to logic-0, manifesting as a glitch. - A **static-0 hazard** similarly arises from a missing sum-of-products consensus term in the dual expression. - A **dynamic hazard** occurs when **more than one input variable changes** simultaneously *and* the output has both static and dynamic sensitivity, producing multiple spurious transitions. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Circuit Illustration (2 Marks):** Consider an XOR-based comparator feeding an AND gate. Inputs $A$ and $B$ go through different paths: ``` A ──► [XOR Gate] ──► ┐ ├──► [AND Gate] ──► Output (prone to glitch) B ──► [Buffer] ─────► ┘ ``` The XOR path is intrinsically slower than the buffer path, creating a delay mismatch and hence a glitch at the AND output. **Buffering Strategy (3 Marks):** Given: $t_{p,fast} = 1.5$ ns, $t_{p,slow} = 4.0$ ns, $t_{buf} = 0.5$ ns. The delay mismatch is: $$\Delta t = 4.0 - 1.5 = 2.5 \text{ ns}$$ To equalize, we need to add delay to the *fast* path: $$N_{buf} = \frac{\Delta t}{t_{buf}} = \frac{2.5}{0.5} = 5 \text{ buffers}$$ **Verification:** $$t_{p,fast,new} = 1.5 + 5 \cdot 0.5 = 1.5 + 2.5 = 4.0 \text{ ns}$$ $$t_{p,slow,new} = 4.0 \text{ ns}$$ $$\Delta t_{new} = 4.0 - 4.0 = 0 \text{ ns}$$ **Glitch Power Reduction Estimate (2 Marks):** The glitch pulse width $T_{glitch}$ is directly proportional to $\Delta t$, and the glitching energy per pulse is approximately proportional to the integral of the pulse (which scales linearly with $T_{glitch}$ for a triangular pulse). Hence: $$\%P_{glitch, reduction} = \frac{\Delta t_{old} - \Delta t_{new}}{\Delta t_{old}} \times 100\%$$ $$\%P_{glitch, reduction} = \frac{2.5 - 0}{2.5} \times 100\% = \boxed{100\%}$$ In practice, residual mismatches (process, voltage, temperature variations) limit real-world reduction to **85%–95%**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Glitching Power** - ✅ **Definition:** Glitching power = power wasted by spurious (extra) transitions before a gate output stabilizes. - ✅ **Root Cause:** Unequal propagation delays in reconvergent multi-path combinational logic. - ✅ **Glitch Energy per Pulse:** $E_{glitch} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ (twice a useful transition, due to charge + discharge). - ✅ **Glitching Power Formula:** $P_{glitch} = \alpha_{glitch} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$. - ✅ **Balance Factor:** $\beta = N_{glitch} / N_{functional}$; ranges from 0.05 (well-balanced) to 1.0+ (unbalanced arithmetic). - ✅ **Glitch Pulse Width:** $T_{glitch} \approx \vert t_{p1} - t_{p2} \vert$. - ✅ **Glitch Masking:** Occurs when inertial delay of receiver > $T_{glitch}$, or when receiver is logically insensitive, or when noise margin is not crossed. - ✅ **Reduction Techniques:** Path balancing, hazard-free (consensus) logic, gate sizing, pipelining, inertial filtering, and domino keepers. - ✅ **Industry Reality:** Glitching power can be **20%–70%** of total dynamic power in poorly designed circuits; balancing carry-chains in adders and using tree multipliers is a standard mitigation. - ✅ **Examiner's Mantra:** Always quote the formula $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ and clearly distinguish **functional** vs. **glitching** activity factors. - ✅ **Don't Confuse:** Glitching power ≠ Short-circuit power ≠ Leakage power. They are *distinct* components of total CMOS power dissipation.

topics.title: Static Power Dissipation: Sources of Leakage Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Power Dissipation: Sources of Leakage Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Power Dissipation** is the power consumed by a CMOS digital circuit when it is in a *steady state* — i.e., when the circuit is not switching and inputs are held constant. In modern deep-submicron CMOS technologies (≤ 65 nm), static power is predominantly caused by **leakage currents** that flow through the transistor even when it is nominally OFF.

Mathematically, the total static (leakage) power dissipated by a CMOS gate is given by:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$

where $I_{leakage}$ is the sum total of all leakage current components flowing from $V_{DD}$ to ground through the OFF-state transistors.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Leaky Faucet:** Imagine a kitchen faucet that is *closed* (analogous to an OFF transistor). Even when tightly shut, a few drops of water still seep through the rubber seal every minute. You are not actively *using* the water, yet the bucket beneath slowly fills up — this is "wasted" resource. In CMOS, the "OFF" transistor is never perfectly sealed; small currents leak from the supply rail to ground. In millions of transistors on a chip, these drops add up to a substantial power bill, especially in battery-operated devices like smartphones that sit idle 99% of the time.

### 1.3 Why Static Power Became Dominant

Historically (in older 0.5 µm and above technologies), **dynamic power** dominated total chip power consumption. However, due to aggressive technology scaling (Moore's Law driving feature sizes to 7 nm, 5 nm, 3 nm), three trends caused leakage to rise sharply:

1. **Supply voltage ($V_{DD}$) reduction** has been slower than threshold voltage ($V_{th}$) reduction → increased subthreshold leakage.
2. **Gate oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$)** has been reduced to ~1.2 nm → quantum-mechanical **direct tunneling** through the oxide.
3. **Higher electric fields** in short-channel devices → **band-to-band tunneling (BTBT)**.

In modern nanometer CMOS, **static leakage can contribute 30%–50% of the total chip power**, especially in high-performance processors and always-on IoT devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under static power, students must enumerate and mathematically model **six primary leakage mechanisms**: (i) Reverse-biased p-n junction leakage (BTBT), (ii) Subthreshold leakage, (iii) Gate-oxide tunneling leakage, (iv) Gate-Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL), (v) Punchthrough, and (vi) Hot-Carrier Injection related leakage.

### 1.4 Six Sources of Leakage Power — Quick Overview

| # | Leakage Mechanism | Primary Physical Cause | Order of Magnitude (per device) |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Reverse-biased junction leakage (BTBT) | Quantum tunneling across reverse-biased p-n junction | pA–nA |
| 2 | **Subthreshold leakage** | Carrier diffusion with $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | **nA–µA (Dominant)** |
| 3 | Gate-oxide tunneling (Fowler-Nordheim / Direct) | Quantum tunneling through thin $SiO_2$ | nA–µA |
| 4 | Gate-Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL) | High field at drain under gate overlap | pA–nA |
| 5 | Punchthrough | Drain and source depletion regions merge | nA–µA |
| 6 | Hot-Carrier Injection (HCI) | High-energy carriers injected into oxide | pA–nA |

**Subthreshold leakage** is the **most dominant contributor** in standard CMOS logic and must be analyzed in maximum depth for KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Leakage Mechanisms

#### A. Subthreshold Leakage (Most Important)

- **What:** Even when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, a small current flows because the channel is not *instantaneously* cut off — the MOSFET operates in the **weak inversion** region where the channel behaves like a weakly-conducting semiconductor.
- **Why it matters:** $V_{th}$ has been scaled down aggressively (from ~0.7 V at 0.25 µm to ~0.3 V at 7 nm) to maintain drive current at lower $V_{DD}$. Lower $V_{th}$ → exponentially higher leakage.
- **How:** The transistor behaves like a **BJT-like bipolar device** in weak inversion, with the channel acting as the base.

#### B. Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage (BTBT)

- **What:** At the drain and source p-n junctions of a MOSFET (which are always reverse-biased when the transistor is OFF), a small reverse-bias saturation current flows.
- **Why:** In heavily doped shallow junctions of nanometer devices, the depletion region is very narrow, allowing electrons to *quantum-mechanically tunnel* from the valence band of the p-side to the conduction band of the n-side. This is called **Band-to-Band Tunneling (BTBT)**.
- **How:** Governed by the **Kane/Keldysh tunneling model**, dependent on the electric field across the junction.

#### C. Gate-Oxide Tunneling Leakage

- **What:** Carriers tunnel directly through the thin gate oxide ($SiO_2$) when a voltage is applied across the gate.
- **Why:** Gate oxide thickness has shrunk to ~1.0–1.5 nm to maintain gate control over the channel. At this thickness, the oxide is no longer a perfect insulator — it behaves as a finite potential barrier (~3.1 eV for $SiO_2$).
- **How:** Two modes — **Fowler-Nordheim (FN) tunneling** (high field, oxide conduction band mechanism) and **Direct tunneling** (low field, dominant in modern devices).

#### D. Gate-Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL)

- **What:** Leakage current at the drain-gate overlap region due to a high vertical electric field when the gate is at low/negative voltage and the drain is at high voltage.
- **Why:** The high field creates deep depletion in the drain under the gate overlap, leading to BTBT from drain valence band to gate-induced conduction band.
- **How:** Especially severe in **PMOS transistors** with heavily doped p+ drain under N+ gate overlap (halo doping).

#### E. Punchthrough

- **What:** The drain and source depletion regions physically merge in short-channel devices, creating a direct conduction path between source and drain.
- **Why:** As channel length ($L$) decreases, depletion widths from drain and source become comparable to $L$.
- **How:** Controlled by increasing channel doping or using **retrograde wells** and **halo implants**.

#### F. Hot-Carrier Injection (HCI) Related Leakage

- **What:** Carriers accelerated by the high lateral field near the drain gain enough kinetic energy to "jump" into the gate oxide, creating interface traps.
- **Why:** Over time, these trapped charges shift $V_{th}$ and increase leakage.
- **How:** A **reliability degradation mechanism**, not a steady-state leakage source — but contributes to long-term static power growth.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula required for KTU 2024 ESE problems on Static Power Dissipation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Parameters / Units | Typical Range |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Static Power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$ | $V_{DD}$ in V, $I_{leak}$ in A | µW – mW per gate |
| 2 | Subthreshold Current | $I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$ | $\mu$: mobility; $C_{ox}$: gate capacitance per unit area; $V_T = kT/q$ (~26 mV at 300 K); $n$: subthreshold swing factor (1.0–1.5) | nA – µA |
| 3 | Subthreshold Swing (S) | $S = n \cdot V_T \cdot \ln(10)$ | Units: mV/decade | ~70–100 mV/dec |
| 4 | Threshold Voltage (with DIBL) | $V_{th} = V_{th0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$ | $\eta$: DIBL coefficient (V/V); $V_{th0}$: long-channel $V_{th}$ | $V_{th0} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ V |
| 5 | BTBT Reverse Saturation Current | $I_{BTBT} = A \cdot E \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E}\right)$ | $E$: electric field across junction (V/cm); $A, B$: technology constants | pA – nA |
| 6 | Gate Oxide Tunneling Current (Direct) | $I_{gate} = A_{gate} \cdot E_{ox}^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B_{gate}}{E_{ox}}\right)$ | $E_{ox} = V_{ox}/t_{ox}$ | nA – µA |
| 7 | GIDL Current | $I_{GIDL} = A \cdot E_s^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{E_s}\right)$ | $E_s$: surface electric field at drain | pA – nA |
| 8 | Body Effect on $V_{th}$ | $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$ | $\gamma$: body-effect coefficient ($\sqrt{V}$); $\phi_F$: Fermi potential | — |
| 9 | Short-Channel $V_{th}$ Roll-off | $\Delta V_{th} \propto \dfrac{1}{L_{eff}}$ | Empirical | — |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Mobile & IoT Devices:** Standby (sleep) power is dominated by leakage. Smartphones in idle mode consume ~60% power through leakage paths.
- **Server Farms:** Even at low utilization, thousands of cores leak continuously — leading to massive cooling costs.
- **Medical Implants (Pacemakers):** Leakage directly determines battery life — must be reduced to µW levels.
- **Cryptographic Hardware:** Side-channel attacks exploit leakage currents to extract secret keys (e.g., **DPA — Differential Power Analysis**).
- **Space Electronics:** Radiation-hardened designs require careful leakage management because total ionizing dose increases leakage dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Reducing $V_{th}$ improves performance (higher $I_{ON}$) but **exponentially increases** $I_{OFF}$. The Power-Delay Product (PDP) and Energy-Delay Product (EDP) capture this trade-off and are favorite KTU problem statements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Subthreshold Leakage Current

The subthreshold conduction mechanism is derived from the assumption that the channel behaves like a weakly-inverted semiconductor whose surface potential $\phi_s$ varies almost linearly with gate voltage.

**Starting Equation — Channel Charge Density in Weak Inversion:**

The inversion layer charge per unit area in weak inversion is given by:

$$Q_{inv}(y) = \sqrt{\dfrac{q \epsilon_{si} N_A}{2 \phi_s}} \cdot kT \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_s(y) - 2\phi_F}{kT/q}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Relate $\phi_s$ to Gate Voltage:**

Using the gate capacitor voltage divider model:

$$\phi_s = \dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n}$$

where $n = 1 + \dfrac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}$ is the subthreshold ideality factor, accounting for depletion capacitance $C_{dm}$ below the oxide.

**Step 2 — Apply Drift-Diffusion in Weak Inversion:**

The drain current in weak inversion is dominated by **diffusion** (drift is negligible because the lateral field-induced mobile charge is small):

$$I_{DS} = \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{d Q_{inv}}{dy} \cdot V_T$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from source (y = 0) to drain (y = L):**

$$\int_0^L I_{DS} \, dy = \mu W V_T \int_{Q_{inv}(0)}^{Q_{inv}(L)} dQ_{inv}$$

The boundary conditions give:

$$Q_{inv}(0) = Q_{inv0} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

$$Q_{inv}(L) = Q_{inv}(0) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final Closed-Form Expression:**

After full integration and simplification (assuming $V_{DS} \gg V_T$, the transistor is in saturation):

$$I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The subthreshold current has an **exponential dependence** on $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$. For every 100 mV reduction in $V_{th}$, leakage increases by approximately **one order of magnitude (10×)** at room temperature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Subthreshold Swing (S)

The **subthreshold swing** $S$ is defined as the inverse slope of the $\log(I_{DS})$ vs $V_{GS}$ curve, measured in **mV per decade** (mV/decade).

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\ln(I_{sub})$ with respect to $V_{GS}$:**

$$\dfrac{\partial \ln I_{sub}}{\partial V_{GS}} = \dfrac{1}{n V_T}$$

**Step 2 — Invert and multiply by $\ln(10)$:**

$$S = \left(\dfrac{\partial \log_{10} I_{sub}}{\partial V_{GS}}\right)^{-1} = n V_T \ln(10)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at T = 300 K:**

$$S = n \times 26\,\text{mV} \times 2.303 = 60 \cdot n \,\text{mV/dec}$$

For an ideal MOSFET ($n = 1$): $S = 60$ mV/dec (the Boltzmann limit).
For real silicon: $S = 70$–$100$ mV/dec.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **60 mV/decade limit** at room temperature is a *fundamental physical barrier* — it cannot be crossed by conventional silicon MOSFETs. This is why alternative device structures (FinFET, GAA, NCFET) are researched.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: DIBL Effect on Threshold Voltage

**Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** is the phenomenon where increasing $V_{DS}$ lowers the source-to-channel potential barrier, reducing $V_{th}$.

**Step 1 — 2D Poisson Equation in the Channel:**

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = -\dfrac{q N_A}{\epsilon_{si}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve with boundary conditions at source and drain:**

Using the depletion approximation and assuming the minimum surface potential occurs at $x = x_m$, $y = 0$:

$$\phi_{min} = \phi_{0} - \dfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L_{min}}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize and define DIBL coefficient $\eta$:**

$$\eta = -\dfrac{\partial V_{th}}{\partial V_{DS}}$$

Thus:

$$V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into subthreshold current:**

$$I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th0} + \eta V_{DS}}{n V_T}\right)$$

This shows that **higher $V_{DS}$ directly increases subthreshold leakage** — a critical consideration in stacked transistor and multi- $V_{th}$ designs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Template

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Problem Pattern:** "Calculate the subthreshold leakage current for an NMOS transistor with the following parameters..."

**Template Solution:**

1. Identify given: $W$, $L$, $V_{GS}$, $V_{th}$, $V_{DS}$, $T$, $\mu$, $C_{ox}$, $n$.
2. Compute thermal voltage: $V_T = kT/q$.
3. Compute exponential factor: $\exp((V_{GS} - V_{th})/(n V_T))$.
4. Compute pre-factor: $\mu C_{ox} (W/L) V_T^2$.
5. Multiply and report in nA or µA.
6. Repeat for different $V_{th}$ values to demonstrate exponential sensitivity.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analysis: Leakage vs. Technology Node

| Technology Node | $V_{DD}$ (V) | $V_{th}$ (V) | $t_{ox}$ (nm) | Dominant Leakage |
|-----------------|--------------|--------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 0.5 µm | 5.0 | 0.7 | 12 | Junction |
| 0.18 µm | 1.8 | 0.45 | 3.5 | Subthreshold |
| 90 nm | 1.2 | 0.35 | 2.0 | Subthreshold + Gate Tunneling |
| 45 nm | 1.0 | 0.30 | 1.4 | Gate Tunneling rising |
| 22 nm | 0.85 | 0.28 | 1.2 | Subthreshold (FinFET introduced) |
| 7 nm (FinFET) | 0.75 | 0.25 | HKMG | Subthreshold (controlled by fin width) |
| 3 nm (GAA) | 0.70 | 0.22 | — | Subthreshold + GIDL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Leakage Sources (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Static Power Dissipation VDD x Ileak"] --> B["Subthreshold Leakage Dominant"]
    A --> C["Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage"]
    A --> D["Junction Leakage BTBT"]
    A --> E["GIDL Leakage"]
    A --> F["Punchthrough Leakage"]
    A --> G["Hot Carrier Injection Leakage"]
    B --> B1["Weak Inversion Diffusion"]
    B --> B2["Exponential Vth Dependence"]
    B --> B3["60 mV per dec Limit"]
    C --> C1["Direct Tunneling"]
    C --> C2["Fowler Nordheim Tunneling"]
    D --> D1["Heavy Doping Depletion Narrow"]
    E --> E1["Drain Gate Overlap High Field"]
    F --> F1["Short Channel Effect"]
    G --> G1["Long Term Vth Shift"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Leakage Reduction Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Leakage Source Layer"] --> lvl1["Device Level Fixes"]
    src --> lvl2["Circuit Level Fixes"]
    src --> lvl3["Architecture Level Fixes"]
    lvl1 --> fix1A["High Vth Transistors"]
    lvl1 --> fix1B["Thicker Tox where Possible"]
    lvl1 --> fix1C["FinFET and GAA Geometry"]
    lvl2 --> fix2A["Power Gating"]
    lvl2 --> fix2B["Multi Vth Assignment"]
    lvl2 --> fix2C["Stacked Transistor Effect"]
    lvl2 --> fix2D["Reverse Body Bias RBB"]
    lvl3 --> fix3A["Clock Gating"]
    lvl3 --> fix3B["Sleep Transistor Insertion"]
    lvl3 --> fix3C["Dynamic Voltage Scaling"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Subthreshold Leakage Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VGS as Gate Voltage VGS
    participant Phi as Surface Potential Phi
    participant Ch as Channel Charge Qinv
    participant I as Subthreshold Current
    VGS->>Phi: VGS below Vth creates weak inversion
    Phi->>Ch: Exponential modulation of inversion charge
    Ch->>I: Diffusion drift across channel
    I-->>I: I sub mu Cox W over L VT squared exp VGS minus Vth over n VT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - *Section 4.1* maps the **6 KTU-mandated leakage sources** in hierarchical order of dominance.
> - *Section 4.2* maps the **3-tier leakage reduction hierarchy** — Device / Circuit / Architecture — which is a recurring KTU question pattern.
> - *Section 4.3* presents the **physical flow** of subthreshold conduction as a sequential cause-effect chain, useful for KTU short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define subthreshold leakage current in a MOSFET. Why does it increase exponentially with reduction in threshold voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Subthreshold leakage is the current flowing between source and drain of a MOSFET when the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) is **below the threshold voltage** ($V_{th}$). The transistor is nominally OFF, but weak inversion conduction occurs because the surface potential is not abruptly cut off below $V_{th}$.

The current is expressed as:

$$I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

The exponential dependence on $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$ arises from the Boltzmann distribution of carriers over the source-to-channel potential barrier. As $V_{th}$ decreases, this barrier lowers, allowing exponentially more carriers to diffuse across it. For every 100 mV reduction in $V_{th}$, $I_{sub}$ increases by approximately **one order of magnitude** (~10×), making subthreshold leakage the dominant static power component in nanometer CMOS. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any six sources of leakage power in a CMOS transistor. Which one is dominant and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six sources of leakage power in CMOS are:

1. **Subthreshold leakage** — weak-inversion diffusion current when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$.
2. **Reverse-biased junction leakage (BTBT)** — band-to-band tunneling at p-n junctions.
3. **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage** — quantum tunneling through thin $SiO_2$.
4. **Gate-Induced Drain Leakage (GIDL)** — high-field BTBT at drain-gate overlap.
5. **Punchthrough** — source and drain depletion regions merging in short channels.
6. **Hot-Carrier Injection related leakage** — long-term $V_{th}$ shift due to trapped oxide charges.

**Subthreshold leakage is dominant** because threshold voltage has been aggressively scaled down (from ~0.7 V to ~0.25 V in modern nodes) to maintain performance at lower supply voltages. This exponential reduction in $V_{th}$ causes leakage to grow by orders of magnitude, making it the largest contributor to static power. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice pattern, students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for subthreshold leakage current in a short-channel MOSFET and explain the significance of the subthreshold swing parameter S. Show how DIBL affects the threshold voltage and leakage current.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Subthreshold Current Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the diffusion-dominated drain current in weak inversion, and integrating the channel charge density from source to drain:

$$I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

where $V_T = kT/q$ is the thermal voltage (≈ 26 mV at 300 K), and $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ is the body-effect coefficient.

**Step 2 — Subthreshold Swing (2 marks):**

$$S = \dfrac{d V_{GS}}{d \log_{10} I_{sub}} = n V_T \ln(10)$$

At T = 300 K, the **Boltzmann limit** is $S_{min} = 60$ mV/decade. Real silicon devices achieve 70–100 mV/decade. Lower S means sharper turn-off → lower leakage.

**Step 3 — DIBL Effect (2 marks):**

In short-channel devices, the drain voltage $V_{DS}$ lowers the source-channel barrier:

$$V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

where $\eta$ is the DIBL coefficient (typically 0.05–0.2 V/V). Substituting:

$$I_{sub} \propto \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th0} + \eta V_{DS}}{n V_T}\right)$$

Higher $V_{DS}$ → lower effective $V_{th}$ → exponentially higher leakage. This is why stacked transistors (lower $V_{DS}$ per device) are used for leakage reduction.

---

**(b) A 65 nm CMOS inverter has the following parameters: NMOS W/L = 4, PMOS W/L = 8, $V_{DD}$ = 1.0 V, $V_{th0}$ = 0.30 V for both devices, n = 1.4, $\mu_n C_{ox}$ = 250 µA/V², T = 300 K. Calculate the total subthreshold leakage power when the input is held at logic '0'. Compare it with the leakage when $V_{th}$ is reduced by 100 mV.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute thermal voltage:**

$$V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Identify transistor state when input = 0:**

When $V_{in} = 0$ V:
- NMOS: $V_{GS} = 0$ V, $V_{DS} = V_{DD} = 1.0$ V → **OFF**, conducting subthreshold leakage.
- PMOS: $V_{GS} = -V_{DD} = -1.0$ V, $V_{DS} = 0$ V → **ON**, no leakage.

**Step 3 — Compute $I_{sub}$ for NMOS:**

$$\dfrac{W}{L} = 4, \quad \mu_n C_{ox} = 250 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$$

$$\mu_n C_{ox} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot V_T^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times (0.02585)^2$$

$$= 1000 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.685 \times 10^{-4} = 6.685 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A} \cdot V^{-2}$$

Exponential factor:

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{0 - 0.30}{1.4 \times 0.02585}\right) = \exp(-8.296)$$

$$= 2.49 \times 10^{-4}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{sub,1} = 6.685 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.49 \times 10^{-4} = 1.665 \times 10^{-10} \text{ A} = 166.5 \text{ pA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute static power (Case 1: $V_{th} = 0.30$ V):**

$$P_{static,1} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sub} = 1.0 \times 166.5 \,\text{pA} = 166.5 \text{ pW}$$

**Step 5 — Recompute with $V_{th} = 0.20$ V (reduced by 100 mV):**

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{0 - 0.20}{1.4 \times 0.02585}\right) = \exp(-5.531) = 3.96 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$I_{sub,2} = 6.685 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.96 \times 10^{-3} = 2.647 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A} = 2.647 \text{ nA}$$

**Step 6 — Static power (Case 2: $V_{th} = 0.20$ V):**

$$P_{static,2} = 1.0 \times 2.647 \,\text{nA} = 2.647 \text{ nW}$$

**Step 7 — Comparison:**

$$\dfrac{P_{static,2}}{P_{static,1}} = \dfrac{2.647 \text{ nW}}{166.5 \text{ pW}} \approx 15.9\times$$

A 100 mV reduction in $V_{th}$ increases leakage power by **~16×**, confirming the exponential sensitivity predicted by theory.

| Quantity | $V_{th} = 0.30$ V | $V_{th} = 0.20$ V |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| $I_{sub}$ | 166.5 pA | 2.647 nA |
| $P_{static}$ | 166.5 pW | 2.647 nW |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students mistakenly compute $I_{sub}$ using the saturation formula $\frac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ which is the **above-threshold** equation and gives **zero** current when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$. Always use the **exponential (subthreshold) equation** for OFF-state leakage. Also, remember to use $V_T = 25.85$ mV (NOT 26 mV) if your final answer requires 3-digit precision — this is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) With suitable diagrams, explain the different sources of leakage power in CMOS circuits. Discuss how gate-oxide tunneling leakage has become a serious concern with technology scaling.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**

Static power dissipation in CMOS arises from various leakage currents that flow even when transistors are nominally OFF. With aggressive technology scaling (from µm to nm regime), these leakage currents have grown exponentially and now dominate the power budget of modern ICs.

**Step 2 — Source 1: Subthreshold Leakage (1 mark):**

The dominant leakage mechanism in modern CMOS. When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the channel is in weak inversion, and current flows due to carrier diffusion. Given by:

$$I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Source 2: Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage (BTBT) (1 mark):**

The p-n junctions between source/drain and the body are always reverse-biased. A small reverse saturation current $I_s$ flows due to thermally generated carriers. In heavily doped shallow junctions of nanometer devices, **Band-to-Band Tunneling (BTBT)** dominates:

$$I_{BTBT} = A E \exp(-B/E)$$

where $E$ is the electric field at the junction.

**Step 4 — Source 3: Gate-Oxide Tunneling Leakage (2 marks):**

When a voltage is applied across the gate oxide, carriers can quantum-mechanically tunnel through the thin $SiO_2$ barrier. Two modes exist:
- **Fowler-Nordheim (FN) Tunneling:** Dominant at high oxide fields (> 1 V/nm); carriers tunnel into the oxide conduction band.
- **Direct Tunneling:** Dominant at low fields and ultra-thin oxides ($t_{ox} < 3$ nm); carriers tunnel directly from the gate to the channel.

The tunneling current density is:

$$J_{gate} = A_{ox} E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B_{ox}/E_{ox})$$

**Step 5 — Source 4: GIDL (1 mark):**

Gate-Induced Drain Leakage occurs at the drain-gate overlap region when the gate is biased low and the drain is biased high. The high vertical field creates deep depletion, causing BTBT.

**Step 6 — Source 5 & 6: Punchthrough & HCI (1 mark):**

Punchthrough occurs when source and drain depletion regions merge. HCI is a long-term reliability issue where hot carriers get trapped in the oxide, shifting $V_{th}$ and increasing leakage over time.

**Step 7 — Why Gate-Oxide Tunneling Has Become a Serious Concern (graded into above):**

With technology scaling, $t_{ox}$ has reduced from ~12 nm (at 0.5 µm) to ~1.0–1.2 nm (at 22 nm and below). At such thicknesses, the wavefunction of carriers in the gate has a finite probability of penetrating through the oxide barrier — making the gate "leaky". This is a fundamental quantum-mechanical effect and cannot be eliminated by clever design alone. Solutions include:
- **High-k dielectrics** ($HfO_2$, $HfSiO_x$) replacing $SiO_2$ — physically thicker but electrically thinner.
- **Metal gates** instead of polysilicon — reduces depletion and field.
- **Multi-gate structures** (FinFET, GAA) — better electrostatic control allows thicker effective oxide.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of Body Effect on threshold voltage and derive the body-effect equation. How does Reverse Body Bias (RBB) help in reducing leakage?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Body Effect (1 mark):**

The body effect describes the dependence of threshold voltage $V_{th}$ on the source-to-body voltage $V_{SB}$. When $V_{SB} > 0$ (source at higher potential than body), the depletion region under the channel widens, requiring more gate voltage to form the channel — thus $V_{th}$ increases.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the threshold condition $V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$

For $V_{SB} > 0$, the surface potential at threshold becomes $2\phi_F + V_{SB}$. Substituting:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

Subtracting the $V_{SB} = 0$ case and defining $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$:

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$$

This is the **body-effect equation**, where $\gamma$ is the body-effect coefficient (in $\sqrt{V}$), typically 0.3–0.6 $\sqrt{V}$ for modern CMOS.

**Step 3 — Impact on Leakage (1 mark):**

Since $I_{sub} \propto \exp(-V_{th}/nV_T)$, increasing $V_{SB}$ (forward body bias) **decreases** $V_{th}$ and increases leakage, while **decreasing** $V_{SB}$ (or making it negative, i.e., RBB) **increases** $V_{th}$ and reduces leakage.

**Step 4 — Reverse Body Bias (RBB) for Leakage Reduction (2 marks):**

In RBB, a **negative $V_{SB}$** is applied (body is biased above the source). This:
- Increases the depletion charge under the channel.
- Raises $V_{th}$ via the body-effect equation.
- Exponentially reduces subthreshold leakage.
- Reduces BTBT and GIDL by lowering the drain-to-body field.

**Quantitative Example:** A 200 mV RBB typically raises $V_{th}$ by 50–100 mV, reducing leakage by **5–10×** with negligible performance penalty in non-critical paths.

**Step 5 — Limitations:**

- RBB increases junction leakage at the source-body junction (which is now forward-biased in extreme cases).
- RBB degrades $I_{ON}$ (drive current), increasing delay on active paths.
- Effectiveness diminishes in highly scaled nodes where short-channel effects dominate over body effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** Students often confuse **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** with **Forward Body Bias (FBB)**. RBB **raises** $V_{th}$ (reduces leakage, slows transistor), while FBB **lowers** $V_{th}$ (increases leakage, speeds up transistor). Getting this backwards can cost up to **2 marks**. Also, remember to **draw the diagram** of the body connection — examiners explicitly award 1 mark for labeled body-terminal connections showing the bias polarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Power Dissipation: Sources of Leakage Power**

- **Static Power Formula:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$ (in W).
- **Subthreshold Current Formula:** $I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} (W/L) V_T^2 \exp((V_{GS}-V_{th})/(nV_T))$ — **memorize this verbatim**.
- **Subthreshold Swing Limit:** $S = n V_T \ln(10) = 60 \cdot n$ mV/decade at T = 300 K; minimum 60 mV/dec for ideal silicon.
- **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV (use 26 mV only when rough estimation is acceptable).
- **Six Leakage Sources:** Subthreshold, BTBT junction, Gate-oxide tunneling, GIDL, Punchthrough, HCI — **enumeration order matters**.
- **DIBL Effect:** $V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$ — DIBL **lowers** $V_{th}$ at higher $V_{DS}$.
- **Body Effect:** $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ — RBB **raises** $V_{th}$ and reduces leakage.
- **Exponential Sensitivity:** 100 mV reduction in $V_{th}$ ⇒ ~10× increase in subthreshold leakage.
- **Technology Trend:** Subthreshold leakage dominates from ~0.18 µm downwards; gate-oxide tunneling becomes critical below 45 nm.
- **Modern Solutions:** High-k dielectrics, Metal gates, FinFET/GAA geometries, Multi-$V_{th}$, Power gating, Reverse body bias, Stacked transistors.
- **Units to Watch:** nA, µA, pW, nW, mV/decade, mV, $V_T$ in mV (not V).

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Static Power Dissipation: Sources of Leakage Power**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Vdd and Vt on Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effects of Vdd and Vt on Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — EFFECTS OF Vdd AND Vt ON SPEED

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In CMOS VLSI design, the **propagation delay** (speed) of a digital gate is fundamentally governed by two designer-controllable electrical parameters:

- **Vdd** — the **supply voltage** (power rail voltage) that drives the transistor channel.
- **Vt** — the **threshold voltage** of the MOS transistor (the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to form a conducting channel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):**
> The **propagation delay** $\tau_p$ of a CMOS gate is the time required for the output to transition from **50% of Vdd to 50% of Vdd** (or vice versa) when the input is driven by a step waveform. The delay is a function of the **load capacitance** $C_L$, the **drive current** $I_D$, and the **supply voltage** Vdd, with $I_D$ itself being a strong function of Vdd and Vt.

Mathematically, the canonical first-order expression is:

$$\tau_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{2 \cdot I_{D,sat}}$$

where $I_{D,sat}$ is the saturation drain current of the switching transistor.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap & Pipe" Model 💧

Imagine a water tank mounted at a height **H** above a bucket. The height **H** plays the role of **Vdd** (potential energy pushing the water out). The **pipe diameter** plays the role of $1/V_t$ (how easily water can start flowing). A narrow pipe = high Vt = water struggles to start. A wide pipe = low Vt = water gushes out.

- **Increase Vdd (raise the tank higher)** → water flows faster → circuit switches faster. ✅ Speed UP.
- **Decrease Vt (widen the pipe)** → water starts flowing at a smaller pressure → circuit switches faster. ✅ Speed UP.
- **BUT** increasing either parameter makes water *waste* more energy:
  - Raising the tank (↑Vdd) → more potential energy wasted per drop.
  - Widening the pipe (↓Vt) → even when the tap is "off," water slowly drips (this is **leakage current** in CMOS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Speed and power are **traded-off** through Vdd and Vt. The art of low-power VLSI design is to find the **optimal Vdd/Vt pair** that meets the speed target while consuming the least energy per operation.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The course outcome mapped here is typically **CO2**: *"Analyze the impact of voltage scaling and threshold voltage scaling on circuit performance metrics."* This topic is **high-yield** because:
1. It appears in **Part A (3 marks)** as a direct conceptual question almost every exam cycle.
2. It is the foundation for **Part B (14 marks)** questions on voltage scaling techniques (covered later in Module 2).
3. It directly enables design decisions in **dynamic voltage scaling (DVS)** and **adaptive Vt** techniques.

---

## 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics to Remember

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Sub-100nm CMOS) |
|--------|----------|-------------------------------|
| **Vdd** | Supply voltage | **0.7 V – 1.2 V** |
| **Vt** | Threshold voltage | **0.3 V – 0.5 V** |
| **τp** | Propagation delay | **10 ps – 100 ps** |
| **C_L** | Load capacitance | **1 fF – 100 fF** |
| **I_leak** | Subthreshold leakage | **nA – μA** per gate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — HOW Vdd AND Vt CONTROL SPEED

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Logic of Delay Formation

Every CMOS gate delay is the result of three sequential physical events:

1. **Stage 1 — Channel Formation:** When the input crosses **Vt**, the transistor enters *strong inversion* and a conducting channel forms. The smaller the Vt, the *earlier* the channel forms, giving more of the clock cycle for current to flow. *(This is the Vt-dependence.)*

2. **Stage 2 — Current Drive:** Once the channel is formed, electrons are pulled from drain to source by the **Vds** field. The current magnitude is roughly proportional to **(Vdd − Vt)** in velocity-saturated short-channel devices, or **(Vdd − Vt)²** in long-channel devices. *(This is the combined Vdd–Vt dependence.)*

3. **Stage 3 — Capacitor Discharge:** The current discharges the load capacitance $C_L$ from Vdd down to Vdd/2. The time required is $C_L \cdot V_{dd} / I_D$. *(This is the Vdd-dependence on the voltage swing.)*

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** *"Vt starts the engine, Vdd fuels it, C_L is the cargo it has to carry."*

---

## 2.2 Long-Channel vs. Short-Channel Behavior

Modern CMOS transistors (≤ 90 nm) are almost always **velocity-saturated**. KTU 2024 expects you to know **both** regimes because the syllabus covers device physics fundamentals.

### (a) Long-Channel (Square-Law) Model

The saturation current follows the classical square-law:

$$I_{D,sat}^{LC} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t)^2$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_p^{LC} \propto \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^2}$$

### (b) Short-Channel (Velocity-Saturated) Model

When the lateral field exceeds the critical field, carrier velocity saturates at $v_{sat}$:

$$I_{D,sat}^{SC} = \mu_n C_{ox} W \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t) \cdot v_{sat}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_p^{SC} \propto \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** In modern nanometer CMOS, the **linear (velocity-saturated) model** is the more accurate one. Exam answers that use the square-law model for sub-100nm devices will be marked down unless explicitly stated as an approximation.

---

## 2.3 Sensitivity Analysis — How Sensitive is Speed to Vdd and Vt?

Two dimensionless sensitivity metrics are used:

$$\text{Voltage Sensitivity: } S_{V_{dd}} = \frac{V_{dd}}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_{dd}}$$

$$\text{Threshold Sensitivity: } S_{V_t} = \frac{V_t}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_t}$$

For the short-channel model, taking derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p^{SC}}{\partial V_{dd}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_t}{I_{D,sat}^{SC} \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p^{SC}}{\partial V_t} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{I_{D,sat}^{SC} \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t)}$$

The **ratio** of sensitivities tells us the relative effectiveness of each knob:

$$\frac{S_{V_t}}{S_{V_{dd}}} = \frac{V_t}{V_{dd}}$$

Since $V_t \ll V_{dd}$ in modern designs, **S_Vt is much smaller than S_Vdd in magnitude, but its sign is opposite.** This means:

- A **10% increase in Vdd** cuts delay by roughly **10–15%**.
- A **10% increase in Vt** *increases* delay by roughly **15–25%** (delay is more sensitive to Vt in velocity-saturated regime).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Regime |
|---|---------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{C_L V_{dd}}{2 I_{D,sat}}$ | First-order delay | General |
| 2 | $I_{D,sat}^{LC} = \dfrac{\mu C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{dd}-V_t)^2$ | Saturation current (long-channel) | Long-channel |
| 3 | $I_{D,sat}^{SC} = \mu C_{ox} W (V_{dd}-V_t) v_{sat}$ | Saturation current (velocity-saturated) | Short-channel |
| 4 | $\tau_p^{LC} \propto \dfrac{V_{dd}}{(V_{dd}-V_t)^2}$ | Delay (long-channel) | Long-channel |
| 5 | $\tau_p^{SC} \propto \dfrac{V_{dd}}{(V_{dd}-V_t)}$ | Delay (short-channel) | Short-channel |
| 6 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \, e^{\frac{V_{gs}-V_t}{n V_T} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{ds}}{V_T}}\right)}$ | Subthreshold leakage | Sub-Vt regime |
| 7 | $f \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \tau_p}$ | Maximum operating frequency | General |
| 8 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{dd}^2 f$ | Dynamic power | General |
| 9 | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Static (leakage) power | General |
| 10 | $E = P \cdot \tau_p$ | Energy per switching event | General |

> [!NOTE]
> ⚠️ **All pipe symbols replaced with `\vert` or `\mid`** to preserve KTU 2024 markdown rendering integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, this analysis underpins the design of:

- **ARM Cortex mobile SoCs:** Use **adaptive voltage scaling (AVS)** combined with **adaptive body biasing (ABB)** to dynamically tune Vdd and Vt per workload.
- **Intel/AMD server CPUs:** Maintain a "**V/F curve**" (voltage-frequency curve) where the optimal Vdd is selected for each target frequency.
- **IoT/Edge devices (e.g., Mediatek, Qualcomm wearable chips):** Operate near-threshold (Vdd ≈ Vt ≈ 0.4 V) to extend battery life by **5–10×** at the cost of **2–3×** slower operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Delay Expression in Velocity-Saturated CMOS

**Goal:** Derive $\tau_p$ as an explicit function of $V_{dd}$ and $V_t$ for a short-channel CMOS inverter.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of propagation delay.**

A CMOS inverter's output node is a capacitor $C_L$ that must charge (or discharge) through the PMOS (or NMOS) transistor. The propagation delay is defined as the time for the output to swing from $V_{dd}$ to $V_{dd}/2$ (for the falling edge driven by NMOS).

$$\tau_{PHL} = \int_{V_{dd}}^{V_{dd}/2} \frac{C_L}{I_D(V_{out})} \, dV_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the velocity-saturated current equation.**

For a short-channel NMOS, the drain current during discharge (with $V_{out}$ as the instantaneous $V_{ds}$) is:

$$I_D(V_{out}) = \mu_n C_{ox} W \cdot \left[(V_{dd} - V_t) V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}^2}{2}\right] \cdot \frac{v_{sat}}{V_{out} \cdot E_{crit}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by assuming the transistor is in velocity saturation throughout the transition.**

In deep sub-micron devices, the drain voltage remains well above the critical saturation voltage $V_{dsat} = (V_{dd}-V_t)/E_{crit} \cdot L$ for most of the transition. We approximate:

$$I_D \approx I_{D,sat}^{SC} = \mu_n C_{ox} W \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t) \cdot v_{sat}$$

**Step 4 — Treat the current as approximately constant during the voltage transition.**

$$\tau_{PHL} \approx \frac{C_L}{I_{D,sat}^{SC}} \int_{V_{dd}/2}^{V_{dd}} dV_{out} = \frac{C_L}{I_{D,sat}^{SC}} \cdot \frac{V_{dd}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Write the final result.**

$$\boxed{\tau_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{2 \cdot \mu_n C_{ox} W \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t) \cdot v_{sat}}}$$

This shows that **delay is proportional to Vdd and inversely proportional to (Vdd − Vt).**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity of Delay to Vdd

**Goal:** Find the percentage change in delay for a small percentage change in $V_{dd}$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\tau_p^{SC}$ with respect to $V_{dd}$.**

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p^{SC}}{\partial V_{dd}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial V_{dd}} \left[ \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{k (V_{dd} - V_t)} \right]$$

where $k = 2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat}$ is a constant.

**Step 2 — Apply the quotient rule.**

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_{dd}} = \frac{C_L}{k} \cdot \frac{(V_{dd} - V_t) - V_{dd} \cdot (1)}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^2} = \frac{C_L}{k} \cdot \frac{-V_t}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the relative sensitivity.**

$$S_{V_{dd}} = \frac{V_{dd}}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_{dd}} = V_{dd} \cdot \frac{(k/C_L) \cdot k(V_{dd}-V_t)}{C_L V_{dd}} \cdot \frac{-C_L V_t}{k(V_{dd}-V_t)^2}$$

Simplifying (after cancellation):

$$S_{V_{dd}} = -\frac{V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

$$\boxed{S_{V_{dd}} = -\frac{V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}}$$

For $V_{dd} = 1.0\,\text{V}$ and $V_t = 0.4\,\text{V}$:

$$S_{V_{dd}} = -\frac{0.4}{0.6} \approx -0.67$$

This means a **1% increase in Vdd → ~0.67% decrease in delay.** The negative sign confirms that higher Vdd reduces delay (faster speed).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity of Delay to Vt

**Goal:** Find the percentage change in delay for a small percentage change in $V_t$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\tau_p^{SC}$ with respect to $V_t$.**

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial V_t} \left[ \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{k (V_{dd} - V_t)} \right] = \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the relative sensitivity.**

$$S_{V_t} = \frac{V_t}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_t} = V_t \cdot \frac{k(V_{dd}-V_t)}{C_L V_{dd}} \cdot \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{k(V_{dd}-V_t)^2}$$

Simplifying:

$$S_{V_t} = \frac{V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

$$\boxed{S_{V_t} = +\frac{V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}}$$

The **positive** sign indicates that an **increase in Vt increases delay** (slows the circuit down).

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For $V_{dd} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $V_t = 0.4\,\text{V}$:

$$S_{V_t} = +\frac{0.4}{0.6} \approx +0.67$$

A **1% increase in Vt → ~0.67% increase in delay.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** $|S_{V_t}| = |S_{V_{dd}}|$ in the short-channel model. In the long-channel (square-law) model, the sensitivities are *doubled*: $S_{V_{dd}} = -2V_t/(V_{dd}-V_t)$ and $S_{V_t} = +2V_t/(V_{dd}-V_t)$. KTU examiners may ask this as a Part A question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optimal Vdd for Minimum Energy

**Goal:** Find the Vdd that minimizes the **energy per operation** $E = C_L V_{dd}^2$.

**Step 1 — Write the energy per switching event.**

$$E = C_L V_{dd}^2$$

**Step 2 — For a fixed performance (fixed delay constraint), the minimum Vdd is bounded by:**

$$V_{dd,min} = V_t \left( 1 + \frac{\tau_{req}}{\tau_0} \right)$$

where $\tau_{req}$ is the required delay and $\tau_0$ is a technology constant.

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the energy expression.**

$$E_{min} = C_L V_t^2 \left( 1 + \frac{\tau_{req}}{\tau_0} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

The energy grows **quadratically** with the required performance (inverse delay). Doubling the speed (halving $\tau_{req}$) quadruples the energy — this is the **fundamental energy-delay tradeoff** in CMOS.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Energy-Delay Product (EDP) and Its Optimum

The **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is the standard figure of merit combining power and speed:

$$EDP = E \cdot \tau_p = C_L V_{dd}^2 \cdot \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{k(V_{dd}-V_t)} = \frac{C_L^2 V_{dd}^3}{k(V_{dd}-V_t)}$$

Taking the derivative and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{d(EDP)}{dV_{dd}} = 0 \implies V_{dd,EDP-opt} \approx 3 V_t$$

**Interpretation:** The supply voltage that minimizes the EDP is approximately **three times the threshold voltage**. For $V_t = 0.3$ V, $V_{dd,opt} \approx 0.9$ V.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "*Triple the threshold, optimum is reached.*" ($V_{dd,opt} \approx 3 V_t$)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Speed Control Loop in CMOS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Voltage Step] --> B{Vgs exceeds Vt?}
    B -- YES --> C[Channel Forms - Current Flows]
    B -- NO --> D[Transistor OFF - Leakage Only]
    C --> E[Drive Current ID proportional to Vdd minus Vt]
    E --> F[Charge or Discharge Load Capacitance CL]
    F --> G[Output Voltage Transitions]
    G --> H[Propagation Delay Tp measured]
    D --> I[Static Leakage Current flows]
    I --> J[Static Power Dissipation]
    E --> K[Dynamic Power Dissipation Vdd squared times CL times f]
    H --> L[Speed Performance metric]
    J --> M[Total Power Consumption]
    K --> M
    L --> N[Trade-off Analysis: Vdd down, Vt up reduces power but slows speed]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Delay vs Vdd Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Vdd Region] --> B[Delay is HIGH - Slow Speed]
    B --> C[Power is LOW - Energy Efficient]
    C --> D{Vdd crosses Vt threshold}
    D -- Below threshold --> E[Near Threshold Computing NTC - Best Energy per Op]
    D -- Above threshold --> F[Super Threshold - High Speed High Power]
    E --> G[Optimal Energy Point Vdd approx 3 Vt]
    F --> H[Maximum Performance Region]
    G --> I[Designer selects based on Application]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture: Vt Control Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Performance Requirement Specification] --> B[Determine Required Clock Frequency f]
    B --> C[Calculate Required Delay Tp]
    C --> D{Vdd and Vt Selection Phase}
    D --> E[Option 1: Fixed Vdd and Fixed Vt]
    D --> F[Option 2: Dynamic Voltage Scaling DVS]
    D --> G[Option 3: Adaptive Body Biasing ABB]
    D --> H[Option 4: Multi-Vt Design]
    E --> E1[Static Power Constant, Simple Control]
    F --> F1[Adjust Vdd based on Workload, Saves Dynamic Power]
    G --> G1[Adjust Vt by Body Bias, Reduces Leakage]
    H --> H1[Use High Vt for non-critical paths, Low Vt for critical paths]
    E1 --> Z[Final CMOS Implementation]
    F1 --> Z
    G1 --> Z
    H1 --> Z
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Speed Sensitivity Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Parameters: Vdd, Vt, CL, W, L] --> B[Compute ID,sat using Short Channel Model]
    B --> C[Compute Tp using Delay Equation]
    C --> D[Compute SVdd Sensitivity to Vdd]
    C --> E[Compute SVt Sensitivity to Vt]
    D --> F{Is Sensitivity Acceptable?}
    E --> F
    F -- YES --> G[Proceed to Layout and Fabrication]
    F -- NO --> H[Adjust Vdd upward or Vt downward]
    H --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*How does the supply voltage Vdd affect the propagation delay of a CMOS inverter? Justify with the velocity-saturated delay equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The propagation delay of a CMOS inverter in the velocity-saturated regime is given by:

$$\tau_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{dd} - V_t)}$$

This equation shows that $\tau_p$ is **directly proportional to Vdd** (numerator) and **inversely proportional to (Vdd − Vt)** (denominator). Therefore, **decreasing Vdd increases delay** (slows the circuit), while **increasing Vdd decreases delay** (speeds the circuit). However, the relationship is **non-linear** because Vdd appears in both the numerator and denominator. As Vdd is reduced toward Vt, the denominator shrinks faster than the numerator, causing delay to grow rapidly. This is why modern low-power designs cannot simply scale Vdd down to zero — speed degrades catastrophically near the threshold voltage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define threshold voltage sensitivity of delay. Why is it important in low-power VLSI design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **threshold voltage sensitivity** of delay is defined as the percentage change in propagation delay per percentage change in threshold voltage:

$$S_{V_t} = \frac{V_t}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_t} = +\frac{V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}$$

This metric is **important in low-power VLSI design** for two reasons:

1. **Design Margin Determination:** It tells the designer how much the circuit speed will degrade if Vt varies due to process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variations.
2. **Leakage-Speed Trade-off:** Since subthreshold leakage current depends exponentially on (Vgs − Vt), even a small reduction in Vt (to gain speed) causes a large increase in leakage power. Quantifying $S_{V_t}$ helps balance this trade-off.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the propagation delay of a CMOS inverter in the velocity-saturated regime and show that the delay sensitivity to Vdd is given by $S_{V_{dd}} = -V_t / (V_{dd} - V_t)$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For a 65 nm CMOS process, the following parameters are given: $V_{dd} = 1.0$ V, $V_t = 0.35$ V, $C_L = 10$ fF, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200$ μA/V², $W/L = 2$, $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^5$ m/s. Calculate:
  (i) The propagation delay $\tau_p$
  (ii) The dynamic power dissipation at 1 GHz operating frequency, assuming activity factor $\alpha = 0.1$
  (iii) The new delay if Vdd is scaled down to 0.7 V (Vt unchanged). **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q1(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Write the velocity-saturated drain current expression. **[1 mark]**

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} W (V_{dd} - V_t) v_{sat}$$

**Step 2:** Write the propagation delay as the time to charge/discharge the load capacitor through half the supply swing. **[1 mark]**

$$\tau_p = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{D,sat}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}/2}{I_{D,sat}} = \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{2 I_{D,sat}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to get the explicit delay expression. **[1 mark]**
