$$\tau_p = \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{dd} - V_t)}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $\tau_p$ with respect to $V_{dd}$ using the quotient rule. **[2 marks]**

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_{dd}} = \frac{C_L}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat}} \cdot \frac{(V_{dd} - V_t) - V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^2} = \frac{-C_L V_t}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{dd} - V_t)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the dimensionless sensitivity. **[1 mark]**

$$S_{V_{dd}} = \frac{V_{dd}}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_{dd}} = V_{dd} \cdot \frac{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{dd}-V_t)}{C_L V_{dd}} \cdot \frac{-C_L V_t}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{dd}-V_t)^2}$$

$$\boxed{S_{V_{dd}} = -\frac{V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}}$$

**Step 6:** Conclude with physical interpretation. **[1 mark]**
The negative sign confirms that increasing Vdd reduces delay. For $V_{dd} = 1.0$ V and $V_t = 0.35$ V, $S_{V_{dd}} = -0.54$, meaning a 1% Vdd increase reduces delay by 0.54%.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q1(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Propagation Delay at Vdd = 1.0 V:** **[2 marks]**

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} W v_{sat} (V_{dd} - V_t)$$

With $W/L = 2$, we need to assume a minimum channel length. For 65 nm, take $L = 65$ nm, so $W = 130$ nm.

$$I_{D,sat} = (200 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (130 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.0 - 0.35)$$

$$I_{D,sat} = 2.6 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.65 = 1.69 \times 10^{-8} \text{ A} = 16.9 \text{ μA}$$

$$\tau_p = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (1.0)}{2 \cdot (16.9 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-15}}{33.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.96 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_p \approx 296 \text{ ps}}$$

**(ii) Dynamic Power Dissipation at 1 GHz:** **[2 marks]**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{dd}^2 f = 0.1 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^9)$$

$$P_{dyn} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 1.0 \text{ μW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} \approx 1.0 \text{ μW}}$$

**(iii) New Delay at Vdd = 0.7 V:** **[3 marks]**

$$I_{D,sat}^{new} = 2.6 \times 10^{-8} \cdot (0.7 - 0.35) = 2.6 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.35 = 9.1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A}$$

$$\tau_p^{new} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (0.7)}{2 \cdot (9.1 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{7 \times 10^{-15}}{18.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.85 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_p^{new} \approx 385 \text{ ps}}$$

**Discussion:** A 30% reduction in Vdd (from 1.0 V to 0.7 V) causes only a 30% increase in delay (from 296 ps to 385 ps). This is a hallmark of the velocity-saturated model — delay scales roughly linearly with Vdd in this regime, unlike the long-channel model where delay scales quadratically. The trade-off is that dynamic power drops by a factor of $(0.7/1.0)^2 = 0.49$, giving a 51% power saving for a 30% speed penalty — a highly favorable trade for many low-power applications.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Explain with mathematical derivation how the propagation delay of a CMOS gate depends on the threshold voltage Vt in the long-channel (square-law) regime. Compute the threshold voltage sensitivity $S_{V_t}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CMOS ring oscillator has 11 stages. Each stage has a load capacitance of 50 fF, threshold voltage $V_t = 0.4$ V, supply voltage $V_{dd} = 1.2$ V, and effective on-resistance of 1.2 kΩ per transistor.
  (i) Compute the propagation delay per stage.
  (ii) Compute the oscillation frequency.
  (iii) If Vdd is reduced to 0.8 V, what is the new oscillation frequency? **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q2(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Write the long-channel saturation current. **[1 mark]**

$$I_{D,sat}^{LC} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{dd} - V_t)^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the delay equation. **[1 mark]**

$$\tau_p^{LC} = \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{2 I_{D,sat}^{LC}} = \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{dd} - V_t)^2}$$

$$\tau_p^{LC} \propto \frac{V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to Vt. **[2 marks]**

Let $k = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) / C_L$. Then $\tau_p = V_{dd} / [k(V_{dd} - V_t)^2]$.

$$\frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_t} = V_{dd} \cdot \frac{2}{k (V_{dd} - V_t)^3} = \frac{2 V_{dd}}{k (V_{dd} - V_t)^3}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the dimensionless sensitivity. **[2 marks]**

$$S_{V_t} = \frac{V_t}{\tau_p} \cdot \frac{\partial \tau_p}{\partial V_t} = V_t \cdot \frac{k (V_{dd} - V_t)^2}{V_{dd}} \cdot \frac{2 V_{dd}}{k (V_{dd} - V_t)^3}$$

$$S_{V_t} = \frac{2 V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude. **[1 mark]**

$$\boxed{S_{V_t}^{LC} = +\frac{2 V_t}{V_{dd} - V_t}}$$

For $V_{dd} = 1.2$ V, $V_t = 0.4$ V: $S_{V_t} = +1.0$ — a 1% increase in Vt causes a 1% increase in delay. This is **double** the short-channel sensitivity, confirming that long-channel devices are more sensitive to Vt variation.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q2(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Propagation Delay per Stage:** **[2 marks]**

The RC delay formula: $\tau_p \approx 0.69 R_{on} C_L$ for a single RC stage (or $0.5 R C$ in the simplified KTU form).

Using $\tau_p = 0.5 R_{on} C_L$:

$$\tau_p = 0.5 \cdot (1.2 \times 10^3) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-15}) = 3.0 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_p = 30 \text{ ps}}$$

**(ii) Oscillation Frequency of Ring Oscillator:** **[2 marks]**

For an N-stage ring oscillator, the period is $T = 2 N \tau_p$ (each stage contributes two transitions per cycle).

$$T = 2 \cdot 11 \cdot 30 \text{ ps} = 660 \text{ ps}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{660 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.515 \text{ GHz}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 1.515 \text{ GHz}}$$

**(iii) New Frequency at Vdd = 0.8 V:** **[3 marks]**

The on-resistance is inversely proportional to the overdrive voltage $(V_{dd} - V_t)$:

$$R_{on}^{new} = R_{on}^{old} \cdot \frac{V_{dd,old} - V_t}{V_{dd,new} - V_t} = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot \frac{1.2 - 0.4}{0.8 - 0.4} = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.4} = 2.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

New delay per stage:

$$\tau_p^{new} = 0.5 \cdot (2.4 \times 10^3) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-15}) = 6.0 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s} = 60 \text{ ps}$$

New period and frequency:

$$T_{new} = 2 \cdot 11 \cdot 60 \text{ ps} = 1320 \text{ ps}$$

$$f_{new} = \frac{1}{1320 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 0.758 \text{ GHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{new} \approx 758 \text{ MHz}}$$

**Discussion:** A 33% reduction in Vdd (from 1.2 V to 0.8 V) caused a 50% reduction in frequency (from 1.515 GHz to 758 MHz). The frequency reduction is greater than the Vdd reduction because the on-resistance grew by 2× (since the overdrive voltage halved). This illustrates the **non-linear** nature of the speed vs. Vdd trade-off when Vt becomes a significant fraction of Vdd.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (PEEVT741):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 0.5 (or 0.69) in the RC delay formula.** Many students write $\tau_p = R C$ instead of $\tau_p = 0.5 R C$ (for a 50% transition) or $\tau_p = 0.69 R C$ (exact exponential). This is a **2-mark penalty** in numerical problems.
>
> 2. **Confusing long-channel and short-channel sensitivities.** The short-channel sensitivity is $|S| = V_t/(V_{dd}-V_t)$, while the long-channel sensitivity is **double**: $|S| = 2V_t/(V_{dd}-V_t)$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Wrong ring oscillator period formula.** Some students use $T = N \tau_p$ instead of $T = 2 N \tau_p$. The factor of 2 arises because each stage contributes both a rising and a falling edge per full clock cycle.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Energy in Joules, power in Watts, delay in seconds. Many students mix up pF vs. fF, μA vs. nA, etc. Always do a **unit check** before writing the final answer.
>
> 5. **Not stating the operating regime.** If you use the square-law model, write "assuming long-channel operation." If you use the velocity-saturated model, write "assuming short-channel / velocity-saturated operation." Vague answers lose the "methodology" marks (typically 1–2 marks).
>
> 6. **Ignoring leakage power.** In modern CMOS, static (leakage) power can equal or exceed dynamic power. A complete answer should mention **both** $P_{dyn}$ and $P_{stat}$.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ⏱️ **Speed ∝ (Vdd − Vt) / Vdd** in velocity-saturated (short-channel) regime.
- ⏱️ **Speed ∝ (Vdd − Vt)² / Vdd** in long-channel (square-law) regime.
- ⚡ **Lowering Vdd** reduces dynamic power **quadratically** ($P_{dyn} \propto V_{dd}^2$) but increases delay.
- 🔋 **Lowering Vt** increases speed but increases **subthreshold leakage exponentially** ($I_{leak} \propto e^{-V_t}$).
- 📐 **Sensitivity to Vdd (SC):** $S_{V_{dd}} = -V_t / (V_{dd} - V_t)$.
- 📐 **Sensitivity to Vt (SC):** $S_{V_t} = +V_t / (V_{dd} - V_t)$.
- 📐 **Sensitivity to Vdd (LC):** $S_{V_{dd}} = -2V_t / (V_{dd} - V_t)$.
- 📐 **Sensitivity to Vt (LC):** $S_{V_t} = +2V_t / (V_{dd} - V_t)$.
- 🎯 **EDP-optimal Vdd:** $V_{dd,opt} \approx 3 V_t$.
- 🎯 **Energy-optimal Vdd:** $V_{dd,opt} \approx 2 V_t$ (for a fixed performance constraint).
- 🔄 **Ring oscillator period:** $T = 2 N \tau_p$ (where N is the number of stages).
- 💡 **Master formula:** $\tau_p = C_L V_{dd} / (2 I_{D,sat})$ — memorize this and adapt $I_{D,sat}$ to the regime.
- 🚫 **Never** write absolute value with `|` inside KTU markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- ✅ **Always** state the operating regime (long-channel vs. short-channel) in your answer.
- ✅ **Always** do unit checks in numerical problems (fF → F, ps → s, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraints on Vt Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constraints on Vt Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Threshold Voltage ($V_t$)** in a MOSFET is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) required to create a conducting channel (inversion layer) between the source and drain terminals. Mathematically, for a long-channel device, it is given by:

$$V_t = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_a (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

where $V_{FB}$ is the flat-band voltage, $\phi_F$ is the Fermi potential, $N_a$ is the substrate doping, and $C_{ox}$ is the gate oxide capacitance per unit area.

In the context of low-power design, **Constraints on $V_t$ Reduction** refer to the physical, electrical, and reliability limitations that restrict how aggressively the threshold voltage of a CMOS transistor can be scaled down to minimize dynamic and active power dissipation. The fundamental trade-off is between the desired quadratic reduction in dynamic power (since $P_{dyn} \propto C V_{DD}^2 f$) achievable through lower supply voltages (which forces $V_t$ reduction) and the exponential growth in **subthreshold leakage current** that accompanies a lower $V_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> The reduction of $V_t$ is one of the most powerful knobs in low-power VLSI design, but it is **bounded by leakage, noise margin, process variability, and reliability constraints**. KTU expects students to understand why $V_t$ cannot be arbitrarily reduced and the quantitative limits imposed by the subthreshold conduction mechanism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water dam** holding back a reservoir:

- The **threshold voltage ($V_t$)** is like the **height of the dam wall**. A taller dam holds water (charge) more securely but requires more effort (higher voltage) to let water through.
- A **shorter dam** (lower $V_t$) lets water (current) flow easily, but it also **leaks** continuously because small waves (thermal noise) can spill over the top.
- The **leakage** is like the water that seeps over a low dam even when no gate is intentionally opened.

If you keep lowering the dam wall to make the controlled flow easier (faster switching, lower power), you reach a point where:
1. **Seepage becomes enormous** (subthreshold leakage).
2. The dam becomes unstable against small tremors (process variation).
3. Flood waters can no longer be reliably held (noise margin loss).
4. Structural integrity is compromised (device reliability, NBTI aging).

This is precisely why **$V_t$ cannot be reduced indefinitely** in modern CMOS circuits.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following **bold** values are the physical constants and standard device metrics central to $V_t$ scaling analysis:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (300 K) |
|--------|----------|------------------------|
| $k$ | Boltzmann constant | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| $q$ | Electron charge | **$1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **300 K** |
| $V_T = kT/q$ | Thermal voltage | **~25.85 mV** |
| $n$ | Subthreshold ideality factor | **1.0 – 1.5** |
| $S$ | Subthreshold slope | **~70–100 mV/decade** |
| $I_0$ | Reverse saturation current | Process-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A 100 mV reduction in $V_t$ leads to roughly a **10× increase in subthreshold leakage** at room temperature, since the subthreshold swing is approximately 100 mV/decade. This exponential relationship is the root of all $V_t$ reduction constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Trade-off: Why Lower $V_t$?

In digital CMOS circuits, the **dynamic power** dissipated by a gate is:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$$

To reduce $P_{dyn}$, the most effective approach is to **scale $V_{DD}$**. However, reducing $V_{DD}$ degrades the **drive current** $I_{on}$ and consequently degrades the circuit's **performance (delay)**. To compensate, designers lower $V_t$ so that the overdrive voltage $(V_{GS} - V_t) = (V_{DD} - V_t)$ remains large. This keeps $I_{on}$ and switching speed acceptable.

Thus, **$V_t$ reduction is a performance-preserving strategy** under low $V_{DD}$ operation — but it comes with severe physical penalties.

## 2.2 Major Constraints on $V_t$ Reduction

The constraints can be classified into four primary categories:

### 2.2.1 Subthreshold Leakage Constraint (The Dominant Limitation)
When $V_{GS} < V_t$, the transistor is in the **subthreshold (weak inversion)** region. The drain current is **not zero** but follows an exponential dependence on $V_{GS}$:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

For $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ (typical digital operation), the bracketed term $\approx 1$, so:

$$I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

The **subthreshold slope (S)** is defined as the change in $V_{GS}$ required to change $I_{sub}$ by one decade:

$$S = n V_T \ln(10) = \frac{n k T}{q} \cdot \ln(10) \approx 2.3 \cdot n \cdot V_T$$

At room temperature with $n \approx 1.3$: $S \approx 2.3 \times 1.3 \times 25.85 \text{ mV} \approx 77 \text{ mV/decade}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** A reduction in $V_t$ of just $\Delta V_t$ multiplies the leakage current by a factor of $10^{\Delta V_t / S}$. Hence the **static (leakage) power** explodes exponentially:

$$P_{leak} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} \propto V_{DD} \cdot 10^{-V_t / S}$$

### 2.2.2 Noise Margin Constraint
The **static noise margin (SNM)** of a CMOS inverter — derived from the butterfly curve — is directly related to $V_t$. Lowering $V_t$ reduces the trip point and shrinks the noise margin, making the circuit vulnerable to **coupling noise, crosstalk, and ground bounce**. For robust operation:

$$V_t \gtrsim 0.2 \cdot V_{DD}$$

### 2.2.3 Process Variation & Threshold Voltage Sensitivity
The actual fabricated $V_t$ deviates from the design value due to **random dopant fluctuations (RDF)**, line-edge roughness (LER), and oxide thickness variations. The standard deviation of $V_t$ variation is:

$$\sigma_{V_t} = \frac{q}{C_{ox}} \sqrt{\frac{N_a W_{dep}}{3 L_{eff} W}}$$

The **sensitivity of $I_{on}$ to $V_t$ variation** is:

$$\frac{\partial I_{on}}{\partial V_t} \propto -I_{on} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sigma I_{on}}{I_{on}} \propto \frac{\sigma V_t}{V_t - V_{th,ref}}$$

As $V_t$ is reduced, the **relative variation** increases, causing severe timing unpredictability.

### 2.2.4 Short-Channel Effects (SCE) and DIBL
As channel length $L$ shrinks into the deep-submicron regime, $V_t$ itself becomes a function of $L$ and $V_{DS}$:

$$V_t(L, V_{DS}) = V_{t0} - \Delta V_{t,DIBL} - \Delta V_{t,RCS}$$

where **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** shifts $V_t$ lower as $V_{DS}$ increases:

$$\Delta V_{t,DIBL} = \eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

The **roll-off coefficient** $\eta$ increases dramatically in nanoscale MOSFETs, amplifying leakage variability.

### 2.2.5 Reliability Constraint: NBTI / PBTI
**Negative Bias Temperature Instability (NBTI)** in PMOS transistors and **PBTI** in NMOS transistors cause a **time-dependent increase in $V_t$**. Lower initial $V_t$ means smaller headroom, and aging pushes the device into slower, leakier operation. NBTI shifts are typically modeled as:

$$\Delta V_t(t) \propto A \cdot t^{0.25} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{kT}\right)$$

### 2.2.6 Manufacturing Yield & Tunneling Leakage
Aggressively thin gate oxides (needed to support low $V_t$ with adequate $I_{on}$) increase **gate oxide tunneling current** ($I_{gate}$), which can be comparable to or exceed subthreshold leakage. Additionally, very low $V_t$ devices are highly sensitive to manufacturing defects, reducing yield.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $V_t = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ | Body-effected threshold voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS}-V_t}{nV_T}\right)$ | Subthreshold leakage current | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $S = nV_T \ln 10 \approx 2.3\,nV_T$ | Subthreshold slope | mV/decade (~70–100) |
| 4 | $I_{leak}(V_t) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_t / S}$ | Leakage vs $V_t$ relation | A |
| 5 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $P_{leak} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Static (leakage) power | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{t,DIBL} = \eta V_{DS}$ | DIBL-induced $V_t$ shift | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\sigma_{V_t} = \dfrac{q}{C_{ox}}\sqrt{\dfrac{N_a W_{dep}}{3 L_{eff} W}}$ | $V_t$ mismatch (Pelgrom) | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $V_t \gtrsim 0.2 V_{DD}$ | Noise margin lower bound | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $\Delta V_t(t) \propto t^{0.25} \exp(-E_a/kT)$ | NBTI aging shift | Volts (V) |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This analysis is the cornerstone of techniques like **Multi-$V_t$ (MVT)** CMOS design, **power gating**, **adaptive body biasing (ABB)**, and **dynamic voltage scaling (DVS)** used in virtually all modern mobile SoCs (smartphone chips, IoT devices) where the active-to-leakage power ratio must be carefully balanced.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Exponential Dependence of Leakage on $V_t$ Reduction

**Goal:** Show quantitatively how a reduction in $V_t$ by $\Delta V_t$ amplifies subthreshold leakage.

**Starting point — Subthreshold current equation:**

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Define the leakage at a reference threshold voltage $V_{t,ref}$:**
When $V_{GS} = 0$ (transistor nominally OFF), the off-current is:

$$I_{off}^{(1)} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{0 - V_{t,ref}}{n V_T}\right) = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{V_{t,ref}}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Now reduce $V_t$ to a new value $V_t^{'} = V_{t,ref} - \Delta V_t$:**

$$I_{off}^{(2)} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{V_{t,ref} - \Delta V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio of new leakage to old leakage:**

$$\frac{I_{off}^{(2)}}{I_{off}^{(1)}} = \frac{I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{t,ref} - \Delta V_t}{n V_T}\right)}{I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{t,ref}}{n V_T}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $I_0$ and split the exponent using exponent arithmetic:**

$$\frac{I_{off}^{(2)}}{I_{off}^{(1)}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{t,ref} + \Delta V_t + V_{t,ref}}{n V_T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to base-10 using $\ln 10 \approx 2.3026$:**

$$\frac{I_{off}^{(2)}}{I_{off}^{(1)}} = 10^{\,\Delta V_t / (n V_T \ln 10)} = 10^{\,\Delta V_t / S}$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 mV reduction in $V_t$ (with $S = 77$ mV/decade) leads to:

$$\frac{I_{off}^{(2)}}{I_{off}^{(1)}} = 10^{100/77} \approx 10^{1.30} \approx 19.95 \approx 20\times$$

The off-state leakage increases by **roughly 20× for every 100 mV drop in $V_t$**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Minimum $V_t$ Lower Bound from Noise Margin

**Goal:** Establish the qualitative bound $V_t \gtrsim 0.2 \cdot V_{DD}$ for a CMOS inverter.

**Step 1 — Inverter switching threshold (trip point) $V_M$:**

For a symmetric inverter ($k_n = k_p$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp} \vert = V_t$):

$$V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \quad \text{(for symmetric design)}$$

**Step 2 — Noise margin high $NM_H$ and low $NM_L$ definitions:**

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \qquad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

**Step 3 — The DC transfer characteristic near the trip point is governed by the gain:**

$$\frac{\partial V_{out}}{\partial V_{in}} = -g_m \cdot R_{eq}$$

For a single-stage inverter, the input range over which the output is well-defined (i.e., $V_{IH} - V_{IL}$) is approximately:

$$V_{IH} - V_{IL} \approx 2 \cdot \frac{V_{DD}/2}{g_m R_{eq}}$$

**Step 4 — Relating $g_m$ to $V_t$ in saturation:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_t) = k_n (V_{DD} - V_t)$$

**Step 5 — As $V_t$ decreases, $g_m$ increases, sharpening the transition but narrowing the input high/low levels. Empirically, for $NM_H = NM_L \geq 0.1 V_{DD}$:**

$$V_{DD} - V_t \gtrsim 4 V_T \approx 100 \text{ mV} \quad \text{(lower bound on overdrive)}$$

**Step 6 — Combined with the constraint $V_t > 0$ (no negative $V_t$ allowed in normal operation) and the requirement that $V_t$ should not be so low that $NM$ collapses, designers use the rule of thumb:**

$$V_t \gtrsim 0.2 \cdot V_{DD}$$

For $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V: $V_t \gtrsim 200$ mV. For $V_{DD} = 0.7$ V: $V_t \gtrsim 140$ mV — a regime where leakage explodes.

## 3.3 Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** A 65 nm CMOS inverter operates at $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V. The nominal $V_t = 0.30$ V, $n = 1.3$, and $T = 300$ K. Calculate (a) the subthreshold slope $S$, and (b) the leakage ratio if $V_t$ is reduced to 0.20 V.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Compute the thermal voltage:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.025875 \text{ V} \approx 25.88 \text{ mV}$$

Then the subthreshold slope:

$$S = 2.3 \times n \times V_T = 2.3 \times 1.3 \times 0.02588 = 0.0774 \text{ V/decade} = 77.4 \text{ mV/decade}$$

**(b)** $\Delta V_t = 0.30 - 0.20 = 0.10$ V. Leakage ratio:

$$\frac{I_{off}^{(2)}}{I_{off}^{(1)}} = 10^{\,\Delta V_t / S} = 10^{0.10 / 0.0774} = 10^{1.292} \approx 19.6$$

**Result:** A 100 mV reduction in $V_t$ causes approximately a **20× increase in subthreshold leakage current**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The $V_t$ Reduction Trade-off Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Low Power VLSI Design Goal: Reduce Power"] --> B["Scale Down V_DD"]
    B --> C["Performance Loss - I_on decreases"]
    C --> D["Compensate by Reducing V_t"]
    D --> E{New V_t Value}
    E --> F["I_on restored - Good"]
    E --> G["Subthreshold Leakage - BAD"]
    G --> G1["I_sub increases exponentially"]
    G --> G2["P_leak rises sharply"]
    G --> G3["SNM shrinks"]
    G --> G4["Process variation impact grows"]
    G --> G5["DIBL and SCE worsen"]
    G --> G6["NBTI and PBTI aging effects"]
    G1 --> H["Constraint 1: Leakage Power Budget"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> I["Constraint 2: Robust Noise Margin"]
    G4 --> J["Constraint 3: Yield and Variability"]
    G5 --> K["Constraint 4: Short-Channel Effects"]
    G6 --> L["Constraint 5: Device Reliability"]
    H --> M["Optimal V_t Selection"]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Multi-Vt Design / DVS / ABB"]
```

```mermaid

        D1["LVT - Low Vt"]
        D2["SVT - Standard Vt"]
        D3["HVT - High Vt"]
    end

    A1 --> B1

    B1 --> B4
```

## 4.3 Graphical Summary: Leakage vs Vt (Conceptual Curve)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Subthreshold Leakage vs Threshold Voltage (Illustrative)"
    x-axis "V_t (V)" [0.10 0.15 0.20 0.25 0.30 0.35 0.40 0.45 0.50]

    line [10000 1500 200 30 5 1 0.2 0.04 0.01]
```

> **Reading the chart:** As $V_t$ falls below ~0.25 V, leakage rises **by orders of magnitude** — this is the forbidden region for energy-constrained designs.

## 4.4 Summary Table: Constraints vs Design Parameter Impact

| Constraint | Affected Metric | Severity at Low $V_t$ | Mitigation Technique |
|------------|-----------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Subthreshold leakage | $I_{off}$, $P_{leak}$ | **Critical (exponential)** | Multi-$V_t$, power gating, high-$k$ metal gate |
| Noise margin | $NM_H$, $NM_L$, SNM | High | Reserve low-$V_t$ for non-critical paths |
| Process variation | $\sigma V_t / V_t$ ratio | High | Larger device sizing, redundancy |
| DIBL / SCE | Effective $V_t(L, V_{DS})$ | Medium-High | Halo implants, retrograde wells |
| NBTI / PBTI aging | $\Delta V_t$ over lifetime | Medium | Adaptive body bias, wear-leveling |
| Gate tunneling | $I_{gate}$ | Medium | High-$k$ gate dielectrics |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain why the threshold voltage ($V_t$) of a MOSFET cannot be reduced indefinitely in nanometer CMOS technologies. List any three physical constraints.**

**Model Answer:**

The threshold voltage of a MOSFET cannot be reduced indefinitely because lower $V_t$ degrades multiple device and circuit parameters:

1. **Subthreshold Leakage Constraint:** $I_{sub}$ is exponentially dependent on $V_t$ as $I_{sub} \propto 10^{-V_t/S}$. A 100 mV reduction in $V_t$ increases leakage by approximately 20×, which dominates total power consumption in nanometer nodes.

2. **Noise Margin Constraint:** The static noise margin (SNM) of CMOS gates shrinks as $V_t$ decreases. Empirically, $V_t \gtrsim 0.2 V_{DD}$ is required to maintain acceptable noise immunity.

3. **Process Variation Constraint:** The ratio $\sigma V_t / V_t$ grows for low $V_t$, causing severe timing unpredictability. Random dopant fluctuation (RDF) effects in small-geometry devices amplify $V_t$ mismatch.

(Other valid constraints: DIBL/SCE worsening, NBTI aging, gate oxide tunneling.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Define subthreshold slope (S). What is its theoretical lower limit at 300 K, and why is it a fundamental limit on $V_t$ scaling?**

**Model Answer:**

The **subthreshold slope (S)** is defined as the change in gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) required to reduce the subthreshold drain current ($I_{sub}$) by one decade (factor of 10):

$$S = n V_T \ln 10 = \frac{n k T}{q} \cdot \ln 10 \approx 2.3 \cdot n \cdot V_T$$

At room temperature ($T = 300$ K), the **theoretical lower limit** is when $n = 1$:

$$S_{min} = 2.3 \times \frac{kT}{q} \approx 60 \text{ mV/decade}$$

In real devices, $n > 1$ (typically 1.2–1.5), so $S \approx 70$–$100$ mV/decade.

**Significance for $V_t$ scaling:** Because $S$ is **bounded by thermal voltage $V_T$**, the subthreshold current cannot be turned off any faster than one decade per 60 mV. This sets the maximum tolerable leakage at any given $V_t$ and is the **fundamental thermodynamic limit** preventing arbitrarily low $V_t$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for subthreshold leakage current in a MOSFET and show that a 100 mV reduction in $V_t$ causes approximately a 10×–20× increase in leakage at 300 K. Assume subthreshold slope $S = 77$ mV/decade.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss in detail the various constraints that limit $V_t$ reduction in modern nanometer CMOS circuits. How do multi-$V_t$ design and adaptive body biasing (ABB) help mitigate these constraints?

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The subthreshold current of a MOSFET (for $V_{DS} \gg V_T$) is given by:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

For a transistor in the OFF state ($V_{GS} = 0$):

$$I_{off} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

Now consider two scenarios with threshold voltages $V_{t1}$ and $V_{t2} = V_{t1} - \Delta V_t$:

$$I_{off,1} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{V_{t1}}{n V_T}\right)$$

$$I_{off,2} = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{V_{t1} - \Delta V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{I_{off,2}}{I_{off,1}} = \exp\left(\frac{\Delta V_t}{n V_T}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{\Delta V_t \cdot \ln 10}{S}\right) = 10^{\,\Delta V_t / S}$$

Substituting $\Delta V_t = 100$ mV and $S = 77$ mV/decade:

$$\frac{I_{off,2}}{I_{off,1}} = 10^{100/77} = 10^{1.299} \approx 19.95$$

Hence, a 100 mV reduction in $V_t$ leads to **~20× increase in leakage current** at room temperature. (For $S = 100$ mV/decade, it would be exactly 10×.)

---

**Part (b) — Constraints and Mitigation:**

The major constraints on $V_t$ reduction are:

1. **Subthreshold Leakage:** As derived above, leakage rises exponentially — the dominant limit. The total power becomes $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{leak}$, and the optimum $V_t$ minimizes the sum.

2. **Static Noise Margin (SNM):** Low $V_t$ lowers the trip point and shrinks $NM_H$ and $NM_L$, reducing noise immunity. The empirical bound is $V_t \gtrsim 0.2 V_{DD}$.

3. **Process Variation:** Random dopant fluctuations and line-edge roughness give $\sigma V_t \propto 1/\sqrt{WL}$. Lower $V_t$ increases $\sigma V_t / V_t$, causing parametric yield loss.

4. **Short-Channel Effects (DIBL):** $\Delta V_{t,DIBL} = \eta V_{DS}$. In nanometer devices, $\eta$ grows, causing $V_t$ to depend strongly on $V_{DS}$ — unpredictable leakage.

5. **Reliability — NBTI / PBTI:** $\Delta V_t(t) \propto t^{0.25}$ — slow aging shifts the threshold upward, reducing headroom and causing timing failures over the device lifetime.

6. **Gate Tunneling:** Thin oxides required for low $V_t$ produce gate leakage $I_{gate} \propto \exp(-t_{ox})$ which can rival subthreshold leakage.

**Mitigation Techniques:**

- **Multi-$V_t$ Design (MVT):** The standard-cell library contains LVT (low-$V_t$), SVT (standard-$V_t$), and HVT (high-$V_t$) variants. Critical timing paths use LVT cells, while non-critical paths use HVT cells. This achieves the performance of low $V_t$ with the leakage of high $V_t$ on average.

- **Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB):** By dynamically applying a body bias $V_{SB}$, the threshold voltage can be modulated via the body effect: $V_t = V_{t0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$. Forward body bias (FBB) reduces $V_t$ in active mode (high performance); reverse body bias (RBB) increases $V_t$ in standby mode (low leakage). This allows **runtime $V_t$ tuning** to balance performance and leakage dynamically.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the concept of Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL). How does it affect $V_t$ scaling decisions?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 45 nm CMOS process has $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, nominal $V_t = 0.25$ V, $n = 1.4$, and operates at $T = 320$ K (hot chip). Calculate (i) the subthreshold slope $S$, and (ii) the leakage penalty if $V_t$ is reduced by 80 mV for a performance-critical path.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Part (a) — DIBL Explanation:**

Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL) is a **short-channel effect** in which a high drain voltage $V_{DS}$ lowers the source-to-channel potential barrier, effectively reducing the threshold voltage $V_t$. This happens because the drain's electric field penetrates into the channel region in short-channel devices and "pulls down" the barrier that the gate must overcome.

**Effect on $V_t$:**

$$V_t^{eff}(V_{DS}) = V_{t0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$$

where $\eta$ is the **DIBL coefficient** (in V/V), typically 50–150 mV/V in nanometer nodes.

**Implications for $V_t$ scaling:**

- A transistor in the OFF state (low $V_{DS}$) and one in the ON state (high $V_{DS}$) experience **different effective $V_t$** values.
- In OFF state, $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$, so DIBL reduces $V_t$ further — **amplifying leakage**.
- This makes $V_t$ design **state-dependent** and worsens as channel length shrinks.
- Designers must budget additional $V_t$ margin to compensate, limiting how low the nominal $V_t$ can go.
- Halo implants and retrograde wells are used to suppress DIBL, but they add process complexity.

(Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.2 for the broader Vt management scheme.)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_t = 0.25$ V, $n = 1.4$, $T = 320$ K, $\Delta V_t = 80$ mV.

**(i) Subthreshold slope:**

Thermal voltage at 320 K:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 320}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{4.416 \times 10^{-21}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.0276 \text{ V} = 27.6 \text{ mV}$$

Subthreshold slope:

$$S = 2.3 \times n \times V_T = 2.3 \times 1.4 \times 0.0276 = 0.0889 \text{ V/decade} \approx 88.9 \text{ mV/decade}$$

**(ii) Leakage penalty:**

$$\frac{I_{off}^{new}}{I_{off}^{old}} = 10^{\,\Delta V_t / S} = 10^{0.080 / 0.0889} = 10^{0.900} \approx 7.94$$

**Result:** A **~8× increase in subthreshold leakage** occurs for an 80 mV $V_t$ reduction at elevated (hot) operating temperature. This is less severe than at room temperature because the higher temperature gives a larger $S$, spreading the leakage increase over a wider voltage range — but absolute leakage is much higher at 320 K than at 300 K due to the exponential $\exp(-E_a/kT)$ dependence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $V_t$ units:** Always express $V_t$ and $S$ in the same units (mV or V) before taking the ratio $\Delta V_t / S$.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_T$ (thermal voltage) with $V_t$ (threshold):** Use $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — it is **not** the threshold voltage.
> 3. **Ignoring the ideality factor $n$:** Never assume $n = 1$ in real devices; use 1.2–1.5 unless specified.
> 4. **Conflating $P_{dyn}$ with $P_{leak}$:** $P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$ (quadratic), while $P_{leak} \propto 10^{-V_t/S}$ (exponential). The two scale very differently.
> 5. **Skipping the noise margin bound:** KTU examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for not mentioning the $V_t \gtrsim 0.2 V_{DD}$ constraint.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Constraints on $V_t$ Reduction**

- [x] **Lowering $V_t$** is a key technique to maintain performance when $V_{DD}$ is scaled down for dynamic power reduction.
- [x] **Subthreshold leakage grows exponentially** with $V_t$ reduction: $I_{off} \propto 10^{-V_t/S}$.
- [x] **Subthreshold slope:** $S = 2.3 \cdot n \cdot V_T \approx 60$ mV/decade (theoretical min at 300 K).
- [x] **100 mV drop in $V_t$ → ~20× leakage** increase (at $S = 77$ mV/decade).
- [x] **Thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV** at 300 K — a fundamental physics constant that bounds $S$.
- [x] **Noise margin constraint:** $V_t \gtrsim 0.2 V_{DD}$ for robust digital operation.
- [x] **Process variation** ($\sigma V_t$) grows in significance at low $V_t$ due to random dopant fluctuations.
- [x] **DIBL** reduces effective $V_t$ at high $V_{DS}$, worsening leakage in OFF state.
- [x] **NBTI / PBTI aging** cause a time-dependent $V_t$ increase, reducing device lifetime margins.
- [x] **Gate tunneling leakage** ($I_{gate}$) becomes significant with thin oxides needed for low $V_t$.
- [x] **Mitigation strategies:** Multi-$V_t$ libraries, adaptive body biasing (ABB), power gating, dynamic voltage scaling (DVS), and high-$k$ metal gate stacks.
- [x] The **optimum $V_t$** balances dynamic power savings against static leakage penalty — it is **never** chosen arbitrarily low.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supply Voltage (V_DD)** in CMOS digital circuits is the primary DC bias potential applied between the power rail and ground that drives the switching activity of MOS transistors. In the context of **Low Power VLSI Design**, *voltage scaling* refers to the deliberate reduction of V_DD below its nominal value to simultaneously reduce dynamic power dissipation and switching energy, while accepting an associated increase in propagation delay.

The **Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power** is a foundational analysis topic because V_DD is the single most influential design parameter governing the power-performance trade-off in CMOS logic. The classical relationships, derived from the alpha-power law and switching energy principles, are:

- **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$
- **Propagation Delay (long-channel):** $t_p \propto \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_T)^{\beta}}$, with $\beta \approx 2$ for long-channel and $\beta \approx 1$ for short-channel (velocity-saturated) devices.
- **Static / Leakage Power:** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$, where $I_{leak}$ grows exponentially as $V_T$ is reduced to maintain performance at low $V_{DD}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to the module objective of *quantifying how dynamic power scales quadratically with V_DD, how delay scales roughly linearly with V_DD (in velocity-saturated nanometer CMOS), and how the quadratic energy savings from voltage scaling is the fundamental justification for the entire sub-1V design movement.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Water Tap (Faucet):**
> Imagine a water tap where the supply voltage V_DD is the **water pressure**, the load capacitance C_L is the **size of the bucket** that must be filled, and the propagation delay t_p is the **time taken to fill the bucket**.
>
> - **Higher pressure (high V_DD):** The bucket fills very fast (low delay), but you consume far more water energy per fill because the water shoots out violently and splashes. This is analogous to **high dynamic power** with **low delay** — performance is high, but energy efficiency is poor.
> - **Lower pressure (low V_DD):** The bucket fills slowly (higher delay), but the water trickles in a controlled, efficient manner. **Quadratically less energy** is wasted per switching event, and although the circuit runs slower, the *energy per operation* drops dramatically.
> - **Leakage (dripping tap):** Even when the tap is "closed" (transistor OFF), some water drips through. This is **leakage power**, and it gets worse as you reduce the tap's internal seal (threshold voltage V_T). So lowering V_DD indefinitely eventually makes the dripping (leakage) dominate the total water bill (total power).
>
> This is the central **energy-delay trade-off** in low-power VLSI design.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Modern CMOS) |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **0.7 V – 1.2 V** |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_T$ | **0.3 V – 0.5 V** |
| Subthreshold Swing Factor | $n$ | **1.1 – 1.5** |
| Thermal Voltage (300 K) | $V_T^{th} = kT/q$ | **≈ 25.85 mV** |
| Load Capacitance | $C_L$ | **fF – pF range** |
| Activity Factor | $\alpha$ | **0.01 – 0.5** |
| Velocity Saturation Index | $\alpha_{vel}$ | **1.0 – 1.5** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Supply Voltage is the Most Powerful Design Knob

Out of all low-power techniques — clock gating, power gating, transistor sizing, adiabatic logic, and architectural pipelining — **voltage scaling offers the largest energy savings per unit of design effort**. This is because the dominant dynamic power component contains a **V_DD² term**, meaning that a 2× reduction in V_DD yields a 4× reduction in dynamic power, even before accounting for the frequency reduction that typically accompanies voltage scaling (which then gives a cubic 8× total energy reduction for the same throughput).

### 2.2 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Dynamic Power Origin**
Every time a logic gate toggles, it charges its output load capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ (draws energy $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ from the supply) and then discharges it from $V_{DD}$ to 0 (dissipates $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ as heat in the pull-down network). The total energy per switching event is therefore $C_L V_{DD}^2$, and the average power at switching frequency $f_{clk}$ with activity factor $\alpha$ is $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$.

**Step 2 — Static (Leakage) Power Origin**
Even when a transistor is nominally OFF, the subthreshold conduction current flows:
$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_T}{n V_T^{th}}} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T^{th}}}\right)$$
This is exponentially sensitive to $V_T$. As $V_{DD}$ is reduced, designers must also reduce $V_T$ to preserve drive current, which **exponentially increases leakage**, giving the classic V-shaped total-power curve.

**Step 3 — Delay Derivation from MOSFET I-V Characteristics**
The propagation delay of a CMOS gate is determined by how quickly the load capacitance can be charged/discharged by the ON-current of the driving transistor:
$$t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{ON}}$$
For a long-channel MOSFET in saturation: $I_{ON,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{DD} - V_T)^2$, leading to:
$$t_p^{LC} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$$
For a short-channel velocity-saturated device: $I_{ON,vel} = k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)$, leading to:
$$t_p^{SC} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)}$$

**Step 4 — The Energy-Delay Trade-off**
- Reducing $V_{DD}$ → **lower energy per operation** (good for battery life).
- Reducing $V_{DD}$ → **higher delay** (bad for performance).
- The product $E \cdot t_p$ (Energy-Delay Product, EDP) is a key figure of merit that captures both axes simultaneously.

**Step 5 — Minimum Energy Point**
There exists an **optimal $V_{DD}$** at which total energy per operation is minimized. Below this point, leakage energy dominates; above it, dynamic energy dominates. This $V_{DD,opt}$ is typically just slightly above $V_T$ (often 1.5× to 2× $V_T$).

**Step 6 — Critical Voltage Limits**
- $V_{DD,min}^{perf} \approx V_T + 0.1\,\text{V}$ (performance limit, where drive current collapses)
- $V_{DD,min}^{leak} \approx 2V_T$ (subthreshold conduction becomes uncontrollable when $V_{DS} < 4V_T^{th}$)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Dynamic switching power | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static (leakage) power | W (Watts) |
| 3 | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} + P_{short}$ | Total chip power | W (Watts) |
| 4 | $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per switching event | J (Joules) |
| 5 | $t_p^{LC} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_T)^2}$ | Long-channel delay | s (Seconds) |
| 6 | $t_p^{SC} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)}$ | Short-channel delay | s (Seconds) |
| 7 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \, e^{(V_{GS}-V_T)/nV_{T}^{th}}$ | Subthreshold leakage current | A (Amperes) |
| 8 | $f_{max} \propto \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_T)^{\beta}}{V_{DD}}$ | Max operating frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $EDP = E_{sw} \cdot t_p$ | Energy-Delay Product | J·s |
| 10 | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Power-Delay Product | W·s = J |
| 11 | $V_{DD,opt} \approx 2V_T$ (approx.) | Minimum-energy operating voltage | V |
| 12 | $P \propto V_{DD}^2$ at constant freq | Power scaling law | — |
| 13 | $t_p \propto V_{DD}$ (short-channel) | Delay scaling law | — |
| 14 | $E \propto V_{DD}^2$ | Energy scaling law | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — LaTeX Table Escape Rule:** All absolute value bars and conditions use `\vert` / `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. Example: $V_{GS} \vert_{OFF} = 0$ is safe, but $V_{GS} = 0$ is preferred inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage scaling is the backbone of every modern mobile System-on-Chip (SoC). Examples include:
- **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS):** ARM Cortex-A cores scale V_DD from 0.6 V to 1.2 V based on workload.
- **Near-Threshold Computing (NTC):** Operates at $V_{DD} \approx V_T$ for 10–100× energy reduction in IoT endpoints.
- **Sub-Threshold Computing:** Operates at $V_{DD} < V_T$ for ultra-low-power biomedical implants (pacemakers, neural recorders).
- **Adaptive Voltage Scaling (AVS):** Closed-loop hardware monitors critical path delay and adjusts V_DD in real time for minimum energy.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Dynamic Power from First Principles

We derive the energy dissipated by a CMOS inverter charging a load capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ through a PMOS transistor, and then discharging from $V_{DD}$ to 0 through an NMOS transistor.

**Step 1:** Energy drawn from the supply during charging (current $i(t)$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to $C_L$):
$$E_{drawn} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Since all of this current ends up charging $C_L$ to voltage $V_{DD}$:
$$E_{drawn} = V_{DD} \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} i(t)\, dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Energy stored in the capacitor (the energy that is *not* dissipated):
$$E_{stored} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging:
$$E_{PMOS} = E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Energy dissipated in the NMOS during discharge (all stored energy is converted to heat in the NMOS):
$$E_{NMOS} = E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6:** Total energy dissipated per full switching cycle (one charge + one discharge):
$$E_{cycle} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 7:** Average dynamic power, where $\alpha$ is the activity factor (probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle) and $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency:
$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}}$$

This equation is **exact** for a single full-swing gate toggling at rate $\alpha f_{clk}$, and is the cornerstone of CMOS power analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Short-Channel Propagation Delay as a Function of V_DD

In modern nanometer CMOS, the carrier velocity saturates before the drain voltage reaches $V_{DD}$. The ON-current of a velocity-saturated NMOS is:
$$I_{ON} = k_n^{'} \cdot W \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T) = k_n^{'} \cdot W \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)$$

where $k_n^{'} = \mu_n C_{ox} v_{sat} / (V_{DD} - V_T)$ is the velocity-saturated transconductance (note: this is itself a weak function of $V_{DD}$ through the denominator, but for $V_{DD} \gg V_T$ it is treated as constant).

**Step 1:** Assume $C_L$ is charged by a constant current $I_{ON}$ (piecewise-linear approximation). The time to charge from 0 to $V_{DD}$ is:
$$t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{ON}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $I_{ON}$:
$$t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)}$$

**Step 3:** Define the intrinsic delay $\tau = C_L / (k_n^{'} W)$ (technology-dependent constant):
$$t_p = \tau \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_T}$$

**Step 4:** Normalize by $\tau$. Let $V_{DD,norm} = V_{DD} / V_T$ and $v = V_{DD,norm}$. Then:
$$\boxed{\frac{t_p}{\tau} = \frac{v}{v - 1}}$$

**Step 5:** Examine asymptotic behavior:
- As $V_{DD} \to \infty$ ($v \to \infty$): $t_p \to \tau$ (delay saturates — limited by carrier velocity).
- As $V_{DD} \to V_T$ ($v \to 1$): $t_p \to \infty$ (delay diverges — no overdrive available).
- At $V_{DD} = 2V_T$ ($v = 2$): $t_p = 2\tau$ (delay is double the intrinsic velocity-saturated limit).

**Step 6:** Maximum operating frequency:
$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 t_p} = \frac{k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)}{2 C_L V_{DD}}$$

This shows $f_{max} \propto (V_{DD} - V_T) / V_{DD}$, which **approaches a constant** as $V_{DD} \gg V_T$ (velocity-saturated regime).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power-Delay Product (PDP) and Energy-Delay Product (EDP)

These are key figures of merit for evaluating the quality of a logic family or design point.

**Step 1:** Define PDP (per switching event, using $\alpha = 1$ for worst case):
$$PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p = (C_L V_{DD}^{2} f_{clk}) \cdot t_p$$

Since $f_{clk} \cdot t_p \approx 1$ (one cycle per delay):
$$PDP = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute short-channel delay $t_p = \tau V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_T)$:
$$PDP = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Define EDP (penalizes both energy AND delay):
$$EDP = E_{sw} \cdot t_p = (C_L V_{DD}^{2}) \cdot \frac{\tau V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_T} = \frac{\tau C_L V_{DD}^{3}}{V_{DD} - V_T}$$

**Step 4:** Normalize and find minimum. Let $g(v) = v^3/(v-1)$ where $v = V_{DD}/V_T$:
$$\frac{d}{dv}\left(\frac{v^3}{v-1}\right) = \frac{3v^2(v-1) - v^3}{(v-1)^2} = \frac{v^2(2v - 3)}{(v-1)^2} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for critical point: $2v - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow v = 1.5$, i.e.:
$$\boxed{V_{DD}^{EDP,opt} = 1.5 \, V_T}$$

This is the **globally optimum supply voltage** that minimizes the energy-delay product — a famous result attributed to Mark Horowitz and used pervasively in low-power VLSI textbooks.

**Step 6:** Verify it is a minimum (second derivative positive at $v=1.5$):
$$\frac{d^2}{dv^2}\left(\frac{v^3}{v-1}\right)\bigg|_{v=1.5} > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Optimal V_DD for Minimum Total Energy (Including Leakage)

Total energy per operation = dynamic energy + leakage energy over the delay window:
$$E_{tot} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} + I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot t_p$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $I_{leak} = I_0 e^{-V_T / (n V_T^{th})}$ and the short-channel delay:
$$E_{tot} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} + I_0 e^{-V_T/(nV_T^{th})} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \frac{\tau V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_T}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $V_T$ itself is designable: $V_T = V_{T0} - \eta (V_{DD,nom} - V_{DD})$ via body effect or adaptive biasing. Treating $V_T$ as a function of $V_{DD}$ yields the **Minimum Energy Point (MEP)**.

**Step 3:** For fixed $V_T$, take the derivative $\partial E_{tot}/\partial V_{DD} = 0$:
$$2 C_L V_{DD} + I_0 e^{-V_T/(nV_T^{th})} \left[ \frac{2V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_T} - \frac{V_{DD}^{2}}{(V_{DD} - V_T)^2} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4:** The bracketed term simplifies to $V_{DD}(V_{DD} - 2V_T) / (V_{DD} - V_T)^2$.

**Step 5:** Solve numerically. The result is the **MEP voltage**:
$$\boxed{V_{DD,MEP} \approx 2 V_T + \mathcal{O}(n V_T^{th} \ln(...))}$$

i.e., the minimum-energy point is always slightly above **2× the threshold voltage**, confirming the textbook rule of thumb.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: V_DD Scaling Effects Summary

| Parameter | Scaling Law (Long-Channel) | Scaling Law (Short-Channel) | Trend as V_DD ↓ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Power $P_{dyn}$ | $\propto V_{DD}^{2}$ | $\propto V_{DD}^{2}$ | ↓↓ quadratically |
| Switching Energy $E_{sw}$ | $\propto V_{DD}^{2}$ | $\propto V_{DD}^{2}$ | ↓↓ quadratically |
| Delay $t_p$ | $\propto V_{DD} / (V_{DD}-V_T)^2$ | $\propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD}-V_T)$ | ↑ super-linearly |
| Max Frequency $f_{max}$ | $\propto (V_{DD}-V_T)^2/V_{DD}$ | $\propto (V_{DD}-V_T)/V_{DD}$ | ↓ linearly (SC) |
| Leakage Current $I_{leak}$ | $I_0 e^{-V_T/(nV_T^{th})}$ | Same | ↑ (if $V_T$ scaled) |
| Static Power $P_{stat}$ | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Same | depends on $V_T$ |
| Energy-Delay Product | $\propto V_{DD}^{3}/(V_{DD}-V_T)$ | $\propto V_{DD}^{3}/(V_{DD}-V_T)$ | min at $1.5 V_T$ |
| Throughput | $\propto f_{max}$ | $\propto f_{max}$ | ↓ |
| Noise Margin | $\propto V_{DD}$ (approx) | Same | ↓ |
