## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain why dynamic power dissipation in CMOS circuits has a quadratic dependence on supply voltage V_DD.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dynamic power is dissipated each time a logic gate charges its load capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS network and then discharges it from $V_{DD}$ to 0 through the NMOS network. The energy drawn from the supply during one full charge-discharge cycle is exactly $C_L V_{DD}^2$ (derived by integrating $V_{DD} \cdot i(t)$ over the charging interval and noting that all current ultimately charges $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$). Multiplying by the switching rate $\alpha f_{clk}$ gives $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$, which is **quadratic in $V_{DD}$**. Hence, halving $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by a factor of 4. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the expression for short-channel propagation delay of a CMOS gate and explain its dependence on V_DD.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a short-channel (velocity-saturated) MOS device, the ON-current is $I_{ON} = k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)$. The propagation delay, defined as the time to charge the load capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$, is:
$$t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{ON}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)}$$
This shows that $t_p$ is **inversely proportional to the overdrive voltage $(V_{DD} - V_T)$** and **directly proportional to $V_{DD}$**. As $V_{DD}$ is reduced toward $V_T$, the overdrive shrinks, current collapses, and delay increases super-linearly, eventually diverging as $V_{DD} \to V_T$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Adapted]**
*This question appears in two parts. Answer both.*

**(a)** Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS inverter and show that the average dynamic power consumed is $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$. Clearly state the meaning of every symbol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to short-channel CMOS, derive the propagation delay $t_p = C_L V_{DD} / [k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)]$ and sketch the variation of (i) $t_p$ vs $V_{DD}$ and (ii) total power $P_{tot}$ vs $V_{DD}$. Identify the **Minimum Energy Point** on the $P_{tot}$ curve and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a CMOS inverter with load capacitance $C_L$ at its output. Define the activity factor $\alpha$ = probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle, and $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Energy drawn from supply during one 0→1 transition (charging $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$):
$$E_{drawn} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\, dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Energy stored in the capacitor: $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Energy dissipated in PMOS during charging: $E_{PMOS} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** During the 1→0 transition, all stored energy is dissipated in the NMOS: $E_{NMOS} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Total energy per cycle = $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$. Multiply by switching rate $\alpha f_{clk}$:
$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f_{clk}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Discussion: power scales **quadratically with $V_{DD}$**, linearly with $C_L$ and $f_{clk}$. The $\alpha$ factor is empirically $\alpha \approx 0.1$ for typical data paths.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Velocity-saturated NMOS current: $I_{ON} = k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)$, where $k_n^{'} = \mu_n C_{ox} v_{sat}$. Substituting into $t_p = C_L V_{DD} / I_{ON}$:
$$\boxed{t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Rewrite as $t_p = \tau \cdot V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_T)$ with $\tau = C_L / (k_n^{'} W)$. As $V_{DD} \to \infty$, $t_p \to \tau$ (saturation). As $V_{DD} \to V_T$, $t_p \to \infty$ (no drive current).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Sketch: $t_p$ vs $V_{DD}$ is a **hyperbolic curve** that flattens at high $V_{DD}$ and diverges as $V_{DD} \to V_T$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total power $P_{tot} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{leak}$. Since $I_{leak}$ increases exponentially as $V_T$ scales down (required to keep $t_p$ constant), $P_{tot}$ is a **V-shaped curve** with respect to $V_{DD}$:
- Right branch (high $V_{DD}$): dominated by $P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$.
- Left branch (low $V_{DD}$): dominated by $P_{stat} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$.
- **Minimum at the bottom of the V**, called the **Minimum Energy Point (MEP)**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Physical significance of MEP: at this $V_{DD}$, dynamic and static energy contributions are balanced, and total energy per operation is minimized. This is the optimal operating point for energy-constrained applications like IoT and biomedical implants. Typically $V_{DD,MEP} \approx 2 V_T$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Note: the EDP-optimal point is at $V_{DD} = 1.5 V_T$, slightly below the MEP, representing the best energy-delay compromise.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Adapted]**
*This question appears in two parts. Answer both.*

**(a)** Define Energy-Delay Product (EDP). For a short-channel CMOS gate, show that the EDP is minimized at $V_{DD} = 1.5 V_T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various limits on V_DD scaling. Why cannot V_DD be scaled down indefinitely in modern CMOS? Explain with reference to threshold voltage scaling, leakage current, and noise margin. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** EDP is the product of switching energy and propagation delay: $EDP = E_{sw} \cdot t_p$. It penalizes both high energy *and* slow operation, making it a superior figure of merit compared to energy or delay alone.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Switching energy: $E_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.
Short-channel delay: $t_p = \tau \cdot V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_T)$, with $\tau = C_L/(k_n^{'} W)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** EDP:
$$EDP = C_L V_{DD}^{2} \cdot \frac{\tau V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_T} = \tau C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}^{3}}{V_{DD} - V_T}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Normalize by $V_T$: let $v = V_{DD}/V_T$, $K = \tau C_L V_T^2$:
$$EDP = K \cdot \frac{v^3}{v - 1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Differentiate and set to zero:
$$\frac{d(EDP)}{dv} = K \cdot \frac{3v^2(v-1) - v^3}{(v-1)^2} = K \cdot \frac{v^2(2v - 3)}{(v-1)^2} = 0$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Non-trivial root: $2v - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow v = 1.5$, so:
$$\boxed{V_{DD}^{EDP,opt} = 1.5 \, V_T}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Second derivative is positive at $v=1.5$, confirming a **minimum**. The optimal $V_{DD}$ for EDP is therefore **50% above the threshold voltage** — a foundational result in low-power VLSI.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Voltage scaling has three fundamental lower bounds:
- Performance limit (delay explosion as $V_{DD} \to V_T$).
- Leakage limit (subthreshold conduction as $V_T$ is reduced).
- Noise margin and reliability limit (process variation, $V_T$ mismatch).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** **Threshold Voltage Scaling Limit:** To maintain a target delay when $V_{DD}$ is reduced, designers must reduce $V_T$ in proportion. But subthreshold leakage scales as $I_{leak} \propto e^{-V_T/(nV_T^{th})}$ — every 100 mV reduction in $V_T$ **multiplies leakage by ~50×** at room temperature. A practical lower bound is $V_T \approx 0.3$ V due to subthreshold swing limitation (theoretical minimum swing is $n \cdot \ln(10) \cdot V_T^{th} \approx 60$ mV/decade at 300 K, and in real silicon $n \approx 1.3$ giving $\sim 80$ mV/decade).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Subthreshold Leakage Limit:** Even when a transistor is nominally OFF, the channel conducts a small current $I_{sub} = I_0 e^{(V_{GS}-V_T)/nV_T^{th}}$. As $V_{DD}$ approaches $V_T$, the gate can no longer fully turn OFF the transistor (since $V_{GS,OFF} = 0$ and $V_{GS,ON} = V_{DD} \approx V_T$, leaving only marginal overdrive). The static power $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ eventually **exceeds** the dynamic savings.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Performance / Delay Limit:** From $t_p \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_T)$, as $V_{DD} \to V_T$ the overdrive $(V_{DD} - V_T) \to 0$, so $t_p \to \infty$. The circuit becomes too slow to be useful. Practical lower bound: $V_{DD,min}^{perf} \approx V_T + 0.1$ V to keep $t_p$ within 10× nominal.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Noise Margin Limit:** Noise margin scales with $V_{DD}$ roughly as $NM \approx (V_{DD}/2) - V_T \cdot \text{constant}$. For $V_{DD} < 4 V_T^{th} \approx 100$ mV at room temperature, subthreshold operation becomes unreliable, and at $V_{DD} < 2 V_T$ noise margin is severely degraded, leading to functional failures.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Process Variation Limit:** In modern nanometer nodes, $V_T$ mismatch scales as $\sigma_{V_T} \propto 1/\sqrt{WL}$. For $V_{DD}$ comparable to $\sigma_{V_T}$, the gate behavior becomes stochastic — some die in a batch will be functional, others not. This is the **parametric yield problem** that ultimately sets the practical $V_{DD,min}$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Summary of Limits:** The minimum practical $V_{DD}$ in modern 7 nm/5 nm CMOS is around **0.6 V – 0.7 V** (near-threshold), and aggressive research chips operate down to **0.3 V – 0.4 V** (sub-threshold) for ultra-low-power applications. Below this, leakage, variation, and noise conspire to make the circuit non-functional.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing dynamic and static power:** Many students write $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ as "total" power, forgetting the leakage term $V_{DD} I_{leak}$. The board examiner **deducts 2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Long-channel vs short-channel confusion:** Using $t_p \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD}-V_T)^2$ when the question says "nanometer CMOS" (where velocity saturation applies) **loses full method marks** in Part B.
> 3. **Skipping the integration step in energy derivation:** Writing $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ directly without integrating $V \cdot i(t) \, dt$ is considered incomplete reasoning.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ (activity factor):** Dynamic power without $\alpha$ is only valid for a continuously-clocked toggle; for a real circuit with sparse activity, the answer is 2 marks short.
> 5. **No units in the final answer:** KTU examiners **strictly penalize** missing units (W, J, s).
> 6. **V-DD (with hyphen) vs V_DD (with underscore):** Consistent LaTeX notation is expected. Mixing styles is considered careless.
> 7. **Skipping the EDP derivative at the second-derivative verification step:** Examiners want to see that the student has confirmed the critical point is a minimum, not a maximum or inflection.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power**
> - **Dynamic power** = $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ — **quadratic in V_DD**, the single most important low-power equation.
> - **Short-channel delay** = $C_L V_{DD} / [k_n^{'} W (V_{DD} - V_T)]$ — **linearly increasing with V_DD at high V_DD**, diverging near $V_T$.
> - **Long-channel delay** = $C_L V_{DD} / [k_n (V_{DD} - V_T)^2]$ — quadratic dependence on overdrive.
> - **Switching energy** = $C_L V_{DD}^2$ — per 0→1→0 transition.
> - **Static power** = $V_{DD} I_{leak}$, where $I_{leak} = I_0 e^{-V_T/(nV_T^{th})}$ — **exponentially sensitive to $V_T$**.
> - **EDP-optimal $V_{DD}$** = **$1.5 V_T$** (Horowitz's result) — derived by minimizing $v^3/(v-1)$.
> - **Minimum Energy Point** (MEP) ≈ **$2 V_T$** — where dynamic and leakage energy balance.
> - **Lower limits on $V_{DD}$**: (i) delay explosion near $V_T$, (ii) leakage dominance, (iii) noise margin collapse, (iv) $V_T$ variation from process mismatch.
> - **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** is the engineering embodiment of this theory — used in every mobile SoC.
> - **V-shaped $P_{tot}$ vs $V_{DD}$ curve** — fundamental design intuition; minimum at the MEP.
> - **Threshold voltage scaling** is the enabler of voltage scaling — but it exponentially amplifies leakage.
> - **Activity factor $\alpha$** ≈ 0.1 for typical data; in clocks it is 1.0 (worst case).
> - **Subthreshold swing** ≈ 60–90 mV/decade sets the theoretical $V_T$ scaling floor.
> - **Near-Threshold Computing (NTC)** = $V_{DD} \approx V_T$ — best energy efficiency for moderate performance.
> - **Sub-Threshold Computing** = $V_{DD} < V_T$ — best energy efficiency for ultra-low-performance (e.g., biomedical).
> - **Key insight for exams:** When asked to compare scaling, remember the rule of thumb — *halve $V_{DD}$ → quarter the dynamic power → 8× energy reduction (with frequency scaling)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Supply Voltage on Static and Dynamic Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Supply Voltage on Static and Dynamic Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Power ($P_{static}$):** The power dissipated by a CMOS circuit when it is in a steady (quiescent) state — that is, when the circuit is NOT switching. It arises primarily from **leakage currents** flowing from $V_{DD}$ to ground even when the transistors are nominally OFF.

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Power ($P_{dynamic}$):** The power dissipated by a CMOS circuit during **switching (charging/discharging) of load capacitances** and during short-circuit current flow. It is the dominant component of total power in active CMOS circuits.

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance at the output node
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage (the topic of our analysis)
- $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Power** in a CMOS circuit is the sum:
> $$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short\_circuit}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Water-Pipe Analogy 💧**

Imagine a water distribution system where $V_{DD}$ represents the **water pressure** in the main pipe:

| Concept | Water Analogy | VLSI Equivalent |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| Supply Pressure | Water pressure | Supply Voltage $V_{DD}$ |
| Water Flowing Through Open Valve | Water gushing when tap is open | **Dynamic Power** (during switching) |
| Slow Drip from Closed Valve | Tiny leak through faulty washer | **Static Power** (leakage) |
| Pipe Diameter | Pipe width | Transistor width ($W$) |
| Friction in Pipe | Pipe roughness | Capacitive load ($C_L$) |

**Key Insights from the Analogy:**
1. **Dynamic Power (Active Flow):** Every time the "tap" opens, water rushes out with force proportional to **pressure²** — this is why dynamic power has a **quadratic ($V_{DD}^{2}$)** dependence.
2. **Static Power (Leakage):** Even when the tap is fully closed, a slow drip occurs. Reducing pressure reduces this drip **linearly** (proportional to $V_{DD}$).
3. **The Trade-off:** Lowering pressure saves water (power), but the tap delivers water more slowly — this represents the **delay penalty** of low-voltage operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Trade-off in Voltage Scaling:**
> Reducing $V_{DD}$ reduces BOTH static and dynamic power, BUT it also **increases circuit delay** (propagation delay $t_d \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_t)^{\alpha}$). Finding the optimal $V_{DD}$ is the heart of low-power VLSI design.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Subthreshold Swing ($S$):** typically **60–100 mV/decade** at room temperature
- **Thermal Voltage ($V_T = kT/q$):** **≈ 25.85 mV** at 300 K
- **Typical $V_{DD}$ values:** 5V (legacy) → 3.3V → 1.8V → 1.2V → 0.8V (modern nanoscale)
- **Threshold voltage ($V_t$):** typically 0.2–0.5V for modern processes
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-by-Component Analysis of Voltage Dependence

#### A. Dynamic Power vs. $V_{DD}$

The dynamic power equation can be derived from first principles. When a capacitor $C_L$ is charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$, energy drawn from supply is $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$. Half is stored on the capacitor, half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor during charging. On discharge, the stored energy is dissipated in the NMOS. Thus **every switching cycle dissipates $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ of energy**, regardless of switching speed.

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

**The "Why" Behind the Quadratic Dependence:**
- Energy per switching event: $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ (stored) + $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ (dissipated) = $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$
- Power = Energy × Rate = $C_L V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$
- Hence the **$V_{DD}^{2}$** term is fundamental — you cannot avoid it.

#### B. Static Power vs. $V_{DD}$

Static power is primarily due to **subthreshold leakage current** through OFF transistors:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)$$

For $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ (typical case):
$$I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{S}}$$

Where $S = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 60$ mV/decade is the subthreshold swing.

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} \propto V_{DD}$$

**The "Why" Behind the Linear Dependence:**
- Leakage current depends on $V_{GS} - V_t$ (gate overdrive), not on $V_{DD}$ directly (when transistor is OFF, $V_{GS} = 0$).
- However, the power dissipated equals $V_{DD} \times I_{leak}$, giving the **linear** relationship.
- Additionally, $V_t$ itself depends on $V_{DD}$ via the **body effect** and **DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering)**, creating a secondary dependence.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Conditions | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|------|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ | Switching power; $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | Watts |
| 2 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage power (OFF state) | Watts |
| 3 | $P_{short} = I_{mean} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Short-circuit power during transition | Watts |
| 4 | $E_{switch} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy per switching event (key metric) | Joules |
| 5 | $t_d = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{(V_{DD}-V_t)^{\alpha}}$ | Propagation delay (alpha-approx) | seconds |
| 6 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot 10^{(V_{GS}-V_t)/S}$ | Subthreshold leakage current | Amps |
| 7 | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} + P_{short}$ | Total chip power | Watts |
| 8 | $EDP = E \cdot t_d$ | Energy-Delay Product (optimization metric) | J·s |
| 9 | $S = n V_T \ln(10)$ | Subthreshold swing (≈ 60 mV/dec) | V/decade |
| 10 | $V_{T} = kT/q$ | Thermal voltage ≈ 25.85 mV @ 300K | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For dynamic power, $V_{DD}$ enters as $V_{DD}^{2}$, making it the **most powerful knob** for power reduction. For static power, $V_{DD}$ enters linearly. This is why voltage scaling is the **#1 strategy** in low-power VLSI design.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This analysis directly drives decisions in:
- **Mobile/IoT SoC Design:** Battery life extension via dynamic voltage scaling (DVS)
- **High-Performance CPUs:** Per-core voltage islands, turbo boost logic
- **Wearable/Biomedical Implants:** Ultra-low voltage operation near the **minimum energy point**
- **Data Center Servers:** Energy-proportional computing using $V_{DD}$ minimization
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quadratic Dependence of Dynamic Power on $V_{DD}$

**Goal:** Show that $P_{dynamic} \propto V_{DD}^{2}$ from first principles.

**Step 1:** Consider an inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ through a PMOS pull-up network (charging phase).

**Step 2:** The instantaneous current charging the capacitor:
$$i(t) = C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** The instantaneous power drawn from the supply:
$$p(t) = V_{DD} \cdot i(t) = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Total energy drawn from supply during one charging event (integrate over the transition from 0 to $V_{DD}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{drawn} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt \\
&= \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV_{out} \\
&= C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} dV_{out} \\
&= C_L \cdot V_{DD} \cdot V_{DD} \\
&= C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Of this energy, half is stored on $C_L$ as $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ and the other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS resistance (irrespective of resistance value — a beautiful result!).

**Step 6:** On the subsequent discharge, the stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is fully dissipated in the NMOS network. So **total energy dissipated per full cycle**:

$$E_{cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 7:** With switching activity $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f$, the average power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot f \cdot E_{cycle} \\
&= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $P_{dynamic} \propto V_{DD}^{2}$ — proven rigorously.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Power as a Function of $V_{DD}$ and Finding the Optimum

**Goal:** Find the $V_{DD}$ that minimizes total power (or energy-delay product).

**Step 1:** Express $P_{dynamic}$ in terms of $V_{DD}$:
$$P_{dynamic}(V_{DD}) = K_1 \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$ where $K_1 = \alpha C_L f$ is a constant for a given design.

**Step 2:** Express leakage (and thus $P_{static}$) using the subthreshold current. Since $V_{GS} = 0$ for OFF transistor and assuming DIBL reduces $V_t$ approximately as $V_t \approx V_{t0} - \eta V_{DD}$ (where $\eta$ is the DIBL coefficient):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sub}(V_{DD}) &= I_0 \cdot 10^{-(V_t)/S} \\
&\approx I_0 \cdot 10^{-(V_{t0} - \eta V_{DD})/S} \\
&= I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_{t0}/S} \cdot 10^{\eta V_{DD}/S} \\
&= K_2 \cdot 10^{\eta V_{DD}/S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Static power becomes (assuming exponential rise with $V_{DD}$):
$$P_{static}(V_{DD}) = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sub}(V_{DD}) \approx V_{DD} \cdot K_2 \cdot 10^{\eta V_{DD}/S}$$

**Step 4:** Total power:
$$P_{total}(V_{DD}) = K_1 \cdot V_{DD}^{2} + K_2 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot 10^{\eta V_{DD}/S}$$

**Step 5:** Take derivative and set to zero: $\frac{dP_{total}}{dV_{DD}} = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP_{total}}{dV_{DD}} &= 2K_1 V_{DD} + K_2 \cdot 10^{\eta V_{DD}/S} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\eta V_{DD} \ln 10}{S}\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Solving numerically gives an optimal $V_{DD,opt}$ that minimizes total power — typically in the range of $2V_t$ to $3V_t$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Scaling Scenarios

| Scaling Type | $V_{DD}$ | $V_t$ | $P_{dyn}$ Effect | $P_{static}$ Effect | Delay Effect |
|--------------|---------|-------|------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Full Voltage Scaling** | ↓ (scaled) | constant | ↓ quadratically | ↓ linearly | ↑ increases |
| **$V_{DD}$ + $V_t$ Co-scaling** | ↓ | ↓ (proportional) | ↓↓ drastically | ≈ same (danger: leakage ↑) | preserved/optimized |
| **Aggressive $V_{DD}$ Scaling** | ↓↓↓ (near $V_t$) | constant | ↓↓↓ massively | ↓ modestly | ↑↑ unacceptable |
| **Reverse (High $V_{DD}$)** | ↑ | constant | ↑ quadratically | ↑ linearly | ↓ (faster) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Components Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Chip Power P_total] --> B[Dynamic Power P_dyn]
    A --> C[Static Power P_static]
    A --> D[Short-Circuit Power P_sc]
    B --> B1[Switching Power: alpha C VDD^2 f]
    B --> B2[Internal Node Charging]
    B1 --> X1[VDD enters as VDD squared - QUADRATIC]
    C --> C1[Subthreshold Leakage I_sub]
    C --> C2[Gate Oxide Tunneling]
    C --> C3[Reverse-biased Junction Leakage]
    C1 --> X2[VDD enters linearly - LINEAR]
    D --> D1[Brief current during input transition]
    D1 --> X3[VDD enters linearly]
    X1 --> Y1[Most effective knob for dynamic power]
    X2 --> Y2[Leakage grows exponentially with Vt lowering]
    Y1 --> Z[Optimal VDD balances speed, dynamic, leakage]
    Y2 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Power vs. Supply Voltage Curve (Typical CMOS)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph VoltageDomain["Supply Voltage Range"]
        V1[VDD_low] -->|increase| V2[VDD_opt] -->|increase| V3[VDD_high]
    end
    
    P_curve["Power vs VDD Curve"]
    P_curve --> P1[At VDD_low: Leakage dominates, P_static high relative]
    P_curve --> P2[At VDD_opt: Minimum total power point]
    P_curve --> P3[At VDD_high: Dynamic power explodes quadratically]
    
    D_curve["Delay vs VDD Curve"]
    D_curve --> D1[At VDD_low: Delay very high]
    D_curve --> D2[At VDD_opt: Acceptable delay]
    D_curve --> D3[At VDD_high: Delay low but power excessive]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Power Dissipation Sources in a CMOS Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD[VDD Supply Rail] --> INV[CMOS Inverter]
    GND[Ground Rail] --> INV
    INV --> CL[Load Capacitance C_L]
    
    VDD -->|P_switching: C VDD^2 f| Heat1[Dissipation: Charging]
    GND -->|P_switching: C VDD^2 f| Heat2[Dissipation: Discharging]
    VDD -->|I_leak when OFF| Heat3[Static Leakage Power]
    VDD -.->|I_sc during transition| Heat4[Short Circuit Power]
    
    Heat1 --> TotalPower[Total Power P_total]
    Heat2 --> TotalPower
    Heat3 --> TotalPower
    Heat4 --> TotalPower
```

### 4.4 Voltage Scaling Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Design Target] --> Q1{Performance Critical?}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2[Use High VDD: 1.0-1.2V]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q3{Power Critical?}
    Q3 -->|YES| Q4{Battery Life Critical?}
    Q4 -->|YES| Q5[Use Near-Threshold: 0.5-0.7V]
    Q4 -->|NO| Q6[Use Sub-Threshold: 0.3-0.4V]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q7[Use Nominal VDD with DVS]
    Q2 --> Result1[High Performance, High Power]
    Q5 --> Result2[Balanced Power-Performance]
    Q6 --> Result3[Ultra Low Power, Slow]
    Q7 --> Result4[Adaptive: DVS, DVFS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

Explain how the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ affects the dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS circuit. Why is voltage scaling considered the most effective technique for reducing dynamic power?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS circuit is given by:
$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

The supply voltage $V_{DD}$ appears as a **squared term**, meaning dynamic power has a **quadratic relationship** with $V_{DD}$. (1 mark)

If $V_{DD}$ is reduced by half, dynamic power reduces to **one-fourth** of its original value, all else being equal. (1 mark)

Voltage scaling is the most effective technique because it exploits this $V_{DD}^{2}$ dependence, which is stronger than the linear or logarithmic dependencies seen in other parameters (like frequency or capacitance). Hence, even modest reductions in $V_{DD}$ yield substantial dynamic power savings. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

Discuss the effect of reducing supply voltage on the static (leakage) power dissipation of a CMOS circuit. What happens to the threshold voltage and subthreshold leakage during voltage scaling?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Static power due to subthreshold leakage is given by:
$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sub} = V_{DD} \cdot I_0 \cdot 10^{(V_{GS} - V_t)/S}$$

(1) **Linear reduction with $V_{DD}$:** $P_{static}$ decreases linearly as $V_{DD}$ is reduced (assuming $I_{sub}$ remains constant). (1 mark)

(2) **DIBL Effect:** However, at short-channel nodes, **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** causes the threshold voltage $V_t$ to decrease as $V_{DD}$ increases. When $V_{DD}$ is reduced, $V_t$ effectively increases slightly, which can reduce subthreshold leakage. (1 mark)

(3) **Caveat:** Aggressive $V_{DD}$ scaling below $V_t$ can lead to a regime where leakage becomes the dominant power component, requiring careful trade-off analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Clearly show the quadratic dependence on supply voltage $V_{DD}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS circuit operates at $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, clock frequency $f = 100$ MHz, load capacitance $C_L = 10$ pF, and switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$. Calculate the dynamic power. If the supply voltage is reduced to 1.2 V (keeping all other parameters constant), what is the new dynamic power? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ at its output. The output swings between 0 and $V_{DD}$.

**Step 1:** Energy drawn from supply during charging (0 → $V_{DD}$):
$$E_{charge} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Of this, energy stored on $C_L$:
$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Energy dissipated in PMOS during charging:
$$E_{diss,charge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** On discharge, all stored energy is dissipated in NMOS:
$$E_{diss,discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Total energy dissipated per switching cycle:
$$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6:** With activity factor $\alpha$ and frequency $f$, average power:
$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f}$$

The **quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$** is explicit. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{DD1} = 1.8$ V, $V_{DD2} = 1.2$ V
- $f = 100$ MHz $= 100 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- $C_L = 10$ pF $= 10 \times 10^{-12}$ F
- $\alpha = 0.2$

**Case 1: $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn,1} &= 0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.8)^{2} \times 100 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.24 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 0.2 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.648 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \\
&= 0.648 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn,2} &= 0.2 \times 10^{-11} \times (1.2)^{2} \times 10^{8} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.44 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 0.2 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.288 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \\
&= 0.288 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using ratio:**
$$\frac{P_{dyn,2}}{P_{dyn,1}} = \left(\frac{1.2}{1.8}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9} = 0.444$$

$$P_{dyn,2} = 0.444 \times 0.648 = 0.288 \text{ mW} \quad \checkmark$$

**Comment:** A 33% reduction in $V_{DD}$ (from 1.8V to 1.2V) leads to a **55.6% reduction in dynamic power**, demonstrating the strong quadratic dependence on supply voltage. This confirms that voltage scaling is highly effective for power reduction. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the variation of total power dissipation in a CMOS circuit as a function of supply voltage $V_{DD}$. Identify and justify the existence of an optimal $V_{DD}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the effect of $V_{DD}$ scaling on the propagation delay of a CMOS inverter. Discuss the concept of **near-threshold** and **sub-threshold** computing as low-power design strategies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Total Power vs. $V_{DD}$ (7 marks):**

The total power in a CMOS circuit is:
$$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f + V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

**At very low $V_{DD}$ (near or below $V_t$):**
- Dynamic power is very low (quadratic reduction).
- However, leakage current does not reduce proportionally; it may even increase due to prolonged transistor ON-time during slow transitions.
- **Static power dominates** the total power consumption.

**At moderate $V_{DD}$ (typical operating range):**
- Dynamic power increases with $V_{DD}^{2}$.
- Static power is small compared to dynamic power.
- **Dynamic power dominates.**

**At very high $V_{DD}$:**
- Dynamic power explodes quadratically.
- Leakage also increases (DIBL effect, gate oxide tunneling).
- **Excessive power dissipation, reliability concerns.**

**Curve Shape:** The total power curve typically shows a **U-shape or J-shape**, with a minimum at an **optimal $V_{DD,opt}$** which balances dynamic and static power. The minimum lies typically between $2V_t$ and $3V_t$ for submicron CMOS.

**Justification for optimum:** 
- Reducing $V_{DD}$ below $V_{DD,opt}$ increases delay exponentially, causing the circuit to operate longer, potentially increasing total energy consumed.
- Increasing $V_{DD}$ above $V_{DD,opt}$ increases power quadratically without proportional performance gains.
- The optimum represents the best energy-delay trade-off point. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**(b) Effect on Delay and Low- $V_{DD}$ Computing (7 marks):**

**Delay Equation:**
$$t_{d} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,sat}} \approx \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{K \cdot (V_{DD} - V_t)^{\alpha}}$$

Where $\alpha \approx 1.3$ for short-channel devices.

**Key Effect:** As $V_{DD} \to V_t$, the term $(V_{DD} - V_t)^{\alpha} \to 0$, causing delay to **diverge to infinity**. Thus, operating at $V_{DD} = V_t$ is impractical.

**Three Operating Regimes:**

| Regime | $V_{DD}$ Range | Characteristics |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Super-threshold** | $V_{DD} \gg V_t$ (e.g., 1.0–1.2V) | High performance, high power |
| **Near-Threshold** | $V_{DD} \approx V_t$ (e.g., 0.5–0.7V) | 10× power savings, ~5–10× slower |
| **Sub-Threshold** | $V_{DD} < V_t$ (e.g., 0.2–0.4V) | 100× power savings, ~100× slower, ultra-low power |

**Near-Threshold Computing (NTC):**
- Operates with $V_{DD}$ slightly above $V_t$ (typically 400–700 mV).
- Achieves energy efficiency close to sub-threshold while maintaining reasonable performance.
- Used in energy-constrained applications like IoT, wearables.

**Sub-Threshold Computing:**
- Operates with $V_{DD}$ below $V_t$ where transistor works in weak inversion.
- Ultra-low power operation — ideal for sensors, biomedical implants, energy-harvesting devices.
- Performance is very low (kHz range), but energy per operation is minimized.
- This is the **minimum energy point** for digital CMOS circuits.

**Trade-off Visualization:** The energy-delay product (EDP) is minimized at an optimal $V_{DD}$ that lies in the sub-threshold or near-threshold region for ultra-low-power applications. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ factor:** Many students write $P_{dynamic} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — this is correct ONLY when $\alpha = 1$ (full swing every cycle). Always state $\alpha$ explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $V_{DD}$ vs. $V_{DD}^{2}$ dependence:** Students often incorrectly say "static power is quadratic in $V_{DD}$." It is **dynamic** power that is quadratic; **static** power is **linear** in $V_{DD}$.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring DIBL effect:** When asked about $V_t$ scaling, many students miss that $V_t$ is NOT constant — it varies with $V_{DS}$ (and hence $V_{DD}$) due to DIBL.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always convert MHz → Hz, pF → F before calculation. Marks are deducted for dimensional errors.
> 
> 5. **Missing the delay trade-off:** When discussing voltage scaling benefits, students often forget to mention the **delay penalty** — this is essential for full marks.
> 
> 6. **Over-simplifying leakage components:** Static power has multiple sources: subthreshold, gate-oxide tunneling, reverse-biased junction leakage. Mentioning at least 2–3 sources scores higher.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Supply Voltage on Power**

✅ **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — **Quadratic in $V_{DD}$** (most influential parameter)

✅ **Static Power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — **Linear in $V_{DD}$**

✅ **Total Power:** $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short-circuit}$

✅ **Energy per cycle:** $E = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ — independent of frequency!

✅ **Subthreshold leakage:** $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot 10^{(V_{GS} - V_t)/S}$, where $S \approx 60$ mV/decade

✅ **Propagation delay:** $t_d \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_t)^{\alpha}$ — **increases as $V_{DD}$ decreases**

✅ **DIBL Effect:** $V_t$ decreases with increasing $V_{DS}$ — couples $V_{DD}$ and leakage

✅ **Voltage Scaling Strategies:**
   - Full scaling (just reduce $V_{DD}$): simple but increases delay
   - Co-scaling ($V_{DD}$ and $V_t$ together): preserves performance
   - Near-threshold: $V_{DD} \approx V_t$ — best EDP trade-off
   - Sub-threshold: $V_{DD} < V_t$ — minimum energy point

✅ **Optimal $V_{DD}$:** Typically $2V_t$ to $3V_t$ for minimum total power

✅ **Quadratic Advantage:** Halving $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by **75%** (to 1/4 of original)

✅ **Linear Cost:** Halving $V_{DD}$ reduces static power by only **50%** (to 1/2 of original)

✅ **Real-world Applications:** Mobile SoCs (DVS), IoT (NTC), implants (sub-threshold), servers (DVFS)

✅ **Key Trade-off Formula:** Energy-Delay Product (EDP) is the optimization metric — minimize EDP, not just power or energy alone
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi VDD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multi VDD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Multi-VDD — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multi-VDD (Multiple Supply Voltage Design)** is a circuit-level and system-level low-power design methodology in which different functional blocks (called *voltage islands* or *power domains*) of a single VLSI chip are intentionally supplied with **different operating supply voltages ($V_{DD}$)**, chosen individually to match the performance requirement of each block. Blocks on the *critical timing path* are assigned a **higher $V_{DD}$** to meet timing closure, while non-critical blocks are assigned a **lower $V_{DD}$** to drastically reduce dynamic and leakage power dissipation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Multi-VDD is fundamentally a **Voltage Scaling** technique. It exploits the fact that dynamic switching power is proportional to the **square of the supply voltage** ($P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$). Therefore, even a modest reduction in $V_{DD}$ for non-critical blocks yields large quadratic power savings.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Multi-VDD is taught under **Module 3 — Low-Power Design Approaches at Circuit, Logic, and Architecture Levels**, and forms one of the **Seven Principal Low-Power Design Strategies** taught in the course:

1. Variable-Threshold Voltage ($V_T$) Design
2. Multi-Threshold CMOS (MTCMOS)
3. **Multi-VDD** ← *Current Topic*
4. Multi-Supply Voltage (MSV) Architecture
5. Power Gating
6. Clock Gating
7. Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)

## 1.2 Real-World Intuition — The "Hybrid Car" Analogy

Imagine a modern **hybrid electric car**. The car carries two power sources:

- A **high-power gasoline engine** that drives the wheels when high speed or hill-climbing demands high torque.
- A **small, efficient electric motor** that drives the car at low speed in the city, where the gasoline engine would waste fuel.

Neither source is used for the other’s job. The engine controller automatically *switches* between them, and a small power-conversion circuit (analogous to a **Level Converter**) adapts the voltage levels between the two power rails.

**Multi-VDD is exactly this idea applied to a chip:**

- The **"gasoline engine"** = a critical-path block (e.g., ALU, processor core) running at **High $V_{DD}$** (e.g., 1.2 V) for speed.
- The **"electric motor"** = a non-critical block (e.g., display controller, I/O peripheral) running at **Low $V_{DD}$** (e.g., 0.8 V) for power savings.
- The **"power-conversion circuit"** = a **Level Shifter / Level Converter** placed between the two domains so signals can cross safely.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Voltage Island** | A contiguous physical region of the chip powered by a single $V_{DD}$ value. |
| **Power Domain** | Synonymous with voltage island in logical partitioning. |
| **Level Shifter (LS)** | A special buffer inserted between two voltage islands to convert a low-swing signal to a high-swing signal (or vice versa). |
| **Critical Path** | The longest combinational delay path, which determines the maximum operating frequency. |
| **Non-Critical Path** | A path whose delay has timing slack; it can tolerate slower (lower-$V_{DD}$) gates. |
| **Static Voltage Scaling (SVS)** | Different voltages assigned at design-time and fixed during operation. |
| **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS/DVFS)** | Voltage adjusted at run-time based on workload. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly distinguishes *Multi-VDD* (multiple fixed voltages chosen during physical design) from *DVFS* (dynamic run-time adjustment). Multi-VDD is a **static** partitioning strategy; DVFS is **dynamic**. They are often *combined* in real chips.

## 1.4 Why Multi-VDD is Needed — The Power Wall Problem

As CMOS technology scales below 90 nm, **leakage current** rises exponentially, and **dynamic power** dominates battery-powered mobile SoCs. Reducing $V_{DD}$ is the single most effective knob for both:

$$
P_{total} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f + I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

A blanket reduction of $V_{DD}$ for the whole chip, however, **breaks timing** on critical paths. **Multi-VDD is the engineering compromise:** drop $V_{DD}$ only where it is safe to do so.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Voltage-Dependent Power

The total power dissipated by a CMOS gate is the sum of three components:

$$
P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{short} + P_{leakage}
$$

| Component | Physical Cause | $V_{DD}$ Dependence |
|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Switching Power** | Charging/discharging load capacitance $C_L$ | $\propto V_{DD}^{\,2}$ (quadratic) |
| **Short-Circuit Power** | Brief crowbar current during input transitions | $\propto V_{DD}^{\,3}$ (cubic, but small) |
| **Static / Leakage Power** | Subthreshold + gate-oxide leakage | $\propto V_{DD}$ (linear) + exponential with $V_{DD}$ reduction (DIBL effect) |

The most important equation for Multi-VDD design is therefore the **dynamic power equation**:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 ≤ α ≤ 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance at the output node
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage of the island
- $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency

## 2.2 Delay vs. Voltage — The Alpha-Power Law

The propagation delay of a CMOS gate is modeled by the **Sakurai–Alpha Power Law**:

$$
t_d \;=\; \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)\, (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{\alpha}}
$$

For long-channel devices, $\alpha \approx 2$ and the relation simplifies (ignoring velocity saturation) to the classic **first-order delay model**:

$$
t_d \;\propto\; \frac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{2}}
$$

$$
f_{max} \;=\; \frac{1}{t_d} \;\propto\; \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{2}}{V_{DD}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This non-linear relationship is the *entire reason* Multi-VDD works: lowering $V_{DD}$ increases delay *much faster* than it reduces power. By assigning lower $V_{DD}$ only to non-critical blocks, we trade a small amount of delay for a large amount of power savings — but we never violate the critical-path timing constraint.

## 2.3 Voltage Islands — The Architectural View

A typical Multi-VDD chip is partitioned into **N voltage islands**. If island $i$ has supply voltage $V_i$ and consumes a fraction $\beta_i$ of the total chip capacitance ($\sum \beta_i = 1$), the total dynamic power becomes:

$$
P_{dyn,\,multi} \;=\; \alpha \, C_{total} \, f \, \sum_{i=1}^{N} \beta_i \, V_i^{\,2}
$$

Compared to a single-VDD chip at voltage $V_{high}$:

$$
P_{dyn,\,single} \;=\; \alpha \, C_{total} \, f \, V_{high}^{\,2}
$$

Hence the **power reduction ratio**:

$$
\text{Power Savings} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \beta_i \, V_i^{\,2}}{V_{high}^{\,2}}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet for Examination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every equation** that may appear in a KTU University Exam question on Multi-VDD. Memorize the *form*, not just the symbols — partial credit depends on writing the correct equation even if the numerical substitution is wrong.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} f$ | Dynamic switching power of a CMOS gate/island |
| 2 | $P_{dyn,\,multi} = \alpha C_{tot} f \sum_i \beta_i V_i^{\,2}$ | Total chip dynamic power with N voltage islands |
| 3 | $P_{savings} = 1 - \frac{\sum \beta_i V_i^{\,2}}{V_{high}^{\,2}}$ | Fractional power saving over single-VDD baseline |
| 4 | $t_d \propto \frac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD}-V_{TH})^{2}}$ | First-order gate delay model |
| 5 | $f_{max} \propto \frac{(V_{DD}-V_{TH})^{2}}{V_{DD}}$ | Maximum operating frequency |
| 6 | $E = P \cdot t \;\Rightarrow\; E_{dyn} = C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$ | Energy per switching event |
| 7 | $V_{opt} \approx 2 V_{TH}$ | Energy-minimizing supply voltage (long-channel) |
| 8 | $t_{LS}$ adds to path delay | Level-shifter delay penalty must be counted in critical path |

> ⚠️ **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When using equation (4) and (5), the **delay change is asymmetric** — halving $V_{DD}$ does *not* halve the delay; it can increase delay by 3×–5×. This non-linearity is what allows Multi-VDD to be *useful*: timing slack tolerates this in non-critical paths.

## 2.5 Level Shifter — The Mandatory Bridge

A signal leaving a low-$V_{DD}$ island cannot directly drive a gate in a high-$V_{DD}$ island, because the PMOS in the high-$V_{DD}$ domain would never turn OFF fully (the input swing is too small relative to $V_{DD,high}$), causing **static leakage / shoot-through**.

A **Level Shifter** is therefore inserted at every island boundary. Two common topologies:

**(a) Conventional CMOS Level Shifter (Cross-Coupled Load)**
- Uses a differential pair driven by the low-swing signal.
- A cross-coupled PMOS load restores full $V_{DD,high}$ swing.
- Adds 1–4 gate delays and consumes area.

**(b) Single-Supply Level Shifter (using diode-connected devices or bootstrapping)**
- Used when the source and destination share a common ground.
- Slightly faster but has reduced noise margin.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why is a level shifter required between two voltage islands?"* — Answer: **To prevent static DC current in the high-$V_{DD}$ gate's PMOS network when driven by a low-swing input**, and to restore full logic levels for reliable operation.

## 2.6 Engineering Trade-offs Summary

| Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|
| Large dynamic power savings ($\propto V^2$) | Requires complex power-routing in physical design |
| Significant leakage reduction on low-$V_{DD}$ islands | Level shifters add delay, area, and their own power overhead |
| Granular control — block-by-block optimization | Floor-planning constraints (islands must be physically clustered) |
| Compatible with clock gating and power gating | Multiple power rails need on-chip voltage regulators |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Power Saving Percentage for a Two-Island Chip

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A SoC has two voltage islands. Island A occupies **60 %** of the total switching capacitance ($\beta_A = 0.6$) and runs at $V_A = 1.2$ V. Island B occupies 40 % ($\beta_B = 0.4$) and runs at $V_B = 0.8$ V. The baseline design (single-VDD) uses $V_{high} = 1.2$ V. Compute the **percentage power saving** due to Multi-VDD.

### Step-by-Step Solution

We start with the general Multi-VDD dynamic-power expression:

$$
P_{dyn,\,multi} = \alpha \, C_{tot} \, f \sum_{i} \beta_i V_i^{\,2}
$$

For our two-island chip ($i = A, B$):

$$
P_{dyn,\,multi} = \alpha \, C_{tot} \, f \left[\,\beta_A V_A^{\,2} + \beta_B V_B^{\,2}\,\right]
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
P_{dyn,\,multi} = \alpha \, C_{tot} \, f \left[\,0.6 \times (1.2)^2 + 0.4 \times (0.8)^2\,\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the squared voltages.

$$
(1.2)^2 = 1.44 \quad,\qquad (0.8)^2 = 0.64
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the corresponding capacitance fractions.

$$
0.6 \times 1.44 = 0.864 \quad,\qquad 0.4 \times 0.64 = 0.256
$$

**Step 4:** Sum the weighted contributions.

$$
0.864 + 0.256 = 1.120
$$

So:

$$
P_{dyn,\,multi} = \alpha \, C_{tot} \, f \times 1.120
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the baseline single-VDD power at $V_{high} = 1.2$ V.

$$
P_{dyn,\,single} = \alpha \, C_{tot} \, f \times (1.2)^2 = \alpha \, C_{tot} \, f \times 1.44
$$

**Step 6:** Form the savings ratio.

$$
\text{Power Savings} = 1 - \frac{P_{dyn,\,multi}}{P_{dyn,\,single}} = 1 - \frac{1.120}{1.44}
$$

**Step 7:** Final numerical evaluation.

$$
\frac{1.120}{1.44} \approx 0.7778
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Power Savings} = 1 - 0.7778 = 0.2222 \;\Rightarrow\; 22.22\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even though Island B runs at a *significantly* lower voltage (0.8 V vs 1.2 V, i.e., a 33 % reduction), the *overall* chip power saving is only ~22 %, because the high-$V_{DD}$ island still dominates. This is why KTU problems emphasize **which fraction of capacitance is on which island** — the savings depend on both *how low* the voltage goes *and* *how much* of the chip is at low voltage.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Delay Increase When Lowering $V_{DD}$

**Problem Statement:**
A CMOS inverter has $V_{TH} = 0.3$ V. Using the simplified delay model $t_d \propto V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2$, find the **delay ratio** $t_d(V_{DD}=0.8\text{V}) \,/\, t_d(V_{DD}=1.2\text{V})$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the delay at high voltage.

$$
t_d^{high} \propto \frac{V_{DD,high}}{(V_{DD,high} - V_{TH})^2} = \frac{1.2}{(1.2 - 0.3)^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate numerator and denominator.

$$
1.2 - 0.3 = 0.9 \quad\Rightarrow\quad (0.9)^2 = 0.81
$$

$$
t_d^{high} \propto \frac{1.2}{0.81} = 1.4815
$$

**Step 3:** Write the delay at low voltage.

$$
t_d^{low} \propto \frac{0.8}{(0.8 - 0.3)^2} = \frac{0.8}{(0.5)^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$
(0.5)^2 = 0.25
$$

$$
t_d^{low} \propto \frac{0.8}{0.25} = 3.2
$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio.

$$
\frac{t_d^{low}}{t_d^{high}} = \frac{3.2}{1.4815} \approx 2.16
$$

$$
\boxed{t_d \text{ increases by a factor of } \approx 2.16\times \text{ when } V_{DD} \text{ drops from 1.2 V to 0.8 V}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that we reduced voltage by only **33 %** (1.2 → 0.8 V) but the delay *more than doubled* (2.16×). This super-linear delay penalty is exactly *why* Multi-VDD is mandatory: a blanket $V_{DD}$ reduction would catastrophically break critical timing, but the delay penalty is harmless on a path that has spare slack.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Energy-Optimal Voltage (Long-Channel Approximation)

The energy consumed per switching event is:

$$
E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

Including leakage energy over the gate's operating time $T_{op}$, the total energy per cycle is:

$$
E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} + I_{leak}(V_{DD}) \cdot V_{DD} \cdot T_{op}
$$

In the **sub-threshold regime** (very low $V_{DD}$), leakage current is dominated by sub-threshold conduction:

$$
I_{leak} \;\approx\; I_0 \, e^{\frac{V_{GS}-V_{TH}}{\eta V_T}} \quad\text{(exponential in }V_{DD}\text{)}
$$

Minimizing $E_{total}$ with respect to $V_{DD}$ yields the **energy-optimal voltage**:

$$
\boxed{V_{opt} \;\approx\; 2 V_{TH}}
$$

For $V_{TH} = 0.4$ V, $V_{opt} \approx 0.8$ V — which is exactly the typical *low* rail in Multi-VDD designs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for KTU Problems

When a KTU exam question says *"Design a Multi-VDD partition"*, follow this matrix:

| Question Asks | You Must Compute / State |
|---|---|
| **Power saving %** | Use $P_{savings} = 1 - \sum \beta_i (V_i/V_{high})^2$ |
| **Delay penalty** | Use $t_d \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD}-V_{TH})^2$ |
| **Number of level shifters** | One per signal crossing from low-$V_{DD}$ to high-$V_{DD}$ island boundary |
| **Critical-path check** | Confirm $t_{d,low} + t_{LS} \le T_{clk}$ |
| **Static/leakage** | Show that $I_{leak}$ is exponentially lower at low $V_{DD}$ for DIBL-scaled devices |
| **Energy optimum** | State $V_{opt} \approx 2V_{TH}$ for long-channel, lower for short-channel due to velocity saturation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Island Multi-VDD Architecture

The diagram below shows a typical two-VDD chip partitioned into a *high-performance core* (Island H) and a *low-power peripheral block* (Island L), with the mandatory **Level Shifter (LS)** placed between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighVDDIsland["ISLAND H - HIGH VDD 1.2V"]
        coreBlock["CRITICAL CORE BLOCK\nCritical ALU and Control Logic"]
    end

    subgraph LowVDDIsland["ISLAND L - LOW VDD 0.8V"]
        periBlock["NON CRITICAL PERIPHERAL BLOCK\nDisplay Controller and I/O"]
    end

    vddHigh[("V_DD_HIGH = 1.2 V")]:::powerRail
    vddLow[("V_DD_LOW = 0.8 V")]:::powerRail
    gnd[("GND = 0 V")]:::powerRail

    vddHigh --- coreBlock
    vddLow --- periBlock
    gnd --- coreBlock
    gnd --- periBlock

    coreBlock -- "high swing signal 0 to 1.2V" --> LS1["LEVEL SHIFTER\nRestores full 1.2V swing"]
    LS1 -- "drives high VDD island gate" --> periBlock

    periBlock -- "low swing signal 0 to 0.8V" --> LS2["LEVEL SHIFTER\nBoosts to 1.2V swing"]
    LS2 -- "drives critical core" --> coreBlock

    classDef powerRail fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid connections from power rails show that **two physically separate supply lines** enter the chip. The arrows crossing between islands are *always* routed through a level shifter. KTU examiners expect you to draw (or describe) this exact structure.

---

## 4.2 Multi-VDD Design Methodology Flow

The figure below maps the **step-by-step design flow** that KTU expects students to reproduce in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: RTL Design and Synthesis"] --> B["Perform Static Timing Analysis"]
    B --> C{"Identify critical\nand non critical paths"}
    C -- "critical path gates" --> D["Assign HIGH VDD\ndefault voltage"]
    C -- "non critical path gates" --> E["Assign LOW VDD\nreduce voltage"]
    D --> F["Partition floorplan into\nvoltage islands"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Place level shifters\nat island boundaries"]
    G --> H["Route two separate\npower grids"]
    H --> I{"Re run STA with\nlevel shifter delays"}
    I -- "timing met" --> J["Tape out Multi VDD chip"]
    I -- "timing violated" --> K["Raise LOW VDD\nor move gates to HIGH VDD"]
    K --> F

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style J fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style K fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```
