<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A, Module 3]`
> **Define the term "Transistor Stacking" in CMOS VLSI circuits. How does the "intermediate node voltage" (V_M) lead to leakage reduction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** *Definition:* Transistor stacking refers to a leakage-reduction configuration in which **two or more OFF transistors are connected in series** within a CMOS gate's pull-up or pull-down network. Because the inputs force multiple devices to be OFF simultaneously, the gate effectively presents a *stacked* OFF path.
- **(1 mark)** *V_M formation:* The subthreshold leakage current of the upper transistor (M2) tries to charge the intermediate node. Simultaneously, the leakage of the lower transistor (M1) discharges it. Steady-state KCL gives an intermediate voltage **V_M ≈ 50 mV – 300 mV** in deep-submicron CMOS.
- **(1 mark)** *Leakage reduction mechanism:* This V_M produces (a) **reverse body bias** on M1's source-to-body (raising $V_{th1}$ via the body effect), (b) **negative $V_{GS}$** on M2 (reducing its over-drive), and (c) **reduced $V_{DS}$** on M2 (reducing DIBL). All three combined *exponentially* suppress the subthreshold leakage. The total stack leakage is **5× to 50× smaller** than that of a single OFF transistor.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
> **What is meant by the "Stack Effect Factor" $N_S$? Mention its typical range in 90 nm and 65 nm CMOS processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** *Definition:* $N_S$ is defined as the **ratio of the OFF-state leakage current of a single transistor to the OFF-state leakage current of a stack of *n* series-connected OFF transistors** with the same input condition:

$$N_S = \frac{I_{leak,single}}{I_{leak,stack,n}}$$

- **(1 mark)** *Typical value, 90 nm:* $N_S \approx \mathbf{5 \text{ to } 20}$ for a 2-transistor stack.
- **(1 mark)** *Typical value, 65 nm:* $N_S \approx \mathbf{10 \text{ to } 50}$ for a 2-transistor stack (and even higher for 3+ stacks). The trend is that $N_S$ increases with technology scaling because DIBL amplifies the body-effect contribution.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided)

> **Part B – Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **transistor stacking effect** in a 2-NMOS stack. Show how the intermediate node voltage V_M is established and why it reduces subthreshold leakage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **steady-state value of V_M** using the subthreshold current equation and the body-effect threshold model, clearly stating all assumptions.

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **[2 marks] Circuit diagram & OFF conditions:** Draw a 2-NMOS stack (M2 on top, M1 on bottom). M2: drain = V_DD, gate = 0 V, source = V_M, body = 0 V. M1: drain = V_M, gate = 0 V, source = 0 V, body = 0 V. Both OFF since $V_{GS} \le V_{th}$.
2. **[2 marks] Intermediate node formation:** Explain that M2's subthreshold leakage charges node V_M, while M1's leakage discharges it. KCL at steady state gives $I_{M1} = I_{M2}$, yielding $V_M > 0$.
3. **[2 marks] Leakage-reduction mechanisms (three effects):**
   - *Body effect on M2:* $V_{SB2} = V_M$ increases $V_{th2}$ by $\gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_M} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$.
   - *Negative $V_{GS}$ on M2:* $V_{GS2} = -V_M$ exponentially reduces its drive.
   - *Reduced DIBL on M2:* $V_{DS2} = V_{DD} - V_M < V_{DD}$.
4. **[1 mark] Net effect:** Total stack leakage $I_{leak,stack} = I_{leak,single}/N_S$ with $N_S \approx 5$–$50$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **[1 mark] Assumption list:** Long-channel, $V_{DD} \gg V_T$, no DIBL initially.
2. **[2 marks] Write $I_{M1}$ and $I_{M2}$** using the subthreshold current equation:

$$I_{M1} = I_0 \cdot e^{-V_{th0}/(nV_T)} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-V_M/V_T}\right)$$

$$I_{M2} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_M - V_{th0} - \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_M}-\sqrt{2\phi_F})}{nV_T}\right) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-(V_{DD}-V_M)/V_T}\right)$$

3. **[2 marks] Apply KCL** $I_{M1} = I_{M2}$ and simplify to obtain the transcendental equation:

$$\ln\!\left(1 - e^{-V_M/V_T}\right) + \frac{V_M}{nV_T} + \frac{\gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_M}-\sqrt{2\phi_F})}{nV_T} = \ln\!\left(1 - e^{-(V_{DD}-V_M)/V_T}\right)$$

4. **[1 mark] Small-$V_M$ linearization** using $1 - e^{-V_M/V_T} \approx V_M/V_T$ and Taylor expansion of the body term:

$$V_M \approx V_T \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{V_M}{nV_T}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F}}\right)\right]$$

5. **[1 mark] Result & physical interpretation:** For $V_{DD} = 1$ V, $V_T = 26$ mV, $n = 1.4$, $\gamma = 0.4$ V$^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.7$ V, the numerical solution gives $V_M \approx 0.08$–$0.1$ V. The small magnitude of $V_M$ (just a few times $V_T$) is enough to reduce leakage by an order of magnitude because the dependence is exponential.

---

> **Part B – Question B (Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **OFF-state leakage current of a single OFF NMOS** vs. that of a **2-transistor NMOS stack** in deep-submicron CMOS. Show clearly the role of body effect, negative $V_{GS}$, and DIBL.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **input-vector dependence** of leakage in a 2-input NAND gate. Identify the *minimum-leakage input vector* and explain how this concept is exploited in **Input-Vector Control (IVC)** for low-power standby operation.

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **[2 marks] Single OFF NMOS leakage:** With $V_{GS}=0$, $V_{DS}=V_{DD}$, body tied to source: $I_{leak,single} = I_0 \cdot \exp(-V_{th0}/(nV_T)) \cdot (1 - e^{-V_{DD}/V_T}) \approx I_0 \cdot e^{-V_{th0}/(nV_T)}$ for $V_{DD} \gg V_T$. The threshold is unboosted — no body effect since $V_{SB} = 0$, and DIBL reduces $V_{th}$ slightly via the $-\eta V_{DD}$ term.
2. **[2 marks] 2-NMOS stack leakage:** With both OFF, $V_M$ is positive. Three effects kick in:
   - *Body effect on M2:* $V_{th2} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F+V_M}-\sqrt{2\phi_F}) > V_{th0}$.
   - *Negative gate drive on M2:* $V_{GS2} = -V_M$, reducing the exponential term.
   - *DIBL reduction on M2:* $V_{DS2} = V_{DD} - V_M$, weakening the DIBL-induced $V_{th}$ drop.
3. **[2 marks] Combined effect:** The current through the stack is $I_{leak,stack} = I_0 \cdot e^{-V_{th0}/(nV_T)} \cdot (1 - e^{-V_M/V_T})$, which is much smaller than $I_{leak,single}$ since the (1 − e^(−V_M/V_T)) factor is small. The ratio $N_S = I_{leak,single}/I_{leak,stack} \approx 5$–$50$ in modern CMOS.
4. **[1 mark] Concluding remark:** Stacking exploits the exponential nature of subthreshold conduction to achieve large leakage savings with negligible area overhead.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **[2 marks] 2-input NAND schematic & four input vectors:** List (A=0,B=0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1) and identify the NMOS/PMOS OFF configurations for each.
2. **[2 marks] Leakage values per input vector:**
   - (0,0): 2 NMOS OFF → lowest NMOS leakage (divided by $N_S$); both PMOS ON.
   - (0,1) & (1,0): 1 NMOS OFF → single-transistor leakage.
   - (1,1): 0 NMOS OFF (both ON), 2 PMOS OFF → PMOS-stack leakage on the high side.
3. **[1 mark] Minimum-leakage vector:** For a 2-input NAND, the minimum-leakage vector is **(A=0, B=0)** because both NMOS form a 2-stack.
4. **[2 marks] Input-Vector Control (IVC):** In standby/sleep mode, the designer *freezes* the inputs of each gate to its minimum-leakage vector. A small state machine (or scan-chain register) drives these "idle" values, achieving cumulative leakage savings of 2×–5× across a large chip, with **zero dynamic-power penalty and zero area overhead** beyond the input-control logic.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KSEB Examiner 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing $V_T$ (thermal voltage) with $V_{th}$ (threshold voltage).** The board deducts ½ mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Writing $V_{GS2} = 0$ V instead of $V_{GS2} = -V_M$.** This is the most common mistake. Always state $V_{GS2}$ relative to the **source** of M2 (which is V_M), not the global ground.
> 3. **Forgetting the body effect on M2.** Many students derive V_M using a *constant* $V_{th0}$ for both transistors, missing the crucial $V_{th0} + \gamma(\ldots)$ term.
> 4. **Skipping the KCL statement** ($I_{M1} = I_{M2}$). The intermediate node voltage emerges *from* KCL — without it, the derivation is incomplete.
> 5. **Memorizing numerical V_M values** (e.g., "V_M = 100 mV always"). Examiners expect symbolic answers; technology-dependent numbers may not earn credit.
> 6. **Omitting the input-vector-dependence analysis** when asked about leakage in standard cells. Always tabulate the leakage for *all* input vectors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Transistor Stacking):**
>
> - **Definition:** Two or more OFF transistors in series within a CMOS gate form a *transistor stack*. The intermediate node V_M is non-zero.
> - **Why leakage drops:** Three concurrent effects at the intermediate node:
>   1. *Body effect* on the upper transistor: $V_{th2} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_M} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
>   2. *Negative $V_{GS}$* on the upper transistor: $V_{GS2} = -V_M$.
>   3. *Reduced DIBL* on the upper transistor: $V_{DS2} = V_{DD} - V_M$.
> - **V_M value:** Determined by **KCL** at the intermediate node: $I_{M1}(V_M) = I_{M2}(V_M)$. For 90 nm CMOS, $V_M \approx 80$–$200$ mV.
> - **Stack effect factor $N_S$:** $N_S = I_{leak,single} / I_{leak,stack} \approx 5$–$50$, increasing with technology scaling.
> - **Key formula:** $I_{leak,stack} = I_0 \cdot e^{-V_{th0}/(nV_T)} \cdot (1 - e^{-V_M/V_T})$.
> - **Input-vector dependence (IVD):** Different input vectors yield different stack depths → different leakage. The minimum-leakage input vector is chosen for standby.
> - **Input-Vector Control (IVC):** Hold the minimum-leakage vector in sleep mode to save 2×–5× standby power.
> - **Trade-off:** Stacking reduces leakage but increases **delay** (because the ON-state current is shared across the stack). For 2-stack NAND, delay penalty is ≈ 30%–50% vs. an inverter.
> - **Use cases:** Idle/standby leakage reduction, IoT/wearable power budgets, security hardening (DPA-resistant logic), power-gating complementary technique.
> - **Comparison with power gating:** Power gating gives 100×–1000× leakage reduction (active switch), stacking gives 5×–50× (passive, no extra supply). They are often combined.
> - **NOT to be confused with:** *Stack architecture* (3D IC stacking) or *transistor stacking in SiGe HBTs* — these are unrelated concepts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MTCMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MTCMOS — Multi-Threshold CMOS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **MTCMOS (Multi-Threshold CMOS)** is a circuit-level standby leakage reduction technique in which **high-Vth** sleep transistors (header and/or footer) are inserted between the actual logic block (built using **low-Vth** transistors for speed) and the power/ground rails. During active mode, sleep transistors are ON; during standby (sleep) mode, they are turned OFF, virtually eliminating sub-threshold leakage of the low-Vth logic.

## 1.1 Why MTCMOS? — The Leakage Power Crisis

As CMOS technology scaled into the deep-submicron (DSM) regime, supply voltage $V_{DD}$ was reduced to control dynamic power. To keep delay in check, the threshold voltage $V_{th}$ also had to be lowered. The exponential dependence of sub-threshold leakage on $V_{th}$ caused **static (leakage) power** to rise dramatically.

The sub-threshold leakage current is given by:

$$I_{leak} \;=\; I_{0}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS}-V_{th}}{nV_{T}}\right)\left(1-\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_{T}}\right)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical constant worth remembering:** Thermal voltage at room temperature (T = 300 K) is **$V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$**.

A **30% reduction in $V_{th}$** can produce **~10× higher leakage**. MTCMOS elegantly solves this by **decoupling performance from leakage**: fast low-$V_{th}$ transistors do the computation, while robust high-$V_{th}$ transistors act as "valves" on the supply.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Main Tap and Drain" Model

Imagine your house has two water lines:
- **Main Tap (Low-Vth logic):** A wide, fast pipe that delivers water instantly when you open it — perfect for a shower, but it leaks badly when closed.
- **Drain Valve (High-Vth sleep transistor):** A heavy-duty gate valve installed right at the meter.

When you **need water (active mode)**, you open the drain valve fully — water rushes through the main tap with no perceptible delay. When you **go to sleep at night (standby mode)**, you shut the drain valve tightly — even if the main tap drips (leaks), it cannot reach the pipe network because the master valve is sealed. This is exactly how MTCMOS works.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks

| Element | Transistor Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Logic Block | Low-$V_{th}$ (LVT) | High-speed computation |
| Header Sleep PMOS | High-$V_{th}$ (HVT) | Cuts $V_{DD}$ connection in sleep |
| Footer Sleep NMOS | High-$V_{th}$ (HVT) | Cuts GND connection in sleep |
| Control Signal | Sleep / $\overline{\text{Sleep}}$ | Mode select |

> [!NOTE]
> A circuit can use either the **header**, the **footer**, or **both** — leading to three flavours of MTCMOS studied in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Modes — The Two States

MTCMOS circuits are governed by a single control line **SLP** (active-low sleep, sometimes written $\overline{S}$).

### 2.1.1 Active Mode (SLP = 0)
- The sleep PMOS header is **ON** ($V_{GS} \approx -V_{DD}$); the footer NMOS is **ON** ($V_{GS} \approx V_{DD}$).
- Both sleep transistors behave as small resistors, each contributing a tiny voltage drop.
- The virtual $V_{DD}$ ($V_{DDV}$) ≈ $V_{DD}$ and virtual GND ($GNDV$) ≈ 0 V.
- The internal low-$V_{th}$ logic runs at full speed, identical to a conventional CMOS gate.

### 2.1.2 Standby / Sleep Mode (SLP = 1)
- The header PMOS turns **OFF**; footer NMOS turns **OFF**.
- The virtual rails **float**: $V_{DDV}$ decays toward GND, $GNDV$ rises toward $V_{DD}$.
- The low-$V_{th}$ logic transistors see **$V_{DS} \approx 0$** and **$|V_{GS}| \approx 0$**, suppressing their sub-threshold leakage by orders of magnitude.
- Any residual leakage from the logic block must now flow through the OFF high-$V_{th}$ sleep transistor, which itself has negligible leakage because of its high $V_{th}$.

## 2.2 Sleep Transistor Sizing

A **critical KTU question** is: how wide should the sleep transistor be?

The sleep transistor must be wide enough that during the worst-case active-mode switching event, the voltage drop across it does not slow down the logic.

The worst-case discharge current is the saturation current of an LVT NMOS in the logic block:

$$I_{sleep}^{\text{discharge}} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{logic}\!(V_{GS}-V_{th,L})^{2}$$

Allowable voltage drop on the virtual rail (typically **5%–10% of $V_{DD}$**):

$$\Delta V_{VDD} \;=\; I_{peak}\cdot R_{sleep} \;<\; 0.1\,V_{DD}$$

For a sleep transistor in linear region:

$$R_{sleep} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\mu C_{ox}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep}\!(V_{GS}-V_{th,H}-V_{DS})}$$

Combining gives the **required minimum sleep transistor width**:

$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{peak}}{\mu C_{ox}\,(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})\,\Delta V_{VDD}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU problems, **assume $\Delta V \approx 0.1\,V_{DD}$** unless specified otherwise, and use $\mu_n \approx 2.5\,\mu_p$.

## 2.3 Sleep Transistor Leakage (Standby Mode)

In sleep, the only leakage path is through the high-$V_{th}$ sleep device:

$$I_{off,\ sleep} \;=\; I_{0,H}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,H}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

Because $V_{th,H}$ is roughly **150–200 mV higher** than $V_{th,L}$, the leakage reduction factor is:

$$\dfrac{I_{off,L}}{I_{off,H}} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{th,H}-V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right) \;\approx\; 10^{3}\ \text{to}\ 10^{5}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE questions for Module 3.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sub-threshold leakage | $I_{leak}=I_0\exp[(V_{GS}-V_{th})/nV_T]$ | A (Amperes) |
| 2 | Thermal voltage (300 K) | $V_T=kT/q$ | **25.85 mV** |
| 3 | Leakage reduction factor | $\exp[\Delta V_{th}/nV_T]$ | $10^3$–$10^5$ per 150 mV |
| 4 | Sleep resistance (linear) | $R_{sleep}=1/[\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})]$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Sleep min width | $(W/L)_{sleep}=I_{peak}/[\mu C_{ox}(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})\Delta V]$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Energy in sleep mode | $E_{sleep}=V_{DD}\cdot I_{off,\ sleep}\cdot T_{sleep}$ | Joules |
| 7 | Active-mode penalty | $\Delta t_d \propto R_{sleep}\,C_{L}$ | seconds |

## 2.5 Variants of MTCMOS (KTU-Favoured Comparison)

| Variant | Where Sleep Tx is Placed | Wake-up Time | Voltage Bounce | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Header MTCMOS** | Between $V_{DD}$ and virtual $V_{DD}$ | Fast | Lower noise on GND | Logic dominated by leakage from PMOS |
| **Footer MTCMOS** | Between GND and virtual GND | Fast | Lower noise on $V_{DD}$ | Preferred — better GND integrity |
| **Header + Footer (Dual)** | Both sides | Slower wake-up | Lowest leakage | Ultra-low standby |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Leakage Current Ratio Between LVT and HVT

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Part-B questions on MTCMOS.

**Given:**
- Low-$V_{th}$ transistors with $V_{th,L}$
- High-$V_{th}$ transistors with $V_{th,H}$
- Both biased identically in the OFF state ($V_{GS}=0$, $V_{DS}=V_{DD}$)
- Sub-threshold swing $n \approx 1.3$, $V_T = 25.85$ mV

**Step 1 —** Write the OFF current for an LVT device (using $1-\exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)\approx 1$ when $V_{DS}\gg V_T$):

$$I_{off,L} \;=\; I_{0,L}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 2 —** Write the OFF current for an HVT device:

$$I_{off,H} \;=\; I_{0,H}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,H}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 3 —** Assume $I_{0,L}\approx I_{0,H}=I_0$ (since $I_0\propto \mu C_{ox}$, which depends mainly on process):

$$I_{off,L} \;\approx\; I_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

$$I_{off,H} \;\approx\; I_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,H}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 4 —** Form the ratio:

$$\dfrac{I_{off,L}}{I_{off,H}} \;=\; \dfrac{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,H}}{nV_{T}}\right)}$$

**Step 5 —** Apply the law $\exp(a)/\exp(b)=\exp(a-b)$:

$$\dfrac{I_{off,L}}{I_{off,H}} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{th,H}-V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 6 —** Substitute $\Delta V_{th}=V_{th,H}-V_{th,L}$:

$$\dfrac{I_{off,L}}{I_{off,H}} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta V_{th}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 7 —** Numerical sanity check with $\Delta V_{th}=150$ mV, $n=1.3$:

$$\dfrac{I_{off,L}}{I_{off,H}} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\dfrac{0.150}{1.3\times 0.02585}\right) \;=\; \exp(4.46)\;\approx\; 86.5$$

> So a **150 mV $V_{th}$ increase reduces leakage by ~87×**, and **200 mV gives ~239×**. This justifies why MTCMOS is so effective.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Sleep-Transistor Width

**Given:** A logic block draws a worst-case discharge current $I_{peak}=120\ \mu A$. $V_{DD}=1.2$ V, $V_{th,H}=0.5$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox}=300\ \mu A/V^{2}$, allowable virtual-rail drop $\Delta V=0.1 V_{DD}$.

**Step 1 —** Allowable drop:

$$\Delta V \;=\; 0.1\times 1.2 \;=\; 0.12\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 —** Apply the minimum-width formula:

$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{peak}}{\mu_n C_{ox}\,(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})\,\Delta V}$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute numbers:

$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{120\ \mu A}{300\ \mu A/V^{2}\times(1.2-0.5)\times 0.12}$$

$$=\; \dfrac{120}{300\times 0.7\times 0.12}$$

**Step 4 —** Denominator:

$$300\times 0.7 \;=\; 210,\qquad 210\times 0.12 \;=\; 25.2$$

**Step 5 —** Final ratio:

$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{120}{25.2} \;\approx\; 4.76$$

**Step 6 —** Conclusion: take $W/L \approx 5$ for the footer NMOS to limit the rail bounce to within 10%.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Active-Mode Delay Penalty

The sleep transistor adds a series resistance $R_{sleep}$ to the supply. The delay penalty when charging/discharging load capacitance $C_L$ is:

$$\Delta t_{d} \;=\; R_{sleep}\,C_{L}$$

For a footer of $W/L=5$ with $L=0.13\ \mu m$ and $C_L=50$ fF:

$$R_{sleep} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep}\!(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{300\mu\times 5\times 0.7}$$

$$=\; \dfrac{1}{1.05\times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 952\ \Omega$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta t_{d} \;=\; 952\times 50\times 10^{-15} \;\approx\; 47.6\ \text{ps}$$

If the original gate delay is 100 ps, the **delay overhead is ~48%**, which is significant. Hence sleep transistors are carefully sized.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Comparison

| Quantity | Active Mode (SLP=0) | Sleep Mode (SLP=1) |
|---|---|---|
| Sleep Tx state | ON (linear) | OFF (cut-off) |
| Virtual $V_{DD}$ | ≈ $V_{DD}$ | Floats → decays to GND |
| Virtual GND | ≈ 0 V | Floats → rises to $V_{DD}$ |
| Leakage path | $I_{off,H}$ (very small) | Suppressed $\times$ exp($\Delta V_{th}/nV_T$) |
| Delay | $t_d + \Delta t_d$ | — |
| Noise on real rail | None | In-rush during wake-up |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram (Active / Sleep Switching)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VDDLINE["VDD RAIL 1.2V"]
        A[VDD SUPPLY]
    end
    subgraph SLEEPMODE["MODE CONTROLLER"]
        B[SLP CONTROL SIGNAL]
    end
    subgraph HEADERTX["HEADER PMOS HIGH VTH"]
        C["SLEEP PMOS  W_h / L"]
    end
    subgraph LOGICBLOCK["LOGIC BLOCK LOW VTH"]
        D[INV1 LVT]
        E[INV2 LVT]
        F[NAND LVT]
    end
    subgraph FOOTERTX["FOOTER NMOS HIGH VTH"]
        G["SLEEP NMOS  W_f / L"]
    end
    subgraph GNDLINE["GND RAIL 0V"]
        H[GND REFERENCE]
    end

    A --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H
    B --> C
    B --> G

    style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram (Active ↔ Sleep)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ActiveMode
    ActiveMode: ACTIVE MODE
    ActiveMode: SLP equals 0
    ActiveMode: Sleep Tx are ON
    ActiveMode: Full speed computation
    ActiveMode: Leakage equals IOFF HVT

    SleepMode: STANDBY MODE
    SleepMode: SLP equals 1
    SleepMode: Sleep Tx are OFF
    SleepMode: Virtual rails float
    SleepMode: Leakage reduced by exp delta Vth over nVT

    ActiveMode --> SleepMode: SLEEP ASSERTED
    SleepMode --> ActiveMode: WAKE UP TRIGGERED

    note right of ActiveMode: Wake up time 5 to 20 cycles
    note right of SleepMode: Energy saving up to 99 percent
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit (Sleep-Mode Leakage Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LD1["LVT NMOS STACK  LEAKAGE"]
        Q1["LVT NMOS1  OFF"]
        Q2["LVT NMOS2  OFF"]
    end
    subgraph FTX["FOOTER HVT NMOS"]
        Q3["HVT NMOS  OFF"]
    end
    GNDX["VIRTUAL GND  RISING"] --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> VDDVX["VIRTUAL VDD  FALLING"]

    note1["Only sub threshold current through HVT footer remains"]
    Q1 -.- note1
```

## 4.4 Footer-Only MTCMOS Inverter (Transistor-Level Schematic)

```
         V_DD
          │
          │
          ├──────► (output node: V_out)
          │         │
        ┌─┴─┐       │
        │ P │  LVT  │
        │   │       │
        └───┘       │
          │         │
          ├─────────┘
          │           (virtual V_DD = V_DD because no header)
          │
        ┌─┴─┐
        │ N │  LVT
        │   │
        └───┘
          │
          ●──────────► Virtual_GND
          │
        ┌─┴─┐
        │ N │  HVT  (Sleep Footer)
        │   │  gate = SLP_bar
        └───┘
          │
         GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The two LVT transistors form a regular CMOS inverter. The HVT NMOS at the bottom is the **footer** — its gate is driven by $\overline{SLP}$. When $\overline{SLP}=0$, the footer is OFF and the entire circuit is in standby.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** — when both header AND footer are used, total leakage current passes through **both** sleep transistors. Multiply the reduction by both $V_{th}$ differences.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{th,H}$ with $V_{th,L}$** in the sizing formula — sleep Tx are HVT (higher $V_{th}$ → less drive → need wider device).
> 3. **Wrong exponent argument** — the leakage ratio uses $(V_{th,H}-V_{th,L})/(nV_T)$. Do **not** write $(V_{th,L}-V_{th,H})$.
> 4. **Ignoring $I_0$ assumption** — KTU accepts $I_{0,L}=I_{0,H}$ unless otherwise stated.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define MTCMOS. List its main advantages over standard CMOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Multi-Threshold CMOS (MTCMOS) is a standby leakage-power reduction technique in which **high-$V_{th}$** sleep transistors (header PMOS / footer NMOS) are inserted between the supply rails and a logic block composed of **low-$V_{th}$** transistors.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **High performance** in active mode (low-$V_{th}$ transistors).
- **Drastically reduced sub-threshold leakage** in standby mode (high-$V_{th}$ transistors gate the supply).
- **Simple to integrate** into existing CMOS standard-cell libraries.
- Compatible with existing **clock-gating / power-gating** infrastructure.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the role of a sleep transistor in MTCMOS? Why is it designed with a higher $V_{th}$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (2 marks):** The sleep transistor acts as a **switch** that disconnects the low-$V_{th}$ logic block from the power rail (header) or ground (footer) during standby, suppressing leakage. In active mode it remains ON, providing a near-ideal connection with minimal voltage drop.

**Why higher $V_{th}$? (1 mark):** A higher $V_{th}$ gives the sleep transistor a very low OFF-state leakage current ($I_{off}\propto\exp(-V_{th}/nV_T)$). Since the entire logic block's leakage must flow through this single OFF device in sleep, a high $V_{th}$ keeps the standby current minimal — typically $\mathbf{10^{3}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{5}}$ times smaller than that of the LVT logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ▶ Question A — MTCMOS Sizing and Leakage Analysis

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the operation of an MTCMOS circuit in active and sleep modes with a neat schematic.

**Solution:**

**1. Schematic description (2 marks):** Draw an inverter-style logic block formed by LVT PMOS ($M_1$) and LVT NMOS ($M_2$). Connect a high-$V_{th}$ NMOS footer $M_{slp}$ between $M_2$'s source and ground. The gate of $M_{slp}$ is driven by $\overline{SLP}$. The output $V_{out}$ is taken between $M_1$ and $M_2$.

**2. Active mode (SLP = 0) (2.5 marks):**
- $\overline{SLP}=0 \Rightarrow V_{GS}$ of $M_{slp}=0-0=0\Rightarrow M_{slp}$ is **OFF**... wait, this is wrong. Correct: $\overline{SLP}=0$ means the signal driving the gate is 0 V, but for an **NMOS footer we use SLP directly**, so SLP = 1 in active mode and SLP = 0 in sleep. With SLP = 1, $M_{slp}$ is ON.
- Virtual GND ≈ 0 V; behaviour identical to a standard CMOS inverter.
- Tiny voltage drop $\Delta V = I_{peak} R_{sleep}$ slows the gate slightly.

**3. Sleep mode (SLP = 0) (2.5 marks):**
- $M_{slp}$ OFF. Virtual GND floats up.
- Drain–source voltage of $M_2$ collapses to ≈ 0 V → its sub-threshold leakage is suppressed by stack effect.
- Only the tiny $I_{off}$ of the HVT $M_{slp}$ flows to GND.

#### (b) [7 marks] Compute the minimum width of a footer sleep transistor.

**Given:** $V_{DD}=1.2$ V, $V_{th,H}=0.45$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox}=270\ \mu A/V^{2}$, worst-case discharge current $I_{peak}=150\ \mu A$, allowed voltage drop $\Delta V = 0.1 V_{DD}$.

**Step 1 —** Compute allowed drop:

$$\Delta V \;=\; 0.1\times 1.2 \;=\; 0.12\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 —** Apply formula:

$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{peak}}{\mu_n C_{ox}\,(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})\,\Delta V}$$

**Step 3 —** Plug in:

$$=\; \dfrac{150\ \mu A}{270\ \mu A/V^{2}\times 0.75\times 0.12}$$

$$=\; \dfrac{150}{24.3} \;\approx\; 6.17$$

**Step 4 —** Take $(W/L)_{sleep}\approx 7$ to add a 10 % safety margin. (1 mark)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** With $L=0.13\ \mu m$, $W\approx 0.9\ \mu m$. This wide HVT device guarantees the rail-bounce constraint is met.

---

### ▶ Question B — Leakage Reduction Derivation

#### (a) [7 marks] Derive the leakage current reduction ratio when a logic block is gated by an HVT footer.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the sub-threshold leakage expression for an OFF transistor with $V_{GS}=0$, $V_{DS}=V_{DD}$:

$$I_{off} \;=\; I_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** For the LVT logic block, total leakage is sum across all transistors:

$$I_{logic,\ leak} \;=\; N\cdot I_{0,L}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

where $N$ is the number of parallel leakage paths.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** In sleep mode the leakage must flow through the OFF HVT footer; the HVT device enters the **reverse-mode regime** because its drain and source swap roles. For analysis, treat it as a single HVT transistor in cut-off:

$$I_{leak,\ sleep} \;\approx\; I_{0,H}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{th,H}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Form the ratio:

$$\dfrac{I_{logic}}{I_{leak,\ sleep}} \;=\; N\cdot\dfrac{I_{0,L}}{I_{0,H}}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{th,H}-V_{th,L}}{nV_{T}}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** With $N\gg 1$ and $I_{0,L}\approx I_{0,H}$, the dominant factor is the exponential. Even with $N=1000$, a 200 mV $\Delta V_{th}$ gives $\exp(5.96)\approx 388$, comfortably overcoming the multiplier.

#### (b) [7 marks] An MTCMOS circuit uses LVT logic with $V_{th,L}=0.30$ V and an HVT footer with $V_{th,H}=0.50$ V. Calculate the standby-leakage reduction ratio at 300 K, assuming $n=1.3$.

**Step 1 —** Constants:

$$V_T \;=\; \dfrac{kT}{q} \;\approx\; 25.85\ \text{mV},\qquad \Delta V_{th} \;=\; 0.20\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the exponent:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_{th}}{nV_{T}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.20}{1.3\times 0.02585} \;=\; \dfrac{0.20}{0.033605} \;\approx\; 5.95$$

**Step 3 —** Ratio:

$$\dfrac{I_{logic}}{I_{sleep}} \;\approx\; \exp(5.95) \;\approx\; 383$$

**Step 4 —** Interpretation (2 marks):
- Standby leakage is reduced by **~383×** simply by inserting the HVT footer.
- Energy saved in a 1-second standby:

$$\Delta E \;=\; (I_{logic}-I_{sleep})\cdot V_{DD}\cdot 1\text{s} \;\approx\; 382\cdot I_{sleep}\cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** A mere 200 mV $V_{th}$ increase yields nearly three orders of magnitude leakage reduction, validating MTCMOS as a highly effective standby-power technique.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — MTCMOS**

- ⭐ **Core idea:** Decouple **performance (low-$V_{th}$)** from **leakage (high-$V_{th}$ sleep Tx)**.
- ⭐ **Three flavours:** Header, Footer, Dual — Footer is most common.
- ⭐ **Active mode (SLP=0 / SLP=1 depending on convention):** Sleep Tx ON, virtual rail ≈ real rail.
- ⭐ **Sleep mode:** Sleep Tx OFF, virtual rails float, leakage suppressed by $\exp(\Delta V_{th}/nV_T)$.
- ⭐ **Master formula (Leakage Reduction):**
$$\dfrac{I_{off,L}}{I_{off,H}}=\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{th,H}-V_{th,L}}{nV_T}\right)$$
- ⭐ **Master formula (Sleep sizing):**
$$\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{sleep}=\dfrac{I_{peak}}{\mu C_{ox}(V_{DD}-V_{th,H})\Delta V}$$
- ⭐ **Trade-off:** Wider sleep Tx → less rail bounce but larger area, more leakage, more capacitance.
- ⭐ **Wake-up latency:** 5–20 clock cycles to recharge virtual rail — important for short idle periods.
- ⭐ **In-rush current:** When waking, all internal nodes charge simultaneously → IR drop on real rails.
- ⭐ **Numerical anchors:** $V_T=25.85$ mV @ 300 K; typical $\Delta V_{th}=150$–200 mV gives $10^{2}$–$10^{3}$ leakage reduction per sleep Tx.
- ⭐ **Exam tip:** Always state your assumptions ($I_{0,L}=I_{0,H}$, $\Delta V=0.1V_{DD}$, $n=1.3$) before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VTCMOS, Power Gating & Clock Gating Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** VTCMOS, Power Gating & Clock Gating Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Variable Threshold CMOS (VTCMOS)

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Threshold CMOS (VTCMOS)** is an adaptive body-biasing technique where the substrate bias voltage ($V_{BB}$ for NMOS, applied to the p-substrate / p-well, and $V_{BP}$ for PMOS, applied to the n-well) is dynamically adjusted based on the operating mode of the circuit. In **active mode**, a forward/reverse body bias is applied to **lower** the threshold voltage ($V_T$) for high-speed operation; in **standby (sleep) mode**, a **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** is applied to **raise** the threshold voltage, exponentially suppressing subthreshold leakage current.

**Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
- Thermal Voltage: $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \textbf{25.85 mV}$ at **300 K**
- Body-effect coefficient (typical for bulk CMOS): $\gamma \approx \textbf{0.3 to 0.5 V}^{1/2}$
- Subthreshold swing limit (Boltzmann): $S = 60 \text{ mV/decade}$ at room temperature

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of VTCMOS like a **car with adjustable suspension stiffness**. When you are driving on a highway (active mode), you lower the suspension (lower $V_T$) for a smooth, fast, responsive ride (high drive current). When you park the car in the garage for a week (standby mode), you raise the suspension (higher $V_T$) so that even a small child cannot push it (leakage current is suppressed). The "adjustment knob" here is the body-bias voltage $V_{BB}$.

---

### 1.2 Power Gating

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Gating** is a circuit-level power-reduction technique in which the power supply ($V_{DD}$) of an idle circuit block is **physically disconnected** (using a high-$V_T$ "sleep transistor" header/footer) during standby mode, completely eliminating dynamic as well as static leakage power in that block. A "virtual $V_{DD}$" (or virtual GND) rail is created between the actual supply rail and the gated block.

**Key Components:**
- **Header Switch (PMOS)**: Placed between true $V_{DD}$ and the block's internal $V_{DDV}$ rail (virtual supply).
- **Footer Switch (NMOS)**: Placed between the block's internal GND and the true GND.
- **Sleep Transistor Sizing**: Typically 2–3× larger than a regular transistor to maintain acceptable IR-drop during active mode.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine every room in your house has a **master light switch on the main circuit breaker**. When you leave for vacation, you flip the breaker to OFF (sleep mode). Even if your refrigerator (the circuit block) tries to leak electricity through faulty wiring (subthreshold leakage), the master switch physically cuts the supply — so absolutely zero current flows. Power gating is that master switch at the chip level.

---

### 1.3 Clock Gating

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Gating** is a sequential power-reduction technique that **disables the clock signal** to idle flip-flops, latches, or entire functional units when their outputs are not being used. Since dynamic power is directly proportional to the switching activity ($\alpha$) and the clock toggles the most frequently in a synchronous design, gating the clock is one of the most effective ways to reduce **dynamic (switching) power**.

**Key Metrics:**
- Toggle rate reduction: Clock typically accounts for **30%–50%** of total dynamic power in synchronous CMOS designs.
- Glitch-free gating is enforced using **latch-based AND-gate clock gating** to avoid partial clock pulses that could corrupt flip-flop states.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Picture a row of ceiling fans in a long corridor. Every fan is connected to the same master switch. If only one person is in the corridor at the far end, you wouldn't leave all 20 fans running. You would walk down and switch off the unused fans. Clock gating does exactly this at the silicon level — it identifies which flip-flops are not required to capture new data this cycle and disconnects their clock, so their internal capacitances don't charge/discharge needlessly.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All three techniques — VTCMOS, Power Gating, and Clock Gating — fall under **Module 3: Low-Power Design Techniques** and target different sources of power dissipation: VTCMOS targets **subthreshold leakage**, Power Gating targets **all leakage + idle dynamic**, and Clock Gating targets **active dynamic power**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 VTCMOS — Operational Logic

The substrate bias modulates the threshold voltage via the **body effect**:

1. **Active Mode (High Performance):** $V_{BB} = 0$ V (zero body bias) or slight **Forward Body Bias (FBB)**. The transistor operates at its nominal or even lower threshold $V_T$, providing maximum drive current and shortest delay.
2. **Standby Mode (Low Leakage):** A **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** is applied (e.g., $V_{BB} = -1$ V to NMOS substrate, $V_{BP} = +1$ V to PMOS n-well). This raises $V_T$ by hundreds of millivolts, reducing subthreshold leakage by orders of magnitude.
3. **Transition Overhead:** Switching between modes requires charge-pump circuits and incurs wake-up latency (typically 10–100 µs).

### 2.2 Power Gating — Operational Logic

1. **Active Mode:** The sleep transistor (header PMOS or footer NMOS) is turned ON by applying $\overline{SLEEP} = 0$ (for header). The block receives nearly full $V_{DD}$ and operates normally.
2. **Standby Mode:** $\overline{SLEEP} = 1$ is applied. The header PMOS is switched OFF, disconnecting $V_{DD}$. The internal nodes of the block float, retaining state if needed (state-retention power gating) or being lost (destructive power gating).
3. **Wake-up Penalty:** A large in-rush current charges the block's internal capacitance, causing supply noise ("rush current") and wake-up latency.
4. **Modes:**
   - **Destructive Power Gating:** State lost on power-down.
   - **State-Retention Power Gating (SRPG):** Critical state preserved in retention latches/flip-flops using a separate always-on supply.

### 2.3 Clock Gating — Operational Logic

1. **Enable Signal Detection:** A control logic block generates an `EN` signal indicating whether a flip-flop group needs to capture data this clock cycle.
2. **Latch-Based AND Gating:** To prevent glitches on the gated clock when `EN` changes during the high phase of the master clock, the `EN` signal is first latched on the negative edge of the clock, then ANDed with the master clock:
   $$CK_{gated} = CK \cdot \text{LATCH}(EN)$$
3. **AND-gate Clock Gating:** A simpler form used when `EN` is guaranteed stable during the active high-phase of the clock.
4. **Granularity:** Can be applied at register level, block level, or module level (coarse-grained).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to avoid markdown syntax conflicts.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Subthreshold Leakage Current | $I_{leak} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{-V_T}{\eta V_{th}}}$ | $V_T$: threshold voltage, $\eta$: subthreshold ideality factor $\approx 1.3$, $V_{th}=kT/q$ | Quantifies static power in idle transistors |
| 2 | Body-Effect Threshold Voltage | $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F \vert}\right)$ | $V_{T0}$: zero-bias $V_T$, $\gamma$: body coeff, $\Phi_F$: Fermi potential, $V_{SB}$: source-body bias | Foundation of VTCMOS body-biasing |
| 3 | Leakage Reduction Factor (VTCMOS) | $R_{leak} = 10^{\frac{\Delta V_T}{S}}$ where $S = \eta V_{th} \ln 10$ | $\Delta V_T$: shift in threshold, $S$: subthreshold swing (mV/decade) | Predicts orders-of-magnitude leakage cut |
| 4 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$: activity factor, $C_L$: load cap, $V_{DD}$: supply, $f_{clk}$: clock freq | Drives clock-gating optimization |
| 5 | Static Leakage Power | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $V_{DD}$ in V, $I_{leak}$ in A | Reduced by VTCMOS / Power Gating |
| 6 | Virtual Rail IR-Drop (Power Gating) | $\Delta V = I_{active} \cdot R_{sleep}$ | $I_{active}$: active current, $R_{sleep}$: ON-resistance of sleep transistor | Sizing of sleep transistor |
| 7 | Sleep Transistor Sizing | $W_{sleep} \ge \frac{I_{peak}}{\mu_n C_{ox}(V_{DD}-V_T)}\cdot L$ | Trade-off: area vs. performance | Determines wake-up speed |
| 8 | Clock Gating Power Savings | $P_{saved} = \alpha_{clk}\cdot C_{clk}\cdot V_{DD}^2\cdot f_{clk}\cdot \text{Duty-Cycle Reduction}$ | Direct proportionality to gated FF count | EDA estimator for synthesis |

**Real-world Engineering Utility:**
- VTCMOS is used in **Intel's mobile Core i-series** (via adaptive body bias) for dynamic performance/leakage trade-offs.
- Power Gating is the foundation of **ARM's big.LITTLE** idle states (e.g., Cortex-A series retention + power-down).
- Clock Gating is **standard in every synthesis tool** (Synopsys DC, Cadence Genus) and used in virtually all ASIC designs since the 1990s.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Leakage Reduction via VTCMOS Body Biasing

**Given:** A 90 nm NMOS transistor in standby mode.  
**Zero-bias threshold:** $V_{T0} = 0.30$ V.  
**Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = 0.40$ V$^{1/2}$.  
**Fermi potential:** $2\Phi_F = 0.70$ V.  
**Applied reverse body bias:** $V_{SB} = -1.0$ V.  
**Subthreshold ideality factor:** $\eta = 1.3$.

**Step 1: Express the threshold voltage shift.**

$$V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F \vert}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} &= \sqrt{\vert 0.70 + (-1.0) \vert} = \sqrt{0.30} = 0.5477 \text{ V}^{1/2} \\
\sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F \vert} &= \sqrt{0.70} = 0.8367 \text{ V}^{1/2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the new threshold voltage.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_T &= 0.30 + 0.40 \cdot (0.5477 - 0.8367) \\
V_T &= 0.30 + 0.40 \cdot (-0.2890) \\
V_T &= 0.30 - 0.1156 \\
V_T &= 0.1844 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Critical Note:** Although $V_{SB}$ is negative, the body effect is computed relative to the *reverse-bias* depletion region, so the absolute-value formulation above (with the source-bulk voltage entering the squared term) correctly gives the magnitude. *Wait — let us re-check the sign convention:* For an NMOS, applying $V_{SB} < 0$ means the source is *above* the body potential, which is actually a **forward body bias (FBB)**. For true RBB in NMOS, we need $V_{SB} > 0$ (body is more negative than source). Let us redo with proper RBB: $V_{SB} = +1.0$ V.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\vert 0.70 + 1.0 \vert} &= \sqrt{1.70} = 1.3038 \text{ V}^{1/2} \\
V_T &= 0.30 + 0.40 \cdot (1.3038 - 0.8367) \\
V_T &= 0.30 + 0.40 \cdot 0.4671 \\
V_T &= 0.30 + 0.1868 \\
V_T &= 0.4868 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the shift $\Delta V_T$.**

$$\Delta V_T = V_T - V_{T0} = 0.4868 - 0.30 = +0.1868 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Compute the subthreshold swing $S$ at 300 K.**

$$S = \eta \cdot V_{th} \cdot \ln(10) = 1.3 \cdot 0.02585 \cdot 2.3026 \approx 0.0774 \text{ V/decade} = 77.4 \text{ mV/decade}$$

**Step 6: Compute the leakage reduction factor.**

$$R_{leak} = 10^{\frac{\Delta V_T}{S}} = 10^{\frac{0.1868}{0.0774}} = 10^{2.414} \approx 259 \times$$

**Interpretation:** Applying only $+1.0$ V of reverse body bias reduces the standby subthreshold leakage by approximately **259×** — equivalent to **2.4 decades** of suppression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sleep-Transistor Sizing for Power Gating

**Given:** A logic block with peak active current $I_{peak} = 50$ mA.  
Allowable IR-drop on the virtual rail: $\Delta V_{max} = 50$ mV (5% of 1.0 V $V_{DD}$).  
Sleep transistor: NMOS footer with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_T = 0.4$ V.

**Step 1: Compute the maximum allowed sleep-transistor ON-resistance.**

$$R_{sleep,max} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{I_{peak}} = \frac{0.050}{0.050} = 1.0\ \Omega$$

**Step 2: Express $R_{ON}$ of an NMOS in linear region.**

$$R_{ON} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)}$$

With $V_{GS} = V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_{GS} - V_T = 0.6$ V.

**Step 3: Solve for W/L.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{W}{L} &= \frac{1}{R_{ON} \cdot \mu_n C_{ox} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)} \\
\frac{W}{L} &= \frac{1}{1.0 \cdot 200\times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.6} \\
\frac{W}{L} &= \frac{1}{1.2\times 10^{-4}} \\
\frac{W}{L} &\approx 8333
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Assume minimum channel length $L = 90$ nm.**

$$W = 8333 \cdot 90\ \text{nm} = 750\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Interpretation:** A **750 µm wide** footer NMOS is required to keep the IR-drop within 5% — illustrating why power gating incurs **large area overhead** and motivates **distributed sleep-transistor grids** in modern designs.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Trade-offs

| Parameter | VTCMOS | Power Gating | Clock Gating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power type reduced** | Static (leakage) | Static + Idle dynamic | Active dynamic |
| **Mechanism** | Body bias ($V_{BB}$) | Sleep transistor | AND-gate on clock |
| **Wake-up time** | ~1–10 µs | ~10–100 µs (large $C_{dd}$) | ~1 clock cycle |
| **Energy overhead** | Bias generator (charge pump) | Sleep transistor area, rush current | Latch + AND gate |
| **State preservation** | Yes (always powered) | Optional (SRPG needed) | Yes (FF holds data) |
| **Best use case** | Performance-critical active/standby modes | Long idle periods (mobile sleep) | Any idle FF in active mode |
| **Area penalty** | Low | High (2–3× sleep transistor) | Low–Moderate |
| **Design complexity** | Moderate (well-tap engineering) | High (rush-current mgmt) | Low (synthesis tool) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 VTCMOS Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mode Detection Controller] --> B{Operating Mode?}
    B -- Active --> C[Set VBB = 0 or FBB]
    B -- Standby --> D[Set VBB = RBB]
    C --> E[Low VT Mode]
    D --> F[High VT Mode]
    E --> G[High Performance, High Leakage]
    F --> H[Low Leakage, Lower Performance]
    I[Charge Pump / Bias Generator] --> A
    J[Substrate / Well Contact Network] --> E
    J --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mode Detection Controller** monitors system activity (e.g., NoC traffic, CPU idle signals). It drives the **Charge Pump** to generate the required bias, which is applied via the **Well/Substrate Contact Network** to all NMOS bodies (for $-V_{BB}$) and PMOS bodies (for $+V_{BP}$).

---

### 4.2 Power Gating Architecture (Header + Footer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VDD True Supply] --> B[Header Sleep PMOS]
    B --> C[Virtual VDDV Rail]
    C --> D[Logic Block]
    D --> E[Footer Sleep NMOS]
    E --> F[GND True Ground]
    G[SLEEP BAR Signal] --> B
    H[SLEEP Signal] --> E
    I[Retention Latch Bank] --> C
    C --> I
    J[Always On AOVDD] --> I
```

**Annotations:**
- `VDD True Supply` — Chip-level power pin.
- `Header Sleep PMOS` — Cuts off $V_{DD}$ in standby.
- `Virtual VDDV Rail` — Decays to a held value (state retention) or floats to 0.
- `Retention Latch Bank` — Critical-state storage on separate always-on rail.
- `SLEEP / SLEEP BAR` — Complementary control from power-management unit.

---

### 4.3 Clock Gating Architecture (Latch-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Master Clock CK] --> B[AND Gate]
    C[Enable Signal EN] --> D[Negative Edge Latch]
    A --> D
    D --> B
    B --> E[Gated Clock CKgated]
    E --> F[Flip Flop Bank Group A]
    E --> G[Flip Flop Bank Group B]
    F --> H[Next Stage Logic]
    G --> H
```

**Annotations:**
- `Enable Signal EN` — Combinational signal indicating whether the FF group should capture data this cycle.
- `Negative Edge Latch` — Transparent only when master clock is high; holds EN during the low phase to ensure glitch-free ANDing.
- `Gated Clock CKgated` — Drives only the FFs whose EN is high; idle FFs see a frozen clock → no switching → no dynamic power.

---

### 4.4 Combined Low-Power Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Level Power Budget] --> B[Clock Gating Active Mode]
    A --> C[Power Gating Idle Blocks]
    A --> D[VTCMOS Adaptive Bias]
    B --> E[Register File, Pipeline Stages]
    C --> F[Unused IPs, Memory Banks]
    D --> G[Performance Paths, Critical Cores]
    E --> H[Final Power Savings Report]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** *Define Variable Threshold CMOS (VTCMOS). How does it achieve leakage reduction in standby mode?* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
VTCMOS is a body-biasing technique in which the substrate bias voltage of MOSFETs is dynamically varied based on the operating mode. In **active mode**, the body bias is set to zero (or forward-biased) to keep $V_T$ low for high speed. In **standby mode**, a **reverse body bias (RBB)** is applied, which raises $V_T$ via the body effect, exponentially reducing the subthreshold leakage current. *(2 marks)*

The leakage reduction factor is given by $R_{leak} = 10^{\Delta V_T / S}$ where $S$ is the subthreshold swing (~70–90 mV/decade). A $\Delta V_T$ of ~200 mV yields ~2–3 decades of leakage suppression. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** *What is clock gating? Why is latch-based AND gating preferred over simple AND gating?* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Clock gating is a dynamic-power reduction technique that disables the clock to idle flip-flops so that their load capacitances do not switch. *(1 mark)*

A simple AND gate between the master clock `CK` and the enable signal `EN` can produce **glitches** (partial pulses) if `EN` changes during the high phase of `CK`. These glitches can cause unintended state capture in the flip-flops — a functional hazard. *(1 mark)*

A **negative-edge-triggered latch** is placed before the AND gate. The latch holds `EN` stable during the high phase of `CK`, ensuring that the gated clock is either a clean full pulse or no pulse at all. This eliminates glitches and guarantees correct sequential behavior. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of **Power Gating** using a header PMOS sleep transistor. Discuss the trade-off between sleep-transistor sizing, wake-up latency, and rush current. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Topology:**  
A PMOS header transistor $M_{SLP}$ is placed between the external supply $V_{DD}$ and the internal virtual rail $V_{DDV}$ of the gated logic block. The control signal $\overline{SLEEP}$ is applied to the gate of $M_{SLP}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Active Mode ($\overline{SLEEP} = 0$):**  
$M_{SLP}$ is ON (saturated/linear depending on $V_{DDV}$). The block receives nearly full $V_{DD}$ and operates at full speed. A small IR-drop $\Delta V = I_{active} \cdot R_{ON,sleep}$ appears on $V_{DDV}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Standby Mode ($\overline{SLEEP} = V_{DD}$):**  
$M_{SLP}$ is OFF. The $V_{DDV}$ rail floats; if state-retention latches (powered by an always-on rail $AOV_{DD}$) are used, they preserve critical state. Otherwise, the block loses state ("destructive" gating). *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Trade-off Discussion:** *(4 marks)*
- **Sizing:** A larger $W/L$ of $M_{SLP}$ → lower $R_{ON}$ → smaller IR-drop and faster wake-up, but **larger area**, **larger leakage through the sleep transistor itself**, and **higher gate capacitance** to drive.
- **Wake-up latency:** Proportional to $R_{ON} \cdot C_{block}$ charging time. Larger sleep transistor → faster wake-up.
- **Rush current:** During wake-up, all internal nodes swing from 0 to $V_{DD}$ simultaneously, drawing a large in-rush current $I_{rush} = C_{block} \cdot dV/dt$. This causes supply noise (ground bounce) and can trip ESD clamps. Mitigation: **staged wake-up** or **daisy-chain power-gating**.
- **Energy vs. Latency Trade-off:** If the idle period $T_{idle}$ satisfies $T_{idle} > \tau_{wake} + \frac{E_{saved}}{P_{leak,active}}$, power gating is beneficial.

---

**(b)** Derive the **leakage reduction factor** achievable by VTCMOS if the threshold voltage shift is $\Delta V_T = 0.25$ V and the subthreshold swing $S = 80$ mV/decade. Compute the resulting standby leakage if the original $I_{leak} = 10\ \mu$A. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formula.**  
$$R_{leak} = 10^{\frac{\Delta V_T}{S}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{leak} &= 10^{\frac{0.25}{0.080}} \\
&= 10^{3.125} \\
&\approx 1334
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Standby Leakage.**

$$I_{leak,standby} = \frac{I_{leak}}{R_{leak}} = \frac{10\ \mu\text{A}}{1334} \approx 7.5\ \text{nA}$$

**Step 4 — Power Saving (assuming $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V).**  
$$P_{saved} = V_{DD} \cdot (I_{leak} - I_{leak,standby}) = 1.0 \cdot (10\ \mu\text{A} - 7.5\ \text{nA}) \approx 9.99\ \mu\text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**  
A mere $250$ mV threshold shift yields over **3 decades** of leakage suppression — illustrating the **exponential** effectiveness of VTCMOS. *(1 mark for interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Clock Gating** technique with a neat block diagram of a latch-based clock gating cell. How does it reduce dynamic power? Quantify the power savings for a 32-bit register file with 4096 FFs, where 75% of FFs are gated, clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1$ GHz, clock load capacitance $C_{clk} = 20$ fF per FF, activity factor before gating $\alpha = 1.0$, and $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram.**  
The latch-based clock gating cell consists of:
- A negative-edge-triggered D-latch with input `EN` and clock `CK`.
- A 2-input AND gate whose inputs are `CK` and the latch output.
- Output `CK_gated` drives a bank of FFs. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Operation.**  
When `EN = 1`, the latch passes the master clock through to the FF bank. When `EN = 0`, the latch holds the AND input low, blocking clock transitions. Because the latch captures `EN` only on the falling edge of `CK`, no glitches can occur on `CK_gated`. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Power Savings Formula.**  

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

Before gating (all 4096 FFs active): 

$$P_{before} = 1.0 \cdot (4096 \cdot 20\ \text{fF}) \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot 1\ \text{GHz} = 81.92\ \mu\text{W}$$

After gating (only 25% active): 

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{after} &= 1.0 \cdot (1024 \cdot 20\ \text{fF}) \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1\ \text{GHz} \\
&= 20.48\ \mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

(Here the active 25% still toggle with $\alpha = 1.0$; the gated 75% contribute $\alpha = 0$.) *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Savings.**

$$\Delta P = P_{before} - P_{after} = 81.92 - 20.48 = 61.44\ \mu\text{W} \quad (\approx 75\%\ \text{saving})$$

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Power Gating** and **VTCMOS** in terms of: (i) leakage reduction mechanism, (ii) wake-up time, (iii) state preservation, (iv) area overhead, and (v) typical use case. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Power Gating | VTCMOS |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Mechanism** | Disconnects $V_{DD}$ using sleep transistor; leakage → near 0 | Adjusts $V_T$ via body bias; leakage reduced exponentially but not eliminated |
| **(ii) Wake-up time** | High (10–100 µs) due to $R \cdot C$ charging of large virtual rail | Low (1–10 µs) — bias generator settling |
| **(iii) State preservation** | Requires separate retention latches (SRPG) on $AOV_{DD}$ | Implicit — supply is always connected |
| **(iv) Area overhead** | High — sleep transistor 2–3× normal size | Low — only well/substrate taps and charge pump |
| **(v) Use case** | Long idle periods (smartphone sleep, SoC deep-sleep) | Continuous performance-scaling (DVFS cores, mobile SoCs) | *(1.5 marks per row, 1 mark for concluding statement)*

**Concluding remark:** Modern designs use **both in tandem** — VTCMOS for fine-grained active/standby tuning within powered-on blocks, and power gating for coarse-grained shutdown of entire IPs. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in body effect** — Students often write $V_{SB}$ without noting that for **NMOS RBB**, $V_{SB} > 0$, while for **PMOS RBB**, $V_{BS} > 0$ (i.e., the body is more positive than source). Mixing signs costs **2 marks** in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting the "virtual rail"** — When drawing a power-gating schematic, students frequently connect the sleep transistor directly to logic without labeling $V_{DDV}$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this omission.
> 3. **Confusing clock gating with clock disabling** — A gated clock still toggles during active mode; it does not stay LOW forever. Students must clarify that gating is **per-cycle, based on EN**.
> 4. **Subthreshold swing value** — Use $S = \eta V_{th} \ln(10) \approx 60\ \eta$ mV/decade at room temperature. Writing $S = 60$ mV/decade assumes $\eta = 1$ (ideal), but real CMOS has $\eta = 1.2$–$1.5$.
> 5. **In Part B (14-mark) answers,** students often **skip the numerical interpretation**. Always end with a one-sentence engineering interpretation to capture the final 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [x] **VTCMOS** uses **dynamic body biasing** to modulate $V_T$ — active mode lowers $V_T$ (speed), standby mode raises $V_T$ (low leakage).
- [x] **Body effect equation:** $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F + V_{SB}\vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\Phi_F \vert})$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Leakage reduction** in VTCMOS is **exponential** in $\Delta V_T$: $R_{leak} = 10^{\Delta V_T / S}$.
- [x] **Power gating** physically disconnects $V_{DD}$ via a **sleep transistor** (header PMOS or footer NMOS).
- [x] **Sleep transistor sizing:** $W/L \approx I_{peak} / [\mu C_{ox}(V_{DD}-V_T) \cdot \Delta V_{max}/I_{peak}]$.
- [x] **Wake-up penalty:** $T_{wake} \propto R_{sleep} \cdot C_{block}$ — large for power gating.
- [x] **State Retention Power Gating (SRPG)** uses a separate always-on rail + retention latches.
- [x] **Clock gating** reduces **dynamic power** by stopping clock toggling in idle FFs.
- [x] **Latch-based AND gating** is mandatory for glitch-free clock gating.
- [x] **Dynamic power equation:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ — Clock gating reduces $\alpha$.
- [x] **Subthreshold swing** $S \approx 60$–$90$ mV/decade at 300 K — the Boltzmann limit is 60 mV/dec.
- [x] **Combined strategy:** Modern SoCs use **clock gating (always)** + **VTCMOS (DVFS)** + **power gating (deep sleep)** together.
- [x] **KTU exam tip:** For 14-mark questions, always draw a **neat labeled block diagram** first — it earns 1–2 marks before you even start calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fully Complementary Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fully Complementary Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Fully Complementary CMOS Logic** (also called **Static CMOS** or **Conventional CMOS Logic**) is a digital circuit design style in which every logic gate is constructed using *two mutually dual, mutually exclusive transistor networks*:

1. A **PMOS Pull-Up Network (PUN)** connected between **V_DD** and the output node.
2. An **NMOS Pull-Down Network (PDN)** connected between the output node and **GND (V_SS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The defining property of Fully Complementary Logic is that for every valid input combination, **exactly one of the two networks (PUN or PDN) is conducting**, while the other is in cutoff. The output is therefore always connected to either V_DD or GND through a low-resistance path, producing **full rail-to-rail logic swing (0 to V_DD)** with zero static current.

Mathematically, if the logic function realized at the output is **F**, the PUN implements **F** directly (conducts when F=1), and the PDN implements the complement **F̄** (conducts when F=0). The networks are said to be **topologically dual** — a PDN built from a series-parallel arrangement of NMOS transistors has a PUN that is a **dual graph** (series becomes parallel and vice versa) built from PMOS transistors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (CO4 – KTU 2024):** The student should be able to *analyze and design CMOS logic gates using the fully complementary approach* and *evaluate their power-performance trade-offs* — a core high-weight topic in Low Power VLSI Design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw of Switches"

Imagine a **mechanical see-saw** on a children's playground:

- One side is connected to the **high energy reservoir (V_DD)** via the **PMOS team** (the "Up" team).
- The other side is connected to the **ground (GND)** via the **NMOS team** (the "Down" team).
- The **inputs (A, B, C…)** are the children's feet pushing either end.

A strict playground rule states: **both teams may never push down at the same time** — that would create a direct short between V_DD and GND (a short-circuit, which in CMOS is forbidden because it causes power dissipation). Instead, when the PMOS team pushes, the output flies up to V_DD (logic '1'); when the NMOS team pushes, the output sinks to GND (logic '0'). When neither team is in the right formation, the output simply floats (only momentarily during switching).

The **complementary** word in the name reflects this duality: the PMOS logic and NMOS logic are *mirror images* of each other — wherever the PMOS network has a series connection, the NMOS network has a parallel one, and vice versa. This is called the **duality rule** of static CMOS design.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Transistor count for an n-input gate:** Typically **2n transistors** (n PMOS + n NMOS).
- **Power supply:** A single rail **V_DD** (typically **1.2 V** at 65 nm, **1.0 V** at 45 nm, **0.9 V** at 32 nm in modern KTU-curriculum nodes).
- **Threshold voltage reference:** For symmetric switching, **|V_TP| ≈ V_TN** (NMOS threshold).
- **Logic levels:** V_OH = V_DD, V_OL = 0 V (rail-to-rail).
- **Static power dissipation:** **P_static ≈ 0** (excluding subthreshold leakage).
- **Switching threshold (V_M or V_Inv):** Approximately **V_DD/2** for a balanced inverter.
- **Fan-out:** Each CMOS gate can typically drive **3–5 similar gates** before delay becomes significant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question asks for the "defining feature" of fully complementary logic in a 3-mark question, the correct one-liner answer is: *"Full output voltage swing with no static current path from V_DD to GND."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Heart of Static CMOS

The fully complementary logic gate is governed by **three fundamental design rules** (these are KTU-favorite exam questions):

### Rule 1 — The Duality Rule
The PUN and PDN must be **dual networks** of each other. A practical construction procedure:

> Start by drawing the **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** as a graph of NMOS transistors (series for AND, parallel for OR) implementing **F̄**. Then, to construct the PUN:
> - Replace every **series** NMOS connection with a **parallel** PMOS connection.
> - Replace every **parallel** NMOS connection with a **series** PMOS connection.
> - Invert the logic (PMOS transistors turn ON when their input is LOW).

### Rule 2 — The Conduction Rule (Mutual Exclusivity)
For any input vector, **either PUN conducts OR PDN conducts, but never both simultaneously**. This guarantees:
- **No static short-circuit current** from V_DD to GND.
- **Full rail-to-rail output swing.**

### Rule 3 — The Transistor Type Rule
- **PMOS transistors go in the PUN** (source tied to V_DD; they pull the output up when ON).
- **NMOS transistors go in the PDN** (source tied to GND; they pull the output down when ON).
- This is because of the natural polarity of carriers: PMOS passes a strong '1', NMOS passes a strong '0'.

---

## 2.2 The CMOS Inverter — The Simplest Fully Complementary Gate

The inverter is the fundamental building block, consisting of:
- **One PMOS** (load, source at V_DD, gate = input A, drain = output).
- **One NMOS** (driver, source at GND, gate = input A, drain = output).

**Truth Table:**

| Input A | NMOS | PMOS | Output F = Ā |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 (0 V) | OFF | ON | V_DD (Logic 1) |
| 1 (V_DD) | ON | OFF | 0 V (Logic 0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PMOS in the pull-up?** Because PMOS transistors pass a strong logic '1' (their threshold V_TP is negative, so the gate-to-source voltage V_GS is comfortably negative when ON, yielding a low |V_DS,sat|). NMOS, by contrast, would suffer a threshold voltage drop (V_out max = V_DD − V_TN), causing a "weak 1" and degraded noise margin.

---

## 2.3 NAND and NOR Gate Realization

### 2-Input CMOS NAND
Logic function: **F = (A·B)̄**
- **PDN (NMOS):** Two NMOS in **series** → conducts only when **A = 1 AND B = 1** (so F̄ = AB is pulled to GND, meaning F = 0).
- **PUN (PMOS):** Two PMOS in **parallel** → conducts when **A = 0 OR B = 0** (pulling output to V_DD, F = 1).
- **Transistor count: 4** (2 PMOS + 2 NMOS).

### 2-Input CMOS NOR
Logic function: **F = (A+B)̄**
- **PDN (NMOS):** Two NMOS in **parallel** → conducts when **A = 1 OR B = 1**.
- **PUN (PMOS):** Two PMOS in **series** → conducts only when **A = 0 AND B = 0**.
- **Transistor count: 4** (2 PMOS + 2 NMOS).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight — Series Transistor Degradation:** As more PMOS are stacked in series (e.g., 3-input NAND PUN, or NOR PUN), each transistor must drop less V_DS, reducing the drive current. This makes **wide NOR gates slow** and **wide NAND gates** relatively faster — a recurring question in KTU exams.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Description |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Output HIGH voltage | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Rail-to-rail HIGH level (no V_T drop thanks to PMOS) |
| 2 | Output LOW voltage | $V_{OL} = 0\ \text{V}$ | Rail-to-rail LOW level (strong NMOS pull-down) |
| 3 | Noise Margin HIGH | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - V_{IH}$ | Maximum tolerable noise at logic 1 |
| 4 | Noise Margin LOW | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = V_{IL} - 0$ | Maximum tolerable noise at logic 0 |
| 5 | Switching threshold | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | Point where V_in = V_out (often set to V_DD/2) |
| 6 | Condition for V_M = V_DD/2 | $k_n = k_p \Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}\,(W/L)_n}{2} = \dfrac{\mu_p C_{ox}\,(W/L)_p}{2}$ | Requires $(W/L)_p \approx 2 \text{ to } 3 \times (W/L)_n$ since μ_n ≈ 2.5 μ_p |
| 7 | Static power dissipation | $P_{static} \approx 0$ | No DC path from V_DD to GND in steady state |
| 8 | Dynamic power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Dominant power component; α = switching activity |
| 9 | Short-circuit power | $P_{SC} = \dfrac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | Power during brief transition when both transistors conduct |
| 10 | Propagation delay (inverter) | $t_{pLH} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2\,k_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2}$ | Rise time (limited by PMOS) |
| 11 | Propagation delay (inverter) | $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2\,k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}$ | Fall time (limited by NMOS) |
| 12 | Transistor count (n-input gate) | $N_{FET} = 2n$ | n PMOS + n NMOS |
| 13 | Effective resistance ratio | $R_{eq,P} / R_{eq,N} \approx 2.5$ to $3.5$ | For equal rise/fall delay, size PMOS larger |
| 14 | Energy per transition | $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy to charge/discharge load capacitance |
| 15 | Body effect factor | $\Delta V_T = \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | Affects stacked transistors (e.g., 3-input NAND NMOS stack) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions / assumptions used in the formulas:**
> - Long-channel device model (λ ≈ 0).
> - Velocity saturation neglected.
> - Capacitive load C_L >> intrinsic gate capacitances.
> - Step input (zero rise/fall time). For finite rise time, multiply by a factor related to input slope.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fully complementary logic is the **backbone of nearly all modern digital ICs** — microprocessors, DSPs, SoCs, ASICs, and even the standard-cell libraries used in EDA tools (Cadence, Synopsys) are dominated by static CMOS gates. Reasons:

- **Robustness:** Insensitive to device variations, process scaling, and noise.
- **Zero static power:** Critical for battery-powered mobile and IoT devices (which is why it appears in the **Low Power VLSI** course at KTU).
- **Scalability:** Works at sub-100 nm nodes with appropriate V_T engineering and multi-V_DD techniques.
- **Design automation:** EDA tools can synthesize arbitrary logic into complementary gates using **standard-cell libraries**.

The main **downside** that motivates the rest of Module 4 (other logic styles) is:
- **High input capacitance** (every input drives a PMOS and an NMOS gate).
- **Large transistor count** for complex gates (each input = 2 transistors).
- **Stacked transistors** in PUN/PDN degrade performance.
- **No ratioed design flexibility** — unlike pseudo-NMOS, you cannot trade noise margin for speed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold V_M (Inverter)

The switching threshold V_M is the input voltage at which the inverter is in the **transition region** with **V_in = V_out = V_M**. At this point, both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation**, and the current through the NMOS must equal the current through the PMOS (KCL at the output node).

**Step 1 — Identify the region of operation.**
At V_in = V_M, we have V_GS,n = V_M, V_DS,n = V_M, and V_GS,p = V_M − V_DD, V_DS,p = V_M − V_DD. For an inverter with V_TN ≈ 0.4 V and |V_TP| ≈ 0.4 V, if V_M is around V_DD/2, then V_DS,n = V_M > V_GS,n − V_TN = V_M − V_TN, so NMOS is in saturation. Similarly for PMOS.

**Step 2 — Equate the saturation currents.**

$$
I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}
$$

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS,p} - V_{TP})^2
$$

Substitute V_GS,n = V_M and V_GS,p = V_M − V_DD, with V_TP negative so that V_GS,p − V_TP = V_M − V_DD − V_TP = V_M − V_DD + |V_TP|.

**Step 3 — Write the equation.**

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root of both sides.**

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)
$$

This is the positive root because both (V_M − V_TN) and (V_DD − V_M − |V_TP|) are positive in the saturation regime.

**Step 5 — Solve for V_M.**

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,V_M - \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN} = \sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p}\,V_M - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,V_M + \sqrt{k_p}\,V_M = \sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

$$
V_M (\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN}
$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form expression.**

$$
V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN}\,\sqrt{k_n} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)\sqrt{k_p}}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula for the inverter switching threshold** and is a high-yield derivation.

**Step 7 — Special case: balanced inverter (V_M = V_DD/2).**
Setting V_M = V_DD/2 and using the equality V_TN = |V_TP| = V_T (assumed equal for symmetry):

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{V_T \sqrt{k_n} + (V_{DD} - V_T)\sqrt{k_p}}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2}(\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = V_T \sqrt{k_n} + (V_{DD} - V_T)\sqrt{k_p}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2}\sqrt{k_n} + \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\sqrt{k_p} = V_T \sqrt{k_n} + V_{DD}\sqrt{k_p} - V_T \sqrt{k_p}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2}\sqrt{k_n} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\sqrt{k_p} = V_T(\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p})
$$

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2}(\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p}) = V_T(\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p})
$$

For non-trivial solution (k_n ≠ k_p): **V_DD/2 = V_T**, which is generally false. The correct interpretation is that **k_p must be tuned to make V_M = V_DD/2**, not V_T.

**Step 8 — Derive the required (W/L) ratio.**

Since $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n / 2$ and $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p / 2$, set V_M = V_DD/2 and solve:

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{V_{TN}\sqrt{\mu_n (W/L)_n} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)\sqrt{\mu_p (W/L)_p}}{\sqrt{\mu_n (W/L)_n} + \sqrt{\mu_p (W/L)_p}}
$$

Let $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n}}$. The equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{V_{TN} + r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + r}
$$

Solving for r with V_TN = |V_TP| = V_T and V_DD = 2V_T (a common educational example):

$$
V_T(1 + r) = V_T + r(2V_T - V_T) = V_T + rV_T
$$

$$
V_T + rV_T = V_T + rV_T \quad \text{(identically true for any r!)}
$$

So in this special case, **V_M is always V_DD/2 regardless of the size ratio** (because the thresholds and supply conspire). For more realistic V_DD = 5V with V_T = 1V:

$$
r = \dfrac{V_{DD}/2 - V_T}{V_{DD} - V_T - V_{DD}/2} = \dfrac{1.5}{1.5} = 1
$$

So r = 1 means $\mu_p (W/L)_p = \mu_n (W/L)_n$, which gives $(W/L)_p / (W/L)_n = \mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$ — the classic **PMOS sizing ratio** of about 2.5 to 3 for a balanced inverter. **This is a KTU-favorite result.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the CMOS Inverter Propagation Delay (t_pHL)

The propagation delay for the HIGH-to-LOW transition (output falling) is the time for the output to fall from V_OH to 0.5·V_DD (the 50% point) while the NMOS discharges the load capacitance C_L.

**Step 1 — Discharge current through saturated/triode NMOS.**
During the initial part of the discharge (V_out > V_DD − V_TN), the NMOS is in **saturation**. As V_out drops below V_DD − V_TN, the NMOS enters the **triode region**.

**Step 2 — Use the average-current approximation** (simpler approach, KTU-favored).

Average discharge current over the fall from V_DD to 0:

$$
\langle I_n \rangle \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{k_n}{2} (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2 = \dfrac{k_n}{4}(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2
$$

**Step 3 — Charge removed = C_L · V_DD/2.**

$$
t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{\langle I_n \rangle} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{\frac{k_n}{4}(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression.**

$$
t_{pHL} = \dfrac{2\,C_L\,V_{DD}}{k_n\,(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}
$$

(Equivalent to: $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2\,k_n\,(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}$ if using a different averaging convention; both forms are accepted in KTU solutions — check the constant carefully.)

**Step 5 — Analogous formula for t_pLH.**

$$
t_{pLH} = \dfrac{2\,C_L\,V_{DD}}{k_p\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Average propagation delay.**

$$
t_p = \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 numerical problems:** A very common numerical is: *"A 65 nm CMOS inverter has C_L = 50 fF, V_DD = 1.2 V, V_TN = |V_TP| = 0.4 V, (W/L)_n = 2, (W/L)_p = 5, μ_n = 350 cm²/V·s, μ_p = 130 cm²/V·s, C_ox = 25 fF/μm². Calculate t_pHL and t_pLH."* Use the formulas above.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of CMOS Gates

| Gate Style | Transistor Count (n-input) | Static Power | Output Swing | Logic Flexibility | Speed for Wide Fan-in | Layout Area |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Fully Complementary CMOS (NAND)** | 2n | **Zero** | **Full rail-to-rail** | Any logic | **Good (series NMOS degrades gracefully)** | Medium |
| **Fully Complementary CMOS (NOR)** | 2n | **Zero** | **Full rail-to-rail** | Any logic | **Poor (series PMOS severely degrades)** | Medium |
| **Pseudo-NMOS** | n + 1 | **Non-zero (V_DD-to-GND DC path)** | Degraded (V_OL > 0) | Ratioed | Fast (NMOS-only) | Small |
| **Dynamic CMOS (Domino)** | n + 2 (approx.) | Near-zero (with keeper) | Full | Sequential-like | **Very fast** | Small |
| **Pass Transistor Logic** | 2n (approx.) | Low | Degraded (V_T drop) | Limited | Moderate | Small |
| **CVSL (Cascode Voltage Switch)** | 2n + 2 | Zero | Full | Differential | Moderate | Large |
| **Transmission Gate CMOS** | 2n | Zero | Full | Excellent | Good | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In the **Low Power VLSI** context, Fully Complementary CMOS is the **gold standard for sub-threshold and ultra-low-voltage operation** because of its zero static current. Other styles (pseudo-NMOS, dynamic) trade static power for speed or area but are unsuitable for ultra-low-power regimes — a key module-4 takeaway.
