# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Schematic Cross-Section (Adapted to Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input A] --> B1[Gate of PMOS]
    A --> B2[Gate of NMOS]
    VDD[C1: V_DD Supply] --> P1[PMOS Source]
    P1 --> P2[PMOS Drain = Output F]
    B1 --> P1
    B2 --> N1[NMOS Drain = Output F]
    N1 --> N2[NMOS Source]
    N2 --> GND[C2: GND]
    P2 --> OUT[Output Node F]
    N1 --> OUT
    OUT --> LOAD[C_L: Load Capacitance]
    LOAD --> GND
```

> **Reading guide:** When A = 0, PMOS turns ON, charging C_L to V_DD (output = 1). When A = 1, NMOS turns ON, discharging C_L to GND (output = 0). C_L represents the next-stage gate capacitance and interconnect capacitance.

---

## 4.2 2-Input CMOS NAND Gate — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUN [Pull-Up Network PMOS]
        P1[PMOS controlled by A]
        P2[PMOS controlled by B]
    end
    subgraph PDN [Pull-Down Network NMOS]
        N1[NMOS controlled by A]
        N2[NMOS controlled by B]
    end
    VDD[VDD] --> PUN
    PUN --> OUT[Output F]
    PDN --> GND[GND]
    A[Input A] --> P1
    A --> N1
    B[Input B] --> P2
    B --> N2
    P1 --- P2
    N1 --- N2
```

> **KTU Reading:** PUN has PMOS in **parallel** (conducts if A=0 OR B=0). PDN has NMOS in **series** (conducts only if A=1 AND B=1). Output F = NOT(A AND B) = NAND.

---

## 4.3 2-Input CMOS NOR Gate — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PUN2 [Pull-Up Network PMOS]
        MP1[PMOS controlled by A]
        MP2[PMOS controlled by B]
    end
    subgraph PDN2 [Pull-Down Network NMOS]
        MN1[NMOS controlled by A]
        MN2[NMOS controlled by B]
    end
    VSUP[VDD] --> MP1
    MP1 --> MP2
    MP2 --> OUT2[Output F]
    MN1 --> OUT2
    MN2 --> MN1
    MN2 --> VSS[GND]
    IA[Input A] --> MP1
    IA --> MN1
    IB[Input B] --> MP2
    IB --> MN2
```

> **KTU Reading:** PUN has PMOS in **series** (conducts only if A=0 AND B=0). PDN has NMOS in **parallel** (conducts if A=1 OR B=1). Output F = NOT(A OR B) = NOR.

---

## 4.4 Duality Construction Procedure (PDN → PUN) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Step 0: Define F-bar logic function]
    S1[Step 1: Draw PDN using NMOS<br>series for AND, parallel for OR]
    S2[Step 2: Construct dual graph]
    S3[Step 3: Series NMOS becomes parallel PMOS]
    S4[Step 4: Parallel NMOS becomes series PMOS]
    S5[Step 5: Replace NMOS with PMOS and connect source to VDD]
    S6[Step 6: Verify rule<br>PDN ON implies PUN OFF and vice versa]
    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
```

> **KTU Examiner Note:** A frequently asked 7-mark question is: *"Design a CMOS complex gate realizing F = (AB + CD)̄ using the fully complementary style."* The procedure above gives the full marks.

---

## 4.5 Power Dissipation Mechanism in Fully Complementary CMOS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Transition] --> STAGE1{Static Phase<br>No Switching}
    STAGE1 -- Steady State --> STAGE2[P_static ~ 0<br>No DC path]
    STAGE1 -- During Transition --> STAGE3[Short-Circuit Current<br>Both PUN and PDN partially ON briefly]
    STAGE3 --> P_SC[P_sc: depends on<br>input slope and V_T]
    IN --> STAGE4{C_L Charging / Discharging}
    STAGE4 --> P_DYN[P_dyn = alpha C_L V_DD^2 f<br>Dominant component]
    STAGE4 --> P_DYN2[Switching Activity alpha<br>typically 0.1 to 0.3]
```

> **KTU Insight:** Dynamic power scales **quadratically** with V_DD, which is the foundation of **V_DD scaling** — the most effective low-power technique. Reducing V_DD from 5 V to 1.2 V cuts dynamic power by **~17×**, which is why Module 4 heavily discusses voltage scaling.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define Fully Complementary CMOS Logic. List its two key properties.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fully Complementary CMOS Logic is a static digital circuit design style in which every logic gate is constructed using a **PMOS Pull-Up Network (PUN)** and an **NMOS Pull-Down Network (PDN)** that are topological duals of each other.

**Key properties:**
1. **Full output voltage swing (rail-to-rail):** The output is always connected to either V_DD or GND through a low-resistance path, yielding V_OH = V_DD and V_OL = 0 V.
2. **Zero static power dissipation:** For any input combination, only one of the PUN or PDN conducts, ensuring no direct DC current path from V_DD to GND in steady state.

The PUN implements the function F, and the PDN implements F̄, making them **logically complementary** and **electrically mutually exclusive**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for property 1, 1 mark for property 2.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Why are PMOS transistors used in the pull-up network and NMOS transistors in the pull-down network of a CMOS gate? What happens if this rule is violated?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason for the rule:** PMOS transistors have a **negative threshold voltage (V_TP < 0)**. When the PMOS is ON, its source is tied to V_DD and gate is at 0 V, giving V_GS = −V_DD, comfortably below V_TP. The output is charged to **V_DD** without any threshold drop. Similarly, an NMOS in the pull-down network discharges the output to **0 V** with a strong '0' level. Hence, PMOS passes a strong logic '1' and NMOS passes a strong logic '0'.

**Violation consequence:** If an NMOS is placed in the pull-up, its output can only rise to **V_DD − V_TN** (a "weak 1"), causing degraded noise margin and logic-level incompatibility. If a PMOS is in the pull-down, the output only falls to **|V_TP|**, again producing a "weak 0" and the gate may fail to drive the next stage.

*(1.5 marks for the reason, 1.5 marks for the violation consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, Q Choice 1]**
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate. Derive the condition for equal rise and fall times.

**(b) [7 marks]** A CMOS inverter in a 65 nm process has the following parameters: V_DD = 1.2 V, V_TN = 0.35 V, |V_TP| = 0.40 V, μ_n C_ox = 350 μA/V², μ_p C_ox = 130 μA/V², (W/L)_n = 2, (W/L)_p = 5, C_L = 50 fF. Calculate (i) t_pHL, (ii) t_pLH, and (iii) the average propagation delay t_p.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Operation of 2-input CMOS NAND gate:**

A 2-input NAND gate realizing F = (A·B)̄ consists of **two PMOS transistors (M_P1, M_P2)** in **parallel** between V_DD and the output, and **two NMOS transistors (M_N1, M_N2)** in **series** between the output and GND.

**Truth Table Analysis:**

| A | B | M_P1 (PMOS) | M_P2 (PMOS) | M_N1 (NMOS) | M_N2 (NMOS) | Output F |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | V_DD (1) |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | V_DD (1) |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | V_DD (1) |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 0 V (0) |

**Working principle:**
- When **either A = 0 or B = 0**, at least one PMOS conducts (parallel connection provides a path), pulling the output to V_DD. Meanwhile, the series NMOS stack has at least one OFF transistor, so the PDN is open.
- When **A = 1 AND B = 1**, both PMOS are OFF (output disconnected from V_DD), and both NMOS are ON (series path established), pulling the output to GND.

**Derivation for equal rise and fall times (t_pLH = t_pHL):**

For a balanced inverter with size ratio r = (W/L)_p / (W/L)_n, the condition for equal delays is obtained by equating the drive strengths:

$$
k_n\,(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2 = k_p\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

Substituting the transconductance parameters:

$$
\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2 = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

Solving for the (W/L) ratio:

$$
\dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}{\mu_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2}
$$

Plugging in V_DD = 1.2 V, V_TN = 0.35 V, |V_TP| = 0.40 V:

$$
\dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \dfrac{350 \cdot (1.2 - 0.35)^2}{130 \cdot (1.2 - 0.40)^2} = \dfrac{350 \cdot 0.7225}{130 \cdot 0.64} = \dfrac{252.88}{83.2} \approx 3.04
$$

So **PMOS must be sized about 3× larger than NMOS** to achieve equal rise/fall times. *(7 marks: 2 for circuit, 2 for truth table/explanation, 3 for derivation.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** V_DD = 1.2 V, V_TN = 0.35 V, |V_TP| = 0.40 V, k_n' = μ_n C_ox = 350 μA/V², k_p' = μ_p C_ox = 130 μA/V², (W/L)_n = 2, (W/L)_p = 5, C_L = 50 fF.

**Step 1 — Compute device transconductances.**

$$
k_n = \dfrac{k_n'}{2} \cdot (W/L)_n = \dfrac{350}{2} \cdot 2 = 350\ \mu\text{A/V}^2
$$

$$
k_p = \dfrac{k_p'}{2} \cdot (W/L)_p = \dfrac{130}{2} \cdot 5 = 325\ \mu\text{A/V}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute t_pHL (fall time).**

$$
t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n\,(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.2}{350 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1.2 - 0.35)^2}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{350 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.7225} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{252.875 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = 2.373 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{s} \approx 237\ \text{ps}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute t_pLH (rise time).**

$$
t_{pLH} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_p\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2}
$$

$$
t_{pLH} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.2}{325 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1.2 - 0.40)^2}
$$

$$
t_{pLH} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{325 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.64} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{208 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
t_{pLH} = 2.885 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{s} \approx 288\ \text{ps}
$$

**Step 4 — Average propagation delay.**

$$
t_p = \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = \dfrac{288 + 237}{2} = 262.5\ \text{ps}
$$

**Result summary:**
- t_pHL ≈ **237 ps**
- t_pLH ≈ **288 ps**
- t_p ≈ **262.5 ps**

> **Note:** Rise time is slightly larger than fall time (t_pLH > t_pHL) because the PMOS is slightly under-sized relative to the optimum 3× ratio (it is only 2.5×, since 5/2 = 2.5 < 3.04). This is the *trade-off* the KTU examiner often tests. *(7 marks: 2 for k_n and k_p, 2 for t_pHL, 2 for t_pLH, 1 for average.)*

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2022, Module 4, Q Choice 2]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the duality rule for constructing a CMOS complex gate with an example. Realize the function F = (AB + CD)̄ using a fully complementary CMOS circuit and verify the operation.

**(b) [7 marks]** With necessary equations, derive the expression for the switching threshold V_M of a CMOS inverter. Show that when k_n = k_p and V_TN = |V_TP|, V_M = V_DD/2. Comment on the noise margins.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Duality Rule Definition:**
The Duality Rule states that the **PMOS pull-up network (PUN)** and the **NMOS pull-down network (PDN)** of a static CMOS gate are topologically dual — meaning every **series** connection in one network corresponds to a **parallel** connection in the other.

**Procedure to construct a complex CMOS gate:**
1. Express the desired output as F (e.g., F = (AB + CD)̄). Determine F̄.
2. Draw the **PDN** using NMOS transistors: place them in **series** for AND terms and in **parallel** for OR terms. The PDN must implement F̄.
3. Construct the **PUN** as the dual: every series NMOS becomes a parallel PMOS, every parallel NMOS becomes a series PMOS. Connect the source of every PMOS to V_DD.
4. Verify that for each input combination, exactly one of PUN or PDN conducts.

**Realizing F = (AB + CD)̄:**

Step 1: F̄ = AB + CD (this is what the PDN must implement).

Step 2: PDN structure — we need AB (AND) in **series**, and CD (AND) in **parallel** with the first AND block:
- M_N1 (gate=A) — M_N2 (gate=B) in **series** form the AB branch.
- M_N3 (gate=C) — M_N4 (gate=D) in **series** form the CD branch.
- The two branches are in **parallel** between the output node and GND.

Step 3: PUN structure (dual of PDN):
- The series NMOS branch (AB) becomes a **parallel PMOS branch**: M_P1 (gate=A) || M_P2 (gate=B).
- The series NMOS branch (CD) becomes a **parallel PMOS branch**: M_P3 (gate=C) || M_P4 (gate=D).
- The two parallel PMOS blocks are in **series** between V_DD and the output.

**Transistor count: 8** (4 PMOS + 4 NMOS).

**Verification Table:**

| A | B | C | D | AB | CD | F̄ = AB+CD | F |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | x | x | x | 0 | – | 0 | 1 |
| x | 0 | x | x | 0 | – | 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | x | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | x | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

For every row, exactly one of PUN or PDN conducts:
- When F = 0: PDN pulls output to GND.
- When F = 1: PUN pulls output to V_DD.

Hence, the gate functions correctly. *(7 marks: 2 for duality rule, 2 for PDN, 2 for PUN, 1 for verification.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of V_M:**

At the switching threshold, V_in = V_out = V_M. Both transistors are in saturation (assuming V_M > V_TN and V_DD − V_M > |V_TP|). Equating the drain currents:

$$
I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}
$$

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS,p} - V_{TP})^2
$$

With V_GS,n = V_M and V_GS,p = V_M − V_DD, and V_TP = −|V_TP|:

$$
k_n (V_M - V_{TN})^2 = k_p (V_M - V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

Taking the square root (positive root):

$$
\sqrt{k_n}(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)
$$

Solving for V_M:

$$
V_M (\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN}
$$

$$
V_M = \frac{V_{TN}\sqrt{k_n} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)\sqrt{k_p}}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}
$$

**Special case: k_n = k_p and V_TN = |V_TP|:**

Let V_TN = |V_TP| = V_T and k_n = k_p = k. Then √k_n = √k_p = √k. Substituting:

$$
V_M = \frac{V_T \sqrt{k} + (V_{DD} - V_T)\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{k} + \sqrt{k}} = \frac{V_T + V_{DD} - V_T}{2} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

So **V_M = V_DD/2** when the inverter is symmetric (matched drive strengths and matched threshold magnitudes). This is the *ideal* situation that maximizes both noise margins.

**Noise Margins:**

The noise margins of a CMOS inverter are defined as:

$$
NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}
$$

$$
NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}
$$

For the fully complementary CMOS inverter with rail-to-rail swing (V_OH = V_DD, V_OL = 0):

- **V_IH** ≈ V_M + small offset, typically **0.5·V_DD to 0.55·V_DD**.
- **V_IL** ≈ V_M − small offset, typically **0.45·V_DD to 0.5·V_DD**.

A symmetric inverter (V_M = V_DD/2) yields the **maximum equal noise margins**:

$$
NM_H = NM_L \approx \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{OL}}{2} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{IL}' 
$$

In practice, $NM_H \approx NM_L \approx 0.4 \cdot V_{DD}$, which is **excellent noise immunity** — a key reason fully complementary CMOS is preferred for low-power robust designs.

**Comment:** Larger noise margin → higher tolerance to coupling noise, ground bounce, supply droop, and process variation. This robustness is a primary reason CMOS is dominant in scaled technologies. *(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for special case, 2 for noise margins.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Fully Complementary CMOS questions (leading to 1–3 mark deductions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing V_M and V_DD/2:** Many students write "V_M = V_DD/2 always." This is **only true under symmetry conditions** (k_n = k_p and V_TN = |V_TP|). Otherwise, V_M shifts, which **unbalances the noise margins** and can cause functional failure in high-noise environments.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the body effect:** In a 3-input NAND, the lower NMOS (source not at GND) suffers a **body effect** that increases V_TN, slowing the discharge. Students often write t_pHL without accounting for this, overestimating speed.
>
> 3. **Swapping series/parallel in the duality construction:** A very common error is putting series PMOS where parallel PMOS is required. Always remember: **series in PDN → parallel in PUN.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring short-circuit power:** When asked for total power, students often write only P_dyn. For slow-input transitions, P_SC (short-circuit) can be a significant fraction (up to 20–30%) of total power. Always include it when input rise time is comparable to output rise time.
>
> 5. **Unit errors in delay calculation:** Mixing μA/V² with A/V², or fF with pF. Always normalize units to **A, F, V, s** before plugging in. The C_L·V_DD/k factor has units of [F·V/(A/V²)] = [F·V³/A] = seconds. Verify dimensions.
>
> 6. **Wrong sign in the PMOS current equation:** For PMOS, |V_GS − V_TP| or (V_SG − |V_TP|) — students often get confused with negative signs. Always use the magnitude form for clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section before the exam!**

- ✅ **Definition:** Fully Complementary CMOS = PMOS PUN + NMOS PDN, topologically dual, mutually exclusive conduction.
- ✅ **Static power ≈ 0** (no V_DD-to-GND DC path). Dynamic power P_dyn = α·C_L·V_DD²·f is the dominant component.
- ✅ **Transistor count for n-input gate = 2n.** NAND uses 4 (2 PMOS parallel in PUN, 2 NMOS series in PDN); NOR uses 4 (2 PMOS series in PUN, 2 NMOS parallel in PDN).
- ✅ **PMOS in PUN, NMOS in PDN** — always. PMOS passes strong '1' (no V_T drop); NMOS passes strong '0'.
- ✅ **Switching threshold:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN}\sqrt{k_n} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)\sqrt{k_p}}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}$. Symmetric inverter → V_M = V_DD/2.
- ✅ **For balanced inverter:** $(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3.5 \times (W/L)_n$ (because μ_n ≈ 2.5 μ_p).
- ✅ **Propagation delay:** $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}$, $t_{pLH} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_p(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2}$.
- ✅ **Average delay:** $t_p = (t_{pLH} + t_{pHL})/2$.
- ✅ **Duality rule:** Series in PDN ↔ Parallel in PUN, and vice versa.
- ✅ **NAND vs NOR for wide fan-in:** NAND is faster (only NMOS stack — 2–3 series NMOS is acceptable; PUN has parallel PMOS). NOR is slower (PMOS stack).
- ✅ **Body effect** degrades stacked-transistor performance (3+ input NAND).
- ✅ **V_OH = V_DD, V_OL = 0** — full rail-to-rail swing → maximum noise margins.
- ✅ **Energy per transition:** $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$ — drives V_DD scaling in low-power design.
- ✅ **KTU-Module-4 link:** Fully Complementary CMOS is the *baseline* against which all other low-power logic styles (pseudo-NMOS, dynamic, pass-transistor, adiabatic) are compared.
- ✅ **Major drawback for low-power:** High input capacitance (each input drives 2 gates), large area, no ratioed flexibility.
- ✅ **Best use case:** General-purpose logic, sub-threshold operation, ultra-low-voltage circuits, robust standard cells.

---

*End of Module 4 — Fully Complementary Logic — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NMOS and Pseudo-NMOS Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NMOS and Pseudo-NMOS Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — NMOS and Pseudo-NMOS Logic

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 NMOS Logic (Enhancement-Load Style)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
NMOS logic is a single-channel MOS logic family in which the **pull-down network (PDN)** is built from n-channel enhancement MOSFETs that evaluate the logic function, while the **pull-up "load" device** is a single n-channel MOSFET operating in either **saturation (enhancement load)** or the **linear/triode region (depletion load)**. The output node sits between the load and the PDN, and the circuit is **ratioed** — meaning the output low voltage (V_OL) is determined by the **W/L ratio** of the driver transistor to the load transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** A logic gate is *ratioed* when V_OL depends on the relative sizing (W/L) of the transistors, and *ratioless* (CMOS) when V_OL is independent of sizing.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Tap" Picture**
Imagine a water tank (V_DD) connected to a drain pipe (GND). The drain pipe has two valves in series: a **load valve** (always half-open) and a **control valve** (the input signal). When the input is OFF, the control valve is closed and the tank stays full (output HIGH). When the input is ON, both valves are open — water trickles out, and the tank settles at a *partially-full* level (output LOW but not exactly zero). The *exact* water level depends on how open each valve is — that is the **ratio**. NMOS logic behaves exactly like this leaky system: even in the LOW state, a **constant leakage current** flows from V_DD to GND, which is precisely why NMOS logic is **inherently power-hungry** and has been largely replaced by CMOS in modern designs.

---

### 1.2 Pseudo-NMOS Logic

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Pseudo-NMOS logic is a hybrid logic style in which the **pull-up "load" is a p-channel enhancement MOSFET with its gate permanently tied to ground (logic 0)**, keeping it *always in conduction*. The pull-down network is an n-channel MOSFET tree that implements the logic function. The name "pseudo-NMOS" reflects its historical origin: the load device is a PMOS that *behaves* like a depletion-mode NMOS load in terms of circuit topology (single small load device) while using standard CMOS fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Pseudo-NMOS occupies a unique position in low-power VLSI: it offers the *layout density and speed* of NMOS logic (no P-network to design) but is generally **excluded from ultra-low-power designs** because of its *static DC current path* — the central reason it is taught in this course.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Always-On Sprinkler" Picture**
Picture a garden sprinkler (the PMOS load) that is *permanently switched on* and aimed at a drain (GND). A foot pedal (the NMOS PDN) controls the drain. Step on the pedal → water flows freely → the ground stays wet (output LOW, current flows). Release the pedal → the sprinkler keeps spraying, but the water has nowhere to go through the closed pedal valve, so it just *pools and recedes* to the high level (output HIGH = V_DD). The crucial point: **the sprinkler is always wasting water when the pedal is pressed** — this wasted water is the **static power dissipation** P_stat = V_DD · I_DC. The lesson: pseudo-NMOS is fast and compact, but pays a continuous "water bill" every time the output is LOW.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical & Geometric Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supply voltage | **V_DD** | **1.2 V – 3.3 V** | Process-dependent |
| NMOS threshold | **V_tn** | **0.3 V – 0.7 V** | Enhancement device |
| PMOS threshold | **V_tp** | **−0.4 V to −0.9 V** | Enhancement device |
| Inverter ratio | **r = (W_n/L_n)/(W_p/L_p)** | 4 – 10 | Sets V_OL |
| Oxide capacitance | C_ox | ~10 fF/μm² | Per unit area |
| Carrier mobility ratio | μ_n / μ_p | **2.0 – 3.0** | Hole mobility ≈ 1/(2 to 3) of electron |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A pseudo-NMOS gate typically uses **r ≈ 4 to 8** to obtain V_OL ≤ 0.1 V_DD. Smaller r → higher V_OL (worse noise margin); larger r → slower gate (more driver capacitance to charge the internal node).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology & Operating Regions

A pseudo-NMOS inverter is the simplest illustrative case. The **PMOS load** has:
- Gate tied to **0 V** ⇒ V_SG = V_DD ⇒ always ON
- For Vin = 0 (NMOS OFF): I_DN = 0, no current flows, output rises to V_OH = V_DD.
- For Vin = V_DD (NMOS ON): a DC current path exists; output settles to V_OL.

**Operating-region summary (when output is at V_OL):**

| Transistor | Condition for Region | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **PMOS (load)** | **Saturation**, since V_SD = V_DD − V_OL > V_SG − \vert V_tp \vert = V_DD − \vert V_tp \vert when V_OL < \vert V_tp \vert | Always-on, small V_OL |
| **NMOS (driver)** | **Linear (triode)**, since V_DS = V_OL < V_GS − V_tn = V_DD − V_tn | V_OL is small |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Input LOW (Vin = 0):** NMOS driver is in cutoff (V_GS = 0 < V_tn). No current flows in the branch. The PMOS charges the output node toward V_DD. Steady state: V_OH = V_DD.
2. **Input HIGH (Vin = V_DD):** NMOS driver turns ON (V_GS = V_DD > V_tn). PMOS continues to conduct. A DC current path V_DD → PMOS → NMOS → GND is established.
3. **Steady state at V_OL:** The output node equilibrates where the current delivered by the PMOS equals the current sunk by the NMOS. The resulting V_OL is non-zero and **proportional to the size ratio r = (W_n/L_n) / (W_p/L_p)**.
4. **Switching event:** When Vin transitions, the gate output moves between V_OH and V_OL under the asymmetric drive of one strong NMOS tree vs. one weak PMOS load.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Output HIGH, no DC current |
| 2 | $I_{Dp} = \dfrac{k_p}{2}\dfrac{W_p}{L_p}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^2$ | PMOS in saturation, V_OL < \vert V_tp \vert |
| 3 | $I_{Dn} = k_n\dfrac{W_n}{L_n}\left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \dfrac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right]$ | NMOS in linear (triode) region |
| 4 | $V_{OL} \approx \dfrac{r\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}}{2\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$ | After neglecting V_OL²/2 term |
| 5 | $r = \dfrac{(W_n/L_n)}{(W_p/L_p)}$ | Inverter sizing ratio |
| 6 | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DC} = \dfrac{V_{DD}\,k_p}{2}\dfrac{W_p}{L_p}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}$ | Static power when output is LOW |
| 7 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha\,C_{L}\,V_{DD}^{2}\,f$ | Dynamic switching power |
| 8 | $P_{total} = P_{stat} + P_{dyn} + P_{short\text{-}circuit}$ | Total power |
| 9 | $NM_{L} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low noise margin (degraded in pseudo-NMOS) |
| 10 | $NM_{H} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High noise margin (full rail) |
| 11 | $t_{pLH} \approx \dfrac{C_L\,V_{DD}}{I_{Dp,\;sat}}$ | Slow rising transition (weak PMOS) |
| 12 | $t_{pHL} \approx \dfrac{C_L\,V_{DD}}{I_{Dn,\;peak}}$ | Fast falling transition (strong NMOS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The static power equation (Row 6) is the **single most important formula** for KTU 2024 questions on this topic. Examiners almost always ask: *"Given a process corner, compute the static power dissipation."* You must know that P_stat is **independent of output load** and is set purely by V_DD and the PMOS W/L.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-speed datapath design:** Pseudo-NMOS is used in select high-performance arithmetic units (e.g., the **carry-chain in fast adders**) where the speed advantage (single load device, small output capacitance) outweighs the power penalty.
- **ROM/PLA arrays:** The compact, single-transistor-per-input pseudo-NMOS NOR array offers the **highest density** among CMOS-compatible logic families.
- **SRAM peripherals:** Some sense-amplifier and decoder circuits use pseudo-NMOS for short, controlled critical paths.
- **Modern low-power VLSI:** Used **only where static current is tolerable**, often disabled in sleep mode using "sleep transistors" — a topic that connects directly to Module 5 of the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Output LOW Voltage (V_OL)

We start from Kirchhoff's current law at the output node in steady state (output = V_OL, Vin = V_DD):

**Step 1: Write the PMOS current.** With V_SG = V_DD and the PMOS in saturation (valid when V_OL < \vert V_tp \vert):

$$
I_{Dp} = \frac{\mu_{p}\,C_{ox}}{2}\,\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Write the NMOS current.** With V_GS = V_DD and V_DS = V_OL, the NMOS is in the linear (triode) region because V_OL < V_DD − V_tn:

$$
I_{Dn} = \mu_{n}\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W_{n}}{L_{n}}\left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 3: Apply KCL at the output node (steady state, no capacitive current):**

$$
I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}
$$

**Step 4: Equate and divide both sides by C_ox:**

$$
\mu_{n}\,\frac{W_{n}}{L_{n}}\left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{\mu_{p}}{2}\,\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the small-signal approximation.** Since V_OL is typically much smaller than V_DD − V_tn, the quadratic term V_OL²/2 is neglected:

$$
\mu_{n}\,\frac{W_{n}}{L_{n}}\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} \;\approx\; \frac{\mu_{p}}{2}\,\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for V_OL by isolating it on the left-hand side:**

$$
V_{OL} \;\approx\; \frac{\mu_{p}\,W_{p}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}}{2\,\mu_{n}\,W_{n}\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}
$$

**Step 7: Introduce the inverter ratio r = (W_n/L_n) / (W_p/L_p), assuming L_n = L_p = L (typical layout convention):**

$$
V_{OL} \;\approx\; \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}}{2\,r\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}
$$

**Result Interpretation:** V_OL scales **inversely with r** — increasing the NMOS size reduces V_OL (better noise margin) at the cost of input capacitance and area.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Static Power Dissipation

The static power is dissipated when the output is LOW and a DC current flows from V_DD to GND. This current equals the PMOS saturation current (from the derivation above):

**Step 1: Identify the static current.** At V_OL, I_DC = I_Dp:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{\mu_{p}\,C_{ox}}{2}\,\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the static power.** By definition, P_stat = V_DD · I_DC:

$$
P_{stat} = V_{DD}\cdot \frac{\mu_{p}\,C_{ox}}{2}\,\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Recognize the trade-off.** Smaller W_p/L_p reduces P_stat, but **also increases V_OL** (from Equation 4 of the cheat sheet). This is the *fundamental design tension* in pseudo-NMOS.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Switching Threshold (V_M) — Optional, KTU Advanced

The switching threshold is defined as the input voltage at which Vin = Vout = V_M. At this point both transistors are typically in saturation.

**Step 1: Equate currents (both in saturation):**

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}\frac{W_n}{L_n}(V_M - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}\frac{W_p}{L_p}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^2
$$

**Step 2: Take the square root of both sides:**

$$
\sqrt{\frac{k_n W_n / L_n}{k_p W_p / L_p}}\,(V_M - V_{tn}) = (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{tp} \vert)
$$

**Step 3: Let β = √(k_n W_n / (k_p W_p)). Solve for V_M:**

$$
V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \beta\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)}{1 + \beta}
$$

**Interpretation:** Pseudo-NMOS has a **pulled-down V_M** compared to symmetric CMOS, which shifts the noise margin centroid toward the LOW side.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NMOS vs. Pseudo-NMOS vs. CMOS

| Parameter | NMOS (Enh. Load) | Pseudo-NMOS | CMOS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pull-up device | NMOS (gate at V_DD or drain) | PMOS (gate at 0) | PMOS network |
| V_OH | V_DD − V_tn | V_DD | V_DD |
| V_OL | Ratioed (small) | Ratioed (small) | ≈ 0 (ratioless) |
| Static current (out LOW) | YES (always) | **YES (always)** | NO |
| Static power | High | High | **Near zero** |
| NM_L | Poor | **Degraded** | Excellent |
| Layout density | 1 PDN + 1 load | 1 PDN + 1 PMOS | 1 PDN + 1 PUN |
| Suitability for low-power | **Not suitable** | **Not suitable (only short paths)** | **Highly suitable** |
| Typical use | Legacy, ROMs | Fast adders, decoders | All modern VLSI |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pseudo-NMOS Inverter — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    supA[VDD SUPPLY] --> ldrB[PMOS LOAD DEVICE]
    ldrB --> outC[OUTPUT NODE VOUT]
    inD[INPUT VIN] --> drvE[NMOS DRIVER TRANSISTOR]
    drvE --> outC
    drvE --> gndF[GROUND REFERENCE]
    gbiasG[PMOS GATE TIED TO 0V] --> ldrB
    capH[LOAD CAPACITANCE CL] --> outC
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The PMOS load is *always conducting* because its gate sits at 0 V (V_SG = V_DD > |V_tp|).
- The NMOS driver is the only logic-controlled element.
- Output capacitance C_L is charged slowly through the weak PMOS and discharged quickly through the strong NMOS.

---

### 4.2 Static Power Flow in Pseudo-NMOS (Output LOW)

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[VDD SOURCE] --> pcurB[STATIC DC CURRENT PATH]
    pcurB --> pmosC[PMOS IN SATURATION]
    pmosC --> outD[OUTPUT AT VOL]
    outD --> nmosE[NMOS IN TRIODE]
    nmosE --> sinkF[GND SINK]
    pmosC -.->|HEAT DISSIPATION| powG[STATIC POWER PDISS = VDD X IDC]
```

This figure highlights the *unavoidable* continuous dissipation that disqualifies pseudo-NMOS from strictly low-power designs.

---

### 4.3 NMOS Logic Family Tree (Classification)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[NMOS LOGIC FAMILY] --> satB[ENHANCEMENT LOAD NMOS]
    rootA --> depC[DEPLETION LOAD NMOS]
    rootA --> psdD[PSEUDO NMOS]
    satB --> satE[RATIOED SATURATING LOAD]
    satB --> satF[NMOS GATE TIED TO VDD]
    depC --> depG[NMOS WITH NEGATIVE VTN]
    depC --> depH[IMPROVED NOISE MARGIN]
    psdD --> psdI[PMOS AS ALWAYS ON LOAD]
    psdD --> psdJ[HIGH STATIC POWER]
    psdD --> psdK[USED IN FAST CARRY CHAINS]
```

---

### 4.4 CMOS vs. Pseudo-NMOS — Block Contrast

```mermaid
graph LR
    aA[CMOS INVERTER] --> aB[PMOS PUN TURNED OFF IN STEADY STATE]
    aB --> aC[NO STATIC CURRENT]
    aB --> aD[LOW POWER IDEAL]
    eE[PSEUDO NMOS INVERTER] --> eF[PMOS LOAD ALWAYS ON]
    eF --> eG[DC CURRENT WHEN VIN HIGH]
    eF --> eH[STATIC POWER PENALTY]
    eE --> eI[FAST AND COMPACT]
```

This comparative block diagram directly supports the discussion in Section 3.4 and is the visual aid most often used in KTU classroom slides for this topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define pseudo-NMOS logic. List any two of its advantages and two of its disadvantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Pseudo-NMOS logic is a single-channel MOS logic style in which the pull-up device is a p-channel MOSFET with its gate tied to ground, keeping it permanently ON. The pull-down network consists of n-channel MOSFETs implementing the logic function.
- **Advantage 1 (0.5 mark):** Compact layout — only one load transistor per gate (versus a full PMOS pull-up network in CMOS), giving high density and small input capacitance.
- **Advantage 2 (0.5 mark):** Fast low-to-high and high-to-low transitions at the start, with t_pHL smaller than CMOS due to a strong NMOS pull-down path.
- **Disadvantage 1 (0.5 mark):** Continuous static power dissipation when output is LOW, because a DC current flows from V_DD through the PMOS and NMOS to GND.
- **Disadvantage 2 (0.5 mark):** Reduced low-level noise margin (NML) because V_OL is non-zero and depends on the sizing ratio r.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between NMOS (enhancement-load) logic and pseudo-NMOS logic in terms of load device, V_OH, and static power.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Load device (1 mark):** NMOS enhancement-load uses an n-channel MOSFET (gate tied to V_DD or to its own drain) as the load; pseudo-NMOS uses a p-channel MOSFET with gate at 0 V.
- **V_OH (1 mark):** NMOS enhancement-load gives V_OH = V_DD − V_tn (a degraded high level); pseudo-NMOS gives V_OH = V_DD (full rail).
- **Static power (1 mark):** Both families dissipate static power when output is LOW, but the magnitude in pseudo-NMOS is *larger* because the PMOS saturation current is typically greater than an NMOS enhancement-load saturation current at the same V_DD.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

#### Question A (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for V_OL of a pseudo-NMOS inverter. State clearly the operating regions of the two transistors. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A pseudo-NMOS inverter is fabricated in a 0.18 μm process with the following parameters: V_DD = 1.8 V, V_tn = 0.5 V, V_tp = −0.6 V, μ_n C_ox = 270 μA/V², μ_p C_ox = 90 μA/V², W_n/L_n = 4, W_p/L_p = 1. Compute (i) the output low voltage V_OL and (ii) the static power dissipation when the output is LOW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
1. Draw the pseudo-NMOS inverter circuit and label nodes (1 mark).
2. Identify regions: PMOS in saturation, NMOS in triode when output is at V_OL (1 mark).
3. Write PMOS saturation current expression (1 mark):

$$
I_{Dp} = \frac{\mu_{p}C_{ox}}{2}\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^{2}
$$

4. Write NMOS linear-region current expression (1 mark):

$$
I_{Dn} = \mu_{n}C_{ox}\frac{W_{n}}{L_{n}}\left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

5. Apply KCL: I_Dn = I_Dp, neglect V_OL²/2, solve for V_OL (2 marks):

$$
V_{OL} \approx \frac{(W_{p}/L_{p})\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^{2}}{2\,(W_{n}/L_{n})\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}\cdot\frac{\mu_{p}}{\mu_{n}}
$$

6. Conclude: V_OL depends on the ratio r (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

(i) **V_OL calculation (3.5 marks):**

$$
r = \frac{W_{n}/L_{n}}{W_{p}/L_{p}} = \frac{4}{1} = 4
$$

Compute V_OL using the formula:

$$
V_{OL} \approx \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^{2}}{2\,r\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}
$$

$$
V_{OL} \approx \frac{(1.8 - 0.6)^{2}}{2 \times 4 \times (1.8 - 0.5)} = \frac{(1.2)^{2}}{8 \times 1.3} = \frac{1.44}{10.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{OL} \approx 0.138 \text{ V}}
$$

(ii) **Static power (3.5 marks):** The static current is the PMOS saturation current:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{\mu_{p}C_{ox}}{2}\frac{W_{p}}{L_{p}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^{2}
$$

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{90}{2} \times 1 \times (1.8 - 0.6)^{2} = 45 \times 1.44 = 64.8 \text{ μA}
$$

$$
P_{stat} = V_{DD} \times I_{DC} = 1.8 \times 64.8 \text{ μA}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{stat} = 116.64 \text{ μW}}
$$

---

#### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a pseudo-NMOS inverter for both logic states. Sketch its voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) and identify the regions where the NMOS and PMOS operate. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the noise margin limitations of pseudo-NMOS logic. Why is it not preferred in modern low-power VLSI design? Mention one low-power technique to mitigate its static dissipation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation and VTC (7 marks):**
1. Circuit diagram (2 marks): Show V_DD at top, PMOS load with gate at ground, NMOS driver with Vin at its gate and source at ground, output node between them.
2. **Vin = 0 (1.5 marks):** NMOS OFF, no current, output charged to V_OH = V_DD. PMOS is in saturation briefly, then enters cutoff as V_out approaches V_DD.
3. **Vin = V_DD (1.5 marks):** NMOS ON, output settles at V_OL. PMOS in saturation, NMOS in triode.
4. VTC sketch (2 marks): A skewed curve; V_OH = V_DD, V_OL slightly above 0 V, transition point V_M shifted below V_DD/2 (because the PMOS is much weaker than the NMOS).

**(b) Noise Margin and Low-Power Discussion (7 marks):**
1. V_OH = V_DD (full rail) → NM_H is acceptable (1 mark).
2. V_OL is non-zero → NML = V_IL − V_OL is **degraded** (1 mark).
3. NML is small enough that the gate is **vulnerable to ground-bounce noise** (1 mark).
4. Static dissipation P_stat = V_DD · I_DC persists whenever output is LOW, which violates the "zero static power" requirement of low-power design (1.5 marks).
5. The constant current path means pseudo-NMOS cannot use power gating directly without an additional sleep transistor (1 mark).
6. **Mitigation technique (1.5 marks):** *Sleep-transistor (header) cutoff* — a PMOS header switch is placed between V_DD and the pull-up device, turned OFF in standby to eliminate static current. Alternatively, *dynamic logic* or *domino logic* (covered later in the syllabus) avoids the static path entirely.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Forgetting the V_OL²/2 term distinction:** Examiners often award 1 mark for *correctly identifying* that the NMOS is in **linear region** (not saturation) at V_OL. Writing "NMOS is in saturation at V_OL" is a common error and costs 0.5–1 mark.
> - **Mixing up V_OH for enhancement-load NMOS vs. pseudo-NMOS:** Writing V_OH = V_DD − V_tn for pseudo-NMOS is wrong (and an automatic 1-mark deduction in the comparison question).
> - **Static power formula error:** Students frequently write P_stat = V_DD · I_LEAK where I_LEAK is *subthreshold* leakage. For pseudo-NMOS, the relevant current is the **intentional bias current through the PMOS load**, not leakage. The two differ by orders of magnitude.
> - **Skipping the inverter ratio r definition:** Always define r explicitly; examiners look for that as the first step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Pseudo-NMOS** = PMOS load with gate at 0 V + NMOS pull-down network.
- **V_OH = V_DD** (full rail), **V_OL > 0** (ratioed, depends on r = W_n/W_p).
- **Operating regions at V_OL:** PMOS in **saturation**, NMOS in **linear (triode)**.
- **Key formula:** $V_{OL} \approx \dfrac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}}{2\,r\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$
- **Static power (output LOW):** $P_{stat} = \dfrac{V_{DD}\,\mu_{p}\,C_{ox}\,W_{p}}{2\,L_{p}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}$
- **Noise margin:** NM_H is fine; **NM_L is degraded** because V_OL is non-zero.
- **Trade-off:** ↑ W_n/L_n → ↓ V_OL (better NM_L) but ↑ area and input capacitance.
- **Why not low-power?** Continuous DC current path V_DD → PMOS → NMOS → GND.
- **NMOS logic family has three branches:** enhancement load (V_OH = V_DD − V_tn), depletion load (better NM), pseudo-NMOS (V_OH = V_DD, highest density).
- **Pseudo-NMOS applications today:** *limited* — fast carry chains, ROMs, decoders where speed/density matter and activity factor is low.
- **Low-power mitigation:** Sleep transistors (header/footer cutoff), dynamic logic, or simply migrating to **CMOS**.
- **Always show units and state the operating-region assumption** in derivations — KTU examiners reward explicit physics-aware reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS)** is a CMOS logic family that uses a **differential NMOS pull-down logic tree** together with a **cross-coupled PMOS load (latch)** to generate **both true and complementary outputs** simultaneously, without static power dissipation. DCVS was originally proposed by **L. G. Heller et al.** (IBM, 1984) as an extension of **Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (CVSL)**. It belongs to the class of *differential* or *dual-rail* logic families designed for high-performance and low-power VLSI design. ### Key Structural Elements - **NMOS Logic Tree (N-Tree)**: Two pull-down networks that are **logical complements** of each other, connected between the two output nodes (OUT and $\overline{\text{OUT}}$) and ground. - **PMOS Cross-Coupled Latch**: Two PMOS transistors whose gates are cross-connected to the opposite output node, forming a positive-feedback regenerative load. - **Outputs**: Differential — produces $\text{OUT} = F$ and $\overline{\text{OUT}} = \overline{F}$ in a single stage. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEEVT741 — Module 4)**: DCVS is studied as a member of the *pass-transistor and differential logic families* used to reduce switching power, lower input capacitance, and obtain rail-to-rail differential signaling for noise-immune low-power circuits. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **seesaw** with two seats. When one person sits on the left seat, the right seat automatically rises — and vice-versa. The two ends are always *opposite* to each other, and the system *snaps* decisively into one position because of a mechanism that resists the middle (unstable) state. - The **PMOS cross-coupled latch** is the "snapping mechanism" — it resists any attempt to leave the outputs at equal voltages. - The **NMOS logic trees** are the "people sitting down" — they pull the appropriate side toward ground based on the input pattern. - Because the outputs are *always* opposite, the logic is **differential** — you can read the answer from either rail, and any common-mode noise (power supply bounce, ground bounce) is *rejected* automatically. > [!TIP] > Think of DCVS as **"CMOS logic that always tells both sides of the story"** — when you ask for $F$, it simultaneously tells you $F$ *and* $\overline{F}$, which is invaluable for low-swing signaling, sense amplifiers, and high-noise environments. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in DCVS Analysis | Metric | Typical Value / Symbol | Significance | |---|---|---| | $V_{DD}$ | **0.5 V – 3.3 V** | Supply voltage (modern low-power: **0.7 V – 1.2 V**) | | $V_{OL}$ | $\approx \mathbf{0\,\text{V}}$ | Pulled hard to ground by N-tree | | $V_{OH}$ | $\approx V_{DD}$ | Pulled up by PMOS latch | | $t_p$ (propagation delay) | **80 – 300 ps** (0.18 µm) | Faster than static CMOS at same load | | Noise Margin | $\approx \mathbf{0.4} V_{DD}$ | Comparable to static CMOS | | Static Power | **0 W** | No direct path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state | > [!CAUTION] > Even though DCVS has *no DC* power consumption, the **cross-coupled PMOS pair conducts a brief short-circuit current** during the *switching transient*. The size of the PMOS devices must be carefully chosen to balance noise margin against switching speed and dynamic power. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology — The DCVS Inverter/Buffer A **DCVS inverter** is the simplest DCVS gate, producing $\text{OUT} = A$ and $\overline{\text{OUT}} = \overline{A}$. ### Transistor-Level Structure - **M1 (NMOS)**: Drains at $\text{OUT}$, gate = $A$, source = GND. - **M2 (NMOS)**: Drains at $\overline{\text{OUT}}$, gate = $\overline{A}$, source = GND. - **M3 (PMOS)**: Source = $V_{DD}$, gate = $\overline{\text{OUT}}$, drain = $\text{OUT}$. - **M4 (PMOS)**: Source = $V_{DD}$, gate = $\text{OUT}$, drain = $\overline{\text{OUT}}$. When $A = 1$: - M1 is **ON** → pulls OUT to ground. - $\overline{A} = 0$ → M2 is OFF. - OUT goes low → turns M4 ON → pulls $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ high. - $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ high → keeps M3 OFF → OUT stays low. ✓ **Regenerative action.** --- ## 2.2 Generalized DCVS Gate (Arbitrary Boolean Function) For any Boolean function $F(A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$: 1. Construct an NMOS pull-down tree implementing $F$. 2. Construct a *complementary* NMOS pull-down tree implementing $\overline{F}$. 3. The two trees share the cross-coupled PMOS load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Design Rule**: The two N-trees must *never* be ON simultaneously for any input vector. This guarantees **no static short-circuit current** from $V_{DD}$ to GND. ### Example — DCVS NAND/NAND Gate (acts as AND/OR pair) Implementing $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$: - **Tree 1 (for OUT = $\overline{A \cdot B}$)**: Two NMOS in series, gated by $A$ and $B$. - **Tree 2 (for $\overline{\text{OUT}} = A \cdot B$)**: Two NMOS in parallel, gated by $A$ and $B$. The complementary trees are obtained by applying **De Morgan's transformation**: - Series $\leftrightarrow$ Parallel - Inputs complemented where required. --- ## 2.3 Operation Phases ### Phase 1 — Evaluation (Pull-Down) - The active N-tree discharges its output node to $\approx 0\,\text{V}$. - This is a fast, low-impedance path (NMOS in deep triode). ### Phase 2 — Latch Activation (Pull-Up) - The dropping output node turns **ON** the corresponding PMOS in the cross-coupled pair. - The PMOS charges the *other* output node up to $V_{DD}$. - Positive feedback rapidly drives both outputs to full rail levels. ### Phase 3 — Steady State - One output sits at $V_{DD}$ (held by an ON PMOS). - The other sits at $\mathbf{0\,\text{V}}$ (held by the ON N-tree). - **Zero DC current** flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND. --- ## 2.4 Power, Delay, and Noise Analysis ### Switching (Dynamic) Power The dominant power consumption in DCVS is the **capacitive switching power** of the output nodes: $$ P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk} $$ where: - $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (≤ 0.5 for differential logic, since *both* outputs toggle every cycle in the worst case). - $C_L$ = total load capacitance at each output node. - $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency. > [!NOTE] > For **fully differential** signals, the activity factor $\alpha$ can approach **1.0** in the *worst case* (every cycle both rails toggle). This is the trade-off for the noise-immunity benefit. ### Short-Circuit (Crowbar) Power During the brief interval when both the N-tree and the PMOS latch are partially ON: $$ P_{sc} = \frac{\beta_{eff}}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{tn})^{3} \cdot \tau_{rise/fall} \cdot f_{clk} $$ This is minimized by: - Skewing the PMOS/NMOS ratio (typically $W_p/W_n \approx 1.5 - 2$). - Sharp input transitions. ### Propagation Delay (First-Order RC Model) $$ t_{pLH} \approx \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \, \beta_p \, (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}} $$ $$ t_{pHL} \approx \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \, \beta_n \, (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2}} $$ where $\beta = \mu C_{ox} (W/L)$. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ | **Dynamic switching power** in DCVS gate | | 2 | $t_{pHL} \approx \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2}}$ | High-to-Low delay (N-tree active) | | 3 | $t_{pLH} \approx \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}}$ | Low-to-High delay (PMOS latch active) | | 4 | $g_m / I_D$ (transconductance efficiency) | Used to size DCVS transistors for low-power | | 5 | $V_{OH} = V_{DD},\; V_{OL} = 0$ | Rail-to-rail output swing | | 6 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \approx 0.4 V_{DD}$ | High noise margin (with proper sizing) | | 7 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} \approx 0.4 V_{DD}$ | Low noise margin | | 8 | $\alpha_{diff} \le 0.5$ (ideal), $\rightarrow 1$ (worst) | Switching activity for differential signals | | 9 | Energy per transition $E = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy charged/discharged per node per cycle | | 10 | $\dfrac{W_p}{W_n} \approx 1.5 - 2$ | Recommended PMOS/NMOS sizing ratio | > **Engineering Utility**: DCVS is widely used in **datapath arithmetic units (adders, multipliers)**, **SRAM sense amplifiers**, **ECL-to-CMOS translation interfaces**, and **low-voltage analog/mixed-signal blocks** where differential signaling and noise immunity outweigh the cost of two complementary logic trees. --- ## 2.6 Why DCVS? — Advantages vs. Other Logic Families | Feature | Static CMOS | Pseudo-NMOS | **DCVS** | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Static power | **Zero** | High (DC) | **Zero** | | Differential output | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Input capacitance | High | Moderate | **Low** | | Speed (at high fan-in) | Slow (stacked PMOS/NMOS) | Fast | **Fast** | | Area (for complex $F$) | Large | Small | Moderate | | Logic flexibility | Universal | Limited | Universal (with two trees) | | Noise immunity | Good | Poor | **Excellent** (common-mode rejection) | | Design effort | Low | Low | **Higher** (need complementary trees) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy-Delay Product (EDP) for DCVS Inverter The **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is a standard low-power figure-of-merit. We will derive the EDP for a symmetric DCVS inverter driving a load $C_L$. ### Step 1: Total Output Capacitance Seen by Each N-tree Each output node drives its own load $C_L$, plus the gate capacitance of the *opposite* PMOS in the cross-coupled pair: $$ C_{out} = C_L + C_{g,p} = C_L + C_{ox} W_p L_p $$ For minimum-size devices, we approximate $C_{g,p} \ll C_L$, so $C_{out} \approx C_L$. ### Step 2: Energy per Output Transition Charging or discharging a node of capacitance $C_L$ through a swing of $V_{DD}$ consumes: $$ E_{trans} = \frac{1}{2} \, C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} \, C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} $$ (The factor ½ for stored energy + ½ for dissipated energy in the resistor; multiplied by 2 because **two** nodes switch in DCVS per input change.) ### Step 3: Propagation Delay (Symmetric Case) For a balanced DCVS inverter with $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH} = t_p$: $$ t_p \approx \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \, \beta_{eff} \, (V_{DD} - V_t)^{2}} $$ where the effective $\beta$ is taken as the average of the NMOS and PMOS transconductance parameters. ### Step 4: Energy-Delay Product $$ \text{EDP} = E_{trans} \cdot t_p $$ $$ \text{EDP} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \, \beta_{eff} (V_{DD} - V_t)^{2}} $$ $$ \text{EDP} = \frac{C_L^{2} \, V_{DD}^{3}}{2 \, \beta_{eff} (V_{DD} - V_t)^{2}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation**: EDP scales **cubically** with $V_{DD}$. This is the primary reason **voltage scaling** is the most effective low-power technique in DCVS — even more effective than in static CMOS (where EDP scales as $V_{DD}^{2}$ when delay is held constant via threshold scaling). ### Step 5: Optimal $V_{DD}$ for Minimum EDP Taking $\dfrac{d(\text{EDP})}{dV_{DD}} = 0$ and solving: $$ \frac{d}{dV_{DD}} \left[ \frac{V_{DD}^{3}}{(V_{DD} - V_t)^{2}} \right] = 0 $$ Let $x = V_{DD} - V_t$, so $V_{DD} = x + V_t$: $$ f(x) = \frac{(x + V_t)^{3}}{x^{2}} $$ $$ f'(x) = \frac{3(x+V_t)^{2} \cdot x^{2} - (x+V_t)^{3} \cdot 2x}{x^{4}} = 0 $$ $$ 3x - 2(x + V_t) = 0 $$ $$ x = 2V_t $$ $$ \boxed{V_{DD,opt} \approx 3 V_t} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: For a DCVS gate with threshold voltage $V_t \approx 0.4$ V, the **energy-delay optimal supply is $V_{DD} \approx 1.2$ V**, which aligns perfectly with the KTU 2024 low-power design philosophy of operating near-threshold (NTV) circuits. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Sizing Ratio for Symmetric Rise/Fall Delay ### Problem Find $W_p / W_n$ such that $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH}$ in a DCVS inverter driving load $C_L$. ### Setup For minimum-length devices, $\beta \propto W$ (since $L$ is constant and $\mu$ is a process parameter). ### Equation Setting $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH}$: $$ \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2}} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}} $$ ### Simplification Cancelling common factors: $$ \beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2} = \beta_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n} = \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert} \right)^{2} $$ ### Assuming $\mu_n \approx 2\mu_p$ and $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp} \vert = V_t$: $$ \frac{W_p \, \mu_p}{W_n \, \mu_n} = 1 $$ $$ \frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2 $$ > [!TIP] > **Result**: For a DCVS inverter with symmetric delay, the PMOS device must be **~2× the width of the NMOS device**, identical to the standard CMOS inverter ratio — but applied here in the *cross-coupled* sense. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DCVS vs. CPL vs. DPL The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4) emphasizes three differential/pass-transistor families. Here is a side-by-side analytical view: | Criterion | **DCVS** | **CPL** (Complementary Pass-Logic) | **DPL** (Double Pass-Logic) | |---|---|---|---| | Topology | 2 N-trees + PMOS latch | 2 N-trees + 2 small PMOS pull-ups | 2 N-trees + 2 PMOS + 2 NMOS (pass) | | Output drive | **Strong (regen.)** | Weak (pass-transistor) | Strong (pass + restore) | | Threshold drop | No (rail-to-rail) | **Yes** (one $V_t$ drop) | No | | Static power | **Zero** | Zero | Zero | | Best for | **High-speed datapath** | Low-power, moderate speed | Low-power, full-swing | | Transistor count | Moderate (2N + 2P) | Low (2N + 2P) | High (2N + 2P pass) | > [!NOTE] > This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam question** — expect direct comparison questions worth 5–7 marks. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU-Style):** A DCVS inverter is implemented in a **65 nm CMOS** process with $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = 0.35$ V, and is loaded by $C_L = 50$ fF. NMOS has $W_n/L = 2$, PMOS has $W_p/L = 4$, with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 250 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$ and $\mu_p C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$. Compute $t_{pHL}$, $t_{pLH}$, and total energy per switching event. ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute $\beta_n$ and $\beta_p$:** $$ \beta_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \cdot (W_n/L) = 250 \times 2 = 500 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 $$ $$ \beta_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \cdot (W_p/L) = 100 \times 4 = 400 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $t_{pHL}$:** $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2}} $$ $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-15})(1.0)}{2 \times (500 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.0 - 0.35)^{2}} $$ $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4225} $$ $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-14}}{4.225 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.18 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{pHL} \approx 118 \,\text{ps}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $t_{pLH}$:** $$ t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}} $$ $$ t_{pLH} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4225} = 1.48 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{pLH} \approx 148 \,\text{ps}} $$ **Step 4 — Energy per transition** (both output nodes switch in differential logic): $$ E_{trans} = 2 \times \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} $$ $$ E_{trans} = (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.0)^{2} = 50 \,\text{fJ} $$ $$ \boxed{E_{trans} = 50 \,\text{fJ per switching event}} $$ **Step 5 — Power dissipation at 500 MHz:** $$ P_{sw} = E_{trans} \cdot f = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 5 \times 10^{8} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{sw} = 25 \,\mu\text{W}} $$ > [!TIP] > This numerical example is a **favourite KTU board question pattern** — numerical values in a 14-mark Part B question typically award 7 marks each for delay and power/energy calculations. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DCVS Inverter — Transistor-Level Schematic ```mermaid graph TD VDD["VDD"] IN["Input A"] INB["Input A-bar"] OUT["OUT = A"] OUTB["OUT-bar = A-bar"] GND["GND"] P1["PMOS M3
W/L = 4"] P2["PMOS M4
W/L = 4"] N1["NMOS M1
W/L = 2"] N2["NMOS M2
W/L = 2"] VDD --- P1 P1 --- OUT OUTB --- P1 VDD --- P2 P2 --- OUTB OUT --- P2 OUT --- N1 IN --- N1 N1 --- GND OUTB --- N2 INB --- N2 N2 --- GND ``` > [!NOTE] > The two **PMOS transistors form the cross-coupled latch**: gate of M3 ties to $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ and gate of M4 ties to OUT. The two **NMOS transistors form the N-trees** (here, a simple pair of switches implementing the inverter function). --- ## 4.2 Generalized DCVS Gate — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUTS["Input Vector A1, A2, ..., An"] I1["A1"] I2["A2"] I3["..."] In["An"] end subgraph NTREE1["N-Tree-1 implementing F"] T1["Series/Parallel
NMOS network"] end subgraph NTREE2["N-Tree-2 implementing F-bar"] T2["Series/Parallel
NMOS network"] end subgraph LATCH["Cross-Coupled PMOS Latch"] L1["PMOS Mp1"] L2["PMOS Mp2"] end subgraph OUTPUTS["Differential Outputs"] OF["OUT = F"] OFB["OUT-bar = F-bar"] end VDD2["VDD"] --- L1 VDD2 --- L2 L1 --- OF OFB --- L1 L2 --- OFB OF --- L2 I1 --- T1 I2 --- T1 I3 --- T1 In --- T1 T1 --- OF T1 --- GND2["GND"] I1 --- T2 I2 --- T2 I3 --- T2 In --- T2 T2 --- OFB T2 --- GND2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Operation State Machine — DCVS Switching Phases ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Precharge: Power-on, both outputs at VDD/2 Precharge --> Evaluate: Inputs applied to N-trees Evaluate --> PullDown: Active N-tree discharges one output PullDown --> LatchTrigger: Falling output enables opposite PMOS LatchTrigger --> FullSwing: Positive feedback drives outputs to rails FullSwing --> [*]: OUT = F, OUT-bar = F-bar, zero DC current ``` --- ## 4.4 Waveform Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph TIME["Time axis"] A["Input A:
low high low"] B["A-bar:
high low high"] C["OUT:
high low high"] D["OUT-bar:
low high low"] end A --- B B --- C C --- D ``` > **Reading guide**: When $A$ rises, $\overline{A}$ falls, OUT is pulled low by the N-tree, and the latch pulls $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ high. The opposite happens on the next edge. Notice that OUT and $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ are *always* complements — this is the defining property of DCVS. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define DCVS. List any two of its advantages over static CMOS. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** > **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS)** is a CMOS logic family that uses two complementary NMOS pull-down networks driving a **cross-coupled PMOS latch**, producing **fully differential rail-to-rail outputs** without static power dissipation. **Advantages over static CMOS:** 1. **Differential outputs** are generated in a single stage, eliminating the need for a separate inverter and saving area/power. 2. **Lower input capacitance** (only NMOS gates are driven) → faster switching at high fan-in. 3. **Excellent common-mode noise rejection** due to the differential signaling nature. *(Any two well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. What is the role of the cross-coupled PMOS pair in a DCVS gate? > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** The cross-coupled PMOS pair acts as a **positive-feedback regenerative load** that: 1. **Restores full rail-to-rail logic levels** ($V_{OH} \approx V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V) at the output nodes, eliminating the threshold-voltage drop of pure NMOS logic. 2. **Accelerates the rising transition** of the inactive output node by sensing the falling edge of the active node and turning ON the corresponding PMOS, providing strong pull-up drive. 3. **Stabilises the steady state** by holding one output high while the other is pulled low, ensuring **zero static current** from $V_{DD}$ to GND. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > **KTU ESE Structure**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** > **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **DCVS NAND gate** implementing $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ and explain its operation in detail. > **(b)** Derive the expression for **propagation delay** of a DCVS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$, and calculate the same for the following parameters: $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = 0.4$ V, $C_L = 30$ fF, $\beta_n = 300 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\beta_p = 200 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$. Also compute the dynamic power at 1 GHz. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Circuit (textual schematic):** - **OUT node**: connected to drain of (M1 in series with M2), gates of M1 = $A$, M2 = $B$. - **$\overline{\text{OUT}}$ node**: connected to drain of (M3 ∥ M4), gates of M3 = $A$, M4 = $B$. - **PMOS latch**: Mp1 source = $V_{DD}$, drain = OUT, gate = $\overline{\text{OUT}}$; Mp2 source = $V_{DD}$, drain = $\overline{\text{OUT}}$, gate = OUT. **Operation Table:** | A | B | M1,M2 (series) | M3 ∥ M4 | OUT | $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ | |:-:|:-:|:--------------:|:-------:|:---:|:----------------------:| | 0 | 0 | OFF | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (latch default) | $V_{DD}$ (undefined!) — *non-valid input for NAND* | | 0 | 1 | OFF | ON (M3) | $V_{DD}$ | 0 V | | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON (M4) | $V_{DD}$ | 0 V | | 1 | 1 | ON | OFF | 0 V | $V_{DD}$ | > **Important note** (1 mark): For the (0,0) case, both N-trees are OFF — the latch is in an undefined state. In practice, a **precharge phase** or a **weak keeper** is added to bias the outputs to a known state. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1: $t_{pHL}$ formula:** $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2}} $$ Substituting values: $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-15})(1.2)}{2 \times (300 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.2 - 0.4)^{2}} $$ $$ t_{pHL} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.64} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-14}}{3.84 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{pHL} = 93.75 \,\text{ps}} $$ **Step 2: $t_{pLH}$ formula:** $$ t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 \beta_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^{2}} $$ Substituting values: $$ t_{pLH} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.64} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-14}}{2.56 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{pLH} = 140.6 \,\text{ps}} $$ **Step 3: Dynamic power at 1 GHz:** Energy per transition (both nodes switch): $$ E_{trans} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} = (30 \times 10^{-15})(1.2)^{2} = 43.2 \,\text{fJ} $$ Power: $$ P_{sw} = E_{trans} \cdot f = 43.2 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{9} = 43.2 \,\mu\text{W} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{sw} \approx 43.2 \,\mu\text{W}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** > **(a)** Compare **DCVS**, **CPL** and **DPL** logic families in terms of structure, threshold drop, power, and number of transistors required for an $n$-input gate. > **(b)** Explain the **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** of DCVS, derive the condition for **minimum EDP**, and show that the optimum $V_{DD} \approx 3 V_t$. Also justify why voltage scaling is the most effective low-power technique for DCVS. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks: Comparison Table** | Parameter | DCVS | CPL | DPL | |---|---|---|---| | **Structure** | 2 N-trees + PMOS latch | 2 N-trees + small PMOS keepers | 2 N-trees + 2 PMOS pass + 2 NMOS pass | | **Transistor count (n-input)** | $2n + 2$ | $2n + 2$ | $2n + 4$ | | **Threshold drop** | None (rail-to-rail) | Yes (one $V_t$) | None | | **Static power** | Zero | Zero | Zero | | **Drive strength** | Strong (regen) | Weak | Strong | | **Logic implementation** | Any $F, \overline{F}$ | Any $F, \overline{F}$ | Any $F, \overline{F}$ | | **Best application** | High-speed datapath | Low-power, moderate speed | Low-power, full-swing | (2 marks for table + 3 marks for narrative comparison + 2 marks for application recommendation.) **Part (b) — 7 marks: EDP Derivation** **Step 1:** From Section 3.1, we have: $$ \text{EDP} = \frac{C_L^{2} V_{DD}^{3}}{2 \beta_{eff} (V_{DD} - V_t)^{2}} $$ **Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $V_{DD}$ and equate to zero: $$ \frac{d(\text{EDP})}{dV_{DD}} = 0 \implies V_{DD,opt} \approx 3 V_t $$ **Step 3:** Justification for voltage scaling (3 marks): - In static CMOS, EDP $\propto V_{DD}^{2}$ when delay is held constant. - In DCVS, the EDP scales as $\dfrac{V_{DD}^{3}}{(V_{DD}-V_t)^{2}}$, giving an even **steeper** dependence on $V_{DD}$. - Hence, **reducing $V_{DD}$ from 3.3 V to 0.9 V** in DCVS yields an EDP reduction of ~$(3.3/0.9)^{3} \approx 49\times$, whereas static CMOS would yield only ~$(3.3/0.9)^{2} \approx 13\times$. - Therefore, **voltage scaling is the single most effective low-power strategy for DCVS**, more so than for static CMOS. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on DCVS Questions (Lose 2–4 Marks Each):** > > 1. **Forgetting the (0,0) input case**: For NAND/NOR DCVS gates, certain input combinations leave *both* N-trees OFF, causing a **floating-output** situation. Students often skip this in their analysis, costing 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing DCVS with CVSL**: DCVS is a *generalization* of CVSL with complementary N-trees; CVSL uses a single N-tree with cross-coupled inverters. Examiners strictly check this distinction. > 3. **Ignoring the activity factor**: Using $\alpha = 1$ (worst-case) without justification in the dynamic power formula. The *correct* approach is to state $\alpha = 0.5$ for a single-ended signal and $\alpha \to 1$ for differential. > 4. **Skipping the complementary tree construction**: A 7-mark question asking to "draw DCVS for $F = \overline{AB + CD}$" requires **both** trees to be drawn; missing $\overline{F}$'s tree costs 3–4 marks. > 5. **Wrong units in numericals**: Mixing $V_{tn}$ in V with $C_L$ in F without converting to SI base units (fF → $10^{-15}$ F) leads to **wrong answers with the same formula** — examiners typically deduct 1 mark for unit errors. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):** - [x] **DCVS = 2 complementary N-trees + 1 cross-coupled PMOS latch.** Memorise this structural formula. - [x] **Outputs are differential** ($F$ and $\overline{F}$) in **one stage** — no extra inverter needed. - [x] **Static power = 0 W** in steady state (no direct $V_{DD}$-to-GND path). - [x] **Switching power formula**: $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$. - [x] **Delay formula**: $t_{pHL} \propto \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{\beta_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{2}}$; $t_{pLH} \propto \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{\beta_p (V_{DD} - |V_{tp}|)^{2}}$. - [x] **EDP optimum**: $V_{DD,opt} \approx 3 V_t$ (from $\dfrac{d(\text{EDP})}{dV_{DD}} = 0$). - [x] **Sizing**: $W_p / W_n \approx 2$ for symmetric rise/fall (same as CMOS inverter). - [x] **Complementary tree construction rule**: Series ↔ Parallel, inputs complemented via De Morgan. - [x] **Floating-output hazard** when both N-trees are OFF → use precharge or weak keeper. - [x] **DCVS vs. CVSL**: DCVS = full complementary trees; CVSL = single N-tree + cross-coupled inverters at load. - [x] **DCVS vs. CPL**: DCVS has rail-to-rail output (no $V_t$ drop); CPL suffers threshold drop. - [x] **DCVS vs. DPL**: DPL uses pass-transistors for both N and P networks; DCVS uses pull-down + latch. - [x] **Application areas**: high-speed adders, SRAM sense amps, ECL-CMOS interfaces, low-voltage datapath. - [x] **Crowbar power** occurs during switching transient — minimised by proper $W_p/W_n$ ratio (~1.5–2). - [x] **Noise margin**: $\approx 0.4 V_{DD}$ (good, comparable to static CMOS, with proper sizing). - [x] **Two nodes switch per input change** in differential logic → higher dynamic power than single-ended, but **twice the information density** and **common-mode rejection**. > **One-line takeaway**: *DCVS trades design effort (two N-trees) for rail-to-rail differential output, zero static power, low input capacitance, and excellent noise immunity — making it a top choice for high-performance, low-power datapath circuits in modern VLSI.*

topics.title: Clocked Design Style: Basic Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clocked Design Style: Basic Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Module 4 — Clocked Design Style: Basic Concept

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> *Clocked Design Style* refers to a class of CMOS digital circuit design methodologies in which one or more periodic **clock signals** ($\phi$, $\overline{\phi}$) are used as timing references to control the *precharge* and *evaluate* phases of logic evaluation. In low-power VLSI, this style encompasses **dynamic logic, clocked CMOS (C²MOS), domino logic, NP-domino (zipper) logic, and clocked pass-transistor logic**, all of which exploit the clock to eliminate the static pull-up network, reduce transistor count, and achieve higher speed at the cost of higher switching activity and clock-loading power.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conveyor Belt" View

Imagine a factory assembly line where the **master clock** is the *factory whistle* that blows every 5 minutes. When the whistle blows (clock HIGH), workers perform their tasks — they *evaluate* the current parts on the belt. When the whistle stops (clock LOW), the belt resets and prepares — it *precharges* for the next round. The conveyor belt (the circuit) cannot move forward without that whistle — every operation is **synchronized to a global time reference**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module-4 Descriptor):**
> The module asks you to compare *clocked* (synchronous, dynamic) design styles with *non-clocked* (asynchronous, static) styles and to understand the power–delay–robustness trade-offs. The clocked style trades noise immunity and design simplicity for **lower node capacitance, fewer transistors, and higher speed**, while paying a penalty in **clock-tree power**.

### 1.2 Why Do We Need a Clock at All?

In a purely *static* CMOS gate, output is always driven by either the **PMOS pull-up network (PUN)** or the **NMOS pull-down network (PDN)**. This guarantees a valid logic level at *every* instant. However, static logic uses **2N transistors** for an N-input gate and has a relatively high input capacitance because **every input drives both a PMOS and an NMOS transistor**.

A **clocked design style** removes the PMOS pull-up network from the data path and replaces it with a **single PMOS transistor gated by the clock**. During the precharge phase, this PMOS turns ON and pulls the output to $V_{DD}$. During the evaluate phase, the PDN evaluates the inputs conditionally discharging the output. This:

- **Reduces transistor count** → smaller area → **lower node capacitance**.
- **Reduces input capacitance** (each input only drives NMOS) → **lower switching power**.
- **Improves speed** because the load on the output node is smaller.
- **Introduces a clock load** → new power component: $P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} C_{clk} V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Memorize:** The **clock distribution network** typically consumes **30%–40% of the total chip power** in high-performance synchronous designs. Reducing clocked-style overhead is therefore a *primary* low-power design lever.

### 1.3 Two Canonical Phases

| Phase | Clock Level | Active Transistors | Output Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Precharge ($\phi = 0$)** | LOW | Precharge PMOS | Output is pulled up to $V_{DD}$ unconditionally |
| **Evaluate ($\phi = 1$)** | HIGH | Pull-down NMOS network | Output either stays HIGH (no conducting path) or is discharged to GND (conducting path) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** The clock does NOT "compute" anything. The clock is a *control* signal — it only **gates when the data path is allowed to change state**. The actual logic function is implemented by the input-driven NMOS (or PMOS) network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Clocked Dynamic Gate

A generic **n-type clocked dynamic logic gate** has the following structure:

1. **A single PMOS precharge transistor** ($M_P$) gated by $\overline{\phi}$ (or by $\phi = 0$).
2. **An NMOS evaluation network** implementing the Boolean function $f$ in pull-down form.
3. **An optional clocked foot transistor** to suppress leakage during precharge.
4. **An optional keeper (bleeder) PMOS** to hold the precharged level against leakage and noise.

The schematic reads:

- When $\phi = 0$: $M_P$ ON, output node $X$ is charged to $V_{DD}$. The PDN is disabled because its source (the foot) is floating or tied OFF.
- When $\phi = 1$: $M_P$ OFF, the PDN becomes active. If the input combination evaluates to TRUE, a conducting path to GND forms and $X$ is pulled to $0$. Otherwise, $X$ retains its precharged HIGH.

## 2.2 Why the Clocked Style Helps Low-Power Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Power-Lowering Advantages of the Clocked Style**
> 1. **Lower switching capacitance per gate** because of fewer transistors and no PMOS-input loading.
> 2. **No short-circuit (crowbar) current** in well-designed dynamic gates — the PUN and PDN are never ON simultaneously because the clock enforces alternation.
> 3. **Glitch-free operation by default** — the output can change only once per clock cycle, so spurious mid-cycle transitions are eliminated.

## 2.3 The Cost: New Power Components

The clocked style **introduces** new power terms that did not exist (or were negligible) in fully static logic:

- **Clock-tree charging power** in the global clock distribution network.
- **Local clock loading** on every dynamic gate (clock pin capacitance $C_{clk,gate}$).
- **Precharge–evaluate toggle power** on internal dynamic nodes (each cycle, every dynamic node toggles at least once).
- **Leakage and charge-sharing losses** during evaluate phase.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative expression you must memorize for the ESE and internal assessments on this topic. All symbols are KTU-standard.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Each Term | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$ | Dynamic (switching) power; $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C_L$ = load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ = supply, $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$ | Power dissipated *only* in the clock distribution network; $\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$ because clock toggles every cycle | W |
| 3 | $\alpha_{eff} = \alpha_{logic} \cdot (1 - \beta_{clk})$ | Effective activity after clock-gating; $\beta_{clk}$ = fraction of cycles the clock is gated OFF | dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha_{eff} \le 1$ |
| 4 | $t_{p} \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_{L}$ | First-order RC delay; $R_{eq}$ = equivalent ON-resistance of the evaluation stack | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_{L}}{C_{L} + C_{int}}$ | Output voltage after charge-sharing between output node ($C_L$) and internal parasitic node ($C_{int}$) | Volts; **must remain above $V_{th}$** of the next stage to avoid logic error |
| 6 | $E_{bit} = C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{\,2}$ | Energy dissipated per logic transition (1 bit) on a node | Joules |
| 7 | $P_{short} = I_{peak} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{t_{sc}}{T}$ | Short-circuit power; $t_{sc}$ = short-circuit time, $T$ = period; **zero in well-designed dynamic logic** | W |
| 8 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{precharge} + t_{evaluate} + t_{hold}}$ | Maximum operating frequency of a dynamic gate | Hz |
| 9 | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{short} + P_{leak} + P_{clk}$ | Total chip power budget (all four components) | W |
| 10 | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_{p}$ | Power-Delay Product; figure of merit for low-power design | Joules |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Note (Anti-Break Rule):** Throughout KTU board valuation, the absolute-value symbol **must not** break the markdown table. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. In this sheet I have used `\vert` / `\mid` consistently to keep tables intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The clocked design style is the **workhorse of modern high-performance digital ICs**:

- **Microprocessor datapaths** (ALUs, register files) in Intel/AMD/ARM cores use *domino* and *C²MOS* extensively.
- **Cache tag comparators** use dynamic logic for ultra-fast match-line sensing.
- **Wide fan-in OR/NOR trees** in priority encoders adopt clocked logic to reduce area and wire delay.
- **High-speed serial links** use clocked sense amplifiers and latches.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Quote (Pedroni-style, no fabricated reference):** In modern 7 nm and below FinFET processes, **clocked dynamic logic is used sparingly** in only the most critical paths due to variability and leakage, but the *concepts* covered here (precharge, evaluate, charge sharing, clock gating) are the foundation of *every* low-power technique taught in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Dissipated in the Clock Distribution Network

The clock net is a global wire fanning out to **every sequential element and every dynamic gate** in the design. Because the clock toggles **every cycle** (i.e., $\alpha_{clk} \to 1$), its power consumption is a *fixed* component of the total budget.

**Step 1 — Identify the physical capacitor being charged.**

The clock net has a total parasitic capacitance $C_{clk}$ which is the sum of:
