- $C_{wire}$: metal interconnect capacitance of the H-tree / clock grid.
- $C_{pin}$: gate capacitance of the clock pin of every flip-flop.
- $C_{dyn}$: gate capacitance of the clock pin of every dynamic gate.

$$
C_{clk} = C_{wire} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_{FF}} C_{pin,FF,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N_{dyn}} C_{pin,dyn,j}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy required to charge $C_{clk}$ from $0$ to $V_{DD}$.**

Energy stored in the capacitor at the end of the half-cycle is:

$$
E_{charge} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS charging transistor.**

The PMOS driving the clock net behaves like a resistor $R_{clk}$ charging $C_{clk}$. By energy conservation, *exactly half* the energy delivered by the supply is stored in $C_{clk}$, and the other half is **dissipated as heat** in $R_{clk}$. Hence energy per charge = $C_{clk} V_{DD}^{\,2}$ total delivered, of which $C_{clk} V_{DD}^{\,2}/2$ is dissipated.

**Step 4 — Discharge energy.**

When the clock falls, $C_{clk}$ is discharged to ground through the NMOS, again dissipating $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{clk} V_{DD}^{\,2}$ in the NMOS resistance. Total energy dissipated **per full clock cycle** (one charge + one discharge):

$$
E_{cycle} = C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to power by multiplying by frequency.**

$$
P_{clk} = E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk} = C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}
$$

For multi-phase or multi-clock designs, the activity factor $\alpha_{clk}$ is added:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *exact same form* as the standard dynamic-power equation, but with $\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$ since clocks toggle every cycle. KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is clock power so dominant?"* — the answer is precisely this $\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$ argument.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Charge-Sharing Error Voltage in a Dynamic Gate

Consider a clocked dynamic NAND gate with two inputs A and B. Internally, the PDN source node has a parasitic capacitance $C_{int}$ to ground, while the output node has load capacitance $C_L$.

**Step 1 — Initial state (precharge).**

During $\phi = 0$, the output node $X$ is charged to $V_{DD}$. The internal node $Y$ (between the two series NMOS) is at $0$ V (no conducting path, and it was discharged in the previous evaluate phase). So:

$$
V_X(0^-) = V_{DD}, \qquad V_Y(0^-) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate phase begins, with A = B = 1 (the *only* case that turns on both NMOS).**

When $\phi$ rises, both NMOS turn ON. $X$ and $Y$ become connected through the upper NMOS, and $Y$ is connected to ground through the lower NMOS. The charge on $C_L$ redistributes over $(C_L + C_{int})$.

**Step 3 — Conservation of charge.**

Total charge before evaluation:

$$
Q_{before} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = C_L \, V_{DD}
$$

Total charge after evaluation (common voltage $V_f$ on both nodes):

$$
Q_{after} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_f
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $V_f$.**

$$
C_L \, V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) \, V_f
$$

$$
V_f = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}
$$

**Step 5 — The error voltage on the output.**

$$
\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_f = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}\right) = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board-Exam Trap:** Students often write $V_f = V_{DD}/2$ assuming equal capacitances. **Do not assume symmetry.** If $C_{int} > C_L$, the output can fall *below* the $V_{th}$ of the next stage, causing **functional failure**. This is why a **precharge internal node** and a **keeper transistor** are mandatory in robust clocked designs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Operating Frequency of a Dynamic Gate

A dynamic gate needs three time windows per cycle:

- $t_{pre}$: time to charge $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$ (set by PMOS drive strength).
- $t_{eval}$: time to evaluate and discharge $C_L$ (set by PDN drive strength).
- $t_{hold}$: minimum time the output must remain valid for the next stage to latch (typically one FO4 delay).

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{pre} + t_{eval} + t_{hold}}
$$

For a typical clocked inverter in 180 nm:

$$
t_{pre} \approx 60 \text{ ps}, \quad t_{eval} \approx 40 \text{ ps}, \quad t_{hold} \approx 30 \text{ ps}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{130 \text{ ps}} \approx 7.7 \text{ GHz}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Clocked vs. Static Design Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is **the single most important table** for the 14-mark question on this topic. KTU examiners typically mark against 8–10 of these comparison points.

| Parameter | Static CMOS | Clocked Dynamic | Clocked Domino (C²MOS + static inverter) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transistor count (N-input) | 2N | N + 2 | N + 4 |
| Input capacitance | $C_{g,PMOS} + C_{g,NMOS}$ | $C_{g,NMOS}$ only | $C_{g,NMOS}$ only |
| Static power | **Zero** (no DC path) | **Zero** (no DC path) | **Zero** (no DC path) |
| Logic levels | Always restored | Restored only after evaluate | Restored by static inverter |
| Noise margin | High | **Low** (during evaluate) | High |
| Charge-sharing risk | None | **High** | Low (with precharge-internal-node) |
| Clock load | None | One clock pin per gate | One clock pin per gate |
| Cascadability | Trivial | **Cannot cascade** directly | **Yes — non-inverting stages allowed** |
| Power per gate | Moderate | **Low** (small C, no crowbar) | **Low** |
| Robustness | Excellent | Poor | Good |
| Used in critical paths | Yes | Sparingly (today) | **Yes (ALU, fast paths)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Domino" Cascades Work:** A dynamic gate produces a *non-inverting* output only at the *end* of the evaluate phase. When followed by a **static CMOS inverter**, the result is a *full-rail*, *inverting* signal that is safe to feed into the *next* dynamic stage. Thus the cascade is: **Dynamic → Static Inverter → Dynamic → Static Inverter → ...** This chain is called a *domino* chain because each stage "falls" into the next, like a row of dominos.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Clocked Design Style (Module 4 Map)

The diagram below is the *master map* of the entire Module-4 syllabus. It shows how the basic clocked-design concept branches into the specific sub-styles (C²MOS, Domino, NP-Domino, Clocked Pass-Transistor) that the rest of Module 4 will explore in detail.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Module 4: Clocked Design Style] --> B[Basic Concept]
    A --> C[Specific Sub Styles]
    A --> D[Power vs Performance Tradeoff]

    B --> B1[Clock as Control Signal]
    B --> B2[Precharge Phase]
    B --> B3[Evaluate Phase]

    C --> C1[Clocked CMOS - C2MOS]
    C --> C2[Dynamic CMOS Logic]
    C --> C3[Domino Logic]
    C --> C4[NP Domino Zipper]
    C --> C5[Clocked Pass Transistor]

    D --> D1[Lower Switching Capacitance]
    D --> D2[No Short Circuit Current]
    D --> D3[Clock Tree Power Cost]
    D --> D4[Charge Sharing and Leakage]

    C1 --> E1[Static Inverter at Output]
    C2 --> E2[Single PMOS Precharge plus NMOS PDN]
    C3 --> E3[Dynamic plus Inverter plus Cascade]
    C4 --> E4[PMOS and NMOS Dynamic Pair]
    C5 --> E5[Clocked Transmission Gate]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Precharge / Evaluate State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Precharge
    Precharge --> Evaluate: Clock Rising Edge phi=1
    Evaluate --> Precharge: Clock Falling Edge phi=0
    Precharge: Output node X is pulled to VDD through PMOS precharge transistor
    Precharge: PDN is disabled by clock foot transistor
    Evaluate: PMOS precharge is OFF
    Evaluate: PDN evaluates input combination
    Evaluate: Output X either stays HIGH or discharges to GND
    Evaluate: Static inverter restores full rail logic level
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — A 2-Input Clocked Dynamic NAND Gate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Clk[(Clock phi)] --> MP[PMOS Precharge M1]
    MP --> X[Output Node X]
    MA[NMOS M2 - input A] --> X
    MB[NMOS M3 - input B] --> MA
    MB --> GND[(GND)]
    X --> INV[Static CMOS Inverter]
    INV --> Y[Buffered Output Y]
    Clk --> MF[Clocked Foot M4 - optional]
    MF --> MB
    Y --> K[Keeper PMOS M5 - optional]
    K --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The path from $V_{DD}$ through $M_P$ to $X$ charges $X$ when $\phi = 0$. The path from $X$ through $M_2$ and $M_3$ to GND discharges $X$ **only when $A = B = 1$** during $\phi = 1$. The static inverter restores full-rail levels and makes the gate **domino-cascadable**.

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Conceptual Waveforms

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Phi as Clock phi
    participant VDD as VDD Supply
    participant X as Output X (Dynamic)
    participant Y as Buffered Y (Inverter Out)
    Note over Phi: phi=0 Precharge Phase
    Phi-->>X: phi=0 forces PMOS ON, X rises to VDD
    Note over Y: Y is held at 0 by inverter
    Note over Phi: phi=1 Evaluate Phase with A=B=1
    Phi-->>X: phi=1 turns PMOS OFF, NMOS path active, X falls to GND
    X-->>Y: Y rises to VDD after inverter delay
    Note over Phi: phi=1 Evaluate Phase with A=0
    Phi-->>X: X stays at VDD, no discharge path
    X-->>Y: Y stays at 0
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!TIP]
> These questions test the *Recall* and *Understanding* levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Each carries **3 marks**, expected answer length ~50 words plus a small diagram.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **clocked design style** in CMOS VLSI. Why is it preferred for high-speed low-power datapaths?

**Model Answer:**
The *clocked design style* is a CMOS logic design methodology in which a periodic clock signal $\phi$ (with complement $\overline{\phi}$) controls the **precharge** and **evaluate** phases of logic computation. The PMOS pull-up network of static CMOS is replaced by a single clocked PMOS, and the function is implemented in an NMOS pull-down network. It is preferred for high-speed low-power datapaths because:
1. It **eliminates the PMOS input capacitance**, halving the input load and reducing dynamic power.
2. It uses **fewer transistors** ($\approx N+2$ for an $N$-input gate), reducing node capacitance.
3. It has **no short-circuit current** because PUN and PDN never conduct simultaneously — the clock enforces alternation.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between the **precharge** and **evaluate** phases of a dynamic CMOS gate. What is the role of the **clocked foot transistor**?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Precharge Phase ($\phi = 0$) | Evaluate Phase ($\phi = 1$) |
|---|---|---|
| Clock state | LOW | HIGH |
| Precharge PMOS | ON | OFF |
| Pull-down network | Disabled (foot OFF) | Active, evaluates inputs |
| Output node | Pulled to $V_{DD}$ unconditionally | Either retains $V_{DD}$ or discharges to GND |
| Energy stored | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ on output | $E$ is dissipated in PDN if a path exists |

The **clocked foot transistor** is a small NMOS placed in series with the PDN, gated by $\phi$. During precharge, the foot is OFF, **isolating the PDN from ground**. This prevents sub-threshold leakage of the PDN from corrupting the precharged level and also defeats the *charge-sharing* and *leakage* problems at low $V_{DD}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End-Semester Examination gives an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions, each split into two 7-mark sub-parts. The full, board-style model solutions are written out below.

---

### 📝 Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **clocked CMOS (C²MOS) dynamic NAND gate**. Discuss the role of the precharge PMOS, the NMOS evaluation network, and the static output inverter.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **charge-sharing error voltage** at the output of a clocked dynamic gate, and explain why this is a critical design issue in deep-submicron technologies.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

A **Clocked CMOS (C²MOS) NAND gate** for inputs $A$, $B$ consists of:

1. **Precharge PMOS** $M_P$ with gate tied to $\overline{\phi}$, source to $V_{DD}$, drain to output node $X$.
2. **NMOS evaluation network** — two NMOS $M_2$ (gate = $A$) and $M_3$ (gate = $B$) in **series**, with $M_3$ drain at $X$ and source to ground.
3. **Optional clocked foot** $M_4$ in series with $M_3$, gated by $\phi$ (some textbook variants omit this for simplicity).
4. **Static CMOS inverter** $I_1$ with input $X$ and output $Y$.

**Phase-by-phase operation:**

- **Precharge ($\phi = 0$):** $M_P$ is ON, $M_2$–$M_3$ are disabled. Output $X$ charges to $V_{DD}$. Inverter output $Y = 0$. The gate is in a *known good* state.
- **Evaluate ($\phi = 1$):** $M_P$ is OFF, the NMOS network becomes active. Three input cases:
  - $A = 0$, $B = 0$ or $A = 0, B = 1$ or $A = 1, B = 0$ → at least one NMOS is OFF, **no discharge path** → $X$ remains at $V_{DD}$, $Y = 0$ (NAND output).
  - $A = 1, B = 1$ → both NMOS are ON, **conducting path from $X$ to GND** forms, $X$ discharges to $0$, $Y = 1$ (NAND output).

The **static inverter** serves three critical purposes:
- **Logic restoration:** converts the dynamic, noise-sensitive $X$ to a full-rail $Y$.
- **Non-inverting output** for cascade-ability into the next domino stage.
- **Drive strength** to charge the next stage's clocked PMOS and to drive long wires.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

Re-deriving the charge-sharing result explicitly (this is a KTU *favourite* for 7 marks):

**Setup:** Output node $X$ has capacitance $C_L$ to ground and is precharged to $V_{DD}$. An *internal* node $Y$ inside the PDN has parasitic capacitance $C_{int}$ to ground and starts at $0$ V (it was discharged during the *previous* evaluate cycle).

**Step 1 — Identify the triggering input combination.** For a 2-input NAND, $A = B = 1$ is the only combination that turns ON both $M_2$ and $M_3$.

**Step 2 — Conservation of charge** between $C_L$ and $C_{int}$ once $M_2$ turns ON:

$$
Q_{initial} = C_L \, V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = C_L \, V_{DD}
$$

**Step 3 — After charge sharing, both nodes reach common voltage $V_f$:**

$$
Q_{final} = (C_L + C_{int}) \, V_f
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve:**

$$
C_L \, V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) \, V_f
$$

$$
V_f = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}
$$

**Step 5 — Error magnitude:**

$$
\Delta V_{err} = V_{DD} - V_f = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}
$$

**Critical design issue:** As technology scales, *internal node capacitances* $C_{int}$ can become comparable to $C_L$ (because wires do not scale as well as transistors). When $C_{int} \ge C_L$, the output falls by **at least $V_{DD}/2$**, which can drop *below* the threshold voltage $V_{th}$ of the next static inverter, causing a **functional failure**. KTU examiners expect you to also mention the **precharge-internal-node** technique and the **keeper transistor** as remedies.

---

### 📝 Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **domino logic** style with a suitable 2-input AND example. Why is it called "domino" and why is it limited to non-inverting functions?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **clock network power** for a chip with the following parameters and comment on its share of total power. $C_{clk} = 200$ pF, $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, $\alpha_{clk} = 1$, total chip dynamic power $P_{total} = 2$ W.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

A **domino logic gate** = (clocked dynamic gate) + (static CMOS inverter). To realize a 2-input **AND** ($Y = A \cdot B$), we exploit De Morgan's law: $A \cdot B = \overline{\overline{A \cdot B}}$. So we build a *dynamic 2-input NAND* followed by a *static inverter*:

1. **Dynamic NAND stage** — precharge PMOS + series NMOS $M_A$ and $M_B$.
2. **Static CMOS inverter** — restores full-rail, inverting output.

**Operation:**
- Precharge: dynamic output $X = V_{DD}$, inverter output $Y = 0$.
- Evaluate, $A = B = 1$: $X$ discharges to 0, inverter output $Y = V_{DD}$ → **AND output = 1**.
- Evaluate, otherwise: $X$ stays at $V_{DD}$, $Y = 0$ → **AND output = 0**.

**Why "domino"?** A chain of domino gates is: *Dynamic → Inverter → Dynamic → Inverter → ...* During evaluate, only **one transition per stage per cycle** can occur, and the transitions propagate like a *row of falling dominos* — each stage "falls" only after the previous one has finished. Because each gate produces a clean $0 \to 1$ transition at the *end* of evaluate, the cascade is glitch-free.

**Why only non-inverting?** A dynamic stage on its own produces a *non-inverting dynamic* output (because the precharge level is HIGH). When fed *directly* into another dynamic stage during the *same* evaluate phase, the next gate's PMOS is OFF and the new input HIGH would erroneously start an *evaluate* before its own precharge is complete. The intervening static inverter **converts the dynamic level to a static full-rail** and ensures the next dynamic gate sees a *valid* level only at the start of *its* precharge. Hence the cascade must alternate: dynamic → static → dynamic → static, which is intrinsically **non-inverting overall**.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$C_{clk} = 200$ pF, $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, $\alpha_{clk} = 1$, $P_{total} = 2$ W.

**Step 1 — Apply the clock-power formula:**

$$
P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
P_{clk} = 1 \cdot (200 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot (500 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
P_{clk} = 1 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{clk} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} = 50 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the share of total power:**

$$
\text{Share} = \dfrac{P_{clk}}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{50 \text{ mW}}{2000 \text{ mW}} = 0.025 = 2.5\%
$$

**Comment:** In this design, the clock network consumes **2.5% of total chip power**, which is *lower* than the 30–40% typical in high-performance processors. This indicates the design has already benefited from aggressive **clock gating** or operates at a low-to-moderate frequency. As $f_{clk}$ increases to multi-GHz regimes, the same $C_{clk}$ would consume proportionally more power — *clock power is fundamentally a linear function of frequency*. KTU examiners also accept answers that discuss how reducing $V_{DD}$ (e.g., to 0.7 V) would reduce $P_{clk}$ quadratically to ~24.5 mW.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic — and how to avoid it:**
> 1. **Confusing *dynamic logic* with *Domino logic*.** Dynamic logic = clocked PDN only; Domino = dynamic + static inverter. They are *not* synonyms. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the foot transistor** when drawing the circuit. The foot transistor is a KTU-board favourite: include it whenever drawing a deep-submicron dynamic gate.
> 3. **Writing $V_f = V_{DD}/2$** in the charge-sharing derivation. Always derive it as $V_f = V_{DD} \cdot C_L/(C_L + C_{int})$.
> 4. **Stating that clocked logic has "high power dissipation"** without qualifying. The *per-gate* switching power is low; the *clock-tree* power is high. Be precise.
> 5. **Missing the deactivation mechanism.** Many answers forget to mention that during precharge, the PDN is *isolated* (by the foot or by clock-gating), which is what prevents charge-sharing in *most* cycles.
> 6. **Omitting units and boundary conditions** in numerical problems. KTU evaluators explicitly check for correct units (W, J, Hz, F) in the final answer line.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Clocked Design Style: Basic Concept**

- ✅ **Clocked design style** uses a clock to control **precharge** ($\phi = 0$) and **evaluate** ($\phi = 1$) phases of logic gates.
- ✅ Replaces the static PMOS pull-up network with **one precharge PMOS** and implements logic in an **NMOS pull-down network**.
- ✅ Reduces transistor count from $2N$ (static) to $N + 2$ (dynamic) and **eliminates PMOS input capacitance**.
- ✅ Three canonical sub-styles: **Dynamic**, **Domino** (dynamic + inverter), **C²MOS** (clocked-CMOS latch/flip-flop variant).
- ✅ **Precharge phase:** output is unconditionally pulled to $V_{DD}$ via the PMOS; PDN is disabled.
- ✅ **Evaluate phase:** PMOS is OFF; PDN conditionally discharges the output based on inputs.
- ✅ **Charge-sharing error:** $V_f = V_{DD} \cdot C_L / (C_L + C_{int})$; mitigated by *precharge-internal-node* and *keeper* transistor.
- ✅ **Clock power:** $P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$, with $\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$ (clock toggles every cycle).
- ✅ **Clock networks** consume **30–40% of total chip power** in synchronous high-performance ICs.
- ✅ **Dynamic power per gate is lower** than static CMOS because of smaller node capacitance and no short-circuit current.
- ✅ **Domino logic** is non-inverting only; cascade pattern is **Dynamic → Static Inverter → Dynamic → ...**
- ✅ **Clocked foot transistor** prevents sub-threshold leakage and charge-sharing during precharge.
- ✅ **Maximum operating frequency:** $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{pre} + t_{eval} + t_{hold})$.
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** figure of merit, $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p$, used to compare logic families.
- ✅ **Total chip power:** $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{short} + P_{leak} + P_{clk}$ — always include all four terms in derivations.
- ✅ **Trade-off summary:** Clocked style = *smaller, faster, lower per-gate power* BUT *less robust, harder to cascade, and burdened by clock-tree power*.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** ALU datapaths, cache tag comparators, priority encoders, sense amplifiers in high-speed serial links.
- ✅ **Modern trend:** In advanced FinFET nodes (7 nm and below), clocked dynamic logic is used **only in the most critical paths**; most logic has migrated back to static CMOS with clock gating and power gating for low power.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Clocked Design Style: Basic Concept**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Outcome-Based Education.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Dynamic Logic** is a CMOS digital circuit design style in which the output state is determined dynamically during an **evaluate phase** through a conditional discharge of a precharged capacitance, rather than through a static pull-up/pull-down network as in static CMOS logic. The operation is governed by a single-phase or multi-phase **clock signal ($\phi$)** that alternates the circuit between a **Precharge phase** and an **Evaluate phase**.

In the **precharge phase** ($\phi = 0$), the output node capacitance is charged to the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ via a single PMOS transistor (the precharge device). In the **evaluate phase** ($\phi = 1$), the output is conditionally discharged to ground through a pull-down NMOS logic network based on the inputs. Because the output is valid only during the evaluate phase, dynamic logic is inherently **non-inverting** (with a single output stage) and uses far fewer transistors than fully complementary static CMOS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy: The Water-Tap Bucket System**  
> Imagine a bucket placed on a tap. A valve (the **clock-controlled PMOS**) is opened during the **precharge phase** and water ($V_{DD}$) fills the bucket. Then the valve closes. A sensor (the **NMOS pull-down network**) waits for an "input" condition. If the condition is TRUE, a drain plug is pulled and the bucket empties (output = 0). If the condition is FALSE, the bucket stays full (output = $V_{DD}$). Crucially, the bucket is **read only once** during the evaluate phase — and if no one checks it for a long time, water might slowly leak (subthreshold leakage) and give the wrong reading. The key engineering trick: **you only need ONE tap (PMOS) and ONE plug (NMOS network)** instead of two complete trees.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Key standard metrics in Dynamic Logic design (KTU high-yield):
> - **Precharge Time ($t_{pre}$)** — typically one half-clock period
> - **Evaluate Time ($t_{eva}$)** — typically one half-clock period  
> - **Clock Period ($T_{clk}$)** — Sum of $t_{pre} + t_{eva}$
> - **Output Capacitance ($C_L$)** — determined by fan-out and parasitic wiring
> - **Charge Sharing Voltage ($\Delta V$)** — given by $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_A}{C_A + C_L}$
> - **$V_{tn}$** = **NMOS threshold voltage (typically 0.4 V for modern nodes)**
> - **$V_{tp}$** = **PMOS threshold voltage (typically −0.4 V)**

## 1.4 Classification of Dynamic Logic Families

| Family | Clock Phases | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Dynamic CMOS (n-type)** | 1-phase ($\phi$) | Simple; cascading restricted |
| **Domino Logic** | 1-phase ($\phi$) | Adds static CMOS inverter at output → inverting logic, cascading allowed |
| **np-CMOS** | 2-phase ($\phi_1, \phi_2$) | Alternating n-block / p-block cascades |
| **Zipper CMOS** | 2-phase | Fully static-like with dynamic internal nodes |
| **Dual-Rail Domino** | 1-phase | Differential outputs for glitch-free precharge |

> [!NOTE]  
> The topic of **Dynamic Logic** in KTU Module 4 of Low Power VLSI specifically tests your ability to contrast dynamic logic with static CMOS in terms of **power, delay, area, and noise immunity**, since dynamic logic is a *direct low-power design technique*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step by Step

The dynamic logic gate operates in **two distinct, non-overlapping phases**, synchronized to a global clock $\phi$.

### Phase 1: PRECHARGE ($\phi = 0$)

1. The clock signal $\phi$ goes **LOW (0 V)**.
2. The **PMOS precharge transistor** ($M_p$) turns ON (because $V_{GS} = 0 - V_{DD} = -V_{DD} < V_{tp}$).
3. The **NMOS evaluate transistor** (the foot-switch, if present) turns OFF.
4. Current flows from $V_{DD}$ through $M_p$ to the **dynamic output node (Out)**.
5. The load capacitance $C_L$ at the output node charges to $V_{DD}$.
6. **Time taken:** determined by the RC time constant $\tau_{pre} = R_{p,on} \cdot C_L$.
7. **Resulting state:** `Out = 1` (HIGH), regardless of the logic inputs.

### Phase 2: EVALUATE ($\phi = 1$)

1. The clock signal $\phi$ goes **HIGH ($V_{DD}$)**.
2. The **PMOS precharge transistor** turns OFF.
3. The **NMOS evaluate transistor / pull-down network** becomes active.
4. Based on the **logic inputs (A, B, C, …)** applied to the gates of the NMOS pull-down network, one of two things happens:
   - **Inputs create a conducting path** → $C_L$ discharges through NMOS network → `Out = 0` (LOW).
   - **No conducting path exists** → $C_L$ holds its charge → `Out = 1` (HIGH).
5. **Time taken:** determined by the discharge RC, $\tau_{eva} = R_{n,on} \cdot C_L / k$, where $k$ depends on series NMOS count.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Dynamic Logic — Motivations

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why use dynamic logic in low-power VLSI?**

- **Reduced Capacitance:** Only one transition per cycle at internal nodes. Static CMOS has both PMOS and NMOS network capacitance, while dynamic has effectively half the load on the evaluate path.
- **Higher Speed:** Lower load capacitance $\Rightarrow$ smaller logical effort $\Rightarrow$ faster switching. Useful for critical paths in ALUs, adders, and high-speed datapaths.
- **Lower Switching Power:** $P = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$. Since $C_L$ is reduced, dynamic power drops proportionally.
- **Smaller Area:** For complex functions (e.g., wide OR/NOR), dynamic logic uses $\sim (N+1)$ transistors versus $2N$ for static CMOS.

## 2.3 The "How" — Cascading Rules

A critical KTU theory point: **Standard dynamic gates CANNOT be cascaded directly** because the precharged HIGH of stage 1 would prematurely discharge the output of stage 2 (whose NMOS network is already active during the precharge of stage 1's *next* cycle). This is solved by **Domino logic** (inverting output buffer) or by alternating **n-CMOS** and **p-CMOS** stages (**np-CMOS**).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU University Exam — Dynamic Logic**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Precharge Time Constant | $\tau_{pre} = R_{p,on} \cdot C_L$ | seconds | $\phi = 0$ |
| 2 | Evaluate Discharge Time | $t_{eva} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{dsat,n}}$ | seconds | $N$ series NMOS |
| 3 | Charge Sharing Drop | $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_A}{C_A + C_L}$ | volts | Precharge-phase redistribution |
| 4 | Energy per Cycle (Dynamic) | $E_{cyc} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | joules | Charging once per cycle |
| 5 | Static Leakage Power | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | watts | When output is HIGH (held) |
| 6 | Switching Power | $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | watts | At node transitions |
| 7 | Logical Effort (Dynamic Gate) | $g_{dyn} = \dfrac{C_{in,equiv}}{3 \cdot C_{inv}}$ | dimensionless | Compared to inverter |
| 8 | Subthreshold Leakage Current | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{V_{gs}-V_{th}}{S}}$ | amperes | $V_{gs} < V_{th}$ |
| 9 | Noise Margin (HIGH) | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | volts | Output low-fluctuation |
| 10 | Capacitive Coupling Glitch | $\Delta V_{out} = \dfrac{C_{c}}{C_{c} + C_L} \cdot \Delta V_{neighbor}$ | volts | Cross-talk induced |

> **Note on absolute values in the table:** To prevent markdown table syntax breakage, all "$\vert x \vert$" style absolute-value expressions in this sheet are written as $\vert x \vert$ using the LaTeX vertical bar.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic logic is a **workhorse in the critical-path design** of:

- **Microprocessor ALUs** (e.g., Intel Itanium, Pentium-4 low-power datapath blocks)
- **Wide fan-in OR/NOR gate trees** in address decoders
- **Read-only memory (ROM) decoders** in memory compilers
- **Tag comparators** in cache arrays
- **Sum-bit generation paths** in fast adders (e.g., carry-skip, carry-select) where a single-rail dynamic block feeds a domino gate

It directly contributes to **low-power VLSI design** because it reduces the switching capacitance and the short-circuit current component — both of which dominate the power budget in deep submicron CMOS.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charge Sharing Voltage Drop

**Problem Statement:** A dynamic logic gate has an output capacitance $C_L = 50\ \text{fF}$ initially charged to $V_{DD} = 1.2\ \text{V}$. The internal node A of the pull-down network has capacitance $C_A = 30\ \text{fF}$, initially at $0\ \text{V}$. During evaluation, an internal NMOS turns on connecting A to Out. Find the final voltage at the output.

**Step 1:** Identify the principle — **conservation of charge** at the floating output node.

**Step 2:** Initial charge stored on $C_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{initial} &= C_L \cdot V_{DD} \\
            &= 50\ \text{fF} \times 1.2\ \text{V} \\
            &= 60\ \text{fC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Initial charge on $C_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_A &= C_A \cdot 0 = 0\ \text{fC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** After redistribution, both capacitors are at the same final voltage $V_f$ (since they are now in parallel through the conducting NMOS).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{final} &= (C_L + C_A) \cdot V_f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply charge conservation: $Q_{initial} = Q_{final}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L \cdot V_{DD} &= (C_L + C_A) \cdot V_f \\
50 \cdot 1.2 &= (50 + 30) \cdot V_f \\
60 &= 80 \cdot V_f \\
V_f &= \dfrac{60}{80} = 0.75\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Express in general formula form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the voltage drop (loss in output HIGH level):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_{DD} - V_f = V_{DD}\left(1 - \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_A}\right) = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_A}{C_L + C_A} \\
\Delta V &= 1.2 \times \dfrac{30}{80} = 0.45\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The output drops from 1.2 V to 0.75 V. If $V_{IH}$ of the next stage is, say, 0.7 V, the gate fails. **A keeper transistor must be added** to prevent this.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Consumption per Clock Cycle

**Problem Statement:** A dynamic logic block has an effective output capacitance of $C_L = 100\ \text{fF}$, supply $V_{DD} = 1.0\ \text{V}$, and operates at $f_{clk} = 500\ \text{MHz}$. Compute the per-cycle energy and the average dynamic power (assuming a switching activity factor $\alpha = 0.3$).

**Step 1:** Energy stored in charging $C_L$ once to $V_{DD}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cyc} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cyc} &= 0.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.0)^2 \\
        &= 50\ \text{fJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The energy is dissipated (as heat) only when the capacitor discharges through the NMOS network. The charging energy from $V_{DD}$ is fully dissipated, so use full $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ for cycle-average analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \\
        &= 0.3 \times 100\times10^{-15} \times 1.0^2 \times 500\times10^{6} \\
        &= 15\ \mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: The factor $\alpha = 0.3$ is used because not every clock cycle the node switches. In a **synchronous precharge**, the node toggles **every cycle** (charge + discharge), so $\alpha = 1.0$ for the worst-case bound.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Logical Effort of an n-input Dynamic NAND

**Problem Statement:** Show that a 4-input dynamic NAND has a logical effort $g = \dfrac{4}{3}$ per input (normalized to a unit inverter).

**Step 1:** Recall definition — logical effort $g$ = input capacitance of the gate / (3 × input capacitance of minimum inverter), where 3 is the standard PMOS/NMOS ratio factor.

**Step 2:** For an n-input static CMOS NAND, each input NMOS sees the unit input capacitance $C_{inv} = 1$. The PMOS transistors (in parallel) have widths multiplied by 2 (per input) to maintain drive balance.

**Step 3:** For the **dynamic** version, the PMOS precharge is a single transistor of size 2 (carrying 2 units of capacitance). The NMOS pull-down network has $n$ series transistors, each of size 2 (to fight the wider PMOS). Therefore **each input** sees a gate capacitance of 2 units.

**Step 4:** Logical effort per input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g &= \dfrac{C_{in,per\ input}}{3 \cdot C_{in,min\ inv}} = \dfrac{2}{3 \times 1} = \dfrac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** However, when considering **branching effort** of the parallel PMOS load vs. series NMOS, the **effective** logical effort (used for path delay computation) becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_{path} &= \dfrac{n}{3} = \dfrac{4}{3}\ \ \ \text{(for } n=4\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Practical insight: a 4-input static NAND has $g = 4/3$ per input too — dynamic logic does not reduce logical effort, but it **halves the parasitic drain capacitance** at the internal evaluate node, leading to faster switching.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Their Mitigation

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Loves This Table** — Memorize for the 14-marker.

| Failure Mode | Cause | Mitigation Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Charge Sharing** | Internal node capacitance $C_A$ shares charge with $C_L$ | Add a **precharge PMOS** to internal nodes, or add a **keeper** |
| **Subthreshold Leakage** | Output stays HIGH for many cycles; $I_{leak}$ slowly discharges $C_L$ | **Keeper (bleeder) PMOS** with weak drive, or use **Footer NMOS** |
| **Capacitive Coupling** | Adjacent wire $\Delta V$ couples into Out | Increase spacing, use **shielding wires**, or jitter clock |
| **Clock Feedthrough** | Clock transition couples through $C_{gd}$ of precharge device | Add a **dummy switch** or use **transmission gate** |
| **Race Condition (Cascading)** | Precharge of stage 2 causes pre-discharge of stage 1 | Use **Domino** (inverting buffer) or **np-CMOS** (alternating clocks) |
| **Source-Body Effect in Series Stack** | Lower NMOS in stack sees raised $V_S$ → slower | Use **single-footed** structures, or high-$V_{th}$ foot device |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`  
> **Define dynamic CMOS logic. List any two advantages it has over static CMOS logic.**

**Model Answer:**  
Dynamic CMOS logic is a circuit design style in which the output is computed by **precharging** an output node capacitance to $V_{DD}$ during the LOW phase of the clock $\phi$, and then **conditionally discharging** it through an NMOS pull-down network during the HIGH phase of $\phi$.  
**Advantages over static CMOS:**

1. **Lower input capacitance / reduced load:** Only the NMOS network drives the output, so $C_L$ is roughly halved compared to static CMOS, leading to lower switching power.
2. **Higher speed:** Lower load $\Rightarrow$ smaller logical effort $\Rightarrow$ reduced propagation delay, especially in wide fan-in gates.
3. *(Optional third)* **Smaller area:** For $N$-input functions, dynamic uses $\sim N+1$ transistors vs. $2N$ in static CMOS.

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`  
> **What is charge sharing in a dynamic logic gate? How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer:**  
**Charge sharing** is the phenomenon where an internal node capacitance $C_A$ (at $0\ \text{V}$ initially) inside the pull-down network redistributes its charge with the precharged output node capacitance $C_L$ (at $V_{DD}$), causing the output voltage to drop below the required $V_{IH}$ of the next stage. The voltage drop is given by:

$$
\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_A}{C_A + C_L}
$$

**Mitigation:**  
(i) Add a **keeper (bleeder) PMOS** driven by the buffered output, sized weakly ($\sim \dfrac{1}{4}$ width) to supply just enough current to compensate leakage and restore lost charge.  
(ii) Precharge **internal nodes** as well by adding extra PMOS precharge transistors gated by $\phi$.  
(iii) Reduce internal node capacitance by careful layout.

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**  
> - **Mark-loss Pitfall 1:** Drawing the wrong transistor type (NMOS instead of PMOS) for the precharge device. *Remember:* Precharge device is **PMOS** so it conducts when $\phi = 0$.  
> - **Mark-loss Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to mention the **keeper transistor** in dynamic gates. Without it, a static `HIGH` output will leak away — examiners **deduct 2–3 marks** for omitting the keeper.  
> - **Mark-loss Pitfall 3:** Confusing **domino** (inverting) with **standard dynamic** (non-inverting). Domino *always* gives inverting output because of the trailing static CMOS inverter.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A.** (a) Draw the circuit diagram of a 4-input dynamic NAND gate. Explain the precharge and evaluate phases with the help of a timing diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the charge sharing problem in dynamic logic. For a dynamic gate with $C_L = 40\ \text{fF}$ and $C_A = 20\ \text{fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.0\ \text{V}$, calculate the final output voltage and the voltage drop. Comment on the effect on noise margin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit and Timing:**

The 4-input dynamic NAND consists of:

- One **PMOS precharge transistor** $M_p$ with its gate connected to clock $\phi$ and source at $V_{DD}$, drain at the dynamic output node `Out`.
- One **NMOS evaluate transistor** (the foot switch) with its gate connected to $\phi$, in series with the logic network.
- Four **NMOS transistors** $N_1, N_2, N_3, N_4$ in **series**, each gated by inputs A, B, C, D respectively, forming the AND-style pull-down network.
- One **output load capacitance** $C_L$ at the Out node.
- A **keeper (bleeder) PMOS** $M_k$ (weak, width $\sim 1/4$ of $M_p$) gated by $\overline{Out}$ (via a small inverter) to prevent leakage-induced discharge.

**Timing Diagram Description:**

| Time Interval | $\phi$ | $M_p$ (Precharge) | $M_4$ (Evaluate) | $C_L$ State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0 \rightarrow T/2$ | 0 (LOW) | ON | OFF | Charging to $V_{DD}$ |
| $T/2 \rightarrow T$ | 1 (HIGH) | OFF | ON | Holds or discharges |

- **Precharge (0 to T/2):** Out charges to $V_{DD} = 1.0\ \text{V}$.
- **Evaluate (T/2 to T):** If A·B·C·D = 1, the NMOS stack conducts and $C_L$ discharges to 0 V, so `Out = 0`. Otherwise, `Out` stays at $V_{DD}$.

**(b) Charge Sharing Calculation:**

Given: $C_L = 40\ \text{fF}$, $C_A = 20\ \text{fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.0\ \text{V}$.

Initial charge on $C_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{initial} &= C_L \cdot V_{DD} = 40 \times 1.0 = 40\ \text{fC}
\end{aligned}
$$

Charge conservation after redistribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(C_L + C_A) \cdot V_f &= C_L \cdot V_{DD} \\
(40 + 20) \cdot V_f &= 40 \times 1.0 \\
60 \cdot V_f &= 40 \\
V_f &= \dfrac{40}{60} = 0.667\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Voltage drop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_{DD} - V_f = 1.0 - 0.667 = 0.333\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Noise Margin:** The output HIGH has dropped from 1.0 V to 0.667 V. If $V_{IH}$ of the next stage is 0.7 V (typical for low-$V_{DD}$ processes), the gate will be misinterpreted as logic 0. **Noise margin (HIGH) collapses to 0 V or becomes negative**, leading to functional failure. **A keeper transistor is mandatory** to prevent this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B.** (a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **domino logic** gate. How does it overcome the cascading problem of standard dynamic logic? **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the various sources of power consumption in a dynamic logic gate. Compare static and dynamic CMOS logic in terms of area, speed, and power for a 4-input NAND function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Domino Logic Operation:**

A **domino logic gate** is a dynamic logic gate followed by a **static CMOS inverter** (and optionally a weak keeper). The dynamic stage produces an output `Out_dyn` which goes **monotonically** (only one transition per cycle: from 1→0, never 0→1 during evaluation). The static inverter then produces `Out` which is the **complement** of `Out_dyn`.

**Circuit elements:**

- Precharge PMOS $M_p$, gated by $\phi$.
- Evaluate foot NMOS gated by $\phi$.
- NMOS pull-down network implementing the desired (non-inverting) logic.
- Static CMOS inverter $M_{p2}$–$M_{n2}$ at the output, sized for fan-out.
- Keeper PMOS $M_k$, gated by `Out` (the inverted output), to maintain `Out_dyn = 1` against leakage.

**How it overcomes cascading:** In standard dynamic logic, a HIGH output of stage 1 will pre-discharge stage 2 during stage 1's precharge. In domino, the **static inverter** ensures the next stage's inputs are LOW during precharge (because the inverter output is HIGH only after evaluation). Since the next dynamic stage's pull-down network sees 0s on its inputs during precharge, it will not falsely discharge. Hence, **domino gates can be cascaded freely**, with the output of one stage driving the pull-down network of the next.

**(b) Power Consumption Sources in Dynamic Logic:**

1. **Switching (Dynamic) Power** — $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$. This is the dominant component.
2. **Short-Circuit Power** — During the brief time when both precharge PMOS and pull-down NMOS are partially ON, a direct current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND.
3. **Leakage Power** — Subthreshold and gate leakage cause $C_L$ to slowly discharge, opposing the keeper. $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$.
4. **Clock Distribution Power** — The clock network itself consumes power in driving all the $\phi$ gates.
5. **Charge Sharing Loss** — Wasted energy when internal node capacitance $C_A$ is charged to $V_{DD}$ and then re-shared with $C_L$.

**Comparison Table — 4-Input NAND:**

| Parameter | Static CMOS NAND4 | Dynamic NAND4 (Domino) |
|---|---|---|
| **Transistor Count** | 8 (4 PMOS + 4 NMOS) | ~7 (1 precharge + 1 footer + 4 NMOS + 1 inv + 1 keeper) |
| **Input Capacitance** | Higher (parallel PMOS) | Lower (NMOS only) |
| **Load Capacitance at Out** | Larger | Smaller |
| **Delay** | Higher (logical effort $g = 4/3$ per input) | Lower (no PMOS stack load) |
| **Dynamic Power** | Higher | **Lower** (by 30–50% typical) |
| **Static Power** | Near zero | Non-zero (leakage through keeper) |
| **Area** | Larger | **Smaller** for wide fan-in |
| **Cascadability** | Yes (direct) | Yes (only via Domino) |
| **Noise Robustness** | High | **Lower** (charge sharing, leakage) |
| **Function Realizable** | NAND (inverting) | NAND of inputs (single stage + inverter = NAND) |

**Conclusion:** For low-power VLSI design, **dynamic domino logic is preferred** in critical paths and wide-fan-in structures of the datapath, provided the noise/leakage trade-offs are addressed with keeper transistors and precharged internal nodes.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for Dynamic Logic — KTU Module 4**

- ✅ Dynamic logic has **2 phases**: **Precharge** ($\phi = 0$, PMOS charges Out to $V_{DD}$) and **Evaluate** ($\phi = 1$, NMOS network conditionally discharges).
- ✅ **Precharge device is PMOS**, evaluate foot device is **NMOS**, both clocked by $\phi$.
- ✅ Standard dynamic gate is **non-inverting** (e.g., implements `AND` as `A·B·C·D` at the Out node).
- ✅ **Domino logic** = dynamic stage + static CMOS inverter $\Rightarrow$ **inverting** + cascadable.
- ✅ **np-CMOS** = alternating n-block (during $\phi_1$) and p-block (during $\phi_2$) for cascadability without inverters.
- ✅ **Charge sharing drop** formula: $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_A}{C_A + C_L}$.
- ✅ **Keeper (bleeder) PMOS** is mandatory to prevent leakage-induced discharge of HIGH outputs.
- ✅ Standard dynamic **cannot cascade directly** — only Domino or np-CMOS.
- ✅ **Power advantage:** Lower switching power due to halved $C_L$ at the dynamic node.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Charge sharing, leakage, capacitive coupling, clock feedthrough, single-rail (no complementary output).
- ✅ **Use cases:** Wide fan-in OR/NOR, address decoders, high-speed ALUs, cache tag comparators.
- ✅ **Energy per cycle:** $E_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ for the charging cycle, full $C_L V_{DD}^2$ for the discharge cycle.
- ✅ **Logical effort per input** for an n-input dynamic NAND: $\dfrac{n}{3}$ (path-effort basis).
- ✅ Clock must be **properly buffered** — clock skew can cause race-through in dynamic logic.
- ✅ **Don't forget** the **foot NMOS** in dynamic gates — it prevents evaluation during precharge.
- ✅ Examiners love asking: *"Why does standard dynamic logic fail in cascade?"* — answer: precharge of stage 2 wrongly activates during evaluation of stage 1 due to the lack of an inverting buffer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Current Switch Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Differential Current Switch Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)** is a family of low-power, high-speed differential CMOS logic styles in which a **constant bias current** is *steered* between two output branches by a differential NMOS input pair. The direction of current flow encodes the logic value, yielding **low-swing, fully differential outputs** with intrinsically reduced dynamic power dissipation.

Closely related and often discussed in the same module is **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVSL / DCVS)**, which is a *voltage-mode* differential logic style. It uses a **cross-coupled PMOS load** sitting atop an **NMOS pull-down tree** that simultaneously evaluates a Boolean function $f$ and its complement $\overline{f}$. Because the load is regenerative, DCVSL provides **full rail-to-rail swing**, **static complementary outputs**, and (ideally) **zero static power dissipation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Both DCSL (current-steering) and DCVSL (voltage-regenerative) belong to the **Adiabatic / Differential / Pass-Transistor / Dynamic logic** cluster in Module 4. The examiner frequently tests the **comparison of static power, noise margin, and delay** between DCVSL and conventional CMOS.

| Style | Output Swing | Static Power | Logic Implementation |
|-------|--------------|--------------|----------------------|
| Static CMOS | Full rail-to-rail | ≈ 0 | PMOS + NMOS trees |
| DCVSL | Full rail-to-rail | ≈ 0 (regenerative) | NMOS tree only |
| DCSL | Low-swing (ΔV small) | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$ (constant) | NMOS differential pair |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Way Faucet

Imagine a washbasin with **two drains** sharing a single water supply (the bias current $I_{bias}$):

- **You** (the differential input pair) decide which drain is open by turning the knob.
- Water can flow through **only one drain at a time** — never both simultaneously.
- A **float valve** (the cross-coupled load in DCVSL / the current source in DCSL) ensures that whichever drain is "off" stays firmly closed.
- The *small height difference* of water in the two basins (low swing in DCSL) or *full basin vs empty basin* (rail-to-rail in DCVSL) is what the next stage "reads".

This perfectly captures the **steering** nature of differential current-switch logic: the *current itself is constant*; only its **path** is modulated.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Standard KTU Values)

The following standard CMOS-90 nm / 65 nm metrics are typically assumed:

- **Supply voltage:** $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V} \text{ to } 1.2 \text{ V}$
- **Threshold voltage:** $V_{TN} \approx 0.35 \text{ V}, \quad V_{TP} \approx -0.35 \text{ V}$
- **Bias current (DCSL):** $I_{bias} = 10 \text{ to } 100 \text{ μA}$
- **Output voltage swing (DCSL):** $\Delta V \approx 200 \text{ to } 400 \text{ mV}$
- **Propagation delay target:** $t_{p} \leq 100 \text{ ps}$ per gate

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — "Differential"**: A logic style is called *differential* if every logical signal is represented by **two wires carrying complementary information**. The information is encoded in the **difference** of the two voltages, not their absolute value. This property confers excellent **common-mode noise rejection**, which is invaluable in mixed-signal and low-voltage SoCs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DCVSL — Circuit Topology & Operating Principle

A DCVSL gate consists of **three functional blocks**:

1. **NMOS Pull-Down Network A** — implements the function $f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ and pulls node **Out** to ground when $f = 1$.
2. **NMOS Pull-Down Network B** — implements $\overline{f}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ and pulls node **Out_b** to ground when $\overline{f} = 1$.
3. **Cross-Coupled PMOS Load** — two PMOS transistors $M_{P1}$ and $M_{P2}$ whose gates are cross-connected to the opposite drains. This forms a **positive-feedback latch** that:
   - Latches the result once evaluated.
   - Provides **full-swing restoration** ($0$ to $V_{DD}$).
   - Eliminates the **static short-circuit path** through the pull-down network.

### Step-by-Step Operation

**Step 1 — Quiescent State (no inputs switching):**  
Both PMOS transistors are diode-connected by the cross coupling. The gate–drain connection forces one PMOS fully ON ($V_{SG} = V_{DD}$) and the other nearly OFF ($V_{SG} \approx 0$). Whichever output was last LOW is now firmly pulled HIGH by its PMOS.

**Step 2 — Input Evaluation:**  
The differential inputs $x_i, \overline{x_i}$ drive both NMOS trees simultaneously. Exactly **one** of the two trees conducts a path from its output node to ground (because $f$ and $\overline{f}$ cannot both be 1 for any valid input combination).

**Step 3 — Output Transition:**  
The conducting tree discharges its output node toward **0 V**. The cross-coupled PMOS senses this drop and **turns ON** stronger, yanking the opposite output node up to **$V_{DD}$**.

**Step 4 — Regenerative Latch-up:**  
The HIGH output reinforces the gate of the PMOS connected to the LOW output, locking the state until the next input transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DCVSL has (theoretically) ZERO static power:**  
> In any stable state, *one* NMOS tree is OFF and the corresponding PMOS is ON. No DC path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND. The only power dissipated is **dynamic** — when the output capacitances are charged/discharged.

## 2.2 DCSL — Current-Steering Principle

DCSL replaces the cross-coupled PMOS load with a **tail current source** $I_{bias}$ (typically a saturated NMOS) feeding a **differential pair** $M_1, M_2$. The two branches carry currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ such that:

$$
I_1 + I_2 = I_{bias} \quad \text{(Kirchhoff's Current Law)}
$$

When the differential input $V_{in}^{+} - V_{in}^{-}$ is large and positive, $M_1$ conducts almost all of $I_{bias}$ and $M_2$ is pinched off. The load resistors $R_L$ (often implemented as diode-connected PMOS) develop a small voltage difference:

$$
\Delta V_{out} = (R_{L1} \cdot I_1) - (R_{L2} \cdot I_2) \approx I_{bias} \cdot R_L
$$

The output **swing** $\Delta V$ is intentionally kept small (typically $200 \text{ mV} \text{ to } 400 \text{ mV}$) to minimize dynamic power:

$$
P_{dyn,\,DCSL} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot \Delta V \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

Note the **$\Delta V \cdot V_{DD}$** term instead of the usual $V_{DD}^2$ in static CMOS — a **2–3× reduction** for $\Delta V / V_{DD} \approx 0.3$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Differential Current-Switch Logic is Preferred |
|--------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| **High-speed adders / multipliers** | Reduced swing → smaller $t_p \cdot P$ product |
| **Sense amplifiers in SRAM** | Naturally differential, low noise margin |
| **Mixed-signal ICs** | Common-mode noise rejection from differential signalling |
| **Low-voltage SoCs** | Operates at $V_{DD} < V_{TN} + V_{TP}$ reliably |
| **Clock distribution networks** | Low-swing clock reduces global clock power |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — At a Glance

The table below is the **canonical reference** for numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Typical Range |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | DCVSL Static Power | $P_{stat} = 0$ (ideal) | W |
| 2 | DCVSL Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | W |
| 3 | DCSL Constant Power | $P_{DCSL} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$ | W |
| 4 | DCSL Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn,DCSL} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot \Delta V \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f_{clk}$ | W |
| 5 | DCSL Output Swing | $\Delta V = I_{bias} \cdot R_L$ | V (200–400 mV) |
| 6 | DCSL Propagation Delay | $t_p \approx C_L \cdot \Delta V \,/\, I_{bias}$ | s |
| 7 | DCVSL Discharge Delay (HL) | $t_{pHL} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{K_N (V_{GS}-V_{TN})^{\beta}}$ | s |
| 8 | DCVSL Charge Delay (LH) | $t_{pLH} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{K_P (V_{DD}-V_{TP})^{\beta}}$ | s |
| 9 | Power-Delay Product (DCSL) | $PDP = P_{DCSL} \cdot t_p = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \Delta V$ | J |
| 10 | Noise Margin (DCVSL) | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \quad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | V |
| 11 | Static CMOS Dynamic Power | $P_{CMOS} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | W (baseline) |
| 12 | Power Savings Ratio | $\dfrac{P_{DCSL}}{P_{CMOS}} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{DD}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip:** Note that **Equation 12** elegantly proves the energy-saving claim of DCSL. For $\Delta V / V_{DD} = 0.3$, dynamic power is reduced by **70 %** at the same switching activity and clock frequency.

## 2.5 Comparison: DCVSL vs DCSL vs Static CMOS

| Parameter | Static CMOS | DCVSL | DCSL |
|-----------|-------------|-------|------|
| Output Swing | Full ($0$ to $V_{DD}$) | Full ($0$ to $V_{DD}$) | Low ($\Delta V$ small) |
| Static Power | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 (regenerative) | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$ |
| Number of Transistors | $2N$ | $N + 4$ (PMOS pair + 2 trees) | Differential pair + bias |
| Complementary Outputs | No (needs inverter) | Yes (free) | Yes (free) |
| Noise Margin | High | Moderate | Low (low swing) |
| Speed | Medium | Fast | Very fast |
| Routing Density | Low (single-ended) | Higher (differential wires) | Higher (differential wires) |
| Best Use | General logic | Custom datapath cells | Sense amps, clocks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — DCVSL Pull-Down Delay $t_{pHL}$

Consider the NMOS pull-down tree of a DCVSL gate discharging a load capacitance $C_L$ from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ (the 50 % trip point).

### Step 1 — Equivalent ON-current of the NMOS tree

For an NMOS transistor in saturation during the initial part of discharge, the drain current is:

$$
I_{DN} = K_N \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TN})^{2}
$$

Since $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ (input at rail), this becomes:

$$
I_{DN} = K_N \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Average discharge current over the interval $[V_{DD}, V_{DD}/2]$

Assuming a saturated-region approximation throughout, we take the current at the midpoint $V_{out} = 3 V_{DD}/4$, which gives $V_{DS} = 3 V_{DD}/4 > V_{GS} - V_{TN}$, so the saturation model holds:

$$
\overline{I_{DN}} \approx K_N \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Charge-balance for 50 % discharge

The charge removed from $C_L$ during a HIGH-to-LOW transition is:

$$
\Delta Q = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

Dividing by the average current gives the propagation delay:

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\overline{I_{DN}}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot K_N \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Closed-form expression (KTU-ready)

$$
\boxed{\,t_{pHL} \;\approx\; \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot K_N \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^{2}}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:**  
- The delay is **inversely proportional** to the NMOS aspect ratio (since $K_N \propto W/L$). Doubling $W$ halves the delay.  
- It scales **linearly** with $V_{DD}$ in the numerator, but **quadratically** with $(V_{DD} - V_{TN})$ in the denominator — so delay **decreases** with increasing $V_{DD}$, but with diminishing returns.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — DCVSL Pull-Up Delay $t_{pLH}$ (Cross-Coupled PMOS)

By symmetry, replacing $K_N, V_{TN}$ with $K_P, V_{TP}$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{pLH} \;\approx\; \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot K_P \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}}\,}
$$

> **Note:** The use of $\vert V_{TP} \vert$ (instead of $V_{TP}$) avoids a negative sign inside the squared term — strictly required for dimensional correctness.

### Average propagation delay of DCVSL

$$
t_{p,DCVSL} = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}
$$

$$
t_{p,DCVSL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{4} \left[ \frac{1}{K_N (V_{DD}-V_{TN})^2} + \frac{1}{K_P (V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP} \vert)^2} \right]
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — DCSL Propagation Delay and Power-Delay Product

### Step 1 — Output voltage swing

The differential pair fully steers $I_{bias}$ into one branch. With load resistor $R_L$:

$$
\Delta V_{out} = I_{bias} \cdot R_L
$$

### Step 2 — Delay to charge the load to $\Delta V / 2$

Charge supplied:

$$
\Delta Q = C_L \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{C_L \cdot I_{bias} \cdot R_L}{2}
$$

Average current (assumed constant at $I_{bias}$ during the switching event):

$$
t_p = \frac{\Delta Q}{I_{bias}} = \frac{C_L \cdot R_L}{2}
$$

Substituting $R_L = \Delta V / I_{bias}$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{p,DCSL} \;\approx\; \dfrac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{2 \cdot I_{bias}}\,}
$$

### Step 3 — Static (always-on) power

The tail current source conducts continuously:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{stat,DCSL} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Power-Delay Product (PDP) — the KTU "Figure of Merit"

$$
PDP = P_{stat,DCSL} \cdot t_p = V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias} \cdot \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{2 \cdot I_{bias}}
$$

The $I_{bias}$ terms cancel beautifully:

$$
\boxed{\,PDP_{DCSL} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot \Delta V \cdot V_{DD}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Comparative PDP with static CMOS

For static CMOS switching the same $C_L$ between 0 and $V_{DD}$:

$$
PDP_{CMOS} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}
$$

The **saving ratio** is therefore:

$$
\frac{PDP_{DCSL}}{PDP_{CMOS}} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{DD}}
$$

For $\Delta V = 300 \text{ mV}$ and $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, this ratio is **0.25** — a **75 % reduction** in energy per transition.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Noise Margin of DCVSL

The voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) of a DCVSL gate is steeper than that of static CMOS because of the **positive feedback** of the cross-coupled load. A small-signal analysis around the switching threshold yields:

$$
A_v = g_{mP} \cdot (r_{oP} \parallel r_{oN}) > 1
$$

This gain greater than unity guarantees **regenerative switching**. The noise margins are approximately:

$$
V_{IL} \approx \frac{3 V_{DD} - 2 V_{TP} - 2 V_{TN}}{8}
$$

$$
V_{IH} \approx \frac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_{TP} + 2 V_{TN}}{8}
$$

These yield:

$$
NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{8}
$$

$$
NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{8}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "≈ $V_{DD}/8$" ?** Because of the symmetry introduced by the cross-coupled pair, both high and low noise margins are equal in magnitude. This is a **unique property** of DCVSL — most logic families have asymmetric noise margins.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DCVSL — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below traces the signal flow inside a DCVSL gate from inputs to differential outputs. Because Mermaid cannot render transistor-level schematics, this block-level view is the canonical way KTU examiners expect students to represent DCVSL in written exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["Differential Inputs"]
        nodeInA["Input x1"]
        nodeInB["Input x2"]
        nodeInC["Input xn"]
    end

    subgraph NTREE["NMOS Pull-Down Trees"]
        nodeTreeA["Tree A: implements f"]
        nodeTreeB["Tree B: implements f-bar"]
    end

    subgraph PCPLOAD["Cross-Coupled PMOS Load"]
        nodePM1["PMOS M_P1"]
        nodePM2["PMOS M_P2"]
        nodeCross["Positive feedback latch"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["Differential Outputs"]
        nodeOutF["Out (true)"]
        nodeOutFbar["Out_b (complement)"]
    end

    nodeInA --> nodeTreeA
    nodeInB --> nodeTreeA
    nodeInC --> nodeTreeA
    nodeInA --> nodeTreeB
    nodeInB --> nodeTreeB
    nodeInC --> nodeTreeB

    nodeTreeA --> nodeOutF
    nodeTreeB --> nodeOutFbar

    nodeOutF -. gates .-> nodePM2
    nodeOutFbar -. gates .-> nodePM1

    nodePM1 --> nodeOutF
    nodePM2 --> nodeOutFbar
    nodePM1 --- nodePM2
    nodePM1 --- nodeCross
    nodePM2 --- nodeCross

    classDef pf fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nt fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class nodePM1,nodePM2,nodeCross pf
    class nodeTreeA,nodeTreeB nt
    class nodeOutF,nodeOutFbar out
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The blue block is the **PMOS cross-coupled load** (regenerative feedback).
- The orange blocks are the **two complementary NMOS trees** evaluating $f$ and $\overline{f}$.
- The dashed arrows from outputs back to PMOS gates represent the **cross-coupling** that creates positive feedback.
- Solid arrows from inputs to both trees indicate that **every input** feeds **both** networks in complementary form.

---

## 4.2 DCSL — Current-Steering Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DCSL["DCSL Differential Stage"]
        nodeBias["Tail Current Source I_bias"]
        nodeDiff["Differential NMOS Pair M1, M2"]
        nodeLoadL["Load Resistor R_L (left)"]
        nodeLoadR["Load Resistor R_L (right)"]
    end

    subgraph VIN["Differential Input Pair"]
        nodeVp["Input V_in positive"]
        nodeVn["Input V_in negative"]
    end

    subgraph VOUT["Low-Swing Differential Outputs"]
        nodeOp["Out positive (delta V)"]
        nodeOn["Out negative (delta V)"]
    end

    nodeBias --> nodeDiff
    nodeVp --> nodeDiff
    nodeVn --> nodeDiff

    nodeDiff --> nodeLoadL
    nodeDiff --> nodeLoadR
    nodeLoadL --> nodeOp
    nodeLoadR --> nodeOn

    nodeOp -. feedback .-> nodeLoadR
    nodeOn -. feedback .-> nodeLoadL

    classDef bias fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dif fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ld fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class nodeBias bias
    class nodeDiff dif
    class nodeLoadL,nodeLoadR ld
    class nodeOp,nodeOn out
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **purple block** is the tail current source — it enforces $I_1 + I_2 = I_{bias}$ regardless of inputs.
- The **yellow block** is the steering pair — its transconductance $g_m$ decides the slope of the VTC.
- The **green blocks** are the load resistors that convert current into a small voltage swing.
- The **feedback dashed arrows** indicate active-load or source-follower stages commonly added for gain.

---

## 4.3 Operation State Machine — DCVSL Behaviour

The figure below abstracts the **two steady states** of a DCVSL gate. Although the gate is fundamentally combinational, the cross-coupled load introduces a **memory-like** latching behaviour that KTU examiners love to test.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Eval: Inputs change

    Eval --> StateF: Tree A conducts f equals 1
    Eval --> StateFbar: Tree B conducts f-bar equals 1

    StateF: Out equals V_DD, Out_b equals 0 V
    StateFbar: Out equals 0 V, Out_b equals V_DD

    StateF --> Eval: New input transition
    StateFbar --> Eval: New input transition

    note right of StateF
        PMOS M_P1 OFF, M_P2 ON
        Tree A ON, Tree B OFF
        P_stat equals 0
    end note

    note left of StateFbar
        PMOS M_P1 ON, M_P2 OFF
        Tree A OFF, Tree B ON
        P_stat equals 0
    end note
```

**KTU Examination Tip:** Whenever the question says *"explain the regenerative action"*, draw the cross-coupled pair explicitly and trace the feedback loop. Marks are awarded for showing that **drain of one PMOS drives the gate of the other**.

---

## 4.4 Equivalent RC Model for Delay Analysis

The Mermaid diagram below captures the **small-signal equivalent circuit** used in the delay derivations of Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVdd["V_DD rail"]
    nodeCout["Load Capacitance C_L"]
    nodeRout["Effective ON-resistance R_eq"]
    nodeGnd["Ground rail"]

    nodeVdd --> nodeCout
    nodeCout --> nodeRout
    nodeRout --> nodeGnd

    nodeCout -. measured at .-> nodeVout["Output node V_out"]
    nodeVout -. drives .-> nodeNext["Next stage input"]

    classDef cap fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class nodeCout cap
    class nodeRout res
    class nodeVout,nodeNext sig
```

**Reading the diagram:** The RC time constant $\tau = R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ directly determines the propagation delay. For DCSL, $R_{eq} \approx \Delta V / I_{bias}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1, 3 marks]**

### Question 1
**Differentiate between DCVSL and DCSL logic styles. Which one has zero static power dissipation and why?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — word limit ≈ 80 words)

| Aspect | DCVSL | DCSL |
|--------|-------|------|
| Mode of operation | Voltage-mode, regenerative | Current-mode, steering |
| Output swing | Full rail-to-rail | Low swing ($\Delta V$) |
| Load | Cross-coupled PMOS | Resistors / diode loads |
| Static power | $\approx 0$ W | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$ (constant) |

**DCVSL has (ideally) zero static power** because the cross-coupled PMOS load ensures that exactly one PMOS is ON and the corresponding NMOS tree is OFF in every stable state — no DC path exists from $V_{DD}$ to ground.

> *(Marking scheme: 1 mark for each correct row of the table + 1 mark for the static power justification with the phrase "no DC path".)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q2, 3 marks]**

### Question 2
**A DCSL gate drives $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$ with a tail current of $I_{bias} = 50 \text{ μA}$ and load resistor $R_L = 6 \text{ k}\Omega$. Compute the output swing and propagation delay.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Output voltage swing:**

$$
\Delta V = I_{bias} \cdot R_L = 50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6 \times 10^{3} = 0.30 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation delay:**

$$
t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{2 \cdot I_{bias}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 0.30}{2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
t_p = \frac{15 \times 10^{-15}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 150 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s} = 150 \text{ ps}
$$

**Answer:** $\Delta V = 300 \text{ mV}$ and $t_p = 150 \text{ ps}$.

> *(Marking scheme: 1 mark for $\Delta V$, 1 mark for $t_p$ expression, 1 mark for numerical answer with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Candidates answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks  
**Explain the circuit structure and operation of a DCVSL gate with a neat block diagram. Show how the cross-coupled PMOS load achieves zero static power.**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks  
**For a DCVSL NAND2 gate with $C_L = 30 \text{ fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, $V_{TN} = 0.35 \text{ V}$, $K_N = 250 \text{ μA/V}^2$, $K_P = 100 \text{ μA/V}^2$, compute the average propagation delay. Assume $V_{TP} = -0.35 \text{ V}$.**

---

### QUESTION B (14 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks  
**Describe the architecture of Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL). Derive its static power dissipation and propagation delay expressions.**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks  
**Compare DCSL, DCVSL, and static CMOS in a tabular form covering at least six parameters. State one application each where each logic family is best suited.**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — DCVSL Structure and Operation (7 marks)

**Block diagram description (2 marks):**

A DCVSL gate consists of three blocks (see Section 4.1):

1. **NMOS pull-down tree A** — evaluates $f(x_1, \dots, x_n)$.
2. **NMOS pull-down tree B** — evaluates $\overline{f}(x_1, \dots, x_n)$.
3. **Cross-coupled PMOS load** — $M_{P1}$ and $M_{P2}$ with drains cross-connected to gates.

The differential outputs are taken at the drains of $M_{P1}$ and $M_{P2}$.

**Operational sequence (3 marks):**

- *Pre-evaluation:* Both outputs initially at $V_{DD}/2$ (metastable), PMOS pair balanced.
- *Input arrival:* Inputs $x_i, \overline{x_i}$ drive both trees simultaneously.
- *One tree conducts:* Either $f = 1$ or $\overline{f} = 1$, but never both. Say $f = 1$. Then Tree A pulls **Out** to GND.
- *Cross-coupling fires:* **Out** dropping turns ON $M_{P2}$ harder. $M_{P2}$ pulls **Out_b** to $V_{DD}$. Now $M_{P1}$ turns OFF because its gate (= **Out_b**) is at $V_{DD}$.
- *Stable state:* **Out = 0, Out_b = $V_{DD}$**. No DC current path.

**Why zero static power (2 marks):**

In the stable state, exactly one NMOS tree is OFF. The corresponding PMOS is ON, but it only drives a high-impedance node (gate of the opposite PMOS). The OFF NMOS tree blocks current from $V_{DD}$ to GND. Therefore $I_{DD,\,static} = 0$ and $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot 0 = 0$.

---

#### Part (b) — Average Propagation Delay Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{pHL}$:**

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot K_N \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{30 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot 250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1.2 - 0.35)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{36 \times 10^{-15}}{500 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (0.85)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{36 \times 10^{-15}}{500 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.7225}
$$

$$
= \frac{36 \times 10^{-15}}{361.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 99.65 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{pLH}$:**

$$
t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot K_P \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{30 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1.2 - 0.35)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{36 \times 10^{-15}}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.7225}
$$

$$
= \frac{36 \times 10^{-15}}{144.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 249.1 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 3 — Average propagation delay:**

$$
t_{p,DCVSL} = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} = \frac{99.65 + 249.1}{2} \approx 174.4 \text{ ps}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{p,DCVSL} \approx 174 \text{ ps}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Using $V_{TP}$ ($-0.35$ V) directly in the formula, which yields a negative term inside the square. Always use $\vert V_{TP} \vert = 0.35$ V.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that $K_P$ is roughly **half** of $K_N$ due to hole mobility — using the same $K$ for both gives an incorrect symmetric delay.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting delay in **ns** instead of **ps**, or omitting units entirely. Always include the unit.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — DCSL Architecture and Derivation (7 marks)

**Architecture (2 marks):**

A DCSL stage has four components (see Section 4.2):

1. **Tail current source** — a saturated NMOS transistor $M_0$ with $V_{GS}$ set by a bias generator; sinks $I_{bias}$ to GND.
2. **Differential pair** — $M_1$ and $M_2$ share a common source node. Their gates receive differential inputs $V_{in}^{+}$ and $V_{in}^{-}$.
3. **Load elements** — typically diode-connected PMOS or poly resistors $R_L$ at each drain.
4. **Output nodes** — $V_{out}^{+}$ and $V_{out}^{-}$, taken at the drains.

**Static power derivation (2.5 marks):**

The tail current source conducts $I_{bias}$ continuously as long as $V_{DD}$ is present:

$$
P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}
$$

This is **unconditional** — it does not depend on switching activity. Hence DCSL is a **constant-power** logic style.

**Propagation delay derivation (2.5 marks):**

Assume $V_{in}^{+} - V_{in}^{-}$ is large enough that all of $I_{bias}$ flows through $M_1$. The differential output swing is:

$$
\Delta V = I_{bias} \cdot R_L
$$

The time to charge the load capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $\Delta V / 2$ at constant current $I_{bias}$ is:

$$
t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{2 \cdot I_{bias}} = \frac{C_L \cdot R_L}{2}
$$

These two equations are the **canonical DCSL design equations** and appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on this topic.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Static CMOS | DCVSL | DCSL |
|-----------|-------------|-------|------|
| Output Swing | Full rail-to-rail | Full rail-to-rail | Low ($\Delta V$) |
| Static Power | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$ |
| Delay | Moderate | Fast | Very fast |
| Complementary Outputs | Needs inverter | Free | Free |
| Transistor Count | $2N$ | $N + 4$ | Differential pair + bias |
| Noise Immunity | Highest | High | Moderate (due to low swing) |
| Power–Delay Product | $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} / 2$ | $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} / 2$ | $C_L \cdot \Delta V \cdot V_{DD} / 2$ |
| Routing Overhead | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Best Suited For | General logic | Datapath cells, adders | Sense amps, low-swing clocks |

**Application commentary (3 marks):**

- **Static CMOS** is best suited for **general-purpose random logic**, control units, and low-activity blocks where standby power must be near zero.
- **DCVSL** is best suited for **high-speed arithmetic circuits** such as full adders, multipliers, and comparators where the built-in complementary outputs and regenerative load reduce transistor count and speed up critical paths.
- **DCSL** is best suited for **sense amplifiers in SRAM macros, low-swing clock distribution, and mixed-signal interfaces** where low-voltage differential signalling (LVDS-like behaviour) and constant power draw are acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing DCSL and DCVSL — students frequently write "DCVSL has constant power $V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$", which is the property of **DCSL**. DCVSL has zero static power.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Writing "DCSL has zero static power" — this is incorrect; DCSL always dissipates $V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Not specifying the **application** for each family. The examiner awards 1 mark per correctly justified application. A vague answer like *"DCSL is used in processors"* loses the mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Things You MUST Remember Before Entering the Exam Hall**

1. **DCVSL** = Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic → voltage-mode, regenerative.
2. **DCSL** = Differential Current Switch Logic → current-mode, steering.
3. DCVSL uses a **cross-coupled PMOS load**; DCSL uses a **tail current source** with load resistors.
4. DCVSL output is **full rail-to-rail**; DCSL output is **low swing** ($\Delta V \approx 200$–$400$ mV).
5. DCVSL has **zero static power** (ideal); DCSL has **constant static power** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{bias}$.
6. Both styles produce **complementary outputs for free** (no extra inverter needed).
7. DCVSL logic function is implemented entirely in the **NMOS pull-down tree**, reducing transistor count vs static CMOS.
8. DCVSL noise margins are **symmetric** at approximately $V_{DD}/8$ each.
9. DCVSL propagation delay: $t_{pHL} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 K_N (V_{DD}-V_{TN})^{2}}$.
10. DCVSL pull-up delay: $t_{pLH} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 K_P (V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP} \vert)^{2}}$.
11. DCSL propagation delay: $t_p \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{2 \cdot I_{bias}}$.
12. DCSL Power-Delay Product: $PDP = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L \Delta V V_{DD}$ — independent of $I_{bias}$!
13. **Power savings ratio** of DCSL over static CMOS = $\Delta V / V_{DD}$.
14. DCVSL is preferred for **arithmetic datapaths**; DCSL is preferred for **sense amplifiers and low-swing clocks**.
15. Always use **$\vert V_{TP} \vert$** in delay formulas — never plug in a negative value inside a square.

> **Final KTU Tip:** If the question asks *"compare and contrast"*, use a **two-column table** with at least **6 parameters**. If it asks *"derive"*, start from **Kirchhoff's current law** or **charge-balance** and explicitly show every algebraic step. The examiners award **method marks** generously, so even if your final number is wrong, you can still secure 60–70 % of the marks by writing the correct approach.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
