## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1a]**
Define the term *adiabatic switching* in the context of CMOS VLSI design. Why is the term "adiabatic" used?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adiabatic switching is a low-power CMOS design technique in which the **load capacitance is charged and discharged using a time-varying (ramping) power-clock supply** $\phi(t)$ instead of a fixed DC $V_{DD}$. The term *adiabatic* originates from thermodynamics (Greek: *impassable to heat*), meaning **no energy is exchanged with the environment as heat** during the switching transition. In VLSI, an adiabatic transition is one in which **all energy stored on the capacitor is returned to the power-clock** at the end of the cycle. The energy dissipated per cycle is reduced from the conventional $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ to $\xi \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ where $\xi = \tfrac{RC_L}{T} \ll 1$. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1, Q1c]**
List any **three differences** between conventional CMOS logic and adiabatic logic gates.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Aspect | Conventional CMOS | Adiabatic Logic |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Power supply | Constant DC $V_{DD}$ | Time-varying ramp $\phi(t)$ |
| 2. Energy per cycle | $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | $\xi \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ |
| 3. Charge recovery | Not possible — energy dumped as heat | Yes — returned to the power-clock |

**[3 marks]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Q8]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the energy dissipated when a load capacitance $C_L$ is charged through a resistor $R$ from a **linearly ramping power-clock** $\phi(t) = \tfrac{V_{DD}}{T} t$ over a time interval $T$. Show that the dissipation is proportional to $\tfrac{RC_L}{T}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **2N-2N2P adiabatic inverter**. Discuss its advantages over ECRL. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks] — Adiabatic Energy Derivation**

**Step 1 — KVL for the charging loop** [1 mark]:

$$\phi(t) = i(t) \cdot R + v_C(t)$$

with $i(t) = C_L \tfrac{dv_C}{dt}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $\phi(t)$** [1 mark]:
$$\frac{V_{DD}}{T} t = R C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the slow-switching limit $T \gg RC_L$** [1 mark]:
In this limit the capacitor voltage tracks the supply:

$$v_C(t) \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{T} t = \phi(t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the charging current** [1 mark]:
$$i(t) = C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} \approx C_L \frac{V_{DD}}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Instantaneous power in $R$** [1 mark]:
$$p_R(t) = i^2 R = \frac{R C_L^{2} V_{DD}^{2}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate over $0 \to T$** [1 mark]:
$$E_{adiab} = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{R C_L^{2} V_{DD}^{2}}{T^{2}} dt = \frac{R C_L^{2} V_{DD}^{2}}{T}$$

**Step 7 — Factor into canonical form** [1 mark]:
$$E_{adiab} = \frac{R C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \xi \cdot E_{conv}$$

where $\xi = \tfrac{RC_L}{T}$ is the adiabatic loss factor. As $T \to \infty$, $E_{adiab} \to 0$. **∎**

---

**(b) [7 marks] — 2N-2N2P Inverter**

**Circuit description** [3 marks]:
The 2N-2N2P (two-NMOS, two-NMOS, two-PMOS) adiabatic inverter consists of:
- Two PMOS pull-up transistors (P1, P2) connected to the power-clock $\phi$ at their sources.
- Two NMOS pull-down transistors (N1, N2) forming the logic tree.
- Cross-coupled complementary outputs **OUT** and **$\overline{\text{OUT}}$**.
- A buffer-like structure that is functionally equivalent to a CMOS inverter.

**Operation** [2 marks]:
- When $\text{IN} = 1$ (high), N1 conducts and pulls **OUT** low while P1 is OFF. Meanwhile P2 conducts (with $\overline{\text{IN}} = 0$) and pulls **$\overline{\text{OUT}}$** up to $\phi$. Energy stored on the **OUT** capacitance is returned to $\phi$ during the ramp-down.
- When $\text{IN} = 0$, the symmetric operation occurs.

**Advantages over ECRL** [2 marks]:
1. **No non-adiabatic loss at diode junctions** — full charge recovery to the supply.
2. **Lower $V_{th}$-induced loss** since the output swings fully from 0 to $V_{DD}$.
3. **Better noise immunity** due to cross-coupled complementary structure.
4. **Slightly higher area** is the trade-off.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Q9]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Quasi-Adiabatic Logic**. Compare its energy dissipation behaviour with that of fully adiabatic logic. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a **trapezoidal power-clock waveform**, describe the four phases of operation of an adiabatic gate. Why is a slow ramp preferred over an abrupt $V_{DD}$ step? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks] — Quasi-Adiabatic Logic**

**Definition** [2 marks]: Quasi-adiabatic logic families (e.g., **ECRL — Efficient Charge Recovery Logic**, **QAL — Quasi-Adiabatic Logic**) use a **single power-clock** and a **simpler structure** than fully adiabatic designs, but **do not achieve full charge recovery**. A fraction of the stored energy is dissipated as a **non-adiabatic loss** whenever a transistor with a $V_{th}$ drop is abruptly switched off.

**Energy equation** [2 marks]:
$$E_{quasi} = \xi \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{th}^{2}$$

The second term ($\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{th}^{2}$) is the **non-adiabatic floor** that limits the minimum energy achievable.

**Comparison with fully adiabatic** [3 marks]:

| Aspect | Quasi-Adiabatic | Fully Adiabatic |
|---|---|---|
| Charge recovery | Partial | Full |
| Energy floor | $\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{th}^{2}$ | Theoretically zero |
| Clock phases | 1 (trapezoidal) | 4 (quadrature) |
| Circuit complexity | Low | High |
| Practical minimum energy | Higher | Lower (at low $f$) |

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Four Phases of an Adiabatic Gate**

**Phase 1 — Wait** [1 mark]: All clocks are low. The output node is held at the previous value.

**Phase 2 — Evaluate (ramp-up)** [2 marks]: The power-clock $\phi$ ramps from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ over time $T$. The conducting transistor pair charges the output capacitance slowly. Energy dissipated in the ON-resistance is $\xi \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.

**Phase 3 — Hold** [1 mark]: $\phi$ stays at $V_{DD}$. The output is stable and can drive the next stage.

**Phase 4 — Recovery (ramp-down)** [2 marks]: $\phi$ ramps back from $V_{DD}$ to $0$. The energy stored on $C_L$ is **returned through the PMOS to the power-clock**, achieving charge recovery.

**Why slow ramp?** [1 mark]: A slow ramp limits the **charging current** $i = C_L \tfrac{d\phi}{dt}$, keeping $i^2 R$ dissipation small. An abrupt step would require an impulsive (infinite) current and dissipate the full $\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2}$ — identical to conventional CMOS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in fully adiabatic designs** — the energy $\xi C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ applies to a *full charge+recovery* cycle, while $\xi \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is for *one direction only*. Examiners routinely deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Confusing quasi- with fully adiabatic** — the $V_{th}^{2}$ floor term **does not vanish** in quasi designs. If a question explicitly says "fully adiabatic", do not include it.
> 3. **Writing $E = \tfrac{1}{2} CV^2$ for adiabatic logic** — this is the conventional CMOS result and is **wrong** for adiabatic. Always include $\xi$.
> 4. **Drawing the power-clock as a square wave instead of a ramp/trapezoid** — the ramp is the **defining feature** of adiabatic logic. Square-wave clocked logic is **not** adiabatic.
> 5. **Mixing up 2N-2N2P with ECRL** — ECRL is a 2-transistor logic-block quasi-adiabatic structure; 2N-2N2P uses 4 transistors in a cross-coupled fully-adiabatic configuration.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adiabatic** means **no heat loss** to the environment — energy is *recycled*, not destroyed.
- Conventional CMOS burns **$\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ per switch**, independent of speed.
- Adiabatic charging with a **ramp clock** burns only **$\xi \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$** where $\xi = \tfrac{RC_L}{T}$.
- Adiabatic **power $\propto f^2$**, conventional **power $\propto f$** — adiabatic wins only at low frequency.
- **Two loss types**: (i) resistive adiabatic loss (vanishes with slow clock), (ii) non-adiabatic $V_{th}$ loss (irreducible in quasi designs).
- **Logic families to know**: ECRL, 2N-2N2P, PFAL, QAL, TSEL.
- **2N-2N2P** is fully adiabatic; **ECRL** is quasi-adiabatic.
- **Power-clock** must be **trapezoidal or sinusoidal**, never square.
- **Minimum supply constraint**: $V_{DD} > 2 V_{th}$ for rail-to-rail output swing.
- **Best application domains**: RFID, IoT sensors, biomedical implants, energy-harvesting systems.
- **Trade-off**: Lower switching energy ↔ Larger area, more clock phases, lower operating frequency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulsed Power Supplies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT741 - LOW POWER VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pulsed Power Supplies # Pulsed Power Supplies — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pulsed Power Supply (PPS)** in the context of Low Power VLSI Design refers to a power delivery architecture that delivers electrical energy to a load (such as a digital sub-system, analog block, or sensor front-end) in the form of **discrete, time-bounded pulses** rather than as a continuous, steady DC waveform. The supply alternates between a high-energy "ON" (charge/pulse) state and a low-energy "OFF" (idle/quiescent) state, typically switched at frequencies ranging from **kHz to MHz**, with pulse widths varying from **nanoseconds to milliseconds**, depending on the application domain. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology aligned with **Course Outcome CO4** of *PEEVT741*, a Pulsed Power Supply is the enabling hardware that supports: - **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** - **Power Gating (PG)** with rapid wake-up - **Sub-threshold and Near-Threshold Computing** with pulsed body biasing - **Energy Harvesting Systems** with pulsed load regulation The governing metric for any PPS is the **Energy per Pulse (E_p)** delivered to the load, and the **Average Power (P_avg)**, defined as: $$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{p}}{T_{rep}}$$ where $T_{rep}$ is the pulse repetition period and $E_p$ is the energy per pulse. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Shower Faucet vs. The Bucket** > > Imagine you need to wash a small dish. You have two options: > > **(a) Continuous Water Supply (Conventional DC Supply):** You leave the tap running the entire time you are in the kitchen, even when you are not actively washing. This wastes enormous amounts of water (energy) because most of it just flows unused into the drain. > > **(b) Pulsed Water Supply (Pulsed Power Supply):** You turn the tap ON only for the **2 seconds** you actively need water, then turn it OFF completely. You only repeat this when another dish arrives. The total water used is **drastically lower**, even though each "pulse" of water has the same pressure (voltage). > > A **Pulsed Power Supply** does exactly the same thing for an electronic circuit. Instead of continuously powering a block (and burning static leakage power), it provides short, intense bursts of energy **only when the block is actually computing**, and then goes completely idle. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following are the **mandatory design parameters** a KTU examiner expects a student to recognize: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Pulse Width | $\tau$ | **1 ns – 10 ms** | Duration of active energy delivery | | Repetition Period | $T_{rep}$ | **1 µs – 1 s** | Time between successive pulses | | Duty Cycle | $D$ | **1% – 50%** | $D = \tau / T_{rep}$ | | Peak Pulse Voltage | $V_{p}$ | **0.5 V – 5 V** | Voltage during ON state | | Peak Pulse Current | $I_{p}$ | **1 mA – 10 A** | Maximum current delivered | | Energy per Pulse | $E_p$ | **µJ – mJ** | Total energy in one pulse | | Storage Capacitance | $C_{store}$ | **1 nF – 1 mF** | Energy reservoir | | Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **100 kHz – 10 MHz** | Pulse repetition rate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The *single most important figure of merit* for a PPS in low-power VLSI is the **duty cycle $D$**, because it directly determines how much leakage and quiescent power is *eliminated* during the OFF state. A duty cycle of **1%** means the system consumes energy only **1/100th** of the time compared to continuous operation. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principles — The Pulse Generation Lifecycle A Pulsed Power Supply operates in **three sequential phases**. A student must internalize these phases because KTU 2024 scheme questions frequently test the lifecycle: ### Phase 1 — Energy Storage (Charging Phase) During this phase, a **primary energy source** (battery, harvested energy, mains adapter) transfers energy into an intermediate storage element. The dominant storage elements are: - **Capacitors** — for fast, high-power pulses (µs to ms range) - **Inductors** — for moderate-duration pulses with high energy density - **Supercapacitors / Battery buffers** — for sub-Hz pulsed loads - **Micro-batteries with charge pumps** — for ultra-low-power sensor nodes For a capacitor-based storage element, the stored energy is given by: $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_{store} V_{store}^{2}$$ ### Phase 2 — Pulse Delivery (Discharge Phase) A **high-speed semiconductor switch** (typically a MOSFET such as the BS170, or in IC form, a transmission gate / power switch) connects the storage element to the load for a precisely controlled duration $\tau$. The switching device must satisfy three constraints: - **Low ON-resistance** ($R_{ds,on}$) to minimize conduction losses - **Fast switching times** ($t_{rise}, t_{fall}$) to produce clean pulse edges - **High peak current rating** ($I_{D,max}$) ### Phase 3 — Recovery (Idle Phase) After the pulse, the switch opens, the load is **electrically isolated**, and the storage element begins recharging for the next cycle. During this phase, leakage currents in both the switch and the load are minimized. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **every equation** a student is likely to be tested on for Module 4 — Pulsed Power Supplies. **Memorize these.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in capacitor | Joules | | 2 | $E_{delivered} = V_{p}^{2} \cdot \tau / R_{L}$ | Energy delivered per pulse to resistive load | Joules | | 3 | $P_{avg} = \tfrac{E_{p}}{T_{rep}}$ | Average power consumption | Watts | | 4 | $P_{peak} = V_{p}^{2} / R_{L}$ | Instantaneous peak power during pulse | Watts | | 5 | $D = \tfrac{\tau}{T_{rep}}$ | Duty Cycle (dimensionless, 0 to 1) | — | | 6 | $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{peak}$ | Average power in terms of duty cycle | Watts | | 7 | $\eta = \tfrac{E_{load}}{E_{total}}$ | Energy Efficiency of PPS | — | | 8 | $I_{peak} = C \cdot \tfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Peak discharge current | Amperes | | 9 | $V_{p}(t) = V_{0} e^{-t/RC}$ | Capacitor discharge waveform | Volts | | 10 | $Q = C \cdot V$ | Total charge delivered per pulse | Coulombs | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Pulsed power supplies are the **backbone of modern low-power IoT devices**, including BLE wearables, RFID tags, energy-harvesting sensor nodes, and the on-chip power gating networks of multi-core processors. The same principles apply to **medical implants** (pacemakers), **automotive sensor clusters**, and **satellite power buses**, where continuous operation is impossible and every microjoule counts. ## 2.3 Why Pulsed Supplies Reduce Power in VLSI The total power consumed by a CMOS VLSI block has **two dominant components**: $$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$$ - **Dynamic power** ($P_{dynamic} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$) occurs only during switching transitions. - **Static (leakage) power** ($P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$) flows **continuously** even when the circuit is idle. A pulsed supply directly attacks $P_{static}$. By cutting $V_{DD}$ to zero (or near zero) during idle intervals, the leakage current collapses exponentially (sub-threshold leakage scales as $10^{-V_{th}/S}$, where $S$ is the sub-threshold swing). The **effective average static power** becomes: $$P_{static,avg} = D \cdot P_{static,continuous}$$ For a 1% duty cycle, this is a **100× reduction** in leakage power. ## 2.4 Switching Topologies — Three Canonical Architectures KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare or draw these topologies: 1. **Series Switch PPS** — A switch in series between the source and the load. Simple, but suffers from $I^{2}R$ conduction losses in the switch. 2. **Shunt Switch PPS** — A switch in parallel with the load, used to dump stored energy into the load rapidly. Common in pulsed laser diode drivers. 3. **H-Bridge / Bidirectional PPS** — Used for loads requiring both charging and discharging phases (e.g., piezoelectric transducers, MEMS actuators). # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitor Discharge into a Resistive Load This is the **single most important derivation** for Pulsed Power Supplies. The KTU examiner expects every student to be able to reproduce it from memory. > **Problem Setup:** A storage capacitor $C$ is initially charged to voltage $V_0$. At $t = 0$, a switch closes, connecting the capacitor to a resistive load $R_L$. Derive the discharge current waveform $i(t)$ and the energy delivered to the load. ### Step 1 — Write Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) Going around the loop: source voltage equals the sum of the resistive drop and the capacitor voltage. $$V_C(t) = i(t) \cdot R_L$$ ### Step 2 — Express the capacitor voltage in terms of charge The fundamental capacitor relation is $Q = C \cdot V_C$, so $V_C = Q/C$. Differentiating: $$\frac{dV_C}{dt} = \frac{1}{C}\frac{dQ}{dt}$$ ### Step 3 — Recognize that current is the rate of charge flow By definition, the discharge current is: $$i(t) = -\frac{dQ}{dt}$$ (The negative sign arises because charge is leaving the capacitor.) ### Step 4 — Combine into a first-order ODE Substituting into KVL: $$\frac{1}{C}\frac{dQ}{dt} = -\frac{Q}{R_L \cdot C}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -\frac{Q}{R_L \cdot C}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve the differential equation by separation of variables $$\frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{dt}{R_L C}$$ Integrating both sides from $Q_0 = C V_0$ at $t = 0$ to $Q(t)$ at time $t$: $$\int_{Q_0}^{Q(t)} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dt}{R_L C}$$ This yields: $$\ln\left[\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0}\right] = -\frac{t}{R_L C}$$ ### Step 6 — Solve for $Q(t)$ and convert back to $V_C(t)$ $$Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-t / (R_L C)} = C V_0 \, e^{-t / \tau_{RC}}$$ where $\tau_{RC} = R_L C$ is the **RC time constant**. Therefore the capacitor voltage is: $$V_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$ ### Step 7 — Compute the discharge current Using $i(t) = -\frac{dQ}{dt}$: $$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{R_L} \, e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$ ### Step 8 — Compute the energy delivered to the load up to time $T$ $$E_{load}(T) = \int_{0}^{T} i^{2}(t) \cdot R_L \, dt = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{V_0^{2}}{R_L} \, e^{-2t/(R_L C)} \, dt$$ Evaluating the integral: $$\begin{aligned} E_{load}(T) &= \frac{V_0^{2}}{R_L} \left[ -\frac{R_L C}{2} \, e^{-2t/(R_L C)} \right]_{0}^{T} \\ &= \frac{V_0^{2} C}{2} \left[ 1 - e^{-2T/(R_L C)} \right] \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 9 — Examine the asymptotic limit ($T \to \infty$) $$\lim_{T \to \infty} E_{load}(T) = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^{2}$$ This is a beautiful and **critical result**: when the capacitor is *fully* discharged through a resistor, **exactly half the stored energy** is delivered to the load, and the **other half is dissipated as heat inside the source resistance** (or in the capacitor's equivalent series resistance). This 50% theoretical limit is the single most important efficiency ceiling for any capacitor-based PPS and is often asked in viva voce. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Power Reduction from Duty Cycling > **Problem Setup:** A VLSI block consumes a constant peak power $P_{peak}$ when active. It is now driven by a pulsed supply with pulse width $\tau$ and repetition period $T_{rep}$. Show that the average power is reduced by a factor of $D = \tau/T_{rep}$. ### Step 1 — Define average power rigorously $$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{rep}} \int_{0}^{T_{rep}} p(t) \, dt$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the pulsed waveform During the pulse ($0 \leq t < \tau$), $p(t) = P_{peak}$. Outside the pulse ($\tau \leq t < T_{rep}$), $p(t) \approx 0$ (ideal switch). ### Step 3 — Evaluate the integral $$\begin{aligned} P_{avg} &= \frac{1}{T_{rep}} \left[ \int_{0}^{\tau} P_{peak} \, dt + \int_{\tau}^{T_{rep}} 0 \, dt \right] \\ &= \frac{1}{T_{rep}} \cdot P_{peak} \cdot \tau \\ &= P_{peak} \cdot \frac{\tau}{T_{rep}} \\ &= P_{peak} \cdot D \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Interpret the result For $D = 0.01$ (1% duty cycle), $P_{avg} = 0.01 \cdot P_{peak}$, a **99% reduction** in average power. This is the entire justification for pulsed supplies in low-power VLSI. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example Consider a wireless sensor node operating from a 3.3 V supply, with a peak current draw of 50 mA during an active transmission burst of 200 µs, repeated every 100 ms. | Quantity | Computation | Value | |---|---|---| | Peak Power $P_{peak}$ | $3.3 \cdot 0.050$ | **0.165 W** | | Pulse Width $\tau$ | given | **200 µs** | | Period $T_{rep}$ | given | **100 ms** | | Duty Cycle $D$ | $200\mu / 100m$ | **0.002 (0.2%)** | | Average Power $P_{avg}$ | $D \cdot P_{peak}$ | **330 µW** | | Energy per Pulse $E_p$ | $P_{peak} \cdot \tau$ | **33 µJ** | | Required Storage Capacitor | $\frac{2 E_p}{V^2}$ | **6.06 µF** | > [!IMPORTANT] > This numerical matrix is the kind of calculation a KTU 14-mark question demands. Always show the units explicitly and round only at the final step. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pulsed Power Supply The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete signal and energy flow** through a generic PPS used in low-power VLSI systems. Missing any of these guarantees a loss of at least 1–2 marks. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Q: Define a Pulsed Power Supply. Why is it preferred over a continuous DC supply in low-power VLSI systems?** **Model Answer:** A Pulsed Power Supply (PPS) is a power delivery system that provides energy to a load in the form of discrete, time-bounded pulses rather than as a steady DC voltage. It is preferred over a continuous supply in low-power VLSI because: 1. **Leakage Power Reduction:** CMOS sub-threshold leakage current flows whenever $V_{DD}$ is applied. Pulsing the supply cuts $V_{DD}$ to zero during idle intervals, eliminating leakage during OFF time and reducing average static power by a factor equal to the duty cycle $D$. 2. **Energy Efficiency:** Energy is delivered only when the circuit is performing useful work, avoiding wasted quiescent consumption. 3. **Compatibility with Energy Harvesting:** Pulsed loads match the bursty nature of harvested energy from sources like solar, piezoelectric, or RF harvesters. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: List the three operational phases of a Pulsed Power Supply and state the role of the energy storage element.** **Model Answer:** The three operational phases are: 1. **Charging Phase** — energy is transferred from the primary source into the storage element. 2. **Discharge Phase** — the storage element releases its stored energy into the load through a controlled switch for a duration $\tau$. 3. **Recovery (Idle) Phase** — the switch is opened, the load is isolated, and the storage element recharges. The **role of the energy storage element** (typically a capacitor) is to decouple the slow primary source from the fast load transients. It acts as a **local energy reservoir** that can supply high instantaneous currents to the load during the pulse without requiring the primary source to react instantly. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pulsed Power Supply Questions** > Examiners consistently deduct marks for the following mistakes. **Avoid them at all costs:** > - **Forgetting to state the assumption** of an ideal switch when deriving the discharge current (always explicitly say "assuming ideal MOSFET with $R_{ds,on} = 0$"). > - **Mixing up $E_{stored}$ and $E_{delivered}$** in capacitor problems. Remember the **50% theoretical efficiency ceiling** for capacitor discharge into a resistor. > - **Not mentioning the duty cycle $D$** when discussing average power — it is the central concept of the entire module. > - **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** showing energy/signal flow direction. > - **Skipping units** in numerical problems. Every numerical answer without units is penalized. ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operating principle of a Pulsed Power Supply used in low-power VLSI systems. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A Pulsed Power Supply for low-power VLSI consists of five functional blocks: **Block 1 — Primary Energy Source:** Provides the raw input energy. In portable VLSI, this is typically a lithium-ion battery (3.0–4.2 V) or an energy-harvesting transducer (solar cell, piezoelectric element, RF antenna). **Block 2 — Energy Storage Element:** A capacitor $C_{store}$ (typically 1 µF to 100 µF in IC-compatible designs, or larger for board-level systems) is charged from the source through a regulator. It stores energy as $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$. **Block 3 — Pulse Control Unit:** A timer circuit, microcontroller, or PWM generator that produces a gate drive signal with controllable pulse width $\tau$ and period $T_{rep}$. **Block 4 — High-Speed Switch:** A power MOSFET (e.g., NMOS in 0.18 µm CMOS) whose gate is driven by the control unit. In the ON state, it connects the storage element to the load. In the OFF state, it isolates the load, eliminating leakage paths. **Block 5 — VLSI Load:** The sub-system being powered — could be a processor core, RF transmitter, sensor readout, or analog front-end. **Operating Principle:** During the charging phase, $C_{store}$ accumulates energy from the source. When the pulse control unit triggers the switch ON, the capacitor discharges into the load for a precise duration $\tau$. The load receives a voltage pulse $V_p$ with peak power $V_p^2/R_L$ during this interval. After $\tau$ seconds, the switch opens and the load is de-energized. The average power delivered is $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{peak}$, where $D = \tau/T_{rep}$ is the duty cycle. This architecture is the foundation of all power-gated and duty-cycled VLSI systems. **(b)** A wireless sensor node requires a burst current of 80 mA at 2.5 V for 500 µs every 200 ms. Calculate: (i) the duty cycle, (ii) the peak power, (iii) the average power, (iv) the energy per pulse, and (v) the minimum storage capacitance assuming the capacitor is charged to 3.3 V. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Duty Cycle:** $$D = \frac{\tau}{T_{rep}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.25\%$$ **(ii) Peak Power:** $$P_{peak} = V_{p} \cdot I_{p} = 2.5 \times 0.080 = 0.200 \text{ W} = 200 \text{ mW}$$ **(iii) Average Power:** $$P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{peak} = 0.0025 \times 0.200 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 500 \text{ µW}$$ **(iv) Energy per Pulse:** $$E_{p} = P_{peak} \cdot \tau = 0.200 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 100 \text{ µJ}$$ **(v) Minimum Storage Capacitance:** Using $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$, and assuming all stored energy is available to the load: $$C_{min} = \frac{2 E_{p}}{V^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{(3.3)^{2}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} C_{min} &= \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{10.89} \\ &= 1.837 \times 10^{-5} \text{ F} \\ &= 18.37 \text{ µF} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore, a standard **22 µF** capacitor would be selected to provide design margin. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Alternative, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the discharge current of a capacitor $C$ initially charged to voltage $V_0$ when connected to a resistive load $R_L$ through an ideal switch. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** At $t = 0$, the switch closes. Applying KVL around the loop: $$V_C(t) = i(t) \cdot R_L$$ Using $i(t) = -C \frac{dV_C}{dt}$ (current flows out of the capacitor): $$V_C(t) = -R_L C \frac{dV_C}{dt}$$ Rearranging as a separable differential equation: $$\frac{dV_C}{V_C} = -\frac{dt}{R_L C}$$ Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $V_C = V_0$) to time $t$: $$\ln\left[\frac{V_C(t)}{V_0}\right] = -\frac{t}{R_L C}$$ Exponentiating: $$V_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$ The discharge current is therefore: $$i(t) = \frac{V_C(t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_0}{R_L} \, e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$ This shows the current **decays exponentially** with time constant $\tau_{RC} = R_L C$. **(b)** Explain with a numerical example how the **average power** consumed by a VLSI block can be reduced through duty cycling, and state the **maximum theoretical efficiency** of a capacitor-based pulsed power supply discharging into a resistive load. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** When a VLSI block is operated with a pulsed supply of duty cycle $D$, the average power is: $$P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{peak}$$ **Numerical Example:** A processor core consumes 1 W when active ($P_{peak} = 1$ W). If it is duty-cycled with $\tau = 100$ µs active every $T_{rep} = 10$ ms, then: $$D = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01 = 1\%$$ $$P_{avg} = 0.01 \times 1 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ mW}$$ This represents a **99% reduction** in power consumption, demonstrating the dramatic effectiveness of pulsed supplies. **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency:** For a capacitor-based PPS discharging into a resistive load, the energy delivered to the load (resistor) and the energy dissipated in the equivalent series resistance (ESR) of the capacitor are *equal* in the limit of complete discharge. Therefore: $$\eta_{max} = \frac{E_{load}}{E_{stored}} = \frac{0.5 \cdot C V_0^{2}}{C V_0^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 50\%$$ The maximum theoretical energy efficiency is **50%**. This is a fundamental limit imposed by the exponential discharge profile. Practical systems use *inductor-based* or *resonant* topologies to exceed this limit by recovering energy from the load reactance. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pulsed Power Supplies** > > ✅ A **Pulsed Power Supply** delivers energy in time-bounded pulses, not as continuous DC. > > ✅ The **duty cycle** $D = \tau / T_{rep}$ is the central design parameter that determines average power savings. > > ✅ Average power = $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{peak}$; for $D = 1\%$, leakage power drops by **100×**. > > ✅ A PPS has **three phases**: charging, discharge (pulse), and recovery (idle). > > ✅ Capacitor stored energy: $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$. > > ✅ Capacitor discharge into resistor: $V_C(t) = V_0 e^{-t/(R_L C)}$, $i(t) = (V_0/R_L) e^{-t/(R_L C)}$. > > ✅ **Maximum theoretical efficiency** of capacitor discharge into resistor = **50%**. > > ✅ Essential blocks: Source → Storage → Switch → Load, with a Pulse Control Unit. > > ✅ Pulsed supplies primarily target **static (leakage) power**, while dynamic power remains roughly the same during each pulse. > > ✅ Three topologies: Series Switch, Shunt Switch, and H-Bridge (Bidirectional). > > ✅ The peak discharge current is $I_{peak} = V_0 / R_L$ (instantaneous at switch closure). > > ✅ Real-world applications: IoT sensors, RFID tags, medical implants, on-chip power gating, satellite power systems. > > ✅ Always state the **assumption of an ideal switch** ($R_{ds,on} = 0$, infinite OFF resistance) in derivations unless otherwise specified.
